sections.title: Welcome to the World's End
sections.body:
This is the setting guide for *Stonetop*, a hearth fantasy roleplaying game set in an iron age that never was. It describes the PCs' home the village of Stonetop—and the World's End, the region in which Stonetop sits. It's a gazetteer of the places, peoples, and powers that make up or shape the region.

This is *Book II*; for the rules of the game and guidance on how to play or run it, see *Book I: Stonetop.*

sections.title: How to use this book
sections.body:
If you're the GM, or thinking about running a game of Stonetop, then this book is for you.

Before your first session, browse this book to familiarize yourself with the setting and to get excited for play. Start with the **[[01 - Welcome to the World's End#**Should the players read this?**|maps]]** to get a sense of place.

Skim the **village of Stonetop** entry (page [[02 - The Village of Stonetop#The village of Stonetop|12]]). When it mentions a topic that catches your interest, jump to that topic

Topics are cross-referenced. If you're reading this as a physical book, use a bookmark or two to keep track of where you came from. If you're reading this as a PDF, all the page references are hyperlinked and the "back" button (or ALT + left arrow in most PDF readers) is your friend.

Between sessions, review topics that you think will be relevant soon. Use the details and resources therein to help you prep.

During play, refer to this book to help you describe the environment, to ask questions, and to make GM moves. Use it to introduce dangers and discoveries, or to improvise sites for the PCs to explore.

sections.title: Exploit the setting guide
sections.body:
This book is a resource, not a bible. It asserts many truths about the setting, but it also poses many questions for you and your group to answer during play. When you

establish some truth that contradicts what's written in the book, your group's truths take precedence.

Use whatever appeals to you and makes sense for your game. Ignore or change the rest. If you think the **[[21 - The Golden Oak#The Golden Oak|Golden Oak]]** would be better as a sacred place out of time, where past and present and future all meet, then by all means make it so in your game.

sections.title: Should the players read this?
sections.body:
Players aren't expected to read this book. There's a Setting Overview in the game's digital files (also downloadable from lampblackandbrimstone.com). Players should definitely read that.

**It's okay for players to read this book if they want to.** Some players like to better understand the setting, to help shape their play or inform their answers to GM questions. Some enjoy that feeling of dread when they recognize what monster the GM is hinting at, or that feeling of awe when the GM introduces an arcanum that they've read about.

Just remember that every group's version of *Stonetop* is different. If you're playing in a game and you've read this book, don't be a snob about it. The lore that you and your group establish in play is what's important.

And to be clear: if you're the kind of player who prefers to discover a game's world through play, to experience it like your character would, then don't read this book. You're certainly not expected to!

sections.title: The village of Stonetop
sections.body: **Size** village (~300 souls)

sections.title: Population +0
sections.body:
- **Prosperity** +0
  - Trade with... ... Gordin's Delve (metal, tools)
    - ... Marshedge (textiles, herbs, glass)

sections.title: Resources
sections.body:
- Farming (beans, potatoes, oats, barley)
- Hunting/trapping (fur, meat, hides)
- Distilling (whisky)
- Stone (taken from the Old Wall)
- Cistern (filled with rain, snow)
- Tradesfolk (midwife, potter, publican, smith, tanner)
- Two draft horses, two iron plows, two carts, a wagon

sections.title: Defenses +0
sections.body:
- Village militia
- The Ringwall (low, stone)
- Three watchtowers
- Spears & shields in every home
- Some bows

A farming village on the eastern edge of the **[[14 - The Flats#The Flats|Flats]]**, on a bluff overlooking the **[[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|Great Wood]]**. A great, rune-etched megalith stands amidst a cluster of squat stone homes, surrounded by an arc of farmland.

The PCs call this village home. It's a tightknit community, poor but a good place to live. Everyone is expected to pull their weight, but few think badly of those who suffer misfortune. The town pulls together; its community is its strength.

sections.title: Places
sections.body:
- The **Stone** is well over 50 feet tall and etched in faded runes. It seems older than even the **Maker**-ruins [[30 - The Makers|The Makers]] upon which the village was founded. When storms roll in, lightning strikes the Stone repeatedly.
- The **Cistern** is an underground vault, clearly an artifact of the Makers. Locals fill it with rain, snow, and water from the **[[44 - The Stream#The Stream|Stream]]**.
- The **public house** is a large hall, where folks meet after sundown to drink and socialize. It offers floor space to travelers and a small stable under the same roof. The town's horses are kept here.
- The **granary** is a warehouse built on a Maker-ruin foundation. Foodstuffs are stored here. Everyone contributes, everyone shares.
- The **pavilion of the gods** is an openair structure with shrines to **[[03 - Aratis, the Lawkeeper#Aratis, the Lawkeeper|Aratis]]**, **[[09 - Danu, the Earth Mother#Danu, the Earth Mother|Danu]]**, **[[25 - Helior, the Daybringer#Helior, the Daybringer|Helior]]**, **[[49 - Tor, the Rain-maker#Tor, the Rain-maker|Tor]]**, and maybe others [[20 - Gods and Religion#Gods and religion|Gods and religion]]. As close as Stonetop gets to a temple.

- The **Ringwall** is a waist-high fence of fieldstones, 2-3 feet thick. It won't keep out serious raiders, but provides a little cover if needed. Homes beyond it are newer, built by immigrants or younger families.
- **Watchtowers** stand along the Ringwall, a dozen feet tall or so. One to the north, one to the southeast, one to the southwest. Villagers rotate through watch duty at night.
- A **switchback path** leads down the bluff to the **[[44 - The Stream#The Stream|Stream]]** and the **[[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|Great Wood]]**. Climbing it takes 10-15 minutes, longer with a heavy load.
- The **fields** stretch out around the town, about 1,000 acres total. A third of the land sits fallow each year, with the rest growing barley, oats, beans, and potatoes. Farmers work the fields communally, rather than as familial plots.
- The **Old Wall** wraps around the fields, about a mile from the Stone itself and at the end of the **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#The Makers' Roads|West Road]]**. Clearly once a mighty rampart, it has tumbled and been buried by the ages. When the villagers need stone for construction, they dig it out of the old wall and haul it back to town.

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- What's your favorite tale about how the Stone came to be?
- In what little way do locals interact with the Stone day-to-day?
- What do the runes on the Stone make you think of?
- What do scholars of the South claim of the Stone's origin? Why do you think that's nonsense?
- What's the opening to the Cistern like? A low stone wall? A building over an open shaft? A crack in the earth? Or what?
- Has anyone been down in the Cistern, that you know of? What did they supposedly find?
- How is the public house laid out? What's the most notable furnishing?
- Where do children most like to play?
- Where do teens sneak off to for romance?

sections.title: Always
sections.body:
- The air: tingling, slight smell of ozone
- People and beasts: unfazed by loud noises
- Voices: always a little bit raised
- Chickens, goats, sheep, pigs, cats, dogs: all about, underfoot, bleating, barking, clucking
- Children: playing, crying, yelling, running errands, doing chores
- Folks around the Cistern: gossiping and chatting
- The public house: music, singing, dancing, chatting

sections.title: Activities
sections.body:
- Cooking, grinding grain, baking
- Spinning, weaving, sewing, hand-crafts
- Rendering fat, making oil & rushlights
- Cleaning pens, coops, homes, clothes
- Collecting & hauling water, to and from the Cistern
- Smithing, tanning, pottery, midwifery
- Maintenance (buildings, clothes, tools, etc.)
- Manning the watchtowers at night
- Drilling with spear and shield

sections.title: Spring
sections.body:
- Drip drip drip of snow melting from roofs, collecting in jugs and puddles
- Petrichor smell on a southerly breeze
- Hopeful green poking through deadbrown grass and soil
- Bare skin reveling in the still-chilly sun; cheerful voices; smiles and songs

sections.title: Activities
sections.body:
- Harvesting winter potatoes
- Spreading seed, planting beans/potatoes
- Harrowing soil (to cover seeds, plantings)
- Chasing birds from the fields (child's work)
- Spreading manure & plowing fallow fields
- Kidding goats, sheep
- Picking spring vegetables
- Clearing and planting gardens
- Harvesting/cutting deadfall for firewood
- Fur trapping, light hunting (for meat)

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- What do you most look forward to each spring?
- What rites precede the planting of the fields?

sections.title: Summer
sections.body:
- Blinding sun outside, cave-like dark inside
- Crops swaying and rippling in the breeze
- Fireflies like galaxies over the fields at dusk
- Dark storm clouds looming in the south, rumbling, flashing
- CRACKOOM of thunder, blinding flash as lightning touches the Stone

sections.title: Activities
sections.body:
- Haymaking (from Flats-grass, fallow fields)
- Weeding crops/gardens
- Spreading manure and replowing fallow fields
- Weaning goat kids and lambs
- Milking goats, shearing sheep
- Picking summer vegetables
- Berry-picking from gardens and the Wood
- Light hunting/trapping (for meat, not fur)

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- What project have you been putting off that you hope to get done this summer?
- How does the village observe midsummer?

sections.title: Autumn
sections.body:
- Crops bobbing heavy with seed, the Great Wood aflame with color
- Work-song, shouts and laughter, grunts of effort
- Rosy cheeks, breath visible in the morning air
- Cold, steady rain

sections.title: Activities
sections.body:
- Harvesting beans, barley, oats, potatoes
- Gleaning fallen seed from fields (child's work)
- Threshing, winnowing, sieving, storing crops
- Plowing fallows and planting winter potatoes
- Picking and preserving autumn vegetables
- Breeding goats, sheep
- Foraging for nuts, fruits in the Wood
- Heavy hunting/trapping (fur & meat)
- (If able): harvesting timber from the **[[16 - The Foothills#The Foothills|Foothills]]**

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- What is your favorite autumn food or drink?
- How does the village celebrate the harvest?

sections.title: Winter
sections.body:
- Roofs covered in thick white blankets
- Trodden-down paths in the snow, brown and gray
- Cloaks drawn tight against a bitter wind
- Families gathered 'round the hearth, hands busy with inside chores
- Yeasty smell of fermenting barley

sections.title: Activities
sections.body:
- Collecting snow for the Cistern
- Distilling & aging whisky
- Tending to livestock, stockpiling manure
- Heavy trapping (for fur)
- Hunting as able (for meat)
- Slaughtering/butchering livestock as needed

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- What's the worst/best part of these long cold nights?
- How do locals mark midwinter/the new year?

sections.title: Names
sections.body: Welsh and Welsh-inspired: Aderyn, Aeronwen, Afanen, Afon, Alun, Andras, Aneirin, Awstin, Bedwyr, Berwyn, Betrys, Braith, Briallen, Bronwen, Bryn, Cadi, Cadoc, Cadwygan, Caron, Cefin, Ceinwen, Ceridwyn, Cerys, Colwyn, Deiniol, Dilwen, Dylis, Eifion, Eirlys, Eluned, Emrys, Enfys, Eurwen, Gaenor, Garet, Gethin, Glyndir, Heledd, Hywel, Ifan, Iorwerth, Iwan, Leuca, Lewela, Linos, Mado, Maldwyn, Malon, Mared, Marged, Martyn, Meirion, Menwen, Mererid, Neirin, Nia, Ofydd, Olwyn, Owain, Padrig, Parry, Pryce, Pryder, Rheinal, Rhisiart, Rhosyn, Rydderch, Sawyl, Siana, Sioned, Talfryn, Tegid, Tiwlip, Tomos, Tudyr, Winifred, Yorath.

sections.title: People
sections.body:
Around 300 souls live in Stonetop, or about 50 families. Everyone knows everyone else, at least by name and reputation. Most families have been here for many generations, tied together by kinship and marriage.

The village supports only a few full time **tradesfolk**: a smith, a tanner, a potter, a midwife, and a publican. (5 souls, plus 5-10 assistants/apprentices)

**Hunters and trappers** ply the wood for meat and fur, and also forage for fruits, vegetables, mushrooms, and useable wood. Trappers produce much of the village's wealth, but hunters range farther and have more prestige. (10-20 souls)

**Farmers** work the fields, producing the village's staple foods. Hard, physical work. Most practice "minor" trades on the side, like carpentry, stonecutting, thatching, husbandry, distilling, etc. (~75 souls)

**Homemakers** keep the village running. They cook, clean, tend gardens, spin, weave, sow, mind children, practice a dozen minor trades, and otherwise manage a household. Families might have 2 or 3 homemakers each, from across generations. (~100 souls)

**Elders** have retired from the fields or the rigors of homemaking, but still help as they can. Many still serve the village as keepers of lore and wisdom. (~25 souls)

**Children** play and learn and grow. They also help with chores, tend livestock, chase birds from the fields, forage near the Stream, haul water, mind younger children, and so forth, gaining more and more responsibilities as they grow. (~100 souls)

sections.title: Home & hearth
sections.body:
Homes are low, squat, made of stone from the Old Wall. Roofs are thatched with barley straw. Each is heated by a central hearth. Most have small cellars, a standing loom, a small vegetable garden, and pens for livestock (inside and out).

Families pass homes down between generations. Larger families might occupy two or even three adjacent buildings. New homes are occasionally built just outside the Ringwall.

Homemakers grind grain with quernstones (as the village has no mill), to make flatbread, porridge, stew, ale, and whisky mash. They catch rainwater in clay vessels or draw water from the Cistern. Washing happens down at the **[[44 - The Stream#The Stream|Stream]]**, but folks rarely go alone.

Clothes are mostly leather, fur, and handspun wool. Belts, pouches, torcs, and brooches are common. Shoes are cut from single pieces of leather, with tabs that wrap up and around the feet, laced together into a simple sandal or pump. To make "boots," folks add thick wool socks, maybe lined with fur.

Timber is relatively scarce. Because of the **[[23 - The Great Wood#**The Compact**|Compact]]**, villagers won't fell a living tree from the Great Wood. Folks scavenge deadfall for firewood and smaller projects, and grow saplings around the fields and Old Wall for spears, bows, and arrows. Large-scale construction requires trading for timber or a logging trip to the **[[16 - The Foothills#The Foothills|Foothills]]**.

- Where does \_\_\_ live?
- What task or chore are you working on?
- What's the best/worst thing about this chore?
- What's cooking on the hearth?
- What here makes the place feel

sections.title: Trade & commerce
sections.body:
Stonetop's economy is based on cooperation and sharing, trade and barter, gifts and favors, debts and honor. "Shops" aren't a thing and prices aren't fixed. Coins are rarely used between neighbors; that stuff 's for outsiders and passing merchants. Crops from the fields go to the granary, to be rationed out to households.

Each family tends its own gardens (vegetables, gourds, fruits, and herbs) and livestock (chickens, goats, sheep, some pigs). Folks practice trades part-time (spinning, weaving, sewing, leatherwork, thatching, carpentry, distilling whisky, stonecutting, husbandry, etc.). Neighbors swap goods and favors freely, with little worry about trades being "fair." If you need a thing that someone isn't using, they'll probably let you borrow or even take it. Maybe you help them fix their roof next month, or your husband sends over a jug of his next batch of whisky.

Hunters, trappers, foragers, and full-time tradesfolk tend to freely share their skills and goods, just as the farmers share their crops. Still, they are often awash in gifts of gratitude from other households, and these positions all carry respect and status in town.

Merchants come through at least once each season (except winter). Those coming from **[[32 - Marshedge#Marshedge|Marshedge]]** bring textiles, rope, salt, herbs, glass, and finer goods from **[[29 - Lygos and the South#Lygos and the South|Lygos and the South]]**. Merchants coming from **[[22 - Gordin's Delve#Gordin's Delve|Gordin's Delve]]** bring metals, tools, and sometimes timber. Both are keen to acquire whisky and furs. Merchants heading to Gordin's Delve are always interested in grain and livestock.

- What sorts of livestock does your household keep?
- What from your (family's) garden are you (they) most proud of?
- Who taught you how to \_\_\_?
- Who in the village is the best weaver/ carpenter/\_\_\_?
- Who in town has a reputation as a layabout?
- Who tends to handle trade with outsiders?

sections.title: Whisky
sections.body:
Distilling whisky is a local tradition. Most households have a basic still and make a batch or two each year. Ferment some barley mash, distill it into moonshine, age it for a few weeks in a ceramic jug with toasted wood chips. Repeat. A handful of locals (like the publican, perhaps?) have larger operations with charred wooden firkins or barrels.

Stonetop whisky is a much-valued trade good, one of the village's chief exports. The secrets of making it are poorly understood outside the village, and even emigrants have struggled to reproduce it in other locales.

- Who in town makes the best whisky?
- According to local lore, where did the art of distilling whisky come from?
- Why do you think outsiders have failed to reproduce this process?
- What secret ingredient/step sets your (family's) whisky apart?

sections.title: Governance
sections.body:
Stonetop has no government, just leaders. Everyone knows who they are: the wise, the cunning, the brave. It's not a formal thing.

- When big decisions need to be made, how does the village make them? (A big meeting? A council of elders? Or what?)
- Who rations out crops from the granary?
- Whose lead do the farmers follow?
- To whom do the homemakers listen?
- Whom do the hunters/trappers respect?
- To whom do folks look to settle disputes/break up fights/deal with trouble?
- What consequences are traditional for lying/theft/assault/murder/etc.?

sections.title: Defense
sections.body:
Able-bodied adults are expected to know how to fight and protect the village when needed. Everyone keeps a spear, a round wooden shield, and maybe a bow.

Folks rotate through guard duty, manning the watchtowers each night. The chief concerns are monsters from the **[[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|Great Wood]]**, especially **[[07 - Crinwin#Crinwin|crinwin]]**, and **[[14 - The Flats#**Monsters**|bandits]]** or **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk|Hillfolk]]** raiding from the **[[14 - The Flats#The Flats|Flats]]**. Most nights, though, the worst things you have to deal with are boredom, nerves, and bad weather.

- Who runs drills for the militia? Where do they typically drill?
- Have the watchtowers always been there, or are they a recent thing?
- How are watchtower shifts assigned?
- What's the worst part of watch duty?
- What's the best part?
- How do you keep yourself awake?
- How do those on watch signal trouble?

sections.title: Culture and lore
sections.body:
Stonetop shares a tongue with **[[32 - Marshedge#Marshedge|Marshedge]]**, though the accents are quite distinct. Much lore is passed on verbally, through stories, songs, and poems.

Local decorations are reminiscent of the runes etched in the Stone itself, and of the designs found on the Maker-ruins from which the village was built.

**Tor** (Rain-maker, Thunderhead, Slayer-of-Beasts, [[49 - Tor, the Rain-maker|Tor, the Rain-maker]]) is the most popular god, and associated with the Stone itself. The pavilion of the gods also holds shrines to **[[03 - Aratis, the Lawkeeper#Aratis, the Lawkeeper|Aratis]]**, **[[09 - Danu, the Earth Mother#Danu, the Earth Mother|Danu]]**, **[[25 - Helior, the Daybringer#Helior, the Daybringer|Helior]]**, and perhaps to other local **[[20 - Gods and Religion#Gods and religion|gods or spirits]]**.

- What clothing/markings/traits are distinctive to the people of Stonetop?
- What game/pastime/tradition is unique to Stonetop?
- What's your favorite tale of Stonetop's history?
- What's the scariest story that the elders tell?
- What's the most obvious way in which the locals honor Tor?
- How do folks mark or celebrate a birth?
- How do folks mark one's coming of age?
- What do folks do with their dead?
- Do folks revere/recognize any local spirits (of house or hearth, of field or stream, of their ancestors, etc.)? How so?

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- Two youths wish to wed, despite objections from (one of?) their parents
- Tools left out have gone missing; now things start to vanish from inside homes
- One villager kills another when a heated argument gets out of hand
- A flock of starlings and a **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Grwgnach**|grwgnach]]** make spring planting impossible
- **[[49 - Tor, the Rain-maker#**Lightning**|Drought]]** strikes the land, threatening ruin and disaster
- A merchant that the PCs were counting on fails to arrive
- **[[14 - The Flats#**Monsters**|Bandits]]** threaten to burn the crops unless paid tribute

sections.title: Mysteries
sections.body: The following are for you and your table to decide.

sections.title: The Stone
The Cistern
Ruins of the past
Origins
Aratis, the Lawkeeper
sections.body:
Aratis is one of the four main **[[20 - Gods and Religion#Gods and religion|gods]]** of Stonetop, the patron god of civilization and records, of laws and wisdom, of order over chaos. Her disciples say that she has been with us since people first stacked stone upon stone and called it home.

If one of your initial PCs has the Judge playbook, they will answer many of the questions in this section via their presence, their background, and the decisions they make during character creation and introductions. Defer to that PC as the expert on Aratis in your game, but don't forget to ask other PCs for their opinions and experiences, too.

sections.title: Themes
sections.body: Choose or roll 1 or 2, and combine.

sections.title: 1d12 theme
sections.body: 1-2 Civilization/society/settlements 3-4 Laws/rules/traditions 5-6 Chronicles/record-keeping/writing 7 Judgement/arbitration/wisdom 8 Cooperation/organization/peace 9 Authority/responsibility/duty 10 Truth/promises/oaths/honor 11 Hospitality/sanctity/generosity 12 Defense/resolve/punishment

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
According to the tales:

- How did Aratis come to be (a god)? What's her origin story?
- Among which people did the worship of Aratis originate?
- Which god is her rival/ally/lover/kin?
- What god or demon did she personally defeat/cast out/seal away?
- What constellation is tied to Aratis? How so?
- Who was the first Judge? What great deed is attributed to them?
- What is said to be contained in the Tower Eternal?

Use the answers to help decide what Aratis's **true nature** is [[20 - Gods and Religion#**1d12** observance|Gods and Religion]].

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- The Judge makes an unpopular ruling; an influential NPC wants them gone.
- A Judge from another settlement arrives and finds things in Stonetop lacking.
- A true disciple has a vision of a powerful agent of chaos breaking its bonds.
- While digging stones or excavating a new foundation, folks in Stonetop discover a long-lost Chronicle of Aratis, sealed and mostly intact.
- Legend tells of the Tower Eternal, made of **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Primordial powers**|black iron]]**, with doors so heavy only a team of horses can open them. Three hermits, all true disciples of Aratis, guard it and whatever it contains.

sections.title: Shrines & temples
Judges
sections.body:
Some communities are home to a Judge of Aratis, who settles disputes, keeps records, and stands guard against agents of chaos. For any given community, consider/ask:

- Does a Judge of Aratis reside here?
- Who are they? What are they like? What sets them apart?
- Has there always been a Judge here? Or is this a recent thing?
- How much influence do they hold?
- Do they have any apprentices?
- How much contact do they have with Judges from other communities?
- What is their greatest strength as a Judge? What is their greatest flaw? Maybe pick or roll a theme (previous page) for each.

Judges are charged with maintaining a Chronicle, a history of the community they serve, its people, their knowledge, and their traditions. If a community has (or had) a Judge, consider/ask:

- How are the records kept?
- Where are they? In the Judge's home, a temple, a vault, or what?
- How far back do they go?
- How well organized are they?

Should an NPC Judge (or a communityspanning order of Judges) oppose or cause trouble for the PCs, write them up as a threat (an *institution*).

Remember: NPCs don't have playbooks. An NPC Judge might have tags or moves reminiscent of a PC Judge, or not. See [[03 - Aratis, the Lawkeeper#**Dangers**|Aratis, the Lawkeeper]] for some potential NPC Judges.

sections.title: True disciples
sections.body:
True disciples are those who pledge themselves to Aratis, her principles, and her observances. For any given community, consider/ask:

- How many true disciples of Aratis reside here?
- Who is most notable among them?
- How devout are they, really?
- How much influence do they have, as a group?
- What contact do they have with disciples from other settlements?
- How are they typically chosen? (By birth, by calling, by invitation, etc.)
- What rites do they lead/perform, both publicly and in secret?

What does Aratis require of her true disciples? The answer might vary from community to community. Perhaps she demands… (pick up to 3)

- … truth, honesty, and forthrightness;
- … hospitality, freely given to all who ask for it;
- … the punishment of thieves & oathbreakers;
- … adherence to strict rules of diet and dress; and/or
- … respect for authority, property, and rank.

Should the true disciples of a community oppose or cause trouble for the PCs, write them up as a threat (an *institution*).

sections.title: Various treasures
sections.body:
- A blanket woven in the knot-language of a long-dead sect of Aratis's (*fragile*, Value 0, or 2 to the right buyer)
- A maul of **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Primordial powers**|black iron]]**, utterly immune to magic (*close*, *forceful*, *awkward*, +1 damage, Value 2)
- A shield of **[[43 - Stone Lords#Various treasures|makerglass]]**, etched with Aratis's symbol (*indestructible*, +1 armor, +1 Readiness of a Defend 7+, Value 2)
- A helm set with a "jewel" of **[[38 - Rime Lords#Dark ice|dark ice]]**, grants advantage to resist mind-affecting magic (*magical*, Value 2)
- An old book (*fragile*, Value 2), the Chronicle of a failed settlement, ending mid-sentence
- A very large sapphire, perfectly cut and polished, a symbol of Aratis floating in its depths (Value 4)

sections.title: Minor arcana
sections.body: Pick 1 or have someone roll.

sections.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
sections.body:
- 1 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A grim peat mound|A grim peat mound]]**
- 2 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#Peacebond|A sealed cave]]**
- 3 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A timeless vault|A timeless vault]]**
- 4 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A crumbling arch|A crumbling arch]]**
- 5 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#Sigil of Authority Authority:|A silvery signet ring]]**
- 6 Some other dangerous minor arcanum, with potential to spread chaos, locked away for safekeeping.

sections.title: Major arcana
sections.body:
The **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Rune-laden Scales|Rune-laden Scales]]**

might be associated with Aratis, or at least revered by her followers.

sections.title: Associated entities
sections.body:
- **Ancestral spirits** or other **[[20 - Gods and Religion#**Little gods**|little gods]]** of home, hearth, and civilization
- The **tomb-bog spirit** (page [[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|85\)]], sent to execute a criminal
- An **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Other primordial entities**|archon]]**, called up to smite some agent of chaos
- Perhaps even the **[[35 - The Pale Hunter#The Pale Hunter|Pale Hunter]]**) or his hounds

sections.title: Judge of Aratis
sections.body:
*Group*, *leader*, *organized*

**HP** 8; **Armor** 2 (shield, leather cuirass) **Damage** mallet d8 (*hand*) or maul d8+2 (*close*, *forceful*, *awkward*)

**Special qualities** bound by the requirements of a **true disciple** (previous page), at least publicly

**Stalwart instinct** to see things as black and white (+*tough*, +4 HP)

- ä Decry someone/thing, publicly
- ä Enforce a law/tradition/contract, even despite good sense
- ä Inspire others with their resolve **Ambitious instinct** to advance their own

agenda (+*cunning*)

- ä Make a reasonable-sounding ruling, to their own advantage
- ä Suss out someone's motives
- ä Keep calm under pressure or scrutiny

**Circumspect instinct** to try to make everyone happy (+*friendly*, +*cautious*, deals damage with disadvantage)

- ä Stall, delay, make excuses
- ä See both sides of an issue
- ä Find a different way

sections.title: Oathbreaker's curse
sections.body:
When you *swear a proper oath before a shrine of Aratis*, in full view of witnesses and with an appropriate offering, you are oathbound. Make note of what you promised, and the nature of the curse you invite should you forswear yourself.

When you *forswear your oath*, hold 3 Ruin. While you hold Ruin, you have disadvantage whenever you or a companion rolls the Die of Fate.

When you *roll a 10+ on a move*, you can spend 1 Ruin to turn the 10+ into a 6 instead. The GM can refuse; if they do, you retain your Ruin. If they accept, the results should reflect the nature of the curse you invited upon yourself.

sections.title: Chaos
sections.body:
A PC Judge can use their Censure move to denounce another as "an agent of chaos." When they do, the move works regardless of what you think. You can ask questions about their motivations or their thinking, but they're the Judge, not you.

With the Hound of Aratis move, the Judge can easily identify things "tainted by chaos," and The Hammer and the Book lets them smite things "of supernatural chaos." The **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]** count, as does anything corrupted by them or infused with their power.

Beyond that, consider what the Judge has Censured and the doctrine of Aratis that's been established in play. Ask the Judge what they think counts. Consider what you know about the entity in question, and make a ruling. If in doubt, defer to the Die of Fate or the results of a relevant move.

sections.title: Barrier Pass
sections.body: **Size** *village* (~200 souls)

sections.title: Population +0 Prosperity +0
sections.body:
- Trade with...
  - ... mammoth herders (ivory, wool, meat)
  - ... other fortress-monasteries

sections.title: Resources
sections.body:
- Taxes from nearby farm valleys
- Farming (terraced gardens, barley)
- Livestock (goats, sheep, milk, fine wool, meat)
- Timber (from Foothills)
- Parchment, vellum, ink
- Trades: smithing, weaving, tanning, scribing

sections.title: Defenses +2
sections.body:
- A score of guardians
- A handful of **[[38 - Rime Lords#**Dangers**|adepts]]**
- A number of **[[38 - Rime Lords#**Tulpa**|tulpas]]**
- Blunt weapons, nets, bolas, bronze shields and mail
- Exceptional fortifications

Five days north of Stonetop via the **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#The Makers' Roads|Highway]]**, after winding through the **[[16 - The Foothills#The Foothills|Foothills]]** and climbing into the **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#The Whitefang Mountains|Whitefang Mountains]]**, you'll come to a pass between two rocky slopes. The pass is blocked by a mighty wall, with dwellings and grander structures on the cliffs above.

Long ago, this was the southernmost outpost of the **[[38 - Rime Lords#Rime Lords|Rime Lords]]**. Today, it is home to descendants of their disciples. They are a stoic, unfriendly people who want little to do with those from the south. In Stonetop, they tell tales of folk coming down from Barrier Pass to trade ancient wonders for goats or grain, but none alive remember such a visit.

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- According to the tales, what did the last visitors from Barrier Pass bring to trade, all those years ago?
- When was the last time anyone from Stonetop tried to trade with Barrier Pass? Were they ignored? Chased off? Or what?
- What went wrong when villagers last crossed paths with a party from Barrier Pass up in the Foothills?
- Who do you know (in Gordin's Delve, perhaps) who hails from Barrier Pass or beyond? Why did they leave?
- What do you think lies past that great big wall?
- Who or what do you think they put in those cages that line the Highway as one approaches?
- Why do you think their warriors carry only blunt weapons?

If a PC has visited Barrier Pass, or beyond:

- What brought you there in the first place?
- Why did they let you in?
- What surprised you the most about how they live?
- What taboo or point of etiquette did you run afoul of? How so?

sections.title: Places
sections.body:
- **The approach**: after winding up switchbacks, the Highway straightens and enters a wide pass with steep rocky slopes on either side.
- **Large cages line the slopes**, swinging in the wind, filled with human (?) bones or starved, raving prisoners.
- **The Wall**, grand and implacable, 50 feet tall and 20 feet wide, spanning the width of the pass.
- **A gate in the Wall**, closed of course, the Highway ending 50 feet before it.
- **The East Slope**, dotted with dwellings, stables, and terraced gardens, all connected with narrow stairs and switch-

- **A waterfall** pours down the east slope, north of the wall. Diverted to gardens and cisterns.
- **The West Slope**, featuring old giantscale structures, now used as dormitories, kitchens, and so forth for monks and guardians.

 **The Great Hall**, its façade elaborately decorated, leading into a vast chamber carved from a mountain cavern. Where residents gather, meditate, train.

sections.title: People
sections.body:
The people of Barrier Pass descend from the **Rime Lords**' [[38 - Rime Lords|Rime Lords]] mortal disciples. Their names are Nepalese- and Tibetan-inspired (see [[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**1d6** point of interest|The Whitefang Mountains]]).

**The Three Honorable Sages** are powerful **[[38 - Rime Lords#**Dangers**|adepts]]** who preside over Barrier Pass. Each leads one of the three sects.

**The Empty Sky sect** values preser vation and stillness; its monks spend much time in meditative stasis. Led by **Gar Torjeng** (**Instinct** to avoid disruption and distraction).

**The Unmoved Mountain sect** teaches extreme ascetic disciplines, pushing adherents to continually test themselves. Led by **Preshi Kav** (**Instinct** to drive her students towards perfection).

**The White Crystal sect** is devoted to order. The majority of guardians and layfolk follow this sect. Its few monks often lead patrols, mete out justice in nearby farms, and hunt monsters. Led by **Oparma Div** (**Instinct** to tame chaos).

**Monks from each sect** dwell in the fortress, ranging wildly in experience and aptitude. (~40 total, including novices)

The **Guardians** are sworn to defend Barrier Pass. Many are failed monks. They bear ancient bronze shields and armor, but wield no edged weapons. (~20 total)

**The rest of the inhabitants** are lay folk: families and individuals who garden, herd, cook, clean, and otherwise keep the fortress-monastery running. (~140 souls total)

sections.title: Stoic and unfriendly
TRADE WITH BARRIER PASS
sections.body:
Surely it would benefit both communities were we to trade with each other!

Requires getting them to talk to you in the first place, which requires 1 of these:

- Bringing them a valuable artifact of the Rime Lords, as a gift or to trade
- A resident in good standing, vouching for you

And then, all of the following:

- Making a compelling offer
- Convincing at least two of the Honored Sages to accept
- Two successful trade missions, each in a different season

When you *mark all the requirements*, increase Fortunes by 1 and add "Trade with Barrier Pass (timber, ivory, parchment, fine wool, goats, sheep)".

Henceforth, when *spring bursts forth and Stonetop has at least 1 Surplus*, Stonetop gains +1 Surplus from trade. Also, when you *Trade & Barter for timber, ivory, fine wool, goats, sheep, parchment, vellum, or ink*, you have advantage.

sections.title: Trade & Barter
sections.body: The PCs must convince the folks of Barrier Pass to deal with them before any of the following matters.

| Bola ( thrown , grabby , awkward , -1 die size)                     |       0 |

| Shield, bronze ( clumsy , +1 Armor, +1 Readiness on a 7+ to Defend) |       1 |

| goods                   | value   |

sections.title: Special items
sections.body: The following might be acquired in Barrier Pass, in addition to special items from the Moves & Gear handout. PCs must roll Trade & Barter to acquire them.

sections.title: When PCs Trade & Barter in Barrier
sections.body:
Pass , they use the local Prosperity (+0 by default). If they wish to acquire or sell a bladed weapon, they have disadvantage. On a 6, they don't mark XP , and it's just not available/no one is interested-they might have better luck with a nearby farm or band of mammoth herders.

| goods | value |

sections.title: Services and favors
sections.body:
PCs don't need to roll Trade & Barter for the following, but they might need to Persuade people—even if they offer something of suitable Value. If the PCs are well-regarded by locals, they might get these boons for free, or without needing to Persuade anyone, or at a reduced price.

| service/favor                            | value |
|------------------------------------------|-------|
| A few nights of austere food and shelter | 0     |
| A layfolk's labor for a day              | 0     |
| A crew of layfolk, working for 1 week    | 1     |
| Consultation with a monk                 | 1     |
| Audience with an Honorable Sage          | 2     |
| Weeks of guided study with a monk        | 2     |
| A guide into the mountains, to           |       |
| a nearby farm valley                     | 0     |
| another known, nearby spot               | 1     |
| a dangerous/unknown spot                 | 2     |
| a secret/deadly place                    | 3     |

sections.title: . trade opportunities .
sections.body:
If the PCs get a trade opportunity (from the Seasons Change move), Barrier Pass might offer the following:

- A fair exchange of goods, plus someone to vouch for them should they wish for future trade (see **Trade with Barrier Pass**, page [[04 - Barrier Pass#. trade opportunities .|29]]).
- A good exchange rate, two or even three Value 2 items for 1 Surplus.
- Two or three ivory mammoth tusks (Value 3), in exchange for help with a problem.
- A Value 3 mundane item, in exchange for some less-valuable artifact of the Rime Lords
- **[[38 - Rime Lords#Dark ice|Dark ice]]** in substantial quantities
- An arcanum from the **[[38 - Rime Lords#Dark ice|Rime Lords]]**, the **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Artifacts|spirits of the wild]]**, or **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Dool trees**|death and the undying]]**.

sections.title: Secrets
sections.body:
- Secret paths/doors lead in and out of the compound. Outsiders are blindfolded when taken through them, or condemned to death if they learn of their existence.
- There is a hidden vault deep in the mountainside, protected by all manner of magics and **[[38 - Rime Lords#**Tulpa**|tulpas]]**. Only a few inhabitants know it exists; fewer know what it contains.
- One of the monks has started down the path of becoming a **[[38 - Rime Lords#**1d12** instinct|false adept]]**. She is popular and (apparently) quite talented. Only Preshi Kav (Honored Sage of the Unmoved Mountain) suspects anything.

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- The PCs acquire an artifact of the Rime Lords; maybe they can leverage it into opening trade negotiations?
- A party from Barrier Pass comes to Stonetop, offering ivory/gold/arcana for food/livestock; they are tightlipped, haunted, on edge.
- A condemned criminal escapes from Barrier Pass and seeks refuge; a **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Shantyara**|shantyara]]** pursues them.

sections.title: Surroundings
sections.body:
- Steep, treacherous, rocky slopes
- **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#Services and favors|Harsh mountain weather]]**
- **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Ice drake**|Hypothermia & frostbite]]**, if you stay out in the cold
- **[[42 - The Steplands#1d6 natural beast or spirit|Rockslides]]** or **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Ice drake**|avalanches]]** in winter

sections.title: Fortifications
sections.body:
- Minor **[[38 - Rime Lords#**Tulpa**|tulpas]]** watching the approach, warning the fortress of visitors
- A wall of smooth stone, 50 feet tall and 20 feet wide
- Walkways and crenelations atop the wall
- A final approach, 50 feet or so with no protection from the Makers' Roads
- A massive gate of wood and bronze
- Beyond the gate, a barbican lined with murder holes
- Cleverly concealed doors that open only from the inside
- A pair of **[[38 - Rime Lords#**Gingkara**|khyagseng]]** guarding the entrance to the Great Hall

sections.title: Folk from elsewhere
sections.body:
- **[[19 - The Frozen Wastes#**Dangers**|Mammoth herders]]** or **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Questions**|valley farmers]]**, here to trade, pay tribute, or seek redress
- An **[[38 - Rime Lords#**Dangers**|adept]]**, visiting from another fortress-monastery or some cave in the wilds
- A **[[38 - Rime Lords#**Mkhalang**|mkhalang]]**, claiming to bring tidings but really just stirring up trouble
- A **[[14 - The Flats#**Monsters**|bandit]]** or criminal from one of the nearby farm valleys, condemned to death, pleading for mercy
- A **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Spirits of the wild|spirit of the wild]]**, come to visit or debate with the Honored Sages
- A **thrall** of the Things Below [[46 - The Things Below#**Cultist**|The Things Below]]), here under false pretense, urged to spread chaos and woe

sections.title: Guardian
sections.body:
*Group*, *organized*, *skilled*

**HP** 6; **Armor** 3 (bronze shields, mail)

**Damage** bronze mace d8 (*close*, *forceful*) or mancatcher d6 (*reach*, *grabby*) or bola d6 (*thrown*, *grabby*, *awkward*)

**Instinct** to subdue or drive off threats

- ä Deftly execute a group maneuver
- ä Ensnare a foe
- ä Club someone senseless
- ä Resort to deadly force in the heat of the moment

sections.title: Monk
sections.body:
*Group*, *cautious*, *disciplined*, *hardy*

**HP** 10; **Armor** 0

**Damage** staff d8 with disadvantage (*close*) **Special qualities** spirit sight; inured to hunger, cold, deprivation

**Empty Sky instinct** to ignore the suffering or needs of others

- ä Remain unperturbed
- ä Send their mind forth into the spirit world, their bodies insensate
- ä Calm the elements around them

**Unmoved Mountain instinct** to push and test themselves

- ä Perform a feat of extreme athleticism or endurance
- ä Endure or absorb raw elemental power
- ä Refuse to adapt or change

sections.title: White Crystal instinct to impose order
sections.body:
- ä Take charge
- ä Do their duty
- ä Suppress the emotions of others

sections.title: Novice
path: White Crystal instinct to impose order
sections.body:
*Group*, *cautious*, *disciplined*, *meek*

**HP** 6, **Armor** 0

**Damage** staff d8 with disadvantage (*close*) **Special qualities** unreliable spirit sight **Instinct** to veer between their masters'

- teachings and their base needs ä Do what their superiors ask
- ä Hesitate, balk, flinch
- ä Show great promise, or great weakness

sections.title: Barrow Builders
sections.body:
When the servants of the **[[43 - Stone Lords#Stone Lords|Stone Lords]]** rebelled, slaying their masters and unleashing the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**), those who died in the uprising were hailed as martyrs. The survivors raised mounds of earth and stone to honor them, simple barrows built with human hands and human arts.

Society collapsed. Horror swept the land. Petty tyrants and sorcerer-kings rose to power. They had barrows built to honor loyal vassals or to seal away the ghosts of those they had betrayed.

Fleeting kingdoms rose and fell. Rulers raised grand barrows and had themselves interred with riches, slaves, and followers in tow. Fueled by dark magic and the power of the Things Below, some of these tyrants reigned on even after death.

In time, the power of the Things Below diminished. Subjects rose up and overthrew their masters. Tyrants began to fall. Those who could not be killed were sealed away in barrows. The **[[46 - The Things Below#**The current age**|current age]]** arrived.

Many barrows still remain. A few are simple graves. Many are haunted or cursed. Some imprison ancient evil. They dot the World's End, especially in the **[[42 - The Steplands#The Steplands|Steplands]]**. The **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk|Hillfolk]]** shun them, and warn all travelers away. For they descended from the Barrow Builders, and those who cast them off. They know what horrors those earthen mounds contain.

sections.title: Names
sections.body: Breton- or Cornish-inspired, similar to **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk|Hillfolk names]]** but with more vowels: Alana, Arzhel, Brandan, Corent, Deniel, Enora, Gwenael, Herve, Judikael, Karenza, Katell, Maben, Margh, Nolwenn, Piran, Ronan, Winoc, Yanick, Yezekael.

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- An artifact that the PCs want lies in a haunted barrow mound
- Some fool unseals a barrow and lets **[[05 - Barrow Builders#**Phantom rider**|phantom riders]]** loose
- Someone acquires the tether of a **[[05 - Barrow Builders#**Deathless ones**|deathless one]]**
- Hillfolk are furious that one of the barrows was looted

sections.title: Themes
sections.body: Pick 1 or 2, or roll 1d12.

sections.title: 1d12 theme
sections.body:
- 1 Revolution: hunting down the Stone Lords, upending the status quo
- 2 The works of the Makers: torn down, vandalized, repurposed, lost
- 3 Societal collapse: famine, shortage, hoarding, violence
- 4 Sorcerers and necromancers: recklessly wielding power they can't control
- 5 Human sacrifice, blood magic, the courting and open worship of the Things Below
- 6 The horrors of war: pillaging, looting, slaughter, dislocation
- 7 Refugees, desperate and afraid, seeking safety, settling for survival
- 8 Sorcerer-kings and petty warlords with their grand, bloody ambitions
- 9 Conquest/oppression/enslavement
- 10 Intrigue/betrayal/civil war
- 11 Rulers clinging to power, even from the grave
- 12 Heroism and hope and perseverance, the casting off of tyrants, the slaying of monsters; the forging of a new age

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- Where is the nearest barrow-mound that you know of?
- What strange sign marks it?
- What tall tale have you heard of it?
- What story do you recall, from the terrible days after the Makers fell?

sections.title: Everyone knows:
sections.body:
- That old barrows dot the region
- That they're smaller and cruder than Maker-ruins
- That Hillfolk shun them
- That they supposedly contain great treasure and great danger

sections.title: One might know:
sections.body:
- Where a few barrows can be found
- That they were built by the Hillfolk's (reviled) ancestors
- Of a particularly (in)famous figure among them
- The horrors from those times
- A tale about a specific barrow

sections.title: Very few know:
sections.body:
- What a given barrow contains
- Where a specific figure is buried
- The true story behind a barrow

sections.title: Sites
sections.body:
Barrow Builder sites are most common in the **[[42 - The Steplands#The Steplands|Steplands]]**, especially around **[[06 - Blackwater Lake#Blackwater Lake|Blackwater Lake]]** and **[[47 - Three Coven Lake#Three Coven Lake|Three Coven Lake]]**. Plenty can also be found in the **[[14 - The Flats#The Flats|Flats]]**, the **[[40 - South Manmarch#South Manmarch|South Manmarch]]**, and along the **[[11 - The Dread River#The Dread River|Dread River]]**. A few can be found in the **[[27 - Huffel Peaks#Huffel Peaks|Huffel Peaks]]** and as far north as the **[[16 - The Foothills#The Foothills|Foothills]]**.

To create a Barrow Builder site, pick one or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 site
sections.body:
- 1 A **[[42 - The Steplands#Caves|cave]]**, in which they or their victims lived/hid/died/ were interred (combine with 1 or 2 themes, [[05 - Barrow Builders#**Barrow**|Barrow Builders]])
- 2 An old battlefield or mass grave, likely **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|haunted]]**
- 3-4 A **reclaimed Maker-ruin** (see below)
- 5-6 A **[[05 - Barrow Builders#Barrow|barrow]]**

sections.title: Reclaimed Maker-ruin
sections.body: Choose or roll for its origin. Create the ruin from the appropriate tables, rolling with advantage for its condition at the time the Barrow Builders claimed it.

sections.title: 1d12 origin
sections.body:
- 1 A **[[24 - Green Lords#1d6 site|Green Lord ruin]]** 2 A **[[38 - Rime Lords#**A ruin**|Rime Lord ruin]]** 3-4 A **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**A ruin**|Tempest Lord ruin]]** 5-6 A **[[18 - Forge Lords#1d6 site|Forge Lord ruin]]** 7-10 A **[[43 - Stone Lords#1d6 site|Stone Lord ruin]]**
- 11-12 A ruin of multiple groups (roll 1d10 twice)

Then pick or roll for a theme [[05 - Barrow Builders#**Barrow**|Barrow Builders]] to reflect how the Barrow Builders used it, 1-3 signs of their presence, and its current condition. Populate the site with artifacts and dangers of the Barrow Builders, as well as those of its original owners.

sections.title: 1d12 signs
sections.body:
- 1-2 **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Ghosts]]**/psychic imprints
- 3-4 Remains/bones/graves/memorials
- 5-6 Damage/plunder/vandalism/graffiti
- 7-8 Distinctive material culture: refuse/ pottery/tools/art/statuary/textiles/etc.
- 9-10 Crude, human-sized additions/ adaptations/repairs
- 11 Altars/idols/offerings/arcane symbols
- 12 Maker artifacts, repurposed

sections.title: 1d12 current condition
sections.body:
- 1-4 Shattered and laid waste by time, disaster, or vandalism; mostly buried, overgrown, collapsed; its purpose unrecognizable
- 5-8 Crumbling, largely buried, overgrown, or hidden away
- 9-10 Buried, overgrown, or hidden, but mostly intact
- 11-12 Fully visible and strangely well-preserved

For more examples, see **[[06 - Blackwater Lake#**1d12** ruin or barrow|Blackwater Lake]]** and **[[47 - Three Coven Lake#**1d12** stone lord site|Three Coven Lake]]**.

*The PCs are searching for the fiery heart of the Mindgem [[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Mysteries of the Mindgem|Mindgem]]. I think it's in a Stone Lord ruin that the Barrow Builders reclaimed. Looking at the tables on [[43 - Stone Lords#1d6 site|1d6]] I end up with a warren of multi-storied, brutalist apartments—housing for laborers—built around a single, giant-sized manor where the overseers dwelt. Maybe this was a plantation, with a small army of lithic servants* [[43 - Stone Lords#**1d12** sapience|Stone Lords]] *maintaining the complex?* 

*For the Barrow Builders' theme, I roll an 11 ("rulers clinging to power, even from the grave"). For signs, I roll a 5 ("damage/plunder/vandalism/graffiti") and 10 ("crude, human-sized" modifications). For the current condition, I roll a 9 ("buried, overgrown… mostly intact"). I ponder a bit, and come up with this:*

*A sorcerer led the humans here to rise up against their overseers. The sorcerer then claimed the manor as his own, along with the device that charged and controlled the lithic servants —a device powered by the Mindgem's makerglass heart! He turned the manor into a crude palace and sent "his" people back to work the fields. When they eventually rebelled, he was mortally wounded but transferred his soul into the device that ran the lithic servants. With his mind now spread among the many constructs, he squashed the uprising. His rule grew increasingly erratic, brutal, and ineffective. The tenements became more squalid and desperate. Folks fled. The sorcerer didn't care.*

*Now, the complex lies empty: overgrown, half-buried, stripped of anything of value, and home only to a dwindling army of lithic servants and the fractured mind of a paranoid sorcerer.*

*[[32 - Marshedge#Marshedge|The PCs are following the River Sruth out of Marshedge]]* *and through the South Manmarch when they encounter a barrow. I roll a 10 for terrain ("tree(s), savannah, thickets"), a 9 for a theme ("conquest/oppression/enslavement"), a 12 for size ("manmade hill"), a 1 for purpose ("to honor the interred"), a 3 for architectural elements ("obvious entrance"), an 11 for condition ("strangely intact, fully visible"), and a 12 for features ("roll twice"). I then get a 4 ("corrupted by the Things Below") and a 10 ("treasure," with a 2 for the amount remaining). For the corruption, I turn to [[46 - The Things Below#**1d12** origin|The Things Below]] and come up with a poisonous place, with themes of death and broken bones, first caused by a blood-offering and then by a seal being broken. I ponder a little, and come up with this:* 

*A lone hill rising from sparse woods on the river's south bank. The daughter of a warlord commissioned it. Hundreds of slaves built it. When it was done, she had the slave's skulls caved in, to be interred with her father and his riches. After the daughter's brief reign ended, some fools pried open the stone doors to loot the warlord's tomb. They found chambers full of death, madness, and cursed gold. Those few who made it out were never the same. The barrow still lies open and treasure still remains, but no one with a lick of sense goes near it.*

sections.title: Barrow
sections.body:
They are the most obvious remnants of these bloody times: dome shaped earthen mounds. Most folks assume they were all tombs, but that's hardly the case.

Pick or roll for themes [[05 - Barrow Builders#**Barrow**|Barrow Builders]], its size, its purpose, 1 or 2 architectural elements, its condition, and a feature. Place the barrow in an appropriate terrain for its region. Fill it with **[[05 - Barrow Builders#**Artifacts**|artifacts]]** and **[[05 - Barrow Builders#**Dangers**|dangers]]** of the Barrow Builders or the appropriate region.

sections.title: 1d12 size
sections.body:
- 1-4 Small: just a few paces across, no chambers to speak of
- 5-8 Medium: big as a house, a few cramped halls/chambers
- 9-12 Large: a manmade hill, riddled with chambers/passages

sections.title: 1d12 purpose
sections.body:
1-3 To honor the interred 4-6 To bind/torment the interred 7 To protect the interred 8-9 To hide something away

10 To leverage/maintain fell magic

11-12 Roll 1d10, twice

sections.title: 1d12 architectural elements
sections.body:
- 1-2 Megalith(s)/cairn(s)/dolmen(s)
- 3-4 Obvious entrance (likely sealed)
- 5-6 Irregular/elaborate/oval shape
- 7-8 Works of the Makers, recycled
- 9-10 Walls of dry-stacked stones
- 11-12 Built around a spring: water bubbling up/pooling/flowing out

Even in large barrows, chambers and passages are cramped and claustrophobic, often with packed-dirt floors and lined with large slabs of stone.

sections.title: 1d12 condition
sections.body:
- 1-4 Collapsed, torn open, only hints remaining
- 5-6 Partially collapsed or torn open, but largely intact
- 7-10 Mostly intact, but overgrown or partly buried; easy to miss
- 11-12 Strangely intact, fully visible

sections.title: 1d12 feature
sections.body:
- 1-2 **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|Haunted]]**, marked by dool trees
- 3-4 Corrupted by the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**
- 5 Protective spirit(s)/construct(s)/demon(s)/spell(s)/trap(s)
- 6 Connects to **[[42 - The Steplands#Caves|caves]]**
- 7 Inhabited by local beasts/bandits
- 8 **[[50 - Useful or Valuable Flora#Useful or valuable flora|Useful or valuable flora]]** growing on/in/near it
- 9-10 Treasure (roll 1d6 to inform how much remains)
- 11-12 Roll again, twice

For more examples, see **[[06 - Blackwater Lake#**1d12** ruin or barrow|Blackwater Lake]]**.

sections.title: Artifacts
sections.body:
The Barrow Builders gladly looted and repurposed whatever treasures they could, and often dabbled with dangerous powers. Their sites often contain artifacts related to:

- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Dool trees**|Death and the undying]]**
- The **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**
- The **[[43 - Stone Lords#Stone Lords|Stone Lords]]**
- Other **[[30 - The Makers#The Makers|Makers]]**, especially the **[[18 - Forge Lords#Forge Lords|Forge Lords]]** or the **[[45 - Tempest Lords#Tempest Lords|Tempest Lords]]**

sections.title: Various treasures
sections.body:
- A rusty Y-shaped pendant (Value 0), symbol of the cult who fed the **[[46 - The Things Below#**1d12** origin|Voice of the Eternal Maw]]**
- A bronze blade from a **metal construct**'s [[18 - Forge Lords#**Metal constructs**|Forge Lords]] limb, remade into a brutal axe (*close*, *messy*, *awkward*, +1 damage, Value 1)
- A map (*fragile*) to the barrow of a famously rich warlord
- Ancient grains in wax-sealed jugs, still good and worth 1 Surplus (*immobile*)
- A Maker-era gold coin (Value 2), found in a mass grave, the tether for a **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|ghost]]**
- A fine torc of **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**Aetherium**|aetherium]]** around a skeleton's neck; shocks its wearer if they try to remove it or engage in any sort of violence
- A spear (*close*, *thrown*, *beautiful*, *indestructible*, 2 piercing, Value 3) made of **[[43 - Stone Lords#Various treasures|makerglass]]**, shaft and all, found in a hero's tomb
- A Stone Lord's cleft skull, coated in gold and studded with gems (*cumbersome*, Value 4)

sections.title: An old bronze dagger
sections.body:
*hand*, *magical*

Tinged green, but sharp and sturdy. In the chaotic days after the Makers fell, it was the weapon of a desperate father. He killed his own brother with it, to protect his children's food. He killed others, too, to make sure his family had enough. So many, many others.

The dagger holds a shard of that father's desperate will. When you *Seek Insight about someone while carrying the dagger*, add the following to the list of questions you can ask. If *your hand is on the hilt*, you can ask one of these questions for free, even on a 6-.

- What of mine do they most want?
- How am I most vulnerable to them?
- What do they carry that is useful to me?

If a PC keeps the dagger, treat it as a threat (a *MacGuffin*; **Instinct** to cause paranoia).

sections.title: The Three-star Crown
sections.body:
*magical*, *beautiful*, Value 4 A circlet of whitest gold, set with three black sapphires that shine like stars. It was made by a renowned smith, an apprentice of the **[[18 - Forge Lords#Forge Lords|Forge Lords]]**, at the command of a petty warlord. The warlord was so pleased, he maimed the smith's hands so that she could never make its rival. The smith killed herself in despair, cursing the crown to steal the hopes of all who wore it.

When you *wear the crown and interact with someone who fears or respects you*, you roll +CHA with advantage. Alas, *each time you don the crown*, roll +WIS: **on a 10+**, tell the GM one of your hopes and why you doubt it can be achieved; **on a 7-9**, tell them a hope you have abandoned; **on a 6-**, you fall into a deep despair that lasts until you rouse yourself to face a mortal danger.

If *the crown is destroyed*—the gems shattered, the metal reduced to slag then the despair is lifted and all lost hopes are restored.

sections.title: A lead bracelet
sections.body:
Makes you feel important.

*magical*, Value 1 This heavy, black metal torc is set with a red gem. It feels good upon your wrist.

When you *wear the torc and threaten someone that you consider beneath you*, lose HP equal to the torc's current Cost (1d4 to start). They are filled with fear; the craven will flee, cower, or grovel, and even the brave will flinch and hesitate. Each time you use the torc, the Cost increases by 1 die size (max 1d10).

When you *wear the torc for a week without anyone questioning your authority*, decrease the Cost by one step (min 1d4).

sections.title: Minor arcana
sections.body: Pick 1 or have someone roll.

sections.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
sections.body:
- 1 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#An old scroll case , fragile|An old scroll case]]**
- 2 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A tattered mantle|A tattered mantle]]**
- 3 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#Fetch's Mask Ken|A disturbing mask]]**
- 4 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A vellum scroll , fragile|A vellum scroll]]**
- 5 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#Sigil of Authority Authority:|A silvery signet ring]]**
- 6 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A mummified hand|A mummified hand]]**

sections.title: Major arcana
sections.body:
The following are particularly likely to be associated with the Barrow Builders:

- The **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Blood-quenched Sword|Blood-quenched Sword]]**
- The **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Hec'tumel Codex|Hec'tumel Codex]]**
- The **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Blackwood Fetishes|Blackwood Fetishes]]**

sections.title: Hazards
sections.body:
- **[[43 - Stone Lords#**Dangers**|Unstable construction]]**
- **[[43 - Stone Lords#Perilous stone|Perilous stone]]**
- **[[11 - The Dread River#**Dangers**|Cursed objects]]**
- Darkness, deep in a barrow
- Low ceilings, cramped quarters, poor lines of sight

sections.title: Banefire
sections.body:
Curtains or pillars of heatless flame, blue or green or purple, with no obvious source of fuel. Banefire features in many tales of the barrows: to repel, to imprison, or just as an uncanny feature of the mounds.

- <sup>ä</sup> Spring forth with little/no warning
- <sup>ä</sup> Blind them with sudden light
- <sup>ä</sup> Block a path or a line of sight
- <sup>ä</sup> Burn spirits, the undead, and living souls (d8 damage, ignores armor)

**Something interesting:** Ghosts and other spirits fear banefire as much as mortals, but the inhabitants of some barrows can control it.

**Something useful:** Banefire burns only where it's meant to; it never spreads. Each banefire is anchored to a blackened bone, all that remains of the mortal whose body and soul were sacrificed to fuel the flames.

sections.title: Traps
sections.body:
They put all that effort into building barrows. They weren't going to leave them unguarded.

To create a trap, consider the barrow's purpose and what it contains, and where the trap is found. Then pick or roll for the trap's intent, its trigger, its tell, and as many elements as makes sense. Imagine how it works. If you like, prep GM moves and/or an impending doom for the trap, and maybe a custom player move.

sections.title: 1d12 intent
sections.body: 1-2 To kill/maim/injure 3-4 To repel/separate/deny/isolate 5-6 To weaken/hinder/delay 7-8 To capture/punish/torment 9-10 To alert/awaken/prepare

sections.title: 1d12 trigger
sections.body:
11-12 Roll d10 twice

- 1-2 Stepping/entering/being/staying somewhere
- 3-4 Touching something
- 5-6 Moving something/picking it up
- 7-8 Breaking/disturbing something
- 9-10 Opening/closing something
- 11-12 Perceiving/examining/tinkering with something

| 1d12 | tell |
|---|---|
| 1 | A brief warning before it goes off |
| 2-4 | A tiny detail, easy to miss |
| 5-7 | Unusual construction/decor |
| 8-10 | Bait |
| 11-12 | Clear signs—it's obviously a trap |

| 1d12 | elements |
|---|---|
| 1 | Camouflage/illusion/deception |
| 2 | Gravity/weight/mass/hardness |
| 3 | Blades/spikes/needles/pointy bits |
| 4 | Wire/rope/pulleys/contraptions |
| 5 | Tension/pressure/springs/balance |
| 6 | Chemics/poison/acid/gas/fire |
| 7 | Special materials (see below) |
| 8 | Runes/glyphs/sigils/marks/spells |
| 9 | Light/dark/heat/cold/noise/quiet |
| 10 | Thoughts/emotions/mind games |
| 11 | Spirits/constructs/undead/vermin |
| 12 | Previously triggered/disarmed |

Special materials might include **[[24 - Green Lords#**1d12** ability|hazardous growths]]**, **[[18 - Forge Lords#**1d6** architectural elements|orichalcum]]**, **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Primordial powers**|black iron]]**, **[[38 - Rime Lords#Dark ice|dark ice]]**, **[[43 - Stone Lords#Various treasures|makerglass]]**, **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**Aetherium**|aetherium]]**, **[[46 - The Things Below#**Unnatural phenomena**|red crystal]]**, and/or other things of your devising.

sections.title: Examples:
sections.body:
- A pit, 15-feet deep, the bottom lined with rusty spikes; covered by taut fabric, pebbles, and dust
- A gap runs down the ceiling of a low tunnel; stepping on a flagstone releases a swinging axe
- A puzzle-box on a decoy coffin; futzing with it releases stabby little constructs from the coffin
- A corpse's sword; moving it pulls a wire, which upends an urn full of salt and releases a trapped wraith
- A rune-etched doorway; any who try to enter stop, turn around, and forget the last few minutes
- Opening the door to the final vault requires removing a keystone; the whole barrow will collapse
- Sconces of glowing makerglass; break the rune-seal on the sarcophagus, get fried by lightning
- A dead-end, the word VIGILANCE carved on the far wall, the room full of colorless, odorless gas; causes headaches, irritability, fainting, death

sections.title: People
sections.body:
- **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk|Hillfolk]]**, warning away explorers or chasing them off
- A **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk rider|hdour]]**, **[[29 - Lygos and the South#Adventurer|antiquarian]]**, or **[[46 - The Things Below#**Cultist**|sorcerer]]**, seeking knowledge/artifacts
- **[[29 - Lygos and the South#Adventurer|Adventurers]]**, hunting treasure
- A **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|necromancer]]**, consulting with those long-dead

sections.title: Monsters
sections.body:
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Ghosts]]** and **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|revenants]]**, haunting barrows, reclaimed ruins, old battlegrounds mass graves
- **[[43 - Stone Lords#**Construct**|Constructs]]**, stolen from the Stone Lords or made using their arts
- **[[46 - The Things Below#**1d12** origin|Corrupted beings]]**, ancient pets of ancient sorcerers, or local fauna affected by lingering evil
- **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|Spirits of the wild]]**, bound by old spells

 *Group*, *undead*, *corrupted*, *terrifying*, *hardy*, *hoarder*

**HP** 14; **Armor** 4 (resilience), 2 vs. bronze or fire (mail)

**Damage** rusty sword d8+2 (*close*, *forceful*) or crushing grip d8+2 (*hand*, *grabby*, *forceful*)

**Special qualities** bound to their barrow **Instinct** to protect their hoard

- <sup>ä</sup> Utter an echoing warning
- <sup>ä</sup> Block an exit
- <sup>ä</sup> Stand fast, refusing to be moved
- <sup>ä</sup> Keep fighting with or reattach a severed limb

As you might expect, grave robbing was always a problem for the Barrow Builders. But a clever sorcerer had a solution! So many hateful spirits roamed the world in those days. Why not bind some to the corpses of the deceased, and let the dead guard their own treasure?

sections.title: Phantom rider
sections.body:
*Group*, *large*, *spirit*, *undead*, *organized* **HP** 14; **Armor** 2 (lacks organs, shield) **Damage** spear 1d8 (*close*, *thrown*) or trample d8+2 (*hand*, *forceful*) or bow d8 (*near*)

**Special qualities** bound to their bones; can travel far and wide; can't manifest in daylight; takes +1d4 damage from silver

**Instinct** to seek tribute, to lord over others

- <sup>ä</sup> Speed across the landscape like the wind itself
- <sup>ä</sup> Form a body of soil, dust, and mist
- <sup>ä</sup> Flank and harry their foes

For a time, it was common for warriors to pledge both their lives and their deaths to their lieges, and to be interred with their horses and their fellows in their master's tomb. Thus, they could live on in death, still basking in a grim and joyless glory.

sections.title: Deathless ones
sections.body:
In the time of the Barrow Builders, many warlords and sorcerers were obsessed with immortality. A number of them found it—or something like it—through undeath.

To create a deathless one, imagine a warlord (someone similar to a **[[34 - North Manmarch#**Chief**|Manmarcher chief]]**), a **[[46 - The Things Below#**Cultist**|sorcerer]]**, a **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|necromancer]]**, or a **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk rider|hdour]]**. Consider making them **[[46 - The Things Below#**1d12** origin|corrupted]]** by the Things Below. Add the *undead* tag, and give them +4 HP because they are animated by more than biology.

From there, pick or roll for the tether that sustains them, how it sustains them, and its limitation. Then pick or roll for the deathless one's current state. Consider where and how they (or their influence) might be encountered. Let this all inform their story, their personality, and their stat block.

sections.title: 1d12 tether
sections.body:
- 1-2 Small (needle, torc, ring, etc.)
- 3-4 (sword, scepter, crown, scroll, mummified hand, etc.)
- 5-6 (shield, standard, urn, etc.)
- 7-8 , *cumbersome* (bust, cauldron, coffer, tapestry, etc.)
- 9-10 *immobile* (hoard, tree, cave, statue, sarcophagus, etc.)
- 11-12 *fragile* and roll d10 again (egg, porcelain, mirror, device, etc.)

sections.title: 1d12 how it sustains them
sections.body:
- 1-2 It hides their death/heart/soul
- 3-4 It anchors their soul to the world
- 5-6 It feeds/renews/recharges them
- 7-8 It gives them leverage over the **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death's Door**|Lady of Crows]]**
- 9-10 It shields them from the Lady and the **[[35 - The Pale Hunter#The Pale Hunter|Pale Hunter]]**
- 11-12 It suffers age/death/decrepitude in their place

Unless their tether fails or is destroyed, they cannot truly die. Their body will eventually heal or regenerate, or their spirit will possess some hapless victim.

sections.title: 1d12 limitation
sections.body:
- 1-2 They can't leave its presence
- 3-4 It must be fed/charged/maintained
- 5-6 It must be relocated often
- 7-8 Its destruction results in their instant, terrible, final death
- 9-10 If "killed," they require outside aid to be restored
- 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice

sections.title: 1d12 current state
sections.body:
- 1-3 Greatly diminished, perhaps destroyed and waiting to return
- 4-6 Imprisoned and largely forgotten
- 7-9 Hidden, apathetic, self-involved
- 10 Trapped, but with some influence
- 11 Secretly active, pursuing schemes
- 12 Roll 1d10 twice

*I've established that the Mindgem's fiery heart is in the clutches of a deathless sorcerer, whose soul inhabits an arcane device that controls a number of lithic servants* (see [[43 - Stone Lords#**1d12** sapience|Stone Lords]])*.* 

*[[46 - The Things Below#**Cultist**|I start with a sorcerer]]**[[46 - The Things Below#**For We Are Many**|, corrupted by El'rash-Orra]]**. For gifts, I give him a terrifying gaze and the ability to inflict false sensations. For marks, I give him slow-healing wounds and strange fascinations (he's a voyeur).* 

*I already decided that he had "anchored his soul" to an arcane device in a Stone Lord manor (*immobile*,* fragile*). For a limitation, I think the device "must be fed/charged/maintained," and that "its destruction results in his final death." For a current state, I think he's "trapped, but with some influence" via the lithic servants he can possess.* 

*I picture him in his oversized manor, seated on a makeshift throne, permanently wounded, unable to move on his own. A dozen faceless stone servants attend him, moving him like a puppet when needed. Behind the throne is a contraption of rune-carved stone and crystal, the makerglass heart burning at its core. His mind is spread among his servants, some of whom scour Three Coven Lake for artifacts, gossip, and materials to maintain his device. Should the device fail, his consciousness will splinter and his body will finally die. The servants, each containing a splinter of his soul, will fight each other for primacy. None will succeed.* 

*I write up his stat block like this:*

sections.title: MYGHAL, DEATHLESS SORCERER
sections.body:
Solitary, undead, corrupted, paranoid, magical, hoarder, terrifying HP 16; ARMOR 4 (wards) DAMAGE word of false pain d8 (near,

 area, ignores armor) SPECIAL QUALITIES tethered to the

 device; broken body; aware of all that his servants perceive

- INSTINCT to jealously hoard secrets > Inflict false sensations on others
- > Fix someone with his terrible gaze
- > Reveal a plan or a horrid truth > Control his lithic servants like
- they were extensions of himself > Recover from any wound, slowly

and never fully

sections.title: Blackwater Lake
sections.body:
In the west of the **[[42 - The Steplands#Spring|Steplands]]**, in the shadow of the **[[27 - Huffel Peaks#Huffel Peaks|Huffel Peaks]]**, there is a large and placid lake. A low ridge surrounds it, and a wide, rocky shore. The Aberzh River flows in from the west. No rivers or streams flow out.

The water is dark, and very deep, and very, very still. It is always cold but never freezes. There are no waves. The wind shuns the surface. No birds dare alight.

This was once great Dulenn, city of Dark Waters, the slow-beating heart of the **Stone Lords**' vast domain [[43 - Stone Lords|Stone Lords]]. Whereas **[[47 - Three Coven Lake#Three Coven Lake|Teirstered]]** was a city of towers and splendor, Dulenn was a solemn place. A quiet warren, carved and sung from bedrock with the beauty of a limestone cave.

There was no lake, then. Not above the ground. But it was called "Dark Waters" for a reason. For seven deep tunnels led to seven mighty seals, each guarded by Stone Lord sentinels who sang a ceaseless lulling song. Behind those doors was a vast and lightless lake. A hole in the earth. A **[[06 - Blackwater Lake#**Call of Daagon**|Mouth of Daagon]]**.

When the **[[24 - Green Lords#Green Lords|Green Lords]]** roused the Things Below to help them quell the **[[12 - Fae#Fae|Fae]]**, the seals below Dulenn cracked. Daagon began to whisper. Began to sing. Resentment grew among the Stone Lords' human servants. Leaders rose among them: cunning, confident, and eloquent, knowing things they oughtn't, awash in uncanny power. Cults of liberation formed. A conspiracy grew.

Seven sorcerers infiltrated Dulenn. They found the tunnels. They slew the sentinels. They broke the seals.

And Daagon, Who Waits in Deep Water, waited no more. It thrashed. It opened its yawning dark wet jaws. Dulenn fell into the earth. And the Dark Water, the Blackwater, surged in to flood the hole.

sections.title: Impressions
sections.body:
- Striations in nearby outcrops, tilted towards the lake
- The damp, mineral smell of wet stone
- Clouds, stars, shoreline—all reflected in the lake
- Stillness, quiet, echoes of the slightest sound
- At times: thick banks of fog, wet, cloying, isolating

Tip: lower your voice; speak slowly, softly, make them lean in to hear you.

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- **[[06 - Blackwater Lake#**Servants of Daagon**|Servants of Daagon]]** are roaming further from the lake than usual.
- A **[[46 - The Things Below#**Cultist**|thrall]]** of Daagon drags a victim here to sacrifice.
- A **deathless one**'s [[05 - Barrow Builders#**Deathless ones**|Barrow Builders]] tether is hidden near the lake.
- Hillfolk **[[26 - Hillfolk#Upland horse|spirit-talkers]]** sing a certain song here every 7 years to keep the lake subdued.
- A dangerous artifact needs to be cast into the Mouth of Daagon.
- Certain NPCs hear a silent song, calling them to Blackwater Lake.

sections.title: Getting there
sections.body:
To reach Blackwater Lake from Stonetop:

- First you must travel to the Steplands, which will take at least 4 days.
- From there, it will take at least 2 days and corresponding amount of supplies
- You need a knowledgeable guide.
- Even then, you risk getting lost near the lake.
- The terrain itself is treacherous, you

sections.title: Areas
sections.body:
- **Nearby hills:** A soft place, where paths might change in the fog, full of ruins, **[[05 - Barrow Builders#Barrow|barrow mounds]]**, and **[[42 - The Steplands#Caves|caves]]**. Bedrock and ruins all tilt towards the lake.
- **Outer ridge:** An escarpment that rings the lake, dropping 50-100' to the shores. Lightly wooded up top. Holds a number of ruins, buried at odd angles, some poking out.
- **Shore:** Extends 100-200' from the outer ridge. Lots of rubble, much of it ancient masonry. Thick lichen, grass that leans away from the lake.
- **The Aberzh River:** Cuts through the western ridge, then widens and flows lazily to the lake. Muddy, shallow, rocky. Lots of dark algae.
- **The Pillar:** On the north shore, 40' wide and over 100' tall. Made of salvaged masonry stones, crudely stacked. No obvious entrance. A precarious climb. Feels cursed.
- **The lake:** About 2 x 3 miles. Impossibly still, dark, reflective. Always cold, never freezes. Hundreds of feet deep near the center.

sections.title: Terrain
sections.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12). If you like, roll 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

sections.title: 1d12 terrain
sections.body:
| 1   | Trees/shrubs/succulents/tall grass |
|-----|------------------------------------|
| 2-3 | Ridge wall/prominence/overhang     |
| 4-5 | Scree/rubble/rocks/boulders        |
| 6-7 | ruin or barrow (next page)         |
| 8   | Very large boulder(s)              |
| 9   | Pool/pond/spring/rivulet           |
| 10  | Peninsula/cove/inlet               |
| 11  | Mud/mire/shingle/shallows          |
| 12  | Drop-off/deep water                |
|     |                                    |

sections.title: 1d12 ruin or barrow
sections.body:
- 1 Partly collapsed mound of earth and stones, each carved with fearrunes. Holds bones, clay vessels full of rotted grain.
- 2 Warehouse-sized bunker of massive blocks, buried at a severe angle. Bay doors visible, heavily marred, but still intact.
- 3 Warren of human-sized rooms, dug into outer ridge. Confusing layout, odd angles, unstable, lit by glowing crystals.
- 4 Door into the ridge, leading to a crude, vaulted room. Holds a 12' statue of a squat, fishy beast. Its mouth opens to a deep shaft.
- 5 Dozens of makerglass tubes, each smaller than the last, lined up in ranks and fused together. Chime hauntingly when struck.
- 6 Walls of a cubical stone room with many niches. Tilted ~30°. Crystalline fixture in one wall. Words spoken here blare from somewhere across the lake.
- 7 Series of dissimilar rooms and tunnels, leading to a massive underground amphitheater, like a cyst in the earth. Largely flooded.

sections.title: 1d12 ruin or barrow
