- 2 You contract a **[[13 - Ferrier's Fen#**Dangers**|disease]]**; it merely makes you miserable, but infects those nearby with a deadly sickness.
- 3 Those around you find themselves on edge, their emotions running raw, and are subject to **[[46 - The Things Below#**Voice of the Eternal Maw**|whispers and visions]]**. Unstable folk quickly succumb.
- 4 You grow thin, haggard, and unwell. Mark a debility each week, which you cannot clear. Should you die, you become a **[[Inserts#Revenant|revenant]]**, tethered to the item, obsessed with either protecting it or returning it to where it was found (your choice).
- 5 Wherever you go, plants wither, food spoils, crops fail, beasts grow barren and sickly.
- 6 Those who die in your presence are unable to pass through the Last Door. They linger as **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|revenants]]** or **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|ghosts]]**.

Tailor a curse's specifics to the item, to where and with what it was it found. Incorporate the themes and aspects of any relevant entities. And feel free to create entirely new curses, riffing on the options above.

How does one free oneself of a curse? Possibilities include…

- … returning the item to where it was originally found;
- … destroying the item utterly: burning it, melting it, tossing it into lava, etc.;
- … packing it in salt and burying it at a crossroads;
- … driving a bronze spike through it, into an ancient elder tree;
- … ritually cleansing the item, binding the curse to an appropriate vessel, and burying/hiding the vessel somewhere; or perhaps
- … passing ownership of the item (and thus the curse) on to another willing party; they need not know it is cursed, but must claim the item as theirs.

sections.title: Luglfsk
sections.body:
*Horde*, *small*, *amphibious*, *corrupted* **HP** 3; **Armor** 1 (hide)

**Damage** electric shock d6 (*hand*, *reload*), or chew d4 (*hand*)

**Special qualities** lightning-proof **Instinct** to feed

- ä Sense a creature's electrical field
- ä Stun or paralyze with a touch, bzzzt
- ä Swarm a stunned creature

Each one is a few feet long, like a cuttlefish crossed with a giant maggot, squirming wet and gray, a face full of feelers whose touch stings like lightning.

They spend most of their lives in the water, congregating near **[[43 - Stone Lords#Power-crystal|power-crystals]]** and **aetherium** artifacts [[45 - Tempest Lords#**Aetherium**|Tempest Lords]] that have been claimed by the river. They seem entranced by the passive energy, almost lulled to sleep. But when they sense something more dynamic, something meatier, they wriggle out of the water fast enough.

Some Hillfolk claim that luglfsk are just tadpoles, and tell stories of larger, many-legged terrors that fling lightning from face-feelers that stretch as long as a man grows tall. But those are probably just spook-stories to scare travelers. Probably.

sections.title: Jellied horror
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *stealthy*, *amorphous*, *mindless*

**HP** 19; **Armor** 1 (lacks organs)

**Damage** pseudopod d10+1 (*reach*, *hand*, *grabby*, *messy*, 1 piercing)

**Special qualities** immune to stabbing; takes half damage from weapons unless they're *forceful*; acid-proof; harmed by chalk/quicklime/similar chemics

**Instinct** to seep, to consume, to grow

- ä Lurk in water, moving slowly, seeking or waiting for food
- ä Dissolve organic material—weapons, clothes, flesh, etc.
- ä Engulf, d10+3 damage with advantage (*hand*, *messy*, ignores armor)

Sometimes, the waters of the Dread River overflow their banks and flood the cursed ruins and tainted places. And sometimes, when the floodwaters recede, they leave behind… something. Something that just isn't right.

sections.title: The Suileach
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *huge*, *magical*, *terrifying*, *aquatic*, *corrupted*

**HP** 26; **Armor** 2 (scales)

**Damage** jaws like a cave d10+7 (*reach*, *forceful*, *messy*, 3 piercing) or thrashing coils d10+5 (*close*, *area*, *forceful*)

**Special qualities** many-eyed; impossible to surprise

**Instinct** to dominate the Dread River

- ä Sense an intruder upon its domain
- ä Transfix someone with one of its myriad eyes (see below)
- ä Drag intruders to their doom

It's a thing of legend. A great scaly wurm, they say, hundreds of feet long, festooned with eyes along its bulk. Hundreds of eyes, so very many. They say it sees all that happens along the river. They say it brooks no tresspass, that it drowns or devours all who encroach on its domain.

But that's not entirely true. Some folks have met the Suileach and lived to tell the tale, haunted by what they saw reflected in its eyes.

sections.title: When you stare transfixed into one of the Suileach's eyes, mark dazed and pick 1:
sections.body:
- You see yourself, unburdened. Name an NPC whom you love; your heart grows cold to them. Then, gain a new move from your playbook.
- You see yourself as you might have been. Change your instinct and swap one of your playbook moves for another.
- You see your death, as it will occur. The next time you would roll for Death's Door, don't—you get a 10+. The second time you would roll for Death's Door, don't—you get a 6-.
- You see the truth reflected, darkly. The GM will tell you something interesting and useful, yet thoroughly disturbing.

sections.title: Totnatter
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *stealthy*, *terrifying* **HP** 16; **Armor** 2

**Damage** venomous bite d10+1 (*reach*, *close*, *hand*, 1 piercing)

**Special qualities** thermal vision; extraordinary sense of smell

**Instinct** to toy with its food

- ä Scent prey, track it down
- ä Rattle its tail, paralyzing prey with fear
- ä Pump them full of venom, then retreat
- ä Follow, waiting for its prey to collapse

These grey-white rattlesnakes grow to absurd size, big enough to eat a boar, a deer, or even a person. Manmarchers say that they haunt the bluffs and ruins along the river, waiting months, years, or even decades between meals.

While most rattlesnakes rattle to warn off threats or predators, totnatter use their rattles to hunt. They delight in the terror their rattle causes, and use it even when they could just bite their prey unawares.

When *a totnatter pumps its prey full of venom*, start the following countdown:

l Their hands shake, their bodies shiver (mark *weakened*)

l They grow confused, thinking things that aren't true (mark *dazed*)

l They struggle to breathe, to keep going (mark *miserable*)

l They are dying (roll for Death's Door)

sections.title: Fae
sections.body:
Strange folk live in the **[[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|Great Wood]]** and **[[13 - Ferrier's Fen#Ferrier's Fen|Ferrier's Fen]]**. Wondrous and dangerous, beings out of legend. The **[[17 - Forest Folk#Forest Folk|Forest Folk]]** called them "Good Neighbors" and "Hidden Ones," and took pains to avoid insulting them. They cautioned Stonetop to do the same. And while the Forest Folk are gone, the Fae remain.

The **[[24 - Green Lords#Names|Green Lords]]** bred the Fae long ago, to herd spirits and to weave wondrous realms. They relied on the Fae to manage their estates, to build the **[[53 - Waystones#Waystones|waystones]]**, and, later, to create the Green Lord's idyllic afterlives.

Some Fae resented their servitude. Some found loopholes in the promises and oaths that bound them. Ringleaders arose, creating webs of debt and obligation that undermined their masters' hold.

The Green Lords grew desperate. They called on the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**, corrupted themselves with unholy power. They bred living weapons, hunted down the rebels, and took terrible vengeance.

The uprising nearly failed, but the Green Lords had drawn the ire of their fellow Makers. The **[[43 - Stone Lords#Stone Lords|Stone Lords]]**, in particular, were horrified by the Green Lords' corruption. They worked a terrible spell to shatter the heart of the Green Lord's domain, turning it into the ruined bog that today we call Ferrier's Fen. Then they allied with the Fae, and waged war against the Green Lords who survived.

After the siege of **[[52 - Vor Svetelik#Vor Svetelik|Vor Svetelik]]**, the Fae found themselves adrift and bereft of purpose. Many were no longer bound by oath or obligation. Ringleaders bickered and squabbled. Factions formed. Armies disbanded. And the Fae became the fractured people they are today.

sections.title: The good neighbors
sections.body:
Fae and mortals cross paths, of course. The Forest Folk descended from the Green Lord's mortal servants, and dwelt in the Great Wood for centuries. Fae fought side-by-side with the Stone Lords' human slaves. And some Fae, certainly, know of the humans living on their borders: Stonetop, **[[32 - Marshedge#Marshedge|Marshedge]]**, the **[[34 - North Manmarch#North Manmarch|Manmarchers]]**, even some **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#1d6 point of interest|Whitefang villages]]**. There have always been dealings between them and us.

But Fae are not like us. They breathe magic. Time means little to them. They have no base needs. They are driven by pride, boredom, and obsession. They are shaped by their debts, their obligations, and the ancient scars of servitude and war.

Fae are *dangerous* to mortals. Maybe not hostile (though some are), but alien. Some admire our spark of life, our creativity and passion. Some find our memories and our emotions rich and heady. Some delight in our dignity or our short-lived antics. Some might even care about us. But they also think no more of stealing an infant from the crib than we would at picking a pup out of a litter.

sections.title: 1d12 theme
sections.body:
- 1 Fluidity of time and space: stasis, distortion, dilation, things where (and when) they oughtn't be
- 2 Enchantment, dreams and dreamlogic, the odd accepted as normal
- 3 Illusion/glamour, often hiding a base or even sordid reality
- 4 The intangible made real; metaphor made literal
- 5 Promises, deals, and loopholes; debts and insults weighing heavy as iron chains
- 6 Opaque etiquette, strange norms, values upended, seeming amorality
- 7 Passions, whims, and excess; the autonomy of others ignored
- 8 Extremes of beauty/ugliness, blurred lines between them
- 9 Malleability of one's looks, one's identity, one's true and secret heart
- 10 Liminality: neither spirit nor flesh, here nor there, this nor that, real nor unreal
- 11 Deep ties to the natural world and **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|spirits of the wild]]**
- 12 Enslavement by the **[[24 - Green Lords#Names|Green Lords]]**, the war against them, the lasting scars left by both

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- A local family claims their infant has been replaced with a Fae changeling.
- A widower wants a Fae to take away the memories of his lost love.
- A hunter naively offers Stonetop's hospitality to a mischievous Fae.
- A **[[24 - Green Lords#Zrajedak|zrajedak]]** is released from stasis; terrified Fae seek help.
- An NPC is indebted to a Fae; it wants what they cannot bear to give (a child, their love, a marriage, etc.).
- A Fae falls in love with a villager, and tries to live a mortal life.

sections.title: In general:
sections.body:
- Who among you has lost a sibling or a child, stolen from the crib?
- Who in the village is said to have Fae blood?
- Who in town once made a deal with a Fae? What did they lose? What did they get in return?
- What Fae being did you once meet in the Wood? How did you get away unscathed?

sections.title: About any given Fae they meet:
sections.body:
- What about their presence fills you with awe/longing/sadness/joy/\_\_?
- What forgotten memory do they stir?
- What detail about them will you struggle to remember later?
- What detail could you never forget?

sections.title: Lore
path: About any given Fae they meet:

sections.title: Everyone in Stonetop knows that:
path: About any given Fae they meet:
sections.body:
- Strange, magical folk dwell in the Great Wood
- They vary greatly in appearance, ability, and disposition
- That they are strange, dreamlike, fickle, wondrous, dangerous

sections.title: One might know:
path: About any given Fae they meet:
sections.body:
- Details of a given Fae: behaviors, demeanor, abilities, limits, names, etc.
- The specifics of **[[12 - Fae#Magic and the Fae|Fae nature]]**
- That iron is anathema to them
- Proper etiquette for dealing with one
- Ways to protect yourself from them
- About domains: their entrances, the dangers they pose, specific examples
- Tales of the Wandering Market
- That they served the Green Lords

sections.title: Very few know:
sections.body:
- The specifics of their history, their origin, their rebellion
- The nuances of their politics, debts, relationships, obligations
- How to find the Wandering Market

sections.title: Names
sections.body:
Fae "names" tend to be titles, nouns, or descriptive phrases or borrowed from those they've met—simple, whimsical, ominous, or grandiose as befits their nature.

Mix, match, and riff on words related to plants and beasts and rocks, features of the landscape, natural phenomena, colors, mundane objects, emotions or emotional activities, titles, relations, and local names.

Some simple names: Acorn, Cobweb, Hob, Jackdaw, Kettle, Lark, Moth, Quills, Rill, Robin, Stewpot, Trillium, Uncle Whiskers, Whistler, Wobbles, Yellowtail

Some whimsical names: Bellweather, Bitterlips, Moonbeam, Peasebottom, Poppy o' Twilight, Rainpetal, Stagthorn, Thistledust, Whiteoak, Yellow Britches

Some ominous names: Bonekettle, Father Longfingers, Grinning Nel, the Hangman, the King of Dirges, Old Sorrows, the Pale Sisters, Stonetooth, the Weeping Rose

Some grandiose names: Dawnwhisper, Fireblossom, Breath of Thunder, Hare Starblade, the Midnight Mare, Rain Upon the Valley, Silverstag, the Storm King

Some Fae might go by many names, or change their names over time. If a given Fae is known to the locals, consider what they call it in their local tongue. A Fae named Moonbeam, for example, might be called "Pelydrlloer" in Stonetop or "Mondstrohl" by the Manmarchers.

Perhaps each Fae has a true and secret name, by which they can be commanded and bound. If so, it must be their most treasured and precious secret.

sections.title: Fae nature
Injury
sections.body: Fae are unnaturally tough (Armor 4) and can heal any wound not caused by iron. A Fae who suffers a "mortal" wound might pass through the Last Door. But they might also change their mind and come back later, for Fae are fickle things.

sections.title: Iron
sections.body:
Iron (including steel) is offensive to Fae. Its presence insults them. It disrupts their magic. It burns their skin, ignores their Armor, leaves scars that never fade. Iron can kill a Fae, truly kill them dead.

**[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Primordial powers**|Black iron]]** is even worse. Its presence unravels their magic. Its touch doesn't just burn, it suppurates their flesh (+1d6 damage, ignores armor, messy). It leaves vicious, gaping wounds that never heal and that eventually turn them into **[[12 - Fae#**The Bloody Boots**|Hollow Fae]]**.

sections.title: Magic and the Fae
sections.body:
Fae are Fae, neither spirit nor mortal. As a rule, magic that specifically affects "spirits" or "mortals" does not apply to the Fae. They are their own thing.

With that said, magic is fuzzy and each Fae is unique. When in doubt, consider what feels right in this particular case. A ploy that would work in a fairy tale should probably work against the Fae!

sections.title: Illusions and glamours
sections.body:
Illusions are lies. They have no substance. An illusionary chair bears no weight; the illusion of a sword does not cut. A skillful Fae might create the illusion of pain and blood when you are cut with an illusionary sword, but the wound has no lasting effect.

Glamour is more profound. It changes a thing and how it interacts with the world, at least until the glamour fades. Glamour a rock into a chair and sit comfortably. Glamour a stick into a sword and it will cut, make you bleed real blood. When the glamour ends, the wound remains.

Glamours are like poetry. One might glamour an acorn into a gem, or fluttering leaves into butterflies, or a braying mortal's head into that of a donkey. But one does not just, say, glamour a spear into a puddle of water. These things must rhyme!

Fae can see through each other's illusions and glamours, but as a rule they play along. Doing otherwise can be insulting.

Glamour affects neither iron nor steel, and the touch of either causes illusions to flicker and fade. The touch of **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Primordial powers**|black iron]]** dispels both illusions and glamours, like soap dropped in oily water.

sections.title: Language
sections.body: Fae understand anyone they meet, and can always make themselves understood. Many speak in an affected manner (accented, in verse, with odd sentence construction, etc.) to amuse themselves, to annoy others, or because that's just how they think.

sections.title: Debts and promises
sections.body:
Fae are bound by debts, obligations, and promises. This applies to mortals who treat with them, too. A Fae might:

- <sup>ä</sup> Make an offer (fair or lopsided)
- <sup>ä</sup> Politely decline or dissemble
- <sup>ä</sup> Insist, ask for more, offer less
- <sup>ä</sup> Call in a debt, use it as leverage
- <sup>ä</sup> Make or demand a promise
- <sup>ä</sup> Find/exploit a loophole
- <sup>ä</sup> Trade a debt to another Fae

When a *Fae is beholden to you*, you have advantage on any roll to resist or defend yourself against it. When you *leverage a debt that they owe you or a promise they've made*, gain advantage to Persuade or otherwise influence it.

When you *are beholden to a Fae*, you have disadvantage on any roll made directly against them (including damage rolls). When you *fail to uphold a promise you've made to a Fae*, for any reason, either you are beholden to that Fae or they take it as an insult (their choice).

When you *pass through the Last Door*, all debts you owe and promises you have made are null and void.

sections.title: Insults and etiquette
sections.body:
Fae are also bound by insults, pride, and etiquette. An insulted Fae feels the need for satisfaction like a mortal feels hunger, as a gnawing and physical thing. Their culture is full of little rules and points of etiquette that mortals often stumble over.

One might insult a Fae by…

- … rudely refusing an offer;
- … failing to honor a debt or promise;
- … taking what they think they own;
- … making demands or threats without the proper leverage;
- … sneaking iron into their presence;
- … failing to play along with their games;
- … disparaging their honor/their power/ their cunning/whatever they value about themselves; or
- … accusing them of any of the above.

When the PCs deal with a Fae, that Fae might:

- <sup>ä</sup> Set the PCs up for a faux pas
- <sup>ä</sup> Make an accusation
- <sup>ä</sup> Act affronted, genuinely or as a ploy
- <sup>ä</sup> Truly take insult

And if insulted, they might:

- <sup>ä</sup> Demand satisfaction
- <sup>ä</sup> Storm/scamper off
- <sup>ä</sup> Offer their forgiveness, as a gift
- <sup>ä</sup> Ignore all other rules of etiquette
- <sup>ä</sup> Spread tales or rumors
- <sup>ä</sup> Hold a grudge, lay plans for revenge
- <sup>ä</sup> Resort to violence or magic

sections.title: Creating Fae
sections.body: To create a Fae or a group thereof, think about what you already know, and the context in which they'll appear. Decide what they're like: pick or roll for their preoccupation, their size, and 1-3 aspects.

sections.title: 1d12 preoccupation
sections.body:
1-2 Playing/reveling/tricking/subverting 3-4 Ruling/enforcing/bullying/hurting 5-6 Striving/making/toiling/growing 7-8 Collecting/hoarding/obsessing 9-10 Preening/politicking/gossiping

11-12 Guarding/watching/sulking/hiding

sections.title: 1d12 size
sections.body:
1 *tiny* (bug- or bird-sized) 2-3 *tiny* (like a cat or smaller) 4-6 *small* (like a human child) 7-9 Human-sized 10-11 *large* (like a horse) 12 *huge* (elephant-sized, or bigger)

| 1d12 | aspects                              |
|------|--------------------------------------|
| 1    | Predatory/clawed/fanged/powerful     |
| 2    | Horned/antlered/hooved/prancing      |
| 3    | Furry/tailed/plump/scampering        |
| 4    | Winged/beaked/feathered/spindly      |
| 5    | Scaly/squamous/slimy/sinuous         |
| 6    | Iridescent/chitinous/bug-like/alien  |
| 7    | Treeish/weedish/prickly/stoic/stiff  |
| 8    | Leafy/flowering/winding/delicate     |
| 9    | Ethereal/fluid/frosty/graceful/aloof |
| 10   | Earthy/rocky/craggy/dirty/stolid     |
| 11   | Glittery/starry/luminous/fiery       |
|      |                                      |

12 Puppet-like/artificial/made/hollow

Consider rolling 1d6 to see how personlike and humanoid they are.

Next, pick or roll for 1-3 talents.

sections.title: 1d12 talent
sections.body:
1 Illusion/glamour 2 Shapechanging/transformations 3 Feats of strength/stamina/ labor/skill 4 Influencing the inanimate/the elements/beasts or birds or bugs 5 Flying/burrowing/phasing/etc. 6 Messing with time, space, causality 7 Well-versed in powerful, old magic 8 Enchanting visage/voice/ music/touch 9 Invisibility/camouflage/stealth 10 Cursing/blessing/giving/taking 11 Fae domains: creating/changing them, ensnaring others in them 12 Insight/prophecy/dreams and

*The PCs are heading to a Green Lord's tomb, and I'd like them to meet a Fae who was squatting there until recently.*

memories laid bare

*From the list of preoccupations, I pick "collecting/hoarding/obsessing." I bet he left a hoard back in the tomb! I get a 6 for size ("*small*"), and a 7 and 12 for its aspects ("treeish/weedish/prickly…" and "puppet-like…hollow"). I get a 4 on a d6 for how humanoid it is. I'm picturing a living puppet made of lashed-together, still-growing branches.* 

*For talents, he seems tricksy, so I pick "camouflage/stealth." And "influencing the inanimate" seems right; I think he can control plants and trees a bit?*

**Fae**

Now, pick or roll for what they were bred to do. If it's unclear, decide what manner of spirit they were bred from; pick or roll a theme from the **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|spirits of the wild]]**. Pick or roll for their role in the war against the Green Lords and for a lasting mark of their past.

sections.title: 1d12 bred to...
sections.body:
1-2 …create/maintain a false afterlife 3-4 …command/compel/enforce/punish 5-6 …deceive/obscure/protect 7-8 …entertain/enliven/beautify

9-10 …nurture/protect/cultivate 11-12 …work/labor/toil/produce/build

sections.title: 1d12 role in the war
sections.body: 1-3 Victim/casualty/refugee/prisoner 4-5 Uninvolved/dissident/loyalist 6-8 Conscript/recruit/soldier 9-10 Scout/saboteur/spy/traitor 11 Marshal/chief/overseer/enforcer 12 Hero/ringleader/mastermind

sections.title: 1d12 lasting mark
sections.body:
1 Arrogance/defiance/anger/cruelty 2 Shame/doubt/dread/fussiness 3 Fame/infamy/enmity/rivals

4 Nostalgia/denial/delusion/fixation 5 Patience/exactness/caution/cunning

6 Quick temper/fierce protectiveness

7 Duties/obligations/debts 8 Emptiness/loneliness/grief/ennui

9-10 Scars/impairments/visible wounds

11-12 Roll 1d10 twice, combine

Also: are they **[[46 - The Things Below#**Corrupted being**|corrupted]]** by the Things Below? Roll 1d6; on a 1, they are.

Pick or create a **[[12 - Fae#**1d12** lasting mark|name]]**, and write up their stat block. All Fae have the *fae* tag; many are also *magical*. They have unnatural resilience (4 Armor, 0 vs. iron), being neither fully flesh nor fully spirit.

*My little wicker-man clearly descends from a plant-spirit. I get an 11 for what he was bred to do ("work/labor/toil…"), a 2 for his role in the war ("victim…"), and a 12 for his lasting mark, followed by a 6 ("fierce protectiveness") and 3 ("enmity"). He's not corrupted.*

*I see him tending to some Green Lord's garden, all the while collecting trinkets and plants and rocks from the soil. He was fiercely protective of his collection and cared little for his fellow Fae. His plot burned and his hoard was lost, and he became a loner and outcast, eschewing the war. A few centuries ago, he found the tomb, called it home, and started to rebuild his hoard of trinkets. But a swyn* [[24 - Green Lords#**Rhagedn**|Green Lords]] *has forced him out, and he desperately wants his stuff back.*

*I call him "Thornthumb," for the prickly gardener he is. I write him up like so:*

sections.title: THORNTHUMB
sections.body:
Solitary, small, fae, stealthy, magical, devious

HP 12; ARMOR 5 (resilience, lacks vitals), 1 vs. iron

DAMAGE raking fingers d8 (hand, grabby)

SPECIAL QUALITIES: Fae nature, hollow INSTINCT: to acquire

- > Blend into the terrain
- > Filch something
- > Get help from nearby plants/ trees
- > Hide something in his body

1=

sections.title: Fae domains
sections.body:
Fae domains are hidden realities, existing between the physical and spirit worlds, touching and overlapping both but part of neither. Some are tiny, intimate, cozy. Others are vast and grand.

Not every place where a Fae dwells is truly a domain. Plenty of Fae squat in ruins or caves or hollow trees, perhaps spruced up with glamour but fully in this world. Domains are different, set apart.

To create a Fae domain, start with the real-world terrain it borders or overlaps with. Most are found in the Great Wood [[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|The Great Wood]], in the Foothills [[16 - The Foothills|The Foothills]], or in Ferrier's Fen [[13 - Ferrier's Fen#Ferrier's Fen|Ferrier’s Fen]]; those in the Fen are often corrupted [[46 - The Things Below#**Corrupted being**|The Things Below]]. Many are part of Green Lord ruins [[24 - Green Lords#Ruins|Ruins]].

Once the real-world terrain is established, pick or roll for its entrance . Interpret the result in light of where it's found.

sections.title: 1d6 entrance
sections.body:
- 1 A shimmer, a feeling, little more
- 2 Megalith(s)/a waystone [[53 - Waystones|Waystones]]
- 3 A door/gate/opening, natural or made, literal or just an impression
- 4 A ring of toadstools or some other distinctive pattern of flora (perhaps [[50 - Useful or Valuable Flora#**Useful or valuable flora**|useful or valuable]])
- 5 A path/a climb/a descent/a fall
- 6 Only active at certain times (in moonlight, at sunset, in winter, etc.) and roll again

If an entrance in the Great Wood is at least 10 years old, it's likely to be marked by Forest Folk glyphs [[17 - Forest Folk#**Hooks**|Forest Folk]].

For the domain itself, pick or roll for a theme [[12 - Fae#1d6 entrance|Fae]], the domain's original purpose , the anchor that maintains it, and 1-2 elements . Maybe roll 1d6 to inform its size and scale.

sections.title: 1d6 purpose
sections.body:
- 1 Containment: to imprison/preserve
- 2 Habitat: where Fae live/work/play
- 3 Community: a place to gather, trade, gossip, show off, travel
- 4 Status: a monument, a place to impose, someone showing off
- 5 Creativity: an expression, an exploration, a work of art
- 6 Pleasure: a paradise, a retreat, a false afterlife, a playground

- 2 The instinct or intent of multiple Fae, dwelling (trapped?) within
- 3 A shard of power left by a powerful Fae, buried or hidden away
- 4 One or more Fae, bound/inert, their essence sunk into the domain
- 5 Rune-carved stone infused with Fae power, a work of the Green Lords
- 6 Roll again, but it's failing, fickle, unstable, possibly abandoned

**1d6** element

- 1 Exit(s) to **[[53 - Waystones#Waystones|Fae paths]]** or far-off place(s) in the world
- 2 Surreal landscapes: a forest cave, an underwater kingdom, a castle in the clouds, a tiny place made large, a mundane place made giant, etc.
- 3 "Civilized" things, out of place: a feast hall in a mire, a house carved from a tree, a library in a cave, etc.
- 4 A real place, plucked from the world and the passing of time
- 5 A vast and shifting place, easy to get lost in, always something new
- 6 Decay/squalor/disorder, perhaps hidden by glamour or illusion

Populate the domain with Fae and other inhabitants that make sense. Also fill it with **[[12 - Fae#Artifacts|artifacts]]**, and any domain **[[12 - Fae#Dangers|hazards]]** that make sense. **[[12 - Fae#**Fluid time**|Time dilation]]** is a particular concern to mortal visitors!

*The PCs are exploring a Green Lord's tomb, approaching the burial vault in which the mummified Maker lies. Its spirit lingers on in a Fae domain.* 

*The terrain is established (the inside of the tomb). I choose a "door/gate/opening" for the entrance and the purpose is clearly "pleasure… a false afterlife." I roll a 5 for theme ("promises, deals, and loopholes…") and a 5 for the anchor ("rune-covered stone"). For elements, I get 6 ("decay/squalor/disorder") and 5 ("a vast and shifting place").* 

*The theme and elements prompt me to rethink the anchor. The domain is barely maintained by despondent Fae servants, bound by ancient oaths. An endless series of dusty halls, rooms and feral gardens, full of discarded wealth and surly Fae avoiding their master. The Green Lord's soul swings from boredom to manic fits, during which whatever Fae is at hand conjures threadbare glamours of the Green Lord's half-remembered deeds. An eternal paradise, indeed.*

sections.title: Entering domains
sections.body:
Some entrances work only at particular times: under a full moon, during winter, at sunrise or sunset, etc. This holds true for both Fae and mortal alike.

Otherwise, Fae can freely use the entrance of most domains, like you or I might open a door. For mortals, it's more involved.

**Something interesting:** A mortal might blunder into a Fae domain by accident, or follow a guide, or be drawn into one by a mischievous or malevolent Fae. Or they might fall asleep near an entrance and visit the domain in their dreams. Iron or steel might protect you, or not; you might just find that all your iron was left behind in the world.

**Something useful:** To enter a Fae domain on purpose, you must leave behind all iron and steel, plus one of the following:

- Follow the footsteps of a Fae guide
- Addle (open?) your mind with drugs/ alcohol/madness/intense emotion/etc.
- Have Fae blood in your veins
- Be able to see through Fae magic and glamours, via **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A small, strange book fragile|Old Mother's Wine]]** or a similar effect

sections.title: Locked entrances
Fae domain hazards
sections.body:
- Enchanting, entrancing beauty
- Time passing unnoticed
- Dramatic, rugged, strange landscapes
- Things changing when they look away
- Getting lost
- Capricious weather
- **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Elemental spirits**|Elemental spirits]]**, awake and active and full of opinions
- **[[12 - Fae#Illusions and glamours|Illusions and glamours]]**
- Not being able or allowed to leave

sections.title: Fluid time
sections.body:
Time passes strangely in Fae domains, when it passes at all. Upon returning to the world, one might find that no time has passed, or that time has passed normally, or that they've lost days, weeks, months, or years. Tales tell of folk returning to the world centuries later and crumbling into dust, but those are just tales. Right?

Lost time most often afflicts those who:

- Eat Fae food or drink Fae drink
- Fall asleep
- Lose themselves to revels, work, etc.
- Insult/become indebted to the locals
- Disrupt a place held in stasis

Threaten PCs with lost time, for sure. Use it to explain the absence of PCs whose players have to miss some sessions. But don't have the PCs lose years or decades unless you—and your players—really want to shake up your game.

sections.title: Some Fae domains
sections.body:
- A little door in a rocky outcrop, leading to a dying crystal city of tiny Fae and their talking vole subjects.
- A natural arch of hawthorne trees. Beyond: a tree-lined hall, a runecarved hearth, endless drink & dance.
- A knot in a large dead tree. One must recite a certain poem to enter. Inside is a crowded theater, where callous Fae call up long-dead shades to play out their mortal dramas.
- A **[[53 - Waystones#Waystones|waystone]]** in the mire. A crossroad of many Fae paths, oftused but one must pay a toll of beauty to the wretched troll who guards it.
- A wide stream. Fall face-first into it and enter a watery, dreamlike realm. A catfish-like Fae arranges stones, treasures, and lost memories into a beautiful, ever-shifting mosaic.
- A circle of menhirs, entwined in thorns. Can only be entered in moonlight. Inside, all the stones but one are revealed to be **[[17 - Forest Folk#Forest Folk|Forest Folk]]**, bound in agony.

sections.title: The Wandering Market
sections.body: Little ties the Fae together, except for a desire to show off. And for that, they need an audience. A place to meet and gather. A place to trade stories and wonders and lies. They need the Wandering Market.

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- According to tales, when does the Wandering Market convene?
- If you could purchase any treasure from the Fae, one wondrous thing, what would it be?
- What is your single most treasured possession? Why do you value it so?
- Who among you is most beautiful? Has the best singing voice? Dances the finest? Makes the best food?

sections.title: Getting there
sections.body:
The Wandering Market is a **[[12 - Fae#**Getting there**|Fae domain]]**, made anew by a different host each time it convenes.

To find the market, one must know when it is happening and find an entrance. The entrances are many, but different each time. Any Fae knows where the nearest entrance lies—pick or roll for the terrain near which it is found [[23 - The Great Wood#. steading improvement .|The Great Wood]] for the **[[13 - Ferrier's Fen#**Questions**|Great Wood]]**, for **Ferrier's Fen**), and pick some whimsical requirement such as:

- Climb the seventh alder tree you find; it's up there
- Blindfold yourself; follow the bird who calls your name
- Lay a path of stones into the hollow of a tree; walk the path at sundown
- Go to sleep in a patch of bluebells, and visit the market in your dreams

sections.title: Impressions
sections.body:
- Sensory overload: crowds and barkers, silks and spices, perfumes, feathers and flowers, leering inhuman faces
- Wonders for sale: distilled friendship, liquid silence, crystalline sorrow, etc.
- Snide comments about how this Market pales to previous ones
- Squeals of delight at your smile, your wry comment, your whistled tune
- How long have you been here, again?

sections.title: Chance encounters
sections.body: Pick or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d12 encounter
sections.body:
- 1 A Fae in mismatched finery, selling stolen wares; rejects all offers
- 2 One Fae savagely beating a servant; not a single eyebrow raised
- 3 A kindly green fruit-seller implores the PCs to "sample" his wares
- 4 A troupe's bedazzling performance: fireworks, illusions, bored onlookers
- 5 A crone selling fine futures, fates altered, prophecies (re)written
- 6 A hand (paw?) on your purse, deftly attempting to "trade"
- 7 A tiny sub-market, in a beehive or a stump; astir with bug-sized Fae
- 8 A scandalous, sensual revel in the street; stepped around by most
- 9 Talking beasts, glum Fae, holloweyed children, collared and chained
- 10 An insult; a challenge shouted; a crowd gathering to watch
- 11 Whispers and fuss as one of the **[[12 - Fae#The Four Chieftains|Four Chieftains]]** pass by
- 12 The host's pavilion, thematic and grand, teeming with politics

sections.title: TREASURE HUNT
sections.body:
When you *scour the Wandering Market for a solution*, say what problem you're hoping to solve and roll +CHA: **on a 7+**, you find someone offering just the thing, and can Haggle for it. But before you do, **on a 10+**, you must first pick 1; **on a 7-9**, you must first pick 3:

- Someone else is trying to buy it (or pretending to); you must Haggle with disadvantage
- It's not actually a solution itself, but rather a way to acquire one
- It's cursed, stolen, or otherwise dangerous to use or possess
- To even find it, you've put yourself in some Fae's debt

sections.title: HAGGLE
sections.body:
When you *dicker with a Fae* (anywhere, not just in the Wandering Market), roll +WIS. Roll with advantage if they like you, and disadvantage if they wish you ill. **On a 12+**, it costs but a pittance; **on a 10-11**, pick 1; **on a 7-9**, pick 2; **on a 6-**, pick 3:

- The price is Value 2, at least
- The price is irreplaceable, cherished, heartbreaking to lose
- The price is not truly yours to pay
- It involves a promise, condition, or obligation, sure to cause trouble
- You end up beholden to them

Whatever the price, you've either agreed to it or you've insulted the Fae and the deal is off. Work together to figure out exactly what happened. Reneging on the deal is certain to insult the Fae and/or put you in their **[[12 - Fae#Debts and promises|debt]]**.

sections.title: . trade opportunities .
sections.body:
If the PCs get a trade opportunity (from the Seasons Change move), the Fae might offer the following. The PCs still need to Haggle for it, though!

- An impossible task, like a whole season's worth of work done in a night
- A secret, a memory, an answer to a mystery, help with a sticky problem, etc.
- Fantastical trade goods (Value 2 or 3): cloth-of-clouds, mead brewed from dreams, dyes of glamour, etc.
- A **[[12 - Fae#Artifacts|Fae artifact]]**
- An artifact of the **[[24 - Green Lords#**Artifacts**|Green Lords]]**, the **[[17 - Forest Folk#**Artifacts**|Forest Folk]]**, the **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Artifacts|spirits of the wild]]**, or perhaps the **[[46 - The Things Below#**Unnatural phenomena**|Things Below]]**.

sections.title: Various treasures
sections.body:
- A spinning wheel from your youth (how do you know?) that was lost in a fire (*cumbersome*, Value 0)
- 2d4 vials of misty liquid, each filled with a poignant memory (*fragile*, Value 1 to the right buyer)
- A little jar of honeyed words; grants advantage to your next roll that relies on eloquence (*fragile*, Value 1)
- 12 beeswax candles, their scent pleasing to the spirits of plants, trees, and bugs (~1 hour, *close*, Value 1)
- A silver mirror (*beautiful*, Value 2); reflects how you see yourself
- A necklace of broken promises (*fragile*, Value 2 to the right buyer)
- A few bolts of linen, dyed the color of rain (*immobile*, *beautiful*, Value 2)
- A glass orb, full of swirling mists and infinite depth; fills the heart with cathartic, yearning sorrow (*fragile*, *beautiful*, Value 3)
- A bronze ring with an emerald the size of an acorn; its wearer ages not, but the years catch up all at once if removed (*beautiful*, *magical*, Value 4)

sections.title: Moonlight raiment
sections.body:
*magical*, *beautiful*, *fragile*

A gossamer robe, a joy against your skin. When you *wear the raiment in dim light*, you become more pleasing to the eye. If normally ugly, you become plain. If plain, you become beautiful. If beautiful, you become luminous, breathtaking, magnificent.

Alas, when *the moonlight raiment is soiled or torn while you wear it*, mark a debility. The debility cannot be cleared until the raiment is cleaned or repaired using Fae magic, or you burn it away into silvery ash.

sections.title: A pot of "gold"
The Red Pennant
sections.body:
, *magical*, Blood lll

A woolen flag hanging from a crossbar at the end of a sturdy long spear. It's tattered, crimson, with a crudely embroidered boot for a device. It hangs heavy. Like it's wet.

When you *fly the Red Pennant in battle*, all who fight on your side feel bloodlust boil within them. Hold 3 Blood. While the battle rages, you can spend Blood 1-for-1 to will your allies to:

- Rush forward, driving back their foes
- Hold fast, making their foes pay for every inch
- Fight savagely, giving no quarter

If spending Blood results in you Ordering Followers or Deploying, or another PC Clashing, the roll has advantage. Each time you spend Blood, those affected gain some small, animalistic or stony trait (little horns, weird eyes, crag-like wrinkles, etc.).

When you *spend the last Blood or the battle ends*, the Red Pennant's color fades and a few (~1 in 6) of those manifested traits will linger, especially in those who fought hardest. It will not function again until you soak it in the blood of fallen foes.

This is the battle standard of the **[[12 - Fae#**Blush-of-Dawn**|Bloody Boots]]**, their most sacred relic. Don't expect them to just let it go.

sections.title: A sturdy, banded box
sections.body:
, *magical*

6" by 4" by 3", made of thick rowan wood and banded with bronze. It's not much to look at. It slips easily from one's mind.

When you *let a drop of your blood fall into the empty chest*, name a promise you have made, an oath you have given, or a debt that you owe. Your blood becomes a bead of lead as it falls into the chest, and the promise, oath, or debt holds no power over you as long as the chest is sealed.

sections.title: Minor arcana
sections.body: Pick 1 or roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
sections.body:
- 1 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A small, strange book fragile|A small, strange book]]**

- 2 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#Goldleaf Cloak|A giant oak leaf]]**
- 3 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A humble broom|A redwood basin]]**
- 4 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A humble broom|A humble broom]]**
- 5 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A treasure map|A stone idol]]**
- 6 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A treasure map|A treasure map]]**

sections.title: Hazards
sections.body:
- Offending/insulting a Fae
- Ending up in their debt
- Making a promise, honestly or not
- Drawing their attention, becoming an object of their obsessions
- Stumbling into a Fae domain

sections.title: Related entities
sections.body:
- **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|Spirits of the wild]]**, quite possibly at some Fae's beck and call
- Any of the dangerous beasts who dwell in the **[[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|Great Wood]]**
- **[[07 - Crinwin#Crinwin|Crinwin]]**, enchanted or manipulated or deceived
- Perhaps the **[[35 - The Pale Hunter#The Pale Hunter|Pale Hunter]]**, who might be a Fae himself
- **[[43 - Stone Lords#**Construct**|Stone Lord constructs]]**, left over from the war
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Ghosts]]** of mortals who died in their thrall
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|Shades]]** of Green Lords that they've called up to torment
- **[[09 - Danu, the Earth Mother#**Danu**|Danu's blessed]]**, some of whom will gladly deal with the Fae

sections.title: Auntie Sallow
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *fae*, *magical*, *stealthy*, *corrupted*, *terrifying*

**HP** 16; **Armor** 4 (resilience), 1 vs. iron (bark) **Damage** choking fingers/tendrils d10+1 (*hand*, *close*, *reach*, *grabby*)

**[[12 - Fae#Magic and the Fae|Special qualities Fae nature]]**; takes +1d4 damage from bronze; leaches color from surroundings; knows whatever her moths know

**Instinct** to "protect" beauty and innocence

- <sup>ä</sup> Disperse into an eclipse of moths
- <sup>ä</sup> Find someone needing her "help"
- <sup>ä</sup> Show them their fears/nightmares
- <sup>ä</sup> Lure/drag them back to her home
- <sup>ä</sup> Bury them in the cool safe mud, forever

She appears out of the shadows, when the fear rises up to take you. Tall and grim and looming, draped in willow branches, moths flitting all about. She reaches out a bony hand. "Hush dear," she whispers. "Hush. Auntie Sallow is here to help."

She was beautiful, once. The minder of a sacred grove. A place of clean water, butterflies, and bees. But then came war. Fire and poison and the sundering of earth. Schemes and lies. Grief and endless pain.

'Twas in grief that **[[46 - The Things Below#**L'bin'bozia, Flesh-Candle, the Wax Skull, Roiling Tomb**|L'bin'bozia]]** found her. Gave her the strength to help. To find the innocent who suffered, the beautiful in despair. To offer succor, and the safety of her master's cool embrace.

sections.title: The Bear of Winter
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *huge*, *fae*, *magical*, *hoarder*, *devious*, *fierce*, *terrifying*

**HP** 26; **Armor** 5 (resilience, ice), 2 vs. iron (hide, ice)

**Damage** bite or maul d12+5 (*close*, *hand*, *reach*, *forceful*, *grabby*, *messy*, 1 piercing)

**[[12 - Fae#Magic and the Fae|Special qualities Fae nature]]**; immune to cold; aura of wintry weather, matching his mood

**Instinct** to protect his status and holdings

- <sup>ä</sup> Weave ice and snow, glamours and illusions, entire Fae domains
- <sup>ä</sup> Send a minion (spirit, beast, or indebted Fae) to do his bidding
- <sup>ä</sup> Wield the wintry elements
- <sup>ä</sup> Take the form of a scarred old man or a storm of sleet and snow
- <sup>ä</sup> Make use of a forgotten artifact

When winter snows blanket the Great Wood, the Bear roams the land. He's most often met as a great and snuffling beast, big as a house, icicles clinging to his fur. His retinue follows: nervously, discretely, waiting upon his call. He visits the many Fae who owe him favors and those who have pledged him their support. None are happy to see him. None dare turn him away.

The Bear, you see, is among the most powerful of the Fae. Snapped his own master's neck. Gobbled down its corpse. He freed hundreds of Fae from servitude. He leveraged the debts they owed him to become something of a feudal lord.

The Bear's domain is a great cave, full of hoarded riches and slinking servants. It has many entrances and exits, each leading to a different cave mouth in the world, but only when that cave mouth is surrounded by drifts of snow.

sections.title: The Four Chieftains
sections.body:
The chieftains are among the most powerful of the Fae, personally and politically. The Bear of Winter is one of them. The others might include:

- **The Warbler**, who dwells in the springtime canopy and fills the world with song. An incorrigible gossip, who pretends to be a fool. Often goes incognito. Extremely well-informed.
- **The Crowned Stag**, who troops through deep summer woods with their court and hangers-on. Beautiful, proud, dashing, and noble. Lusty, too, and prone to fall in love.
- **Longshadow**, a stately old tree-wife, bare of leaves. Found brooding in the autumn forest, attended by skittish minions. Speaks prophecy, lays subtle plans. Knows that the war isn't over.

sections.title: Blush-of-Dawn
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *fae*, *magical*, *beautiful*, *passionate* **HP** 12; **Armor** 4 (resilience), 0 vs. iron **Damage** fiery touch d8 w/disadvantage (*hand*)

**[[12 - Fae#Magic and the Fae|Special qualities Fae nature]]**; radiant

**Instinct** to be adored

- <sup>ä</sup> Sing a song to stir the blood
- <sup>ä</sup> Inspire passion/jealousy/devotion
- <sup>ä</sup> Steal away into their Fae domain, perhaps with a new prize in tow
- <sup>ä</sup> Set devotees to a task/challenge
- <sup>ä</sup> Blaze brightly, blind onlookers

They were the shining jewel in some Green Lord's paradise, the bright beauty of a rising sun made flesh. They were happy and in love. (Or so they thought, for can a being bred to love truly do so?)

Then their master's tomb was plundered by the rebels. Their world was upended. They were bereft, vulnerable, alone and unloved. They had no interest in freedom, only the safe warm surety of other beings' full regard. Fortunately, for them, such adoration was easy to find.

Their domain is a stately pavilion in a summer glade, forever in that moment just before the dawn. It's filled with fawning victims of their charms: lovers and would-be lovers, abducted children, devotees. And on the outskirts: the castout wretches, those who've lost favor but whose jealous adoration still feeds the Blush-of-Dawn.

sections.title: The Bloody Boots
sections.body:
*Group*, *small*, *fae*, *ugly*, *violent* **HP** 12; **Armor** 4 (resilience), 2 vs. iron **Damage** oversized club d8 w/advantage (*close*, *crude*) or iron-shod boots d8+2 w/advantage (*hand*, *forceful*)

**[[12 - Fae#Magic and the Fae|Special qualities Fae nature]]**; tolerates iron; can't start violence **Instinct** to brawl, the nastier the better

- <sup>ä</sup> Insult, mock, heap abuse
  - <sup>ä</sup> Get in your face, dare you
  - <sup>ä</sup> Leverage their unlikely strength
- <sup>ä</sup> Stomp someone who's down (*messy*)

A gang of hoodlums, with names like Hobnail, Spitkicker, Big Thunk, Tusk, and Grub. Short, squat, brutes with lopsided limbs, mismatched parts, and craggy skin. They stomp about in oversized, iron-shod boots, delighting in the disgust of other Fae.

Back in the day, they quarried stone for their masters. Didn't even mind it much. But given the chance to fight and brawl and spill all the blood they could, well, no need to ask them twice. The Bloody Boots were among the most feared and storied troops of the war. First into the fray and dyeing their **[[12 - Fae#**The Bloody Boots**|Red Pennant]]** in the blood of trodden foes.

And after the war? Well, you can't have thugs like them just rampaging about. Some little schemer bought and traded for every debt they ever had, then extracted a promise from each: no starting fights. They can finish a fight that someone else starts, and, oh, will they enjoy it. But just about every Fae still living knows better than to give them that chance.

*Solitary*, *small*, *fae*, *magical*, *tough*, *stealthy*, *cautious*, *ugly*

**HP** 16; **Armor** 4 (resilience), 1 vs. iron (thick fur)

**Damage** crushing grip d10+2 (*hand*, *grabby*, *forceful*)

**[[12 - Fae#Magic and the Fae|Special qualities Fae nature]]** **Instinct** to fill the emptiness inside

<sup>ä</sup> Stalk a mortal, curious and lonely

- <sup>ä</sup> Offer aid, in exchange for company
- <sup>ä</sup> Perform an impossible labor
- <sup>ä</sup> Grow when angered: +*large*, +4 HP,

He dwells in a cave full of smoke and bones, tending his stew pot and stewing. He might venture out to fetch firewood, water, or meat. And when he's out, he might cross paths with a mortal. In his loneliness, he might forget just how poorly things went last time.

When you **Haggle** with Grimstew [[12 - Fae#HAGGLE|Haggle]], you can't pick either of the first two options. He wants company, not things.

**Fae**

sections.title: Hollow Fae
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *fae*, *undead*, *confused*, *terrifying*, see below

**HP** 19; **Armor** 5 (resilience, lacks vitals), 1 vs. black iron

**Damage** life-drain d10 (*hand*, *close*, *area*, ignores armor)

**Special qualities** senses life; surrounded by inert death and ash; can be truly destroyed only by black iron

**Instinct** to consume life-force

- <sup>ä</sup> Moan, filling all with dread
- <sup>ä</sup> Tear apart Fae magic
- <sup>ä</sup> Pause in confusion when confronted with who it once was
- <sup>ä</sup> Open wide, drink in the vitality of the living (inflicting debilities)

A Fae wounded by **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Primordial powers**|black iron]]** will pray for death. For to survive such a wound is a curse. They grow weak, then confused, then hungry. Soon, they become a walking husk, surrounded by death, inhaling any source of life they find.

Hollow Fae are no longer vulnerable to mere iron or steel. Debts and promises no longer bind them. They will tear their way free of any Fae domain. Only black iron can destroy them, and what living Fae would be fool enough to try?

When faced with such a terror, most Fae choose to flee. A few might be so clever as to trap it in some forgotten tomb. But the cleverest, oh, the cleverest… they find some fool mortal to handle the problem for them.

A *large* Hollow Fae is tougher than shown above: 23 HP and +1 damage, +*reach*. A *huge* one is tougher still, with 27 HP, +3 damage, and +*reach*.

sections.title: Wee folk
sections.body:
*Horde*, *tiny*, *fae*, *magical*, *stealthy*, *organized*

**HP** 1; **Armor** 6 (resilience, size, speed), 2 vs. iron

**Damage** stings/darts d6-2 (*hand* or *reach*) **[[12 - Fae#Magic and the Fae|Special qualities Fae nature]]**

- <sup>ä</sup> Dart about on agile wings
- <sup>ä</sup> Blend in, watch unseen
- <sup>ä</sup> Weave illusions to dissuade, distract, or confound

**Guardian instinct** to guard against old evil

- <sup>ä</sup> Cause slumber with their darts
- <sup>ä</sup> Make a brave, defiant stand

**Worker instinct** to prepare for lean times

- <sup>ä</sup> Accomplish great feats, together
- <sup>ä</sup> Swarm a threat

**Hooligan instinct** to amuse themselves at the expense of others

- <sup>ä</sup> Rile up beasts or spirits
- <sup>ä</sup> Play pranks

Tiny little things, winged and fluttering, the size of mice or dragonflies. Weird little things, too: mishmashes of fur and feather, scales and chitin, gossamer and wings. Beautiful, cute, or grotesque.

Some lived together under the Green Lords' rule, others came together after the war. A surprising number were born into freedom, unburdened by their elders' scars.

You might find them keeping watch over some corrupted site or crumbling Green Lord ruin. Or you might come across one of their teeming cities, hidden in a burrow or a hollowed-out ancient tree. Or, if you're unlucky, you might encounter a gang of them out roaming the woods or fen, bored and cruel and looking for sport, and you'd be just the thing.

sections.title: The White Billies
sections.body:
*Horde*, *fae*, *athletic*, *raucous* **HP** 7; **Armor** 4 (resilience), 0 vs. iron **Damage** headbutt d6+2 (*hand*, *forceful*) **[[12 - Fae#Magic and the Fae|Special qualities Fae nature]]** **Instinct** to suppress their grief

- <sup>ä</sup> Drag someone into their revels
- <sup>ä</sup> Get huffy if refused
- <sup>ä</sup> Lash out if forced to face the past

Muscular folk with cloven hooves, birchwhite fur, and horns in every size and shape. They seem so happy, their endless days spent dancing and singing, drinking and rutting, indulging in good-natured brawls.

But when you look closer, you see the scars. You see the missing limbs, the broken horns. You see, briefly, the eyes so full of pain. But, no, no, it's gone. Another dance! Another round! Another bawdy song!

When you *join the White Billies in their revels*, lose 1d6 HP, mark a debility, and roll +CON: **on a 10+**, you keep your wits about you and learn something useful; **on a 7-9**, you regain your wits later, drunk and exhausted (mark another debility), with some new excess about to start; **on a 6-**, mark another debility and pick 1:

- You've upset them; real violence is imminent
- You come to later, alone, and who knows how long it's been
- You're enthralled, reveling endlessly until someone rescues you

sections.title: Ferrier's Fen
sections.body:
Ferrier's Fen lies between the **[[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|Great Wood]]**, the **[[42 - The Steplands#Spring|Steplands]]**, and the **[[34 - North Manmarch#North Manmarch|North Manmarch]]**. It's a huge wetland, 100 miles wide, 50 miles north to south, fed by the **[[44 - The Stream#The Stream|Stream]]** the Sruth near **[[32 - Marshedge#Marshedge|Marshedge]]**, and many smaller creeks. It's a maze of firm ground, stagnant pools, shallow streams, and plunging drops into deep deep water. And mud. So much mud.

Long ago, this area was the cradle of **the Green Lords'** civilization [[24 - Green Lords#Names|Names]]. But when their servants rebelled and the Green Lords embraced the**[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**, the other **[[30 - The Makers#The Makers|Makers]]** collapsed the region and shattered the Green Lords' civilization. It's still a broken place, oozing with old magic and corruption.

sections.title: Always
sections.body:
- Willow trees leaning over water
- Chunks of stone ruins, jutting from the mud
- Wobble and shake of peat bogs underfoot
- Sulphur smell of stagnant water and rot
- Some mundane thing but shockingly wrong: a tree with weeping eyes, a three-faced rabbit, a snake with stumpy legs, a deer with a fawn growing from its side, etc.

sections.title: Spring
sections.body:
- Dead brown mixed with pale green mixed with bright-colored flowers
- Squelch of mud, the shocking wet cold, its jealous grip on your feet
- Boisterous, rhythmic calls of frogs and toads
- Thick fog, muffling everything, shrinking the world

- Flies and midges, buzzing at your ears, nose, eyes, mouth
- Press and rustle and scrape of grass, sedge, and rush
- Butterflies and moths in every hue and shape; fireflies like the stars at night
- Baking heat, cloying air, sweat rolling off of you

- Yellow, brown, and gold of dying plants below; dead slate of skies above
- That smell of rot, just overwhelming now
- Flocks of water fowl, their harsh honking, flying in great Vs overhead

sections.title: Winter
sections.body:
- Dead grass poking through a blanket of snow
- A quiet calm
- Gurgle and trickle of unseen water
- Crack and lurch as you put your weight on thin ice

sections.title: Exploring the Fen
sections.body: Ferrier's Fen holds much of value, but it's a shifting, treacherous place.

sections.title: Chart a Course
sections.body:
On any expedition into Ferrier's Fen:

- Choose 1:
  - You need to bring a pouch of bendis root (Value 1 in Marshedge) per day, burnt constantly
  - The way will be perilous, filled with dangers
- You need a knowledgeable guide, like a **fen-walker** (next column)
- You risk getting lost nonetheless
- The terrain itself is treacherous; you risk injury along the way
- The way will be grueling; you risk exhausting yourselves/your resources
- You risk contracting **[[13 - Ferrier's Fen#**Fen blight**|fen blight]]**
- You risk drawing the attention of the suarachan (except in winter)

sections.title: Fen-walker
sections.body:
*Fen-wise*, *cautious*, *stealthy*, *observant* (and see below)

**HP** 8; **Armor** 1 (leather cuirass) **Damage** knife d8 (*hand*), sling d6 (*near*, *reload*)

- ä Spot/avoid a hidden hazard
- ä Berate outsiders for carelessness
- ä Do something gross or unpleasant without flinching

sections.title: Alastar
sections.body: *exceptional*, *old*, *devious*; **Instinct** to feel no remorse; **Cost** payment in hand.

sections.title: Fiadh
sections.body: *curious*, *kind*, *fen-blighted*; **Instinct** to hide his affliction; **Cost** secrets of the Fen.

sections.title: Riona prepared, agile, stubborn; Instinct to assume the worst; Cost competence shown.
sections.body: See also: **People** of Marshedge [[32 - Marshedge#**Names**|Marshedge]].

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- What foul beast is said to lurk in the Fen, and why do fools seek it out?
- Who do you know who was lost to the Fen? What led them out there in the first place?
- What ritual is said to keep the fen blight at bay?
- What do you think that the suarachan —those horrid frog-people—do with folks they capture?

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- A rare orchid can cure a sickness, but it grows only deep in the Fen
- Waters recede in a drought, revealing previously unknown ruins
- Suarachan raid the fields of Marshedge, dragging off multiple captives
- The PCs seek something that only the

sections.title: Terrain
sections.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12). In winter, water and mud might be frozen.

sections.title: 1d12 terrain
sections.body:
- 1 Open water, who knows how deep?
- 2 Shallow pool, pond, or stream
- 3-4 Mud, muck, sodden soil, standing water
- 5-6 Peat, sphagnum moss, floating mats
- 7-8 Grass, reeds, sedges, wildflowers
- 9 Shrubs and scattered pines
- 10 Thicket of trees, or one big tree
- 11 Dead tree(s), fallen log(s)
- 12 Hummock, hill, rocky outcrop

If you like, roll 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

sections.title: Discoveries
sections.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 discovery
sections.body:
- 1 Change of terrain (previous page) and roll again
- 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.)
- 3 An encounter (next column)
- 4 Site or artifact of the **[[24 - Green Lords#Names|Green Lords]]** likely tainted by the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**, at least partially buried or submerged
- 5 Some other point of interest (next column)
- 6 **[[50 - Useful or Valuable Flora#Useful or valuable flora|Useful or valuable flora]]**

sections.title: 1d6 encounter
sections.body:
- 1 A lone **[[13 - Ferrier's Fen#**The Gliomor**|suarachan]]**, hoping to avoid notice
- 2 A mutated beast, like a deer with eight legs and five eyes, or a two-headed hare
- 3 One or more **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|spirits of the wild]]** or **[[12 - Fae#Fae|Fae]]**, curious, indifferent, or forlorn
- 4 Ample game, but unappetizing crawfish, snails, turtles, frogs, fish, etc.
- 5 Good game, but prone to bolt deer, hare, water fowl, etc.
- 6 A **[[13 - Ferrier's Fen#Fen-walker|fen-walker]]**, possibly in dire straits

sections.title: 1d6 point of interest
sections.body:
- 1-2 A place corrupted by **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|the Things Below]]**
- 3 A place **haunted** by death or the undying [[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|Death and the Undying]]
- 4 **[[12 - Fae#**Getting there**|Fae domain]]** or **[[53 - Waystones#Waystones|waystone]]**
- 5 Site sacred to the **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|spirits of the wild]]**
- 6 The abode of the **Willow Witches**  (next page)

sections.title: The Willow Witches
sections.body:
Many tales are told of the weird sisters of the Fen. There are always three, but they might be old, young, or middling aged. They might be tall and regal or twisted and gnarled, dignified and poised or feral things rooting through the mud. Sometimes, the tale's hero seeks them out. Other times, they stumble upon the Witch's abode.

All stories agree that the Willow Witches have what you seek. An answer. An artifact (or knowledge of where to find it). A way to accomplish some impossible deed. Everyone knows that Sianna Ferrier learned the secrets of the Fen from the Witches, allowing Marshedge to be settled.

What the tales often fail to mention is: there's always a price. For Sianna Ferrier, the Witches' gifts were costly indeed: a child of her blood, or the blood of those she settled with, to be delivered every seven years.

The witches are, in truth, a single entity, the inscrutable **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Other primordial entities**|incarnation]]** of a primordial being of knowledge, fate, costs and consequences, greatly diminished and dwelling in this broken land.

sections.title: The Witches' abode
sections.body:
The Willow Witches' abode is rarely found in the same place twice, and in every tale, the details are different. Sometimes they dwell in the hollow of a willow tree, the space inside impossibly big. In other tales, it's a crude shack leaning against a tree. In still others, they huddle around a cookfire in a willow's shade, no real shelter to speak of.

The surest way to find the Witches is to seek out a **[[13 - Ferrier's Fen#Fen-troll|fen-troll]]** and convince them to guide you. They all know the way, though one should expect mischief.

sections.title: Mind your manners
The price
sections.body:
Whatever one asks of the Willow Witches, they require something of similar value. Tailor the price to the character. It's not about what the Willow Witches want or what they'll do with it. It's about sacrifice. It's about what the seeker is asking for, and what it's worth to them.

The Witches might ask for…

sections.title: … a promise, to be fulfilled now or later.
sections.body:
Renege, and calamity follows. "The axiom of local contagion? Such fancy words for so simple a thing. We can teach you, yes, but you must swear to never again open a door without its owner's permission."

**Ferrier's Fen**

… **a possession, worth at least as much to the asker as that which they ask for**. "You seek a weapon against the demon? Ah, yes, the Twisted Spear! We can show you where it lies, take you to it. But what will you give us in return? That mantle you wear, the one with teeth and the hungry ghosts? Give us that."

… **something strange, fraught with potential consequence**. "You seek to appease the anger of the forest? Tsk tsk, its anger cannot be quelled. But you can throw off the scent. Merely chew off your own shadow and let us dispose of it. Hmm? No, of course you can, here, let us show you…"

… **a personal sacrifice, bloody and visceral**. "You wish to know what the old runes say? Surely your right eye is a bargain for such wisdom. Or the left. We are not picky."

… **a personal sacrifice, something intangible**. Work with the player to change their instinct, swap their stats, or lose one of their moves as a result. "You seek a way to defeat the King of the North? We can tell you, yes. But it will cost the memories of your father, and the kindness he showed you."

… **a sacrifice, but of something not truly theirs to give.** "You want your eye for beauty back? Hm. You choose the price: your tall friend's kind heart, which you treasure so. Or your parents' love. It's beauty you seek, and like must pay for like, after all."

Whatever the PCs ask for, it should be a meaningful decision whether they pay the price. Don't ask for something you know they'll never give up, and don't ask for something they won't miss anyhow.

If the Witches like someone—if they've been humble, well-mannered, and wise then they'll be clear and upfront about the costs and what they're offering, and maybe suggest another course of action. Otherwise, the Witches will be cagey about the costs and all too willing to let them have something for cheap if they know that it will lead to ruin.

**Willow Witch**

 *Group*, *primordial*, *cautious*, *devious*,  *organized*, *magical*, *all-wise*

**HP** 16; **Armor** 4 (wards, resilience), 0 vs. black iron

**Damage** gnarled fists and fingers d10+2 w/ disadvantage (*hand*, *forceful*, *grabby*)

**Special Qualities** impossible to deceive or surprise; divide damage taken equally between the three witches (round down)

**Instinct** to enforce the old ways, the old rules

- ä Frustrate, annoy, or provoke someone
- ä Twist someone's perceptions
- ä Vanish, be elsewhere
- ä Turn someone into a swamp creature

**Tip:** use a different voice and different physical position (left, center, right) for each witch, and shift between them as you talk, sometimes mid-sentence.

When you *press or entice the Willow Witches into changing their price or altering their deal*, don't roll. They will not be budged; the price is what it is.

sections.title: Treacherous terrain
sections.body:
- Quaking bog: wobbly footing, some times thin enough to fall through
- Deep, sucking mud
- Slippery footing
- Thin ice cracking open
- Fallen logs or furrows hidden by grass or mud
- Thorns, spines, brambles
- Venomous snakes, underfoot or hiding in hollows
- Poisonous plants that cause rashes, blisters
- Fog and mist, or blanketing snow, obscuring all of the above and worse

sections.title: Swarms
sections.body:
- Midges or mosquitos—annoying, distracting, painful, diseased
- Leeches—clinging to anything that gets in water
- Starlings—obscuring, drowning out sound, buffeting, possibly possessed by a **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Grwgnach|grwgnach]]**

sections.title: Disease
sections.body:
Sickness can come from bad water, bad air, tainted food, and blood-sucking vermin. It's especially risky to have open wounds—in these conditions, it's all too easy for them to go rotten.

- <sup>ä</sup> Show that they've been exposed/infected
- <sup>ä</sup> Hit them with minor symptoms (mark *miserable*)
- <sup>ä</sup> Have symptoms worsen (mark another debility)
- <sup>ä</sup> Deny them rest

The best treatment for disease is rest, clean food, water, and air, and treatment with the proper herbs. Someone who pushes on through serious symptoms is likely Defying Danger (the danger that being that they get worse, or suffer complications).

To name a disease, or describe its symptoms, mix and match 2-3 of the following:

ague, angry, black, blisters, blood, blue, boils, breath, burning, chills, cold, cough, cramps, crows, dry, eye(s), festering, fever, fingers, fluttering, flux, foot, itching, lung, midge, milky, mouth, muddy, oozing, phlegm, pickled, pink, pox, rash, retching, rot, running, runs, scalp, shakes, shivers, skin, snot, sores, spit, spots, sweats, tears, tick, toad, toes, tongue, tremor, trots, warts, weed, weeping, welts, wet, wheezing, wound, yellow

Consider rolling the Die of Fate to see how serious the symptoms are and how hard the disease is to treat.

**Something interesting:** You know what this disease is called, its symptoms and typical course, maybe even what causes it. **Something useful:** You know how best to treat it, what herbs to use and where they might be found.

sections.title: Fen blight
sections.body:
**Instinct** to strip away its victim's humanity

Those who spend significant time in Ferrier's Fen might find themselves… changed. Marshedgers tell many tales about what causes the blight, and what will protect from it, but even the most careful fen-walker will sometimes start to feel the changes.

l Little things, easy enough to hide, like warty skin, thinning hair, fetid odor, a lengthening tongue, a taste for bugs and snails

l More pronounced changes, growing appetite, jealousy, a fear of being discovered

l Growth spurts, increased strength, more obvious mutations, resentment towards the unafflicted

l Difficulty with language, unending hunger, paranoia, urge to hide from sight l **Impending doom:** Transformation into a **fen-troll** (right)

sections.title: Monsters
sections.body:
- Beasts from the **[[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|Great Wood]]**, often mutated
- **[[12 - Fae#Fae|Fae]]**, especially those who delight in ugliness and misfortune
- **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|Spirits of the wild]]**
- **Green Lord** remnants [[24 - Green Lords#Names|Names]], especially chimerae and hazardous growth
- **[[24 - Green Lords#The Unquiet Dead|Llamudwr]]**
- Spirits or beasts **corrupted** by the Things Below [[46 - The Things Below#**1d12** origin|The Things Below]]
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Ghosts]]** of those who died here, lost and alone
- A rotting **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Shade**|pyped]]**, living on carrion and bog-bodies

sections.title: Fen-troll
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *corrupted*, *hoarder*, *gluttonous*, *stealthy*

**HP** 16; **Armor** 1 (thick hide)

**Damage** bone-snapping grip d10+3 (*hand*, *close*, *grabby*, *forceful*), rending maw d10+3 (*hand*, *messy*, 1 piercing)

**Special qualities** sharp senses, horrid odor, ashamed of its visage

**Instinct** to fill its belly and jealously guard its hoard

- <sup>ä</sup> Approach a victim from cover of dark, or fog, or murky water
- ä Demand treasure to let them pass unharmed
- ä Probably try to eat them anyway

They were human once, corrupted by the Fen and the foul forces flowing through it. Little remains of that humanity, though: a rough shape, half-forgotten words, a terrible anger at what it has lost.

They prize finery and mundane goods, anything that reminds them of their former life. Even more, they long for human connection. But hunger, shame, and greed usually lead them to lash out at any who show them kindness.

sections.title: The Gliomor
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *cautious*, *terrifying*, *ancient*

**HP** 22; **Armor** 3 (carapace)

**Damage** smaller claw d12+3 (*reach*, *grabby*), bigger claw d12+5 with advantage (*reach*, *forceful*, *messy*, 3 piercing)

**Special qualities** heals injuries in a few hours, regenerates limbs in a few days

- **Instinct** to eat pretty much anything <sup>ä</sup> Poke its eyestalks out of the water or above grass
  - <sup>ä</sup> Burst into view, making the mind reel with horror
  - <sup>ä</sup> Scuttle back quickly, to escape danger or to drag off prey
  - <sup>ä</sup> Spew forth a cloud of sickening, blinding, corrosive goo: d8 damage (*area*, *close*)

An enormous albino crayfish, older than the Fen itself. If you encounter it, pray it's not hungry. But fat chance of that.

sections.title: Snaplilies
sections.body:
**Damage** snapping "jaws" 1d4 (*hand*, *grabby*) **Instinct** to latch on and drag down

- <sup>ä</sup> Blend in with a field of lily pads
- <sup>ä</sup> Snap shut on a frog or bird, disappearing before anyone sees
- <sup>ä</sup> Snap tight on a limb, yank down
- <sup>ä</sup> SNAP SNAP SNAP on someone who trips and falls among them: 1d8 damage w/advantage (*grabby*, *messy*)

They blend right in, really. But when anything bigger'n a bug lands on one, SNAP, just like a bear trap, the vine contracting and pulling prey underwater. It's a lethal trap for a small creature. For a human? Just a painful mistake. Unless you panic, of course, or flail, or trip. So just, you know, stay calm.

sections.title: Suarachan hunters
sections.body:
*Group*, *stealthy*, *cautious*, *organized*, *amphibious*

**HP** 6; **Armor** 1 (scaly hide)

**Damage** cudgel d8 (*close*, *crude*), snapping tongue d6 (*reach*), poison blowgun needles d6 (*near*)

**Special qualities** excellent night vision, salt burns their skin

**Instinct** to bring home captives for sacrifice

- <sup>ä</sup> Surround their quarry unnoticed
- <sup>ä</sup> Leap a shocking distance
- <sup>ä</sup> Club someone senseless on the head
- <sup>ä</sup> Tie a fast knot

They're tall as a short man, hunched on too-long legs and too-long arms. A toadlike head juts forward from broad shoulders, the eyes entirely too big, the mouth entirely too wide. They're covered in moist scales and horn-like growths, and draped in belts and bandoliers of woven grass.

These are the suarachan most often encountered, the ones that raid Marshedge and the ones that fen-walkers most fear. Truly, they spend more time fishing and hunting than seeking sacrifices, and they're just as happy to claim a deer as a man. But deer are harder to catch, on the whole.

sections.title: Suarachan singers
sections.body:
*Horde*, *amphibious* **HP** 3; **Armor** 0

**Damage** fists and bite d6 w/ disadvantage (*hand*), snapping tongue d4 w/ disadvantage (*reach*)

**Special qualities** excellent night vision, salt burns their skin, mutations

**Instinct** to sing the eternal lullaby

- <sup>ä</sup> Drown out all other noises with their song
- <sup>ä</sup> Raise a shrill call of alarm
- <sup>ä</sup> Scuttle away and hide
- <sup>ä</sup> Keep a prisoner living, long enough

There are places in Ferrier's Fen where horrors sleep, where the Things Below dream unquiet. The air in these places thrums with frog-song, so deep and so loud you can barely hear yourself think. But it's not frogs singing. It's the suarachan.

The suarachan believe that their songs keep the Things Below quiet. And they hope that their sacrifices—given in bulk in late autumn—will keep their dark gods satiated while the suarachan sleep through winter.

Most suarachan bear mutations: extra legs (often twisted and useless), overgrown or atrophied limbs, missing or extra eyes, strange growths, etc. The most mobile become hunters, but the chorus welcomes any suarachan that can sing.

sections.title: Suarachan soothsayer
sections.body: *Solitary*, *corrupted*, *cunning*, *magical*, *amphibious*

sections.title: HP 12; Armor 0
sections.body:
**Damage** poisonous skin d10 (*hand*), snapping tongue d4 (*reach*)

**Special qualities** excellent night vision, salt burns their skin, marked by the Things Below [[46 - The Things Below#**1d12** origin|The Things Below]]

**Instinct** to mollify the Things Below

- <sup>ä</sup> Secrete various poisons: debilitating, hallucinogenic, deadly
- <sup>ä</sup> Commune with outsiders via poison-induced visions
- <sup>ä</sup> Use a gift of the Things Below [[46 - The Things Below#**1d12** origin|The Things Below]]

A few suarachan—one or two per chorus—have bright, colorful scales that ooze with slime. They will often ingest their own hallucinatory toxins, communing with the horrors that lay sleeping, shushing them and soothing them, and promising them meals of screaming flesh, soon, soon.

The suarachan lack language, per se. They can communicate basic concepts via their songs, but true communication requires a soothsayer's touch.

When you *are affected by the hallucinogenic poison of a suarachan soothsayer*, you can perceive spirits around you and speak to those who are active, as well as others affected by the poison. When the poison wears off, mark a debility (*dazed* if it's available).

sections.title: The Flats
sections.body:
Stonetop sits on the easternmost edge of the **Flats**, a rolling tallgrass prairie that stretches all the way to the **[[27 - Huffel Peaks#Huffel Peaks|Huffel Peaks]]**, the **[[16 - The Foothills#The Foothills|Foothills]]** and the **[[42 - The Steplands#Spring|Steplands]]**.

The prairie is dotted with small hills, outcrops, and the occasional copse of trees huddled against the ceaseless wind. Winding streams cut through the soil. But mostly? Just mile after mile after mile of grass.

sections.title: Always
sections.body:
- A southerly wind, ever present
- Mountains looming on the far horizon
- A sky so big it beggars the soul
- Birds of prey, or carrion eaters, floating high above
- Clouds, always in motion: high and wispy, or big and fluffy, or massive and dark like an anvil ready to drop

sections.title: Spring
sections.body:
- Patchworks of hopeful green, pale white, dry yellow
- Carpet of last year's grass, folded flat by wind and heavy snow
- Soft crunch and pop underfoot
- A damp chill in the wind, smell of rain and wet soil
- Chirp chirp chirp, warble

sections.title: Summer
sections.body:
- Green and yellow stems topped with a riot of flowers, rippling and bobbing in the wind
- The itch and scratch of nettles, thistle, and stiff sandpaper stalks
- Constant noise: birdsong, insect-buzz, rustling grass
- Relentless sun, unbroken by shade
- A wall of rain, rolling towards you

sections.title: Autumn
sections.body:
- Crinkle, snap, and rustle with every step, every movement
- Stiff stalks, gold in the low sun, nodding in the breeze
- Lonely chitter of birds, unseen
- Morning frost, or thick blankets of fog

sections.title: Winter
sections.body:
- Snow billowing in the wind, obscuring everything
- A white expanse, still as death, poked through with grassy stubble
- The cold, wet, exhausting trudge through waist-high drifts
- The squeak of snow underfoot on a bitterly cold day

sections.title: The Gwead
sections.body:
Villagers call the Flats-grasses **the Gwead** ("the Weave" or "the Weaving"). It's a bewildering array of species. Some are short and wispy, others thick and taller than a tall man. They bloom in a myriad of shapes and colors, and sport every manner of prickle, thorn, and seedpod.

The Gwead will not be tamed. Burn the grasses, plow the soil, tear things up by their roots—it doesn't matter. The Gwead always come back, thicker and hardier than before. And sometimes, it seems, the grasslands take their revenge.

- When was the last time anyone tried to plant crops beyond the Old Wall?
- What uncanny fate befell them?

Why can crops be managed within the **[[02 - The Village of Stonetop#**The village of Stonetop**|Old Wall]]**, but not beyond? That's for you to decide. Maybe…

- … magic tied to the **[[02 - The Village of Stonetop#**The village of Stonetop**|Stone]]** makes the land more pliable, easier to tame;
