- … something buried beneath the Old Wall keeps the Gwead at bay;
- … something about the soil keeps the Gwead from taking root; or
- … whoever built the ruins on which Stonetop was founded, they made a pact with the spirits of the Gwead.

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- Who among you has ventured deep into the Flats? What did you see there that no one quite believes?
- Aside from sticking to the roads, what keeps the pack drakes at bay?
- Which of you has seen a prairie fire up close and personal?
- What flower, root, seed, or stem that can be found on the Flats is particularly useful? How so? Why is it so hard to harvest?

sections.title: Places
sections.body:
- **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#The Makers' Roads|The Makers' Roads]]**
- **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#The Ruined Tower|The Ruined Tower]]**
- **[[48 - Titan Bones#Titan Bones|Titan Bones]]**

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- A herd of wild horses is seen north of the West Road and east of the Highway, close enough that they might be captured
- Someone is sick, and the cure requires a rare plant that grows deep in the Flats
- Gwead-grasses have started taking root inside the Old Wall, getting hard to weed out, and folks are all sorts of worried
- A traveler's companion was lured off the roads by nosgalau [[31 - The Makers' Roads#**Fellow travelers**|The Makers' Roads]] and hasn't returned; the traveler offers a sizeable reward if the PCs can help

sections.title: Terrain
sections.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

sections.title: 1d12 terrain
sections.body:
- 1 Ash field, burnt stalks, recent wildfire
- 2 Barren ground: sand, stone, dust
- 3 Burrow, dugout, or warren
- 4 Sodden soil, mud, or standing water
- 5-6 Dense thicket of tall (5'-8'), stiff stalks
- 7-8 Open meadow, grass waist-high or shorter
- 9 Low ridge, embankment, rise
- 10 Tree(s), savanna, shrubs
- 11 Rocky outcropping, boulders, jagged stones
- 12 Gully, sinkhole, crater, fissure (maybe full of water)

If you like, roll 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

sections.title: Discoveries
sections.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12 or d6).

sections.title: 1d6 discovery
sections.body:
- 1 Change of terrain (previous column) and roll again
- 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.)
- 3 An encounter (see below)
- 4 A point of interest (next page)
- 5 Useful or valuable flora [[50 - Useful or Valuable Flora#Useful or valuable flora|Useful or valuable flora]]
- 6 A piece of the Ruined Tower (next page)

sections.title: 1d6 encounter
sections.body:
- 1 A shade [[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|Death and the Undying]], called up at the Crossroads [[31 - The Makers' Roads#The Makers' Roads|The Makers' Roads]], or the pyped [[10 - Death and the Undying#**Shade**|Death and the Undying]] that it's become
- 2 A spirit of the wild [[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|Spirits of the Wild]], curious or indifferent
- 3 Critter(s)-fox, skunk, stoat, hares, prairie dogs, starlings, vultures, etc. -curious, annoying, maybe just cute
- 4 Beast(s)-ground sloth, giant ibis, shellback drake [[14 - The Flats#Klezln|Klezln]]-big, majestic, mostly indifferent
- 5 A herd: aurochs [[14 - The Flats#Aurochs|Aurochs]], deer, wild sheep, wild horses [[14 - The Flats#Thunder drake|Thunder drake]], etc.
- 6 A band of Hillfolk [[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk|Hillfolk]], or maybe just their camp

sections.title: Pieces of the Ruined Tower
sections.body: Scattered far and wide, for miles and miles from the tower itself, as if flung by some impossible force. Likely buried or at least overgrown.

sections.title: 1d6 pieces
sections.body:
- 1 Metal instruments or tools, possibly giant-sized, rent asunder, twisted, or surprisingly intact
- 2 Worked stone, or chunks thereof
- 3 Stone covered in scorched runes, possibly a bit of the **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#**The Crossroads**|Broken Roads]]**
- 4 A jagged piece of "indestructible" **[[43 - Stone Lords#Various treasures|makerglass]]**, cracked and broken
- 5 **Aetherium** ingot [[45 - Tempest Lords#**Aetherium**|Tempest Lords]], crackling with power
- 6 A **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#Shards of the Storm Orb|shard of the Storm Orb]]**

sections.title: 1d6 point of interest
sections.body:
- 1 A place corrupted by the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**
- 2 A tomb, ruin, or artifact of the **[[05 - Barrow Builders#Barrow Builders|Barrow Builders]]**
- 3 A battlefield or remains (next column)
- 4 Enigmatic terrain: crystalline growth, sheet of smooth/broken glass, geometric patterns, unnatural colors, grass growing in strange patterns, etc.
- 5 A location important to the **[[30 - The Makers#The Makers|Makers]]**: meeting place, fortification, shrine, etc.
- 6 A site sacred to the **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|spirits of the wild]]**

sections.title: Battlefield or remains
sections.body: Older ones lie well-buried, so ask yourself how they might be discovered (burrowing animals, erosion, earthquakes, excavation, etc.)

sections.title: 1d12 battlefield or remains
sections.body:
- 1 **[[36 - Primordial Powers#Primordial powers|Primordial]]**: from the very shaping of the world
- 2 Truly ancient: from the time of the **[[30 - The Makers#The Makers|Makers]]**
- 3-4 From the **[[55 - Appendix A - Ages of the World#Time of Cataclysm|Time of Cataclysm]]**: when humanity rebelled and woke the Things Below
- 5-7 From the days of the **[[05 - Barrow Builders#Barrow Builders|Barrow Builders]]**
- 8-11 Recent: Hillfolk, bandits, travelers, beasts, etc.
- 12 Very recent: the buzzards and crows still feasting

Picking through the remains might yield valuables or artifacts, but might rouse the ire of **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|ghosts]]** or **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|revenants]]**.

sections.title: Hazards
sections.body:
- Nasty turn in the weather: lashing wind, bitter cold, blistering heat, sleet/ hail/rain/snow; lightning
- Lack of shelter, water, or wood for a long-burning fire (try dried aurochs dung, if you can find it)
- Hidden holes/burrows, twisting an ankle or worse
- Getting lost (lack of landmarks, tall grass and rolling terrain, hard to keep to a straight line)

**Prairie fire**

 The Gwead is prone to burning, sparked by lightning, an errant flame, or (if you believe the tales) Hillfolk intentionally setting the Flats ablaze. Early spring, late summer, and early autumn are particularly prone, especially after a few weeks without rain.

**Instinct** to run unchecked across the land

- <sup>ä</sup> Start with just a few crackling flames, a column of smoke
- <sup>ä</sup> Grow implacably, creeping outward
- <sup>ä</sup> Smother the landscape in choking smoke (debilities)
- <sup>ä</sup> Hit them with a wave of heat (d4 damage)
- <sup>ä</sup> Linger and burn for minutes or hours (d6 damage, messy)
- <sup>ä</sup> Tear a crimson line, faster than you can sprint, bonfire-hot (d10 damage, ignores armor, messy)
- <sup>ä</sup> Run out of fuel, or get smothered by rain

**Something interesting**: To really spread and burn hot, a prairie fire needs years of built-up fuel, dry conditions, and steady wind; even then, it can only burn at its hottest for a short period of time.

**Something useful**: Seek the Makers' Roads, or barren ground, or low-lying patches that still hold some moisture. Failing that, seek patches of grass that burned in the past year. Worst case: seek a narrow

sections.title: Monsters
sections.body:
- **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Cougar**|Cougars]]** or **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Raselbaedd**|wolves]]**, come out of the Great Wood
- **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk|Hillfolk]]**, if they're of a mind to do violence
- **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|Spirits of the wild]]**
- The **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#**Fellow travelers**|nosgalau]]**, whatever they are
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Ghosts]]**, **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|revenants]]**, or **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|shades]]**, called up at the **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#The Makers' Roads|Crossroads]]** or lurking near old battlefields
- **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#Dangers|Blue magpies]]**, messing with the PCs

sections.title: Aurochs
sections.body:
*Group*, *large*, *ornery*, *powerful* **HP** 14, **Armor** 2 (thick hide, tough) **Damage** gore d8+5 (*close*, *forceful*, *messy*, 1 piercing), kick/trample d8+3 (*hand*, *forceful*)

**Instinct** to spook or to get angry, in equal measure

- ä Send an attacker flying
- ä Stampede (*area*, *forceful*, *messy*)
- ä Wheel suddenly, with surprising grace and speed
- ä Keep going past when it should be dead or dying

Big, powerful, wild cattle with horns longer than your arm and thicker than your hand can grip around. Even the does have big horns, and aren't shy about using them.

They winter in the Steplands, in small bands of 3-5 adults. In spring, though, they come down to the Flats, form up into herds of 30 or more, and graze and graze and graze. They'll break up into smaller bands by midsummer, then head back into the hills before first snow.

The Hillfolk hunt them, riding at the flanks of a herd and picking off one or two with arrows, keeping their damned distance. You do not want to get too close to one of these things, especially not after you wound it.

A full-grown aurochs carcass (*immobile*), properly butchered and transported back to town, counts as 1 Surplus. A pair of aurochs horns is Value 1.

sections.title: Bandit
sections.body:
*Group*, *stealthy*, *cautious*, *devious* **HP** 6; **Armor** up to 2 (hides, shields) **Damage** spears d8 (*close*, *thrown*) or slings d6 (*near*)

**Instinct** to survive at the expense of others

- ä Prepare a deception, to lure travelers from the roads
- ä Spring an ambush when their prey takes the bait
- ä Take captives or slit throats, so that no one escapes to tell tales
- ä Flee into the grass if things go south

The magic of the Roads and the open terrain make a life of banditry far from easy, but somehow, they crop up every few years. Might be Hillfolk, disgraced or desperate, but more often than not, they're scoundrels who'd rather bleed their fellows than scrape by up in Gordin's Delve.

sections.title: Bandit chief
sections.body:
*Group*, *leader*, *stealthy*, *cautious*, *devious*, *organized*

**HP** 8; **Armor** 3 (hides, shield, skill) **Damage** spear d8 (*close*, *thrown*, 1 piercing) or bow d8 (*near*)

**Instinct** to make the best out of what they've got

- ä Suss out what people want or need
- ä Exploit a weakness, opportunity, or loophole
- ä Cut a deal
- ä Ruthlessly expand/preserve their power

Most bandit gangs struggle to find victims or shelter or both, and quickly die out or disband. The clever ones last a few seasons, but eventually folk catch wise and grow wary, and the bandits' schemes start to fail. But every generation or so, a leader crops up who's cagey enough to turn a gang of bandits into a serious threat.

What does the chief possess that makes them such a threat? (pick 2 or 3)

- Agents in Gordin's Delve and/or Marshedge
- A way to overcome the wards on the Roads
- Magic that can compel/lure/scare travelers off the Roads
- Magic that hides their crew, or changes their appearance
- Well-trained horses, or some other form of mobility
- A fortified hideout

sections.title: Beznpol
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *cautious*, *fierce*

**HP** 12; **Armor** 3

**Damage** burrowing talons d10 (*hand*), needle teeth d8 (*hand*, *messy*, 3 piercing) **Special Qualities** acid-proof

**Instinct** to constantly search for food

- <sup>ä</sup> Dig up serious quantities of soil
- <sup>ä</sup> Bound out of the grass and onto prey
- <sup>ä</sup> Spray a stupefying musk (*reach*, *area*, debilities)
- ä Curl up into a ball of hard, jagged scales (4 armor)

Great big armored mustelids, like a cross between a stoat, a badger, and a pangolin, but like 6 feet long not counting the tail. They roam the Flats digging up burrows of just about anything, but they especially love to stir up a **[[14 - The Flats#Hlomren|hlomren]]** nest and chomp chomp chomp. They're far from the top of the food chain, though. If startled, they'll spray musk and attempt to flee. If cornered, they'll curl up and hope the attacker grows bored.

A skilled tanner can turn a pair of hides into a magnificent beznpol scale coat (2 armor, *warm*, protects against acid, Value 3). Hillfolk champions are known to wear them.

sections.title: Frythanc
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *stealthy*, *terrifying*

**HP** 16; **Armor** 0

**Damage** swooping talons d10+2 (*hand*, *close*, *forceful*, *grabby*), beak d10 (*reach*, disadvantage)

**Special Qualities** incredible eyesight, terrible hearing

**Instinct** to wait for the right moment

- ä Soar on thermals, thousands of feet up
- ä Swoop down like a thunderbolt, snatching up prey
- ä Drag prey up to 30 or 40 feet with slow, mighty flaps—then drop them

Great scaly birds, wings like 40 feet across, sky blue and white feathers on their undersides to hide in the sky. They're said to roost in the Huffel Peaks but hunt the Flats, snatching up the unwary—man, woman, deer, horse—and dropping them to the ground. The drop usually kills or cripples its prey, and then it alights to dine at its leisure.

They are, thank Tor, very rare, with only one mated pair hunting the Flats at a time. On the other side of the Huffel Peaks, though… who knows?

sections.title: Hlomren
sections.body:
*Horde*, *tiny*, *organized*

**HP** 1; **Armor** 3 (carapace, size)

**Damage** acid-dripping mandibles d6-2 (*hand*, *messy*, 3 piercing), acid glob d6-2 (*reach*, *messy*, *reload*, 1 piercing)

**Instinct** to protect the colony

- <sup>ä</sup> Burrow into/out of/through loose soil
- <sup>ä</sup> Swarm over a threat
- <sup>ä</sup> Burst with acid when killed, d4 damage (*close*, *area*)

These red-shelled insects look like a cross between an ant and a beetle, but the size of a small cat. They form colonies a few-hundred large, typically on higher ground. A colony will harvest all the dead grass in a few miles, hibernate through winter, and then relocate in spring to a part of the Flats that hasn't burned or been harvested in a few years. They mostly ignore people and other large animals, but they'll swarm if threatened.

sections.title: Klezln
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *stealthy*

**HP** 16; **Armor** 3 (carapace)

**Damage** pincers d10+3 (*hand*, *close*, *grabby*) **Special Qualities** senses vibrations,

strong burrower

**Instinct** to wait patiently for food

- <sup>ä</sup> Undermine the soil, creating a sinkhole waiting to happen
- <sup>ä</sup> Wait for prey to trigger its trap and get pinned in loose dirt, d6 damage (*grabby*)
  - <sup>ä</sup> Burst forth from the loose soil to snag a victim
  - <sup>ä</sup> Drag it beneath the soil, to be crushed and supped on at leisure

Great big beetles that live out beneath the Flats, preying on large game like horses and aurochs. The Hillfolk know what signs to watch for, but they rarely tell outsiders. What better way to keep the Flats free of fools?

sections.title: Pack drake
sections.body:
*Horde*, *small*, *stealthy*, *organized*, *cautious* **HP** 3; **Armor** 1 (quick reflexes)

**Damage** sawtooth teeth d6 (*hand*, *grabby*) **Instinct** to bring down easy prey

- ä Dart through tall grass, unseen
- ä Surround their prey
- ä Bite down and pull in one direction while a packmate pulls in the other (+*messy*)

Sneaky little dog-sized critters that prowl the Flats. Hunt in packs ranging from six to a couple dozen. Just the worst.

sections.title: Questions:
sections.body:
- What's the surest sign of their presence?
- What do all wise travelers do to keep them at bay?
- Which of you has seen them tear apart their prey? What haunts you about it to this day?

sections.title: Shellback drake
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *cautious*, *hardy*, *patient*

**HP** 16; **Armor** 3 (carapace, scales)

**Damage** snapping, bone-crushing bite d10 (*hand*, *close*, *grabby*, *messy*, 1 piercing)

**Special Qualities** nearly blind,

senses vibrations

**Instinct** to lash out at "food" in range

- <sup>ä</sup> Burrow into soil
- <sup>ä</sup> Wait for prey half-buried
- <sup>ä</sup> Withdraw into its shell (Armor 5)

Like a snapping turtle, but 6 feet long and 400 pounds, with big, badger-like claws. Way faster than you think. It'll dig itself under a layer of soil, and wait long enough for the gwead to start growing over it. Then, when something comes near? SNAP!

An intact shell is worth quite a bit (*immobile*, Value 2). But good luck killing one without cracking the shell.

sections.title: Thunder drake
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*

**HP** 20; **Armor** 3 (scales, crest)

**Damage** gore, trample, swat d10+3 (*hand*, *close*, *forceful*, *messy*, 1 piercing)

**Special Qualities** lightning-proof,

hard of hearing

**Instinct** to protect its turf

- ä Bellow (*near*, *area*, *loud*, *forceful*, *reload*)
- ä Burrow and gnaw through rock
- ä Sometimes: belch forth a bolt of lightning, d10+3 damage (*near*, *forceful*, *loud*, ignores armor)

Sometimes, out on the Flats, you'll see what looks to be small hills in the distance. I advise you to steer clear. They probably aren't hills, really, but huge piles of dirt that a thunder drake has moved as it digs. And hoo boy, you do not want to stumble into one of them on their home turf.

**Something interesting:** Thunder drakes eat mostly grass and shrubs, but they have a taste for certain ores. They spend their time grazing, sleeping, and digging up rocks.

**Something useful:** They particularly savor the taste of copper, and relish **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**Aetherium**|aetherium]]**. If they find a source of it, they'll chew it right up and spend the next few days belching lightning bolts.

When you *are near a thunder drake when it bellows*, roll +CON: **on a 10+**, pick 1; **on a 7-9,** pick 2; **on a 6-**, all 3.

- Drop what you're holding and cover your ears
- Stagger back a few steps
- You're deafened and disoriented for a few moments

sections.title: Wild horse
sections.body:
*Group*, *large*, *cautious*, *swift*

**HP** 10; **Armor** 0

**Damage** trample d6+3 (*hand*, *close*, *forceful*) **Instinct** to panic

- ä Sniff trouble on the wind
- ä Run free!
- ä Vault over an obstacle

They roam the flats, in herds ranging in size from a half-dozen to a couple dozen. The larger herds will give rise to a **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Andalau|ceffylwraig]]**.

sections.title: Fomoraij
sections.body:
The **[[24 - Green Lords#Names|Green Lords]]** damned themselves. Oh, they'll tell you they had no other choice. That their treacherous servants drove them to it. That they knew not the cost. That they were lied to by the**[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**. But, no, they knew what they were doing. They just didn't care.

And why would they have cared? Had they not lost everything? Had they not been driven from their homes and hunted like beasts, by the very servants they had created? By those who owed them *everything*?

So, yes damn you, the Green Lords made their deals. They called up the darkness and the poison. Let it flow through their minds. Their flesh. Their lands.

And when not even that was enough to turn the tides, when they were betrayed again—this time by their fellow **[[30 - The Makers#The Makers|Makers]]**—and they were corralled into one last trap… well, what did they care? What could they care? Let the traitors have the surface. For now.

And so, twisted and mad with hatred, they went below. Into the bosom of their masters. There, they wait. They spawn new horrors. They hate.

And one day, perhaps, they will return.

sections.title: Names
sections.body: Slavic-sounding, but with more vowels and softer consonants: Aawnaswasiya, Blaagu, Doogromil, Eleema, Loobimoor, Miwooslawa, Oolliyanaw, Rabomoor, Weelimir, Yoanaw

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- **[[15 - Fomoraij#Infected servant|Infected servants]]** are watching villagers near the **[[44 - The Stream#The Stream|Stream]]**
- An earthquake opens a crack in a cave bear's den, exposing them to a Fomoraij's lair and influence
- Hunters/trappers go missing, then turn up weeks later horribly mutated
- A Fomoraij's minions steal children from their beds and drag them to their master
- **[[17 - Forest Folk#Names|Forest Folk]]** glyphs show people filing into a cave, laden with gifts (for the Fomoraij that secretly entranced them)

sections.title: Dwellers beneath
sections.body:
The Fomoraij have dwelt beneath the Great Wood for centuries. Their influence can be felt, certainly, and they sometimes send forth minions, but they have not seen the surface since the fall of **[[52 - Vor Svetelik#Vor Svetelik|Vor Svetelik]]**.

To create a Fomoraij, start with 2 themes from the**[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**. Combine with a constraint that keeps it from emerging, the nature of its lair, and 2-3 forms. Give it a name and perhaps an ominous title. Write it up as a threat (a *villain*, a *beast*, or a *magical entity*). If the PCs are likely to fight it, also write it up as a monster (see example on page [[15 - Fomoraij#**"Typical" Fomoraij**|142]]).

sections.title: 1d12 constraint
sections.body:
- 1-2 Confinement: by accident, by artifice, by its own size or nature (**Instinct** to capture others, to escape)
- 3-4 Disdain: of the world above, of the world that betrayed them (**Instinct** to punish anything that reminds it of its past)
- 5-6 Decay: of its mind, its power, its sense of self (**Instinct** to lash out)
- 7-8 Fear: of the Fae, of the Makers, of the much-hated light (**Instinct** to see threats everywhere)
- 9-10 Obsession: with hoarding, experimenting, digging, etc., to the exclusion of all else (**Instinct** to pursue its obsession)
- 11-12 Patience: it waits, it gathers power, it makes preparations (**Instinct** to further its plans)

sections.title: 1d12 lair
sections.body:
- 1 A yawning cavern, flooded; dotted with islands and unlikely structures
- 2 Endless rough-hewn tunnels, like an ant hive
- 3 An inverted forest of roots, tubers, fungus hanging from the ceiling
- 4 A series of underground streams, rivers, lakes, all teeming with horrors
- 5 A deep, vertical shaft ending at a vault of white stone
- 6 Tunnels and chambers, every inch lined with bones
- 7 The petrified belly of a gargantuan, primordial **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Other primordial entities**|titan]]**
- 8 Vaulted tunnels lined with roots of iron-infused wood, rusted and bleeding
- 9 Limestone caverns, the walls pulsing with **[[46 - The Things Below#**Unnatural phenomena**|red crystal]]**
- 10 Limestone caves and columns, wet and dripping, festooned with slimy life
- 11 Strangely smooth tunnels coated in warm, pulsing organic material
- 12 A vast chamber of white stone, a mighty dead tree growing down from the ceiling

Fomoraij lairs are always found underground and beneath the **[[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|Great Wood]]**, accessed via caves and **[[24 - Green Lords#Ruins|Green Lord ruins]]**. The sites above are often corrupted by the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**.

sections.title: 1d12 form
sections.body:
1 A swarm of smaller things/a hivemind consciousness 2 Boneless/squeezing/amorphous 3 Fungal/lichenous/mossy 4 Insectoid/shelled 5 Too many limbs/eyes/mouths 6 Massive/expansive/sprawling 7 Pallid/fleshy/knobby 8 Powerful/burrowing/rending 9 Plantlike (roots, rhizomes, bulbs) 10 Slimy/oozing/mucous 11 Sporting tentacles/tendrils/feelers/

Combine 2-3 of the above with the vaguely humanoid form it once had as a Green Lord.

12 Worm-like/slug-like/grub-like

antennae

*The PCs have encountered some horrors in the Great Wood—some crinwin oozing silver slime and wasps with hallucinatory venom. I already decided that these things were corrupted by the Things Below, based on the themes of "insanity/chaos/ruin/quicksilver" and "corruption/disease/poison/vermin."* 

*Now I'm thinking that a Fomoraij is behind them. I keep the two themes, and roll a* constraint *of "confinement." For its* lair*, I roll "a series of underground streams, rivers, lakes, all teeming with horrors." For its* form*, I get "too many limbs/eyes/mouths," and "a swarm of smaller things" and "slimy/oozing/mucous." I ponder, and come up with:*

*Yaarowslow the Many is trapped away on an island in an underground lake, fed by tiny rivulets of groundwater. It has transformed its flesh into an oozing, silvery nest, host to an endless swarm of skittering and buzzing horrors that serve as its eyes and hands and mouths. For centuries, the vermin have burrowed through stone to reach the surface. It has recently succeeded, and its silvery spawn now taint a spring deep in the Great Wood.*

sections.title: Various treasures
sections.body:
- Preserved chimera corpse, mounted for display (*immobile*, Value 1)
- A pouch of shimmering powder (lllll doses, Value 2 to the right buyer); ingest a dose and start compulsively telling secrets to those around you
- A polished slice of agate the size of a dinner table, bringing nightmares and dark thoughts to those who linger in its presence (*immobile*, Value 2)
- A pair of what seem to be rubies (Value 3) set as the eyes of a horrible statue; they're actually **[[46 - The Things Below#**Unnatural phenomena**|red crystals]]** and impart a cruel bloodlust on whoever holds them.

sections.title: Bloodshot amber
sections.body:
each, *magical*

1d4 fist-sized gems, riddled with red-black intrusions.

When you *drip blood onto an amber*, name a manifest spirit in your presence and roll +CHA. If the spirit has an intact **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Tethers|tether]]**, roll with disadvantage. **On a 10+**, the spirit is imprisoned in the amber; **on a 7-9**, the amber starts to suck the spirit in, but it may yet fight back; **on a 6-**, the spirit wrests free and the amber shatters.

When you *imprison a spirit in a bloodshot amber*, it is wracked with pain and cannot manifest. Only one spirit can be imprisoned at a time. When you *touch the amber with bare skin*, you can communicate with the spirit within and release it if you want (even if you weren't the one who imprisoned it).

When a bloodshot amber is found, it likely contains a spirit that has been trapped in agony for centuries.

sections.title: Dream-lily pool
sections.body:
A raised pool of clear water. A strange lily grows within, its tendrils pouring over the lip and coating the floor, hiding bleached bones. The tendrils rustle when living things approach, and a gentle wind promises prophecy and wisdom.

When you *let a lily-tendril slither under your skin*, take 1d4 damage (ignores armor) and fall deeply asleep. Roll +WIS: **on a 10+**, you dream of wisdom—ask the GM any question and get a helpful, honest answer, though one cloaked in symbolism; **on a 7-9**, you dream of wisdom (as on a 10+) but also of hunger, desperation, and want—mark a debility when you wake; **on a 6-**, you are trapped in endless nightmare—mark two debilities and you shall not wake unless you find an exit or someone rescues you from without.

Regardless, your dreams are clearly visible in the waters of the pool. You get the sense that something else may be watching, too.

sections.title: Minor arcana
sections.body: Pick 1 or have someone roll.

sections.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
sections.body:
- 1-2 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#Impudent Polymorph Ire|A large wooden jar]]**
- 3-4 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A rusty cauldron|A rusty cauldron]]**
- 5-6 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A strange pendant|A strange pendant]]**

sections.title: Major arcana
sections.body:
These might very well be associated with the Fomoraij.

- The **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Mysteries of the Staff of the Lidless Orb|Staff of the Lidless Orb]]**
- The **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Demonhide Cloak|Demonhide Cloak]]**

sections.title: Various creations
sections.body:
- **[[24 - Green Lords#Bound Entities|Chimerae]]**, bred for war or sick amusement
- **[[24 - Green Lords#**1d12** ability|Hazardous growths]]**, protecting the Fomoraij's lair or extending its influence
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|Revenants]]** of chimerae, possessed by dark spirits
- **[[07 - Crinwin#Crinwin|Crinwin]]**, **[[24 - Green Lords#**The Unquiet Dead**|llamudwr]]**, **[[24 - Green Lords#**Rhagedn**|rhagedn]]**, **[[24 - Green Lords#**Rhagedn**|swyn]]**), or **[[24 - Green Lords#Zrajedak|zrajedak]]**—but somehow different, somehow worse
- Almost any of the horrors found in **[[52 - Vor Svetelik#Vor Svetelik|Vor Svetelik]]**

sections.title: Attendant spirits
sections.body:
- **Emanations** of the Things Below [[46 - The Things Below#**Wretch**|The Things Below]]
- **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|Spirits of the wild]]**, bound into wretched servitude
- Bound **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|ghosts]]**, kept on a tight leash
- Unbound **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|wraiths]]**, like jackals sniffing around a butcher shop

sections.title: Infected servant
sections.body:
Fomoraij sometimes ensnare beings of the world above, capturing them, luring them in with promises of power, or compelling the weak-willed to attend them. They then infect the victim, turning them into extensions of their will.

Start with a base creature, and then pick or roll for the nature of the infection, the means of control, and up to 3 gifts. Interpret them based on what you know about its master. Update the base creature's tags, stats, qualities, instinct, and moves accordingly.

sections.title: 1d12 infection
sections.body:
- 1-2 A bug/worm/vine, burrowed into their brain/spine
- 3-4 A foul substance, injected/inhaled/ imbibed
- 5-6 A fungus/plant, growing under their skin
- 7-8 A spirit possessing their body, bound by intricate scars/tattoos
- 9-10 A mass of vermin/a single horror, eating them from the inside out
- 11-12 One or more shards of **[[46 - The Things Below#**Unnatural phenomena**|red crystal]]** embedded in their skull

sections.title: 1d12 means of control
sections.body:
- 1-2 By crushing their will
- 3-4 By eating away at what they once were
- 5-6 By filling them with euphoria/ pleasure
- 7-8 By promising them power/immortality/revenge/a cure/etc.
- 9-10 By wracking them with pain
- 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice, or make up something new

If you like, roll 1d6 for the extent of control.

sections.title: 1d12 gift
sections.body:
- 1 A *reach*, *near*, or *far* attack (tentacles, spitting globs, flung spines, etc.)
- 2 A terrifying presence; an ability to spread panic/fear/horror
- 3 An area attack (cloud of poison, blast of nettles, psychic scream, etc.)
- 4 An amorphous/malleable/ shapechanging form
- 5 An ongoing link to its master, an ability to share senses/thoughts/ words/power
- 6 A way to spread its infection and/ or its master's influence (intentionally, incidentally, or both)
- 7 Camouflage/stealth/ability to avoid notice
- 8 Exceptional strength/speed/ferocity/deadliness
- 9 Influence/control over the minds of others
- 10 Minions/servants/ability to send out extensions of itself
- 11 Mobility (jumping, flying, climbing, burrowing, squeezing, etc.)
- 12 Unnatural resilience/vigor/durability

Should a PC or an important NPC become infected by a Fomoraij (or one of their servants), write it up as a threat (an *affliction*). If a PC willingly becomes an infected servant, consider creating a playbook insert, using the **Thrall** for inspiration (see *[[Inserts#Thrall|Book I]]*).

*I want a big scary servant of Yaarowslow the Many [[15 - Fomoraij#**"Typical" Fomoraij**|Fomoraij]], a hagr [[23 - The Great Wood#**Instinct** to lure prey|The Great Wood]] that stumbled on the tainted spring. I roll "a foul substance, injected/ inhaled/imbibed" and think of Yaarowslow's silvery slime. I get "by eating away at what they once were" as a means of control, and imagine the silver slime corrupting it from the inside, replacing its blood and organs and overwhelming its mind with voices and delusions. For gifts, I pick "unnatural resilience/vigor/durability" and "a way to spread its infection and/or its master's influence." I then roll for a third gift and get "an area attack."* 

*I update the hagr's stat block to reflect these changes, and end up with this:*

sections.title: INFECTED HAGR
sections.body:
- Solitary, large, terrifying, hardy, magical
- HP 20; ARMOR 4 (resilience), 1 vs. bronze (blubbery hide)
- DAMAGE meaty hands d10+3 (hand, close, grabby, forceful) or tree trunk d10+5 (reach, area, crude, forceful) or hurled heavy thing d10+3 (thrown, forceful)
- SPECIAL QUALITIES infectious blood; suffers from delusions, behaves erratically
- INSTINCT to spread its master's influence
  - > Fix someone with its evil eye, inflicting overwhelming delusions
  - > Pluck things from the ground as if weightless
  - > Obsessively carve its master's sigils into things
  - > Fly into a senseless rage
  - > Spew forth an infectious, silvery cloud (reach, area, reload)

sections.title: "Typical" Fomoraij
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *magical*, *cunning*, *cautious*, *terrifying*

**HP** 26; **Armor** 4 (resilience), 0 vs. bronze **Damage** mighty blows d12+3 (*hand*, *close*, *forceful*)

**Special qualities** can sense anything that happens in its lair (but might not think to)

**Instinct** [Use constraint as a guide]

- ä Overawe anyone in its presence
- ä Twist, mutate, or transform living things with a gesture
- ä Wield nearby plants and beasts as extensions of itself
- ä Regenerate wounds from anything but **[[18 - Forge Lords#**1d6** architectural elements|orichalcum]]**

No Fomoraij is truly typical; use the stats above as a starting point. A Fomoraij's constraint [[15 - Fomoraij#**"Typical" Fomoraij**|Fomoraij]] dictates its instinct. Add or change its tags, stats, special qualities, and moves based on the themes [[46 - The Things Below#**Emanation**|The Things Below]], constraint, lair, and forms [[15 - Fomoraij#**"Typical" Fomoraij**|Fomoraij]] that you've identified.

*Yaarowslow the Many is a mass of flesh that oozes infectious silvery slime, and the host of skittering, slithering, and buzzing horrors that serve as an extension of its will.*

sections.title: YAAROWSLOW, THE MANY
sections.body:
Solitary, large, magical, terrifying, amorphous

HP 24; Armor 5 (resilience, lacks organs), 2 vs. bronze (thick hide)

DAMAGE spurts of corrosive slime d10+1 (hand, close, area, messy, 1 piercing)

SPECIAL QUALITIES largely immobile, hive-mind connection with its vermin swarms; half damage from stabbing or smashing

sections.title: INSTINCT to escape
sections.body:
- > Overwhelm foes with horror and thousands of squealing voices
- > Infect minds and bodies with silvery slime
- > Wield its swarms and its silvery slime as an extension of itself
- > Regenerate wounds from anything but orichalcum

sections.title: The Foothills
sections.body:
Two days northwest of Stonetop, the rolling **[[14 - The Flats#The Flats|Flats]]** give way to hills, climbing up into the **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#The Whitefang Mountains|Whitefang Mountains]]**). The **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#The Makers' Roads|Highway]]** cuts through these hills, leading to **[[04 - Barrier Pass#Barrier Pass|Barrier Pass]]**. To the east, the hills wrap around the **[[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|Great Wood]]**.

The Foothills are rugged and forest-strewn, crisscrossed with streams, dotted with gorges and ponds and small lakes. In ages past, it was a borderland between the **[[38 - Rime Lords#Rime Lords|Rime Lords]]** and the **[[24 - Green Lords#Green Lords|Green Lords]]**, and later it was the extent of the **Barrow Builders'** reach [[05 - Barrow Builders|Barrow Builders]]. Nowadays, it is mostly uninhabited, but both Stonetop and Barrier Pass come here to harvest timber.

sections.title: Always
sections.body:
- Hills rising up from the Flats, mountains looming behind them
- Young thin trees on the southernmost slopes, getting bigger and older as you press into the hills
- Echoes of birdsong, beast-bellows, tumbling rocks, raised voices

sections.title: Spring
sections.body:
- Thin branches drooping with fresh green leaves
- Mud slipping and squishing underfoot
- Surprising chill of shadowed vales, where snow still lingers

sections.title: Summer
sections.body:
- Muggy, humid heat
- Sweat stinging your eyes as you hump up another slope
- Cold clear water from a gurgling stream, tasting better than anything

sections.title: Autumn
sections.body:
- Slopes turned red and orange and yellow, then black and brown and gray
- Plump squirrels and other critters, busily foraging for winter
- Cold, clammy mist lasting well into the day

sections.title: Winter
sections.body:
- Waist-deep snow drifts in the valleys
- Frozen-over streams, water gurgling ominously from below
- Squeak of trees, swaying in the cold wind
- Short, magical twilight as the sun drops below the mountains

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- What striking difference do you notice between the trees here and those in the Great Wood?
- Who typically leads Stonetop's logging trips up to the Foothills?
- What dread beast or monster is said to lurk in the Foothills?
- What is said to draw its ire?
- Who claims to have encountered it? When, and how so?
- Why do you/don't you believe them?
- What mundane dangers up there worry you more than some legendary beast?

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- A logging expedition goes missing
- Loggers stumble upon a ruin or corrupted site
- Someone went missing on a logging trip years ago, and now they reappear
- Heavy rains wash out a stretch of the Highway, blocking access to good timber
- An adventurer claims to know the location of an old barrow in the Foothills, said to be laden with gold and other treasure

sections.title: PERMANENT LOGGING CAMP
sections.body:
Suppose we built a few cabins and kept loggers up there year-round?

Requires all of the following:

- Two or three permanent dwellings, just off the Highway
- Supplies, furnishings, and tools for those dwellings (Value 2)
- 5-6 capable adults, willing and able to relocate and log year-round
- An extra wagon (Value 3) and extra horse or mule (Value 3) to haul timber to and from Stonetop
- Four seasons of operation, each generating a wagonload of timber (Value 2)

When you *mark all the requirements*, add "Logging: timber, wood" to the resources list and increase Fortunes by 1.

Henceforth, as long as the camp is in operation, the steading has a ready supply of timber for improvements and other projects, and it consumes 1 less Surplus than usual in winter. However, the logging camp consumes 1 Surplus every summer or else it ceases operation.

sections.title: Terrain
sections.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

sections.title: 1d12 terrain
sections.body:
- 1 Large pond/small lake
- 2 Creek, stream, gulley
- 3 Steep slope: barren, treacherous, unstable
- 4 Dense thicket/brush
- 5-6 Swath/copse of one type of tree
- 7-8 Gentle slope or valley
- 9 Steep slope: forested, overgrown
- 10 Sheer rock face, bluff, rocky outcrop
- 11 Clearing, meadow, burn site
- 12 Cave, cavern, grotto

If you like, roll 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

sections.title: Discoveries
sections.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 discovery
sections.body:
- 1 A change of terrain [[16 - The Foothills#**1d6** discovery|The Foothills]], and roll again
- 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.)
- 3 folk from barrier pass (next column)
- 4 Some other encounter (next column)
- 5 A point of interest (see below)
- 6 **[[50 - Useful or Valuable Flora#Useful or valuable flora|Useful or valuable flora]]**

sections.title: 1d6 folk from barrier pass
sections.body:
- 1 A war party of 4-8 guardians and/ or monks, hunting something dangerous
- 2 A patrol of 2-4 guardians, led by a White Crystal monk
- 3 A monk and/or novice, on their own to study/practice/meditate/ make pilgrimage
- 4 A logging crew (4-12 layfolk, plus a few guardians)
- 5-6 1-4 layfolk hunting or foraging

Consider having someone roll the Die of Fate for their disposition. See [[04 - Barrier Pass#**Guardian**|Barrier Pass]] for guardian, monk, and novice stats.

sections.title: 1d6 encounter
sections.body:
- 1 A **[[24 - Green Lords#Bound Entities|chimera]]**, wary or indifferent but not outright hostile
- 2 A **[[12 - Fae#Fae|Fae]]**, curious or indifferent
- 3 A **[[38 - Rime Lords#**Tulpa**|tulpa]]**, likely tethered to a nearby Rime Lord site
- 4 A **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Spirits of the wild|spirit of the wild]]**, curious or indifferent
- 5 A dangerous beast—like a **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Dangers**|boar]]**, **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Cave bear**|cave bear]]**, **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Cougar**|cougar]]**, **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**1d6** instinct/moves|ice drake]]**), **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Monk**|mountain apes]]**, or **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Raselbaedd**|wolves]]**—wary or indifferent, but easily provoked
- 6 Game: good eating but prone to bolt (deer, rabbits, pheasant, etc.)

sections.title: 1d6 point of interest
sections.body:
- 1 A place corrupted by the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**
- 2 A place **haunted** by death or the undying [[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|Death and the Undying]]
- 3 A site or artifact of the **[[05 - Barrow Builders#Barrow Builders|Barrow Builders]]**
- 4 A ruin or artifact of the **[[24 - Green Lords#Green Lords|Green Lords]]** or a **[[12 - Fae#**Getting there**|Fae domain]]**
- 5 A ruin or artifact of the **[[38 - Rime Lords#Rime Lords|Rime Lords]]** or their human disciples
- 6 A place sacred to the **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Spirits of the wild|spirits of the wild]]**

sections.title: Hazards
sections.body:
- Getting lost among the hills and trees
- Treacherous terrain (loose footing, scree, slippery slopes, potholes, deadfall, etc.)
- Flash flooding, especially in ravines and valleys
- Harsh weather, especially rain and snow
- Something tall to climb, and reason to climb it
- **[[24 - Green Lords#**1d12** ability|Hazardous growth]]**, from the time of the Green Lords

sections.title: Monsters
sections.body:
- Ornery beasts: **[[23 - The Great Wood#Monsters|boars]]**, **[[23 - The Great Wood#Monsters|cave bears]]**, **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**1d6** instinct/moves|ice drakes]]**, **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Monk**|mountain apes]]**, **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Raselbaedd**|wisents]]**
- Predators: **[[23 - The Great Wood#Monsters|butcherbirds]]**, **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Cougar**|cougars]]**, **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Feathered drake**|feathered drakes]]**, or **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Raselbaedd**|wolves]]**
- **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Spirits of the wild|Spirits of the wild]]**
- **[[12 - Fae#Fae|Fae]]**
- A **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Instinct** to lure prey|hagr]]** or two, come up from the Great Wood
- **[[38 - Rime Lords#**Tulpa**|Tulpas]]**, tied to Rime Lord sites or perhaps to Barrier Pass
- **[[24 - Green Lords#Bound Entities|Chimerae]]** and other remnants of the Green Lords
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Ghosts]]** or **revenants**

sections.title: Forest Folk
sections.body:
All the tales agree: the Folk were in the **[[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|Great Wood]]** long before Stonetop was founded. They welcomed the newcomers, or at least suffered them, but demanded that villagers never fell a living tree from the Wood.

The Forest Folk mostly kept to themselves. But over generations, contacts were made. Favors exchanged, friendships won, stories shared. And more shared, too, judging by the odd eyes that some villagers bear.

The villagers did not notice when the Forest Folk left, not right away. Encounters were never common. Then they became rare. Then there were none at all.

Their absence echoes through the Great Wood. Their crude glyphs can still be found, carved into trees and boulders. Their sacred places stand empty. And dangerous creatures—things once hunted by the Folk—now breed freely.

sections.title: Names
sections.body: Slavic-sounding, often diminutives: Analiya, Asya, Avo, Boyko, Danja, Dimvo, Dobri, Galya, Genko, Ivka, Inko, Kosta, Lyubo, Milada, Milko, Mincha, Mitko, Nadya, Neli, Penko, Polya, Radko, Raya, Sajka, Sasho, Tsvetanka, Valya, Vasko

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- Who (among you/among your kin/that you know) bears the strange eyes of the Forest Folk? What do they look like?
- How are folk with "the forest eyes" treated in Stonetop? What about their parents?
- Did you ever meet one of the Forest Folk? Aside from their eyes, which of their inhuman features did you find most disturbing?
- It's a mystery for sure, but what do you think happened to the Forest Folk? Why?

sections.title: Lore
sections.body:
The Forest Folk's ancestors were mortal servants of the **[[24 - Green Lords#Names|Green Lords]]**. They lived nomadic lives among the trees, traveling in small bands and living off the land.

**Something interesting:** The Folk would often commune with **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|spirits of the wild]]**, and sometimes had dealings with the **[[12 - Fae#Fae|Fae]]**. Maybe they know where the Folk went?

**Something useful:** You know of a place where the Forest Folk used to congregate, not too far from Stonetop, or a Fae they once dealt with frequently, or a sacred site they revered—tell us about it! Also, tell us why you think it's a good place to start investigating what happened to the Folk.

sections.title: Where did they go?
Hooks
sections.body:
- A hunter finds a mass grave of the Forest Folk, from, oh, about 10 years back
- A cave is covered with glyphs and pictograms that reveal valuable lore, but some of the glyphs are visible only to those with the forest eyes
- A trapper swears that they've seen one of the Forest Folk, or signs of their recent presence
- 60 years ago, Stonetop gave the Forest Folk a precious gift, which they took to one of the sacred sites; perhaps it's still there?

sections.title: Forest eyes
sections.body:
Those who inherit the "forest eyes" see well at night. They see by mere starlight as well as most humans can see under a full moon; they see by moonlight as well as most humans can see at dusk.

Beyond that, certain plants, lichens, and rocks shine with indescribable colors that can only be seen with the forest eyes. The Folk would sometimes be seen staring rapt at a mundane grove or meadow, marveling at some unseen beauty. Humans with the forest eyes sometimes do likewise.

If a PC was born with (or develops) the forest eyes, feel free to use these GM moves on them:

- <sup>ä</sup> Reveal glyphs, markings, or patterns that only they can see
- <sup>ä</sup> Distract them with colors and patterns of impossible beauty

sections.title: Glyphs and pictograms
sections.body: The Forest Folk left crude marks and drawings throughout the Great Wood. When the PCs encounter a point of interest in the Great Wood, and it was around before the Folk disappeared, pick or have someone roll the Die of Fate:

sections.title: 1d6 markings
sections.body:
- 1-2 None to be found
- 3 Hidden, incomplete/marred
- 4 Hidden, intact
- 5 Obvious but incomplete/marred
- 6 Obvious and intact

"Hidden" glyphs might be hidden intentionally, obscured by happenstance (vines, growth, etc.), and/or made with pigments that only the forest eyes can see.

To an outsider, these glyphs and pictograms seem like nonsense—arbitrary shapes and crude, stylized drawings. But the Forest Folk had an elaborate visual vocabulary, and most of these markings are rich with meaning.

A PC can Know Things to attempt to discern the meanings of any such glyphs, with disadvantage if they are incomplete or marred. **On a 7-9**, they discern a vague concept, like "dangerous plants," "bound spirit" or "sorrow/loss". **On a 10+**, they glean the full meaning, like "these plants are dangerous to eat" or "this tree binds a spirit of darkness" or "many Forest Folk died

here at the hands of a sorcerer."

sections.title: Sites
sections.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 a place where they...
sections.body:
- 1 … stood guard over something
- 2 … fought/died/interred their dead
- 3 … performed rituals/communed with spirits/worked magic
- 4 … dwelt/worked/made things/ stored things
- 5 … gathered/congregated/held festivals
- 6 … left signs of what happened to them

Pick or roll for an appropriate **[[23 - The Great Wood#. steading improvement .|terrain]]**. Populate it with signs of their presence (below), **[[17 - Forest Folk#**Artifacts**|artifacts]]**, and **[[17 - Forest Folk#Cursed glyphs|dangers]]** of the Forest Folk or the **[[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|Great Wood]]**. Consider combining with another **[[23 - The Great Wood#Discoveries|point of interest]]** from the Great Wood.

Signs of the Forest Folk's presence include:

- Elaborate glyphs and pictograms
- Strips of leather, often dyed, hanging from trees or wrapped around trunks
- Tools/weapons of stone, wood, bone: adzes, scrapers, arrowheads, spears, etc.
- Containers of carved wood, cured leather, woven plant fiber
- Clothes (leather, fur, woven plants), likely in tatters

sections.title: Various treasures
sections.body:
- A small wooden carving of some beast or chimera (Value 0)
- Oversized, exquisitely knapped flint spear head (*crude*, *awkward*, Value 1 to a collector)
- Big wooden bowl and lid, sealed with beeswax, filled with dried medicinal herb (Value 2)
- Mostly-buried skeleton of an elephant-sized beast, bones carved with glyphs and pictograms that tell an important story (*immobile*, Value 2)
- An acre of forest with 3d6 varieties of **[[50 - Useful or Valuable Flora#Useful or valuable flora|useful flora]]** growing in careful harmony; 1d6-1 can be harvested at the moment.

sections.title: A wooden mask
sections.body:
*fragile*, *magical*

Carved from pale elder wood in the shape of a bird's head, but with small horns and fangs. The inside is chiseled with glyphs like those that the Forest Folk used to make.

**Something interesting:** You recognize glyphs for "beast," "spirit," "forest," and… "peace?" Or maybe "treaty."

**Something useful:** When you *wear the wooden mask*, beasts of the Great Wood will do you no harm, though your companions have no such protection. Perversions of nature are unaffected. If you *harm a beast of the Great Wood while wearing the mask*, it loses all power and rapidly rots away.

sections.title: Crystal knife
sections.body:
*hand*, *crude*, *magical*

A blade of knapped quartz, with the hilt wrapped in glyph-covered leather. It gleams to those with the forest eyes.

When you *wield the crystal knife as a weapon*, you can harm the manifest form of any spirit, ignoring armor and dealing +1d4 damage. If the 1d4 comes up a 4, though, the knife shatters into useless shards.

sections.title: Artifacts of others
sections.body:
Held in reverence, used with caution, and/or hidden where they could do no harm.

- Artifacts of the **[[24 - Green Lords#Names|Green Lords]]**
- Items related to the **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|spirits of the wild]]**
- Gifts of the **[[12 - Fae#Fae|Fae]]**
- Objects tied to **[[10 - Death and the Undying#Death and the undying|death or the undying]]**
- Artifacts cursed by **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|the Things Below]]**

sections.title: Cursed glyphs
sections.body: They look like other glyphs the Forest Folk left behind, but pick or roll for both a trigger and an effect.

sections.title: 1d6 trigger
sections.body:
- 1 Merely being in their presence
- 2 Seeing them, even at a glance
- 3 Passing a border that they define, or attempting to
- 4 Attempting to read or comprehend them
- 5 Touching whatever they're inscribed upon
- 6 Erasing, breaking, or marring them

sections.title: 1d6 effect
sections.body:
- 1 Afflicts a lingering, withering disease
- 2 Traps/ensnares/binds
- 3 Sickens/weakens/harms
- 4 Instills a powerful emotion (fear, despair, rage, etc.)
- 5 Draws the attention of certain spirits
- 6 Befuddles/confuses/twists the senses

sections.title: Opportunists
sections.body:
Encroaching on territory vacated by the Forest Folk, or growing now that they are no longer kept in check.

- **[[07 - Crinwin#Crinwin|Crinwin]]**
- **Beasts** of the Great Wood [[23 - The Great Wood#**Dangers**|The Great Wood]]
  - **[[24 - Green Lords#**1d12** ability|Hazardous growths]]** and **[[24 - Green Lords#Bound Entities|chimerae]]**
- **[[12 - Fae#Fae|Fae]]**, released from old oaths and/or keen to claim new territory

sections.title: Spirits
sections.body:
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Ghosts]]** or **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|revenants]]** of Forest Folk who died badly, or with work unfinished
- **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|Spirits of the wild]]** who dwelt happily alongside the Folk, or who were imprisoned by them
- **[[46 - The Things Below#**Wretch**|Emanations]]** and spirits **corrupted** by the Things Below [[46 - The Things Below#**1d12** origin|The Things Below]]), trapped but straining at their bindings.

sections.title: Forge Lords
sections.body:
Forge Lords were **[[30 - The Makers#The Makers|Makers]]** who bent fire and metal to their will. They built a sprawling civilization among the **[[27 - Huffel Peaks#Huffel Peaks|Huffel Peaks]]** and on the shores of the sea beyond. They wrought wonders and traded with other Makers, in what is now **[[22 - Gordin's Delve#Gordin's Delve|Gordin's Delve]]**. They grew wealthy, proud, and strong.

A fire burned inside the Forge Lords. It fueled their magic, and also their passion and creativity, their love of beauty and craftsmanship. Their desire to do better, to be better. But this fire was also a curse. If left to burn unchecked—if they gave in to ambition, jealousy, and greed—it could consume them. Burn away their finer feelings, twist them into terrible, fire-belching beasts.

To guard against this curse, the Forge Lords lauded loyalty and generosity. They observed strict rules of hospitality and decorum. For centuries, it worked. Their lands were hotbeds of politics and betrayal, but bloody-handed murder was rare. Only the most debased citizens lost themselves to the Change.

Then came the **[[55 - Appendix A - Ages of the World#Time of Cataclysm|Time of Cataclysm]]**. Volcanoes belched forth lava and ash. The Forge Lord's principal city, Moratia Rubra, was destroyed. So were many of their mines, foundries, and estates. Their society plunged into chaos. And the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]** stoked the flames of vengeance, greed, and paranoia.

Rivalry gave way to murder. Murder gave way to slaughter. Only those who embraced the Change survived. And soon, the Forge Lords were gone. Only **[[18 - Forge Lords#**Draventao**|draventao]]** remained.

sections.title: Names
sections.body:
Portuguese- and Galician-sounding, but often with 4 or 5 syllables: Anacarbrugel, Barximare, Cagacola, Liainlosoi, Mancaoro, Olarela, Patomecie, Ronofoxur, Toloalare, Velanbria, Xeixabrun

Often have titles like *Dominar* ("Master"), *Ogran* ("the Great").

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- Miners loot and sell treasure guarded by **[[18 - Forge Lords#**Iron hound**|iron hounds]]**, triggering a chain of grisly deaths.
- **[[51 - Ustrina#Ustrina|Ustrina]]** warn the PCs away from a Forge Lord ruin, but why?
- A scoundrel finds a **draventao**'s lair [[18 - Forge Lords#**Draventao**|Forge Lords]], wants help looting it.
- A merchant is secretly hauling a shipment of **[[18 - Forge Lords#**1d6** architectural elements|orichalcum]]** to market.
- Prospectors find a Forge Lord settlement buried and preserved in pumice.

sections.title: Themes
sections.body: Pick, or roll 1d12.

sections.title: 1d12 theme
sections.body:
1 Fire and heat, lava, smoke and embers, ash and cinders

**Forge Lords**

- 2 Industry: forges and furnaces, crucibles and kilns, foundries and fuel, mills and mines, and all that they consume/produce
- 3 Artistry: inspiration, invention, the pursuit of excellence/beauty/truth
- 4 Metal given life: clockwork constructs, spirit-vessels, prosthetics fused to flesh
- 5 Weakness burnt away: to create, to transform, to destroy, to purify
- 6 **[[51 - Ustrina#Ustrina|Ustrina]]** servants/ factions/rivalries/secrets/plots/ loyalty/betrayal
- 7 Passions enflamed: hunger, lust, envy, greed, obsession, paranoia, and wrath
- 8 Cataclysm: eruptions, terror, slaughter, societal collapse, the descent into chaos

sections.title: Sites
sections.body: Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 site
sections.body:
- 1-3 **Lingering signs** of their presence and influence (see below)
- 4-6 **[[18 - Forge Lords#1d6 site|Ruin]]**

sections.title: Lingering signs
sections.body: Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 sign
sections.body:
- 1 **Aftermath of destruction** (next column), a scar on the world
- 2 **Traces of industry** (next column) from long ago
- 3 A **fiery spirit** (next page), bound to some useful purpose
- 4 A **monument or marker** (next page)
- 5 A stretch of the **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#The Makers' Roads|Makers' Roads]]**, intact, broken, and/or buried in tuff
- 6 A hidden hoard, filled with **[[18 - Forge Lords#Various treasures|various treasures]]**

sections.title: Aftermath of destruction
sections.body:
For example:

- Chimneys and bits of wall, sticking out of rubble and tuff, charred and dusty.
- A mountainside, melted and smooth like black glass.
- A clearing above a long-burning coalseam fire: barren, buckled, and cracked, the soil charred white, the air foul to breathe and hot even in winter.
- A mass grave of Ustrina, maybe a Forge Lord or two, buried rather than properly burned.
- The bones of a great reptilian beast, with rusted plates (armor?) fused to the ribs and spine. The skull is caved in, the teeth pried loose and missing.

Such places might very well be **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|haunted]]** and/or corrupted by the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**.

sections.title: Traces of industry For example:
sections.body:
- A gouge in bedrock, like a pit or canyon with terraced walls—an open quarry from long ago.
- A lake/mire/pool: chalky shores, metallic swirls, stunted life.
- A slag-pile: pitted, fist-sized rocks (dull red and rough, or glassy blue and green), mostly barren, quite unstable.
- The top of a mountain just missing, collapsed on itself or turned into a crater.
- The caldera of a dormant volcano. Rusty, rune-carved spires dot the rim, and enormous pitted chains litter the area.
- Fragments of an aqueduct, still adorned with beautiful ceramic tiles.

sections.title: A fiery spirit
sections.body:
For example:

- A furnace, still warm from banked coals, kept smoldering for centuries by a groggy, mopey spirit.
- A rune-etched marble slab set before a magma pool. Binds a spirit that manifests as a figure of molten rock. Helps with metalwork in exchange for gossip.
- A crystalline matrix, home to a dozen small flames that have burnt for centuries without fuel. Opinionated blowhards, but quite clever and craving novelty.
- A huge bronze statue of a boar, its mouth agape. The spirit within consumes the flammability of objects, making them fireproof. But the statue itself ignites with the slightest spark

sections.title: Monument or marker
sections.body:
For example:

- A bronze statue of a regal figure, once 60 feet tall, found toppled, dented, and buried in a valley.
- Another huge bronze statue of a regal figure, still set upon its summit but half melted.
- A portrait on a mountainside, a mosaic made from millions of glass beads.
- A seamless tower of basalt, topped with an open-faced onion dome of brilliant brass, an eternal flame burning within.
- A marble slab, big as a house. One side is covered with a bronze plaque, etched with tight-packed calligraphic runes, mostly illegible under all the lichen.

sections.title: Ruin
sections.body: Pick or roll for themes [[18 - Forge Lords#**Ruin**|Forge Lords]], structure, purpose, architectural elements, condition, and ustrina presence. Place it in an appropriate terrain (see [[22 - Gordin's Delve#Gordin's Delve|Gordin’s Delve]] for Gordin's Delve, [[27 - Huffel Peaks|Huffel Peaks]] for the Huffel Peaks, or [[28 - The Labyrinth|The Labyrinth]] for the Labyrinth). Populate it with **[[18 - Forge Lords#**Ruin**|lingering signs]]**, **[[18 - Forge Lords#**Artifacts**|artifacts]]**, and/or **[[18 - Forge Lords#**Two mirrors**|dangers]]** of the Forge Lords or the appropriate region.

sections.title: 1d6 structure
sections.body:
- 1 A cluster of smaller buildings
- 2 A single larger building, built around an open courtyard/garden/pool
- 3 Large open space
- 4 Warren-like structure, many connected rooms/buildings
- 5 Tunnel(s)/vault(s) carved into mountain/bedrock
- 6 Fortified complex containing a number of the above

sections.title: 1d6 purpose
sections.body:
- 1 Dwelling (home, manor, barracks, apartments, etc.)
- 2 Infrastructure (aqueduct, cistern, bridge, tunnel, fortification, etc.)
- 3 Storage (vault, warehouse, treasury, granary, etc.)
- 4 Production (smithy, studio, mine, etc.)
- 5 Civic life (baths, market, gardens, monument, governance, etc.)
- 6 Pleasure (gallery, restaurant, theater, arena, etc.)

*[[22 - Gordin's Delve#Gordin's Delve|The PCs are in Gordin's Delve]]**, seeking an audience with Mutra the Teeth. I think his compound is one of the Forge Lord ruins in town. I'm picturing something carved into the ridges that cradle Gordin's Delve, so I pick "tunnel(s)/vault(s)" for structure, "buried… but mostly intact" for condition, and "none to speak of " for Ustrina presence. I roll a 3 for theme (artistry), a 6 for purpose (pleasure), and a 1 for architectural elements (domes/ arches/columns/arcades). I envision the following:*

sections.title: 1d6 architectural elements
sections.body:
- 1 Domes/arches/columns/arcades
- 2 Ceramics and glasswork: mosaics, tile, urns, stained glass, mirrors
- 3 Enamel/glaze/gilding/flowing runes
- 4 Bricks/marble slabs/carved porphyry
- 5 Statues/reliefs (bronze or marble)
- 6 Furnaces/crucibles/chimneys/ chains/gears

Most Maker-ruins are sized for giants people two or even three times as tall as humans—but often with smaller-scale rooms, passages, and so forth for their servants.

- 1 A settlement, actively inhabited
- 2 Two or more groups, in conflict
- 3 Pilgrims/scavengers/wardens/etc.
- 4 A settlement, unoccupied/ abandoned
- 5 Offerings/vandalism/excavations/etc.
- 6 None to speak of

If **[[51 - Ustrina#Ustrina|Ustrina]]** are present, or have been, consider rolling 1d6 for a sense of their numbers.

*Mutra's compound was once the showroom for some Forge Lord's collected wealth. Outside, it's like the Treasury of Petra, in Jordan—columns before a recessed entrance and a looming arch. The arch leads to a vast chamber with a lofty domed ceiling and numerous empty plinths, each taller than a tall man. Mutra's people have strung screens between the plinths to split the chamber into barracks and storage and so forth. "Smaller" side chambers, carved further into the rock, hold Mutra's personal quarters—and his own not-so-modest collection.*

**Orichalcum**

A mystical metal that flashes red with fire and burns away the impure, bane to the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]** and perversions of nature. The Forge Lords knew its secrets how to mine it, smelt it, alloy it, and work it—and it was treasured even in their day.

Pick or roll the variety of any orichalcum the PCs encounter.

sections.title: 1d12 variety
sections.body:
- 1 Nearly pure, unworked nuggets/ ingots, full of potential (Value 2)
- 2-3 Nearly pure, cast into runes (repels perversions of nature, protects against their magic, Value 2)
- 4 Alloyed with copper and tin, powdered (burns perversions of nature; Value 1)
- 5 Alloyed with gold, flaked, suspended in elixir (burns away poison/ disease/corruption should the imbiber survive, Value 2)
- 6 Alloyed with lead, powdered (dispels unclean magic, Value 2)
- 7 Alloyed with magnesium, wrought into a charm (burns white-hot when touched by an oathbreaker, Value 2)
- 8 Alloyed with iron, forged into a sphere (glows bright red near illusions, reveals them as false, Value 2)
- 9-10 Alloyed with tin and arsenic, then cast into a weapon/shield/cuirass (+2 Value), counts as bronze whenever it matters
- 11-12 Alloyed as needed and worked into some appropriate form, then imbued with magic related to fire/ truth/purification

Consider rolling 1d6 to inform size, quantity, and/or scale.

Orichalcum often melts or vaporizes as it burns away impurity. Proper alloying helps prevent this, but even a well-made orichalcum spear might turn to slag as it strikes down a corrupted foe.

sections.title: Anthracite
sections.body:
A lustrous, gray-black stone, a form of purified coal. Catches fire like dry wood, but burns hotter, longer, with very little smoke. The Forge Lords used it to fuel their furnaces and kilns, or even to just heat their homes.

Some **[[51 - Ustrina#Ustrina|Ustrina]]** know the secrets to making the stuff (cooking coal, infusing it with flammability leached from other objects), though they (probably?) lack the numbers for extensive coal mining. They occasionally trade anthracite in Gordin's Delve, but only small amounts. The Bosses or southern **[[29 - Lygos and the South#**Merchants**|merchants]]** would start wars to learn how it could be mass produced.

sections.title: Other materials
sections.body:
Many Forge Lords knew how to make **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**Aetherium**|aetherium]]** and invest it with special characteristics. Indeed, they likely taught the Tempest Lords a thing or two.

A few Forge Lords could create **[[43 - Stone Lords#Makerglass|makerglass]]**, or at least shape it, but never with the skill and deftness of the Stone Lords.

The Forge Lords prized **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Primordial powers**|black iron]]**. It can be worked only with fire, strength, and skill—not with magic—and so only master smiths could work it.

A few Forge Lords dabbled with **[[46 - The Things Below#**Unnatural phenomena**|red crystal]]**, inspired by the Things Below or just arrogant enough

sections.title: Various treasures
sections.body:
- **1d6 glass vials** (*fragile*, Value 0), etched with a Forge Lord's mark
- **2d4 lumps of anthracite** (Value 1), each able to fuel a good cookfire for a night
- **A padded case** with 4 clay spheres, each containing potent naphtha that burns hot & sticky when exposed to the air (damage d10, *thrown*, *area*, *dangerous*, ignores armor)
- **A gold mobius strip** on a chain (Value 2)
- **An oversized orichalcum spearhead** (Value 2), too big to use but a smith might recast it
- **A box** holding a clockwork diorama; wind its key and it plays music, the masked figures dance and twirl (*fragile*, *magical*?, Value 3)
- **A flawless vase** (*fragile*, *beautiful*, Value 4), ~2 feet tall with an iridescent glaze; reminds you of the fragility of life
- **A pile of coins, jewelry, art, and wonders** (*immobile*, Value 5), a **draventao**'s hoard [[18 - Forge Lords#**Draventao**|Forge Lords]]

See also: the **[[51 - Ustrina#**Curiosities and wonders**|curiosities and wonders]]** that Ustrina sometimes bring to Gordin's Delve to trade.

sections.title: Orichalcum spear
sections.body:
, *close*, *thrown*, *magical*, *beautiful*, 1 piercing

The haft needs replacing, but the spearhead itself is pristine, beautiful, sharp as hot flame. When you *strike a perversion of nature with the spear*, deal +1d4 damage. When you *strike a perversion of nature and roll max damage* (including any bonus dice), deal another +1d4 damage as the spearhead flares and melts into worthless slag.

sections.title: An oversized ring
sections.body:
, *beautiful*, *magical*, Value 4

A fist-sized signet, large even by the Makers' standards, made of orichalcum. It glows like a dying ember, but is cool to the touch. When anyone lies in the ring's presence, their tongue blisters and burns. The greater the lie, the more damage is done.

**Something interesting:** This was the signet ring of a Forge Lord, one of great wealth and importance; the **[[51 - Ustrina#Ustrina|Ustrina]]** often show keen interest in such things.

**Something useful:** This was the signet ring of Omelor Xonane, a storied artificer. Many of their works were said to be keyed to this signet ring. Who knows what wonders it could unlock?

sections.title: Two mirrors
sections.body:
, *beautiful*, *magical*, Value 4 One is a flawless silvered mirror (*magical*, *beautiful*, *fragile*, Value 4), 3 feet tall and set in a golden frame. Forge Lord runes adorn the frame, translated as: "To my beautiful Esé, my faithful, my rock."

The other is a smaller, humble mirror (*magical*, *fragile*, Value 2), only 12 inches tall in a frame of bronze. It bears the same inscription as the other mirror, but inverted. A small needle protrudes from the frame, stained dark.

When you *prick your finger and touch the smaller mirror*, it shows what is reflected in the larger mirror until you break contact. Your blood soaks into the surface, leaving no stain.

sections.title: Minor arcana
sections.body: Pick 1 or have someone roll.

sections.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
sections.body:
- 1 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#The Fiery Veil|Runes around a ruined hall]]**
- 2 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A richly woven rug , magical|A richly woven rug]]**
- 3 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A prospector's tale|A metal man]]**
- 4 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A prospector's tale|A prospector's tale]]**
- 5 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A runic branding iron|A rusty steel blade]]**

 These might very well be associated with the Forge Lords.

6 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A runic branding iron|A runic branding iron]]**

- The **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Blood-quenched Sword|Blood-quenched Sword]]**
- The **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Red Scepter , magical|Red Scepter]]**

sections.title: Entities
sections.body:
- **[[51 - Ustrina#Ustrina|Ustrina]]**, of course
- **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Elemental spirits**|Fire spirits]]**, bound to an oven/forge/furnace, likely hungry and weak
- **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**Fire vortex**|A fire vortex]]**, set to guard a Forge Lord ruin
- A family of **[[27 - Huffel Peaks#Volcanic activity|caralandrao]]**, scavenging for magic or nesting in a ruin
- **[[27 - Huffel Peaks#**Fire drake**|Fire drakes]]** or **[[28 - The Labyrinth#**Magma worm**|magma worms]]**, basking in the lingering heat of a Forge Lord's furnace
- The **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|ghosts]]** of those who died in some cataclysm or violent purge

sections.title: Hazards
sections.body:
- Unstable construction: weakened by fire, rust, and age
- Piles of coal or anthracite: flammable, dusty, unstable
- Slag heaps, unstable and ready to become a **[[42 - The Steplands#1d6 natural beast or spirit|rockslide]]**
- Pools or flows of magma
- **[[27 - Huffel Peaks#Volcanic activity|Volcanic activity]]**
- **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Instinct** to eat and spawn|Primordial flame]]**, bound by ancient runes, terrible if unleashed

sections.title: Smoke, fire, and heat
sections.body:
Most Forge Lord ruins are cold and ashen, with little left to burn. But some are home to piles of unused fuel (coal, anthracite, etc.) or sources of magical fire.

- <sup>ä</sup> Show a warning: fuel ready to burn, heat pouring off magma or an ancient furnace, air rippling with heat shimmer
- <sup>ä</sup> Scald them with intense heat (1d4 damage)
- <sup>ä</sup> Have someone/thing catch fire
- <sup>ä</sup> Fill the area with choking smoke (debilities)
- <sup>ä</sup> Block a path with smoke/fire (1d6 damage, ignores armor)
- <sup>ä</sup> Catch them in a burst of flames (1d8 damage, *messy*, ignores armor)
- <sup>ä</sup> Engulf someone, set them on fire (1d10 damage, *messy*, ignores armor)

sections.title: Cinderwraith
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *spirit*, *undead*, *fiery*, *terrifying*

**HP** 23; **Armor** 1 (amorphous) **Damage** blistering flames 1d10+1 (*hand*, *grabby*, *messy*, ignores armor)

**Special qualities** fireproof, dormant without fresh air to "breathe," tethered to its bones

sections.title: Instinct to burn the living
sections.body:
- <sup>ä</sup> Manifest a leering pillar of fire and blackened hands (harmed only by water or silver)
- <sup>ä</sup> Melt silver or bronze, weaken steel or iron, ignite wood, char flesh
- <sup>ä</sup> Engulf a living being, char both flesh and soul (deal damage with advantage, inflict debilities, harm even fireproof foes)

The Forge Lords burned with an inner fire, a fire that threatened to consume them even when they lived. And when they died badly—slowly, desperately, betrayed and full of helpless rage—that fire could linger on. First it would burn away the Forge Lord's flesh, and then its spirit, and then its soul. And then it would wait in the bones, like heat in banked coals. Wait for fresh air to give it new life, and fresh souls to burn for fuel.

sections.title: Metal constructs
sections.body:
The Forge Lords could breathe life into metal through clever workmanship, spirit-craft, and magic. Many of these wonders remain, though only a few still function and those might function poorly.

Pick or roll a purpose and structure, the source of its animation, and its attribute, size, and condition. Allow these elements to inform its physical form and construction.

sections.title: 1d12 purpose
sections.body:
- 1-2 Defense/violence/murder 3-4 Labor/service/convenience
- 5-6 Beauty/decoration/aesthetics
- 7-8 Novelty/practice/experimentation
- 9-10 Companionship/diplomacy/business
- 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice, combine

sections.title: 1d12 structure
sections.body:
- 1-3 Clockwork, pistons, springs, gears
- 4-6 Mundane, metal object (pail, mirror, trumpet, sword, plow, oven, chariot, etc.)
- 7-8 Statue, representational (person, beast, creature)
- 9-10 Statue, abstract/stylized (shapes, patterns, etc.)
- 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice, combine

Forge Lord constructs are animated by more than biology (+4 HP). Most have Armor 3 (made of metal) and lack vital organs (another +3 HP, +1 Armor). Clockwork constructs do have vital organs (basically), and can have Armor as low as 1.

sections.title: 1d12 animation
sections.body:
- 1-2 **[[46 - The Things Below#**Unnatural phenomena**|Red crystal]]**
- 3-4 A spirit, possessing it
- 5-6 Fire/heat, harnessed by runes
- 7-8 Energy stored in **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**Aetherium**|aetherium]]**/**[[43 - Stone Lords#Makerglass|makerglass]]**
- 9-10 A spark of its creator's power
- 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice, combine

sections.title: 1d12 attribute
sections.body:
- 1 Nothing of note, just animated metal
- 2 Beautiful/musical/sublime
- 3 Terrifying/unnerving/imposing
- 4 Strong/durable/powerful
- 5 Perceptive/wise/cunning/devious
- 6 Fiery/destructive/vicious
- 7 Multipart/group/horde
- 8 Disguised/camouflaged/innocuous
- 9 Fast/agile/mobile/flies
- 10 Attuned to energy/elements/magic
- 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice

Forge Lord constructs are often fireproof. Most are blind, sensing vibrations or the heat emitted from objects and creatures.

sections.title: 1d12 size
sections.body:
- 1 Cat-sized or smaller (*tiny*)
- 2-3 Like a human child (*small*)
- 4-7 Human-sized
- 8-10 Like a horse, a cart, etc. (*large*)
- 11-12 Like an elephant, or bigger (*huge*)

sections.title: 1d12 condition
sections.body:
- 1-2 Sluggish/low power/inert
- 3-4 Rusted/corroded/damaged
- 5-6 Trapped/pinned/buried/bound
- 7-8 Behaving erratically
- 9-10 Roll 1d8 twice
- 11-12 Fully functional

sections.title: Bronze colossus
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *huge*, *construct*, *plodding*, *implacable*

**HP** 31; **Armor** 4 (metal, lacks organs) **Damage** trample d10+5 (*hand*, *close*, *area*, *forceful*) or crushing hands d10+5 (*close*, *reach*, *forceful*, *grabby*) or hurled object d10+5 (*far*, *area*, *forceful*, *reload*)

**Special qualities** fireproof, too big to hurt with most weapons, vulnerable to lightning

**Instinct** to defend its creator's honor

- <sup>ä</sup> Pretend to be "just" another statue
- <sup>ä</sup> Come to life, screeching as it moves
- <sup>ä</sup> Issue a booming challenge in a forgotten tongue
- <sup>ä</sup> Block their path

It stands nearly 60 feet tall, a muscular Forge Lord cast in bronze. Similar statues loom about, each one grand and imposing. This statue, though, is shot through with blueish veins. And it feels like it's watching you. Because it is.

The colossus guards the entrance to its master's domain. If someone approaches honorably, speaking in the Forge Lord's tongue, it politely tells them to come again later, for its master is away. Intruders, blowhards, and thieves are dealt with forcibly.

**Something interesting:** Those blueish veins are **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**Aetherium**|aetherium]]**, and likely what animates the statue. Which, alas, means there's no single weak-point. Maybe you could harm this thing by drawing the lightning from it.

**Something useful:** Or you could, you know, just blast it with lightning and overload it.

sections.title: Clockwork spider
sections.body:
*Horde*, *small*, *construct*, *creepy* **HP** 7; **Armor** 2 (metal), 0 vs. crushing **Damage** many little pincers d4 (*hand*, *grabby*)

**Special qualities** fireproof, blind, tremorsense, active only for short periods

**Instinct** to get in the way

- <sup>ä</sup> Skitter towards them, ominously
- <sup>ä</sup> "Watch" intently, responding subtly to every move
- <sup>ä</sup> Try to take things from their hands
- <sup>ä</sup> Defend itself if attacked
- <sup>ä</sup> Follow orders given in the Forge Lord's tongue, poorly

They're not really shaped like spiders, but that's what they make you think of. Picture a metal basket, holding gears and springs and a glowing central core. Articulated legs extend out, each ending in a little 3-clawed pincer. They squeak as they move, their many joints needing oil.

They were made to be little servants, extra sets of hands to help around a house or forge. Even now, they're just trying to help.

sections.title: Iron hound
sections.body:
*Horde*, *construct*, *undead*, *corrupted*, *tireless*, *terrifying*

**HP** 10; **Armor** 4 (metal, lacks organs) **Damage** bite d6+2 (*hand*, *grabby*) **Special qualities** fireproof, heat-sensing **Instinct** to punish and torment

- <sup>ä</sup> Follow the trail of its master's property
- <sup>ä</sup> Fix its prey with a soul-chilling stare
- <sup>ä</sup> Once it's bitten down: crush bone, shred muscle, tear apart (+*messy*, 3 piercing)

There was a Forge Lord who prized his hounds as much as he prized his treasure. When one would die, he'd capture its spirit in **[[46 - The Things Below#**Unnatural phenomena**|red crystal]]** and set them as the eyes in a crudely-wrought effigy. If anyone would be so foolish as to steal from him, he would loose his iron hounds to punish not just the thief but all whose hands had sullied his property.
