That Forge Lord is long gone, but his iron hounds remain. They've learned to leave their master's treasures lying about in plain sight, and to give thieves a nice long head start. For the more hands touch the stolen goods, the more victims they can claim.

sections.title: Singing sword
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *small*, *construct*, *lonely*, *dutiful* **HP** 19; **Armor** 4 (quick, made of metal) **Damage** cut, thrust, slash d10 w/advantage (*hand*, *messy*, 2 piercing)

**Special qualities** flies, fireproof, blind, heat-sense, bound to its hall

**Instinct** to defend its hall, to ease its loneliness

- <sup>ä</sup> Make a strange, ethereal song
- <sup>ä</sup> Strike a warning blow, deft but nonlethal
- <sup>ä</sup> Respond enthusiastically to music
- <sup>ä</sup> Block their path with a razor's edge

This naked blade needs no wielder. It dances about the air, making music as it moves: every bob a hum; every slice a whistle.

It was set here long ago, as guardian to this crumbling hall. For ages it has kept its vigil. Yet, this is not some mindless thing waiting to cut down foes. It remembers and yearns for the days in which this hall was filled with light and laughter and—joy of joys—music!

And so it waits. In the dark. Singing songs to itself that have not been heard by any living mortal ear. It waits, dutiful, and worries that it has misremembered the music it once knew. It waits. In the dark. Alone.

sections.title: Draventao
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *hoarder*, *paranoid*, *terrifying*, *fierce*

**HP** 22; **Armor** 4 (scales, steel plates) **Damage** bite d12+3 w/ advantage (*close*, *reach*, *forceful*, *messy*, *grabby*, 1 piercing) **Special qualities** fireproof, bestial intellect,

**Instinct** to protect itself and its hoard, from threats real or imagined

slumbers for years at a time

- <sup>ä</sup> Lure or drive intruders into a trap
- <sup>ä</sup> Attack with sudden, terrible ferocity that makes even stout hearts falter
- <sup>ä</sup> Suck in air, stoking its inner flames
- <sup>ä</sup> Breath sticky fire, d10+3 damage (*near*, *area*, *grabby*, *messy*, *reload*, ignores armor)

Picture a long and sinuous beast with serpentine scales, curled upon a pile of treasure, covered in a layer of long-cold soot or ash. Bits of rusted steel protrude from its flesh. Smoke drifts lazily from its nostrils. Charred and jagged teeth poke out from its sneering lips. An eye snaps open. It fixes you with a glare like a red-hot coal.

Hard to believe that this thing, this beast, was once a Maker. But it was. It was.

A draventao is what remains of a Forge Lord. It survived the **[[55 - Appendix A - Ages of the World#Time of Cataclysm|Time of Cataclysm]]**, when its rivals moved against it, when its allies betrayed it, when even its loved ones came for it in the dark. It survived by Changing, by letting its inner fire burn until its heart was a blackened coal of wrath. It shed its once-beautiful form. It forced its servants to bolt living metal onto its now-twisted flesh and bones. Then it ate those servants.

Now, mostly, it sleeps. For years at a time, it sleeps. It broods upon its treasure heap, loathe to leave its secret, trap-strewn lair. Only hunger draws it forth. Hunger, or vengeance to be had upon some brazen thief.

Pick or roll its particular strength, its metal augmentation, and what it remembers.

sections.title: 1d6 particular strength
sections.body:
- 1 A steel-rending bite (3 piercing)
- 2 Great size (*huge*, +4 HP, +2 damage)
- 3 A glance that befuddles (*reach*, *area*)
- 4 Breath that poisons as well as burns
- 5 Blood that burns like lit naphtha, d10+1 damage (*hand*, *area*)
- 6 A venomous stinger in its tail, d12+3 damage (*reach*, *forceful*, 1 piercing, weakens or paralyzes)

sections.title: 1d6 augmentation
sections.body:
- 1 Aetherium wings (Special quality +flies)
- 2 Clockwork claw with sword-like talons, 1d10+5 damage (*hand*, *close*, *messy*, *forceful*,  1 piercing)
- 3 Overlapping plates, protecting its belly (Armor  5 instead of 4)
- 4 Nasty spikes all over, d12+3 damage (*close*, *area*, *forceful*, *messy*,  1 piercing)
- 5 Implanted bellows, stoking its breath (+2 damage)
- 6 Roll twice

sections.title: 1d6 what it remembers
sections.body:
- 1 A word that makes water boil and metal glow red hot (*near*, *reload*)
- 2 The bosom friend who betrayed it
- 3 The child it could not save
- 4 The lover who died to protect it
- 5 The name by which others called it
- 6 The lullaby its mother sang to it

sections.title: The Frozen Wastes
sections.body:
North of the **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#The Whitefang Mountains|Whitefang Mountains]]** lies a vast steppe, a windswept sea of shortgrass, sedge, and lichen. No trees grow here. The landscape is broken only by rocky outcrops, low moraines, and kettle lakes. It rarely snows and almost never rains, but hail is somewhat common.

Caribou and mammoths graze the plateau, preyed on by wolves and saber-toothed cats and terrible things that crawl forth from the lakes in summer. Bands of mammoth herders roam in warmer months. But for the most part, it's an empty and forlorn place.

Farther north, the plateau gets even colder, even windier, the steppe giving way to tundra. Eventually, a mountain-like wall of **[[38 - Rime Lords#Dark ice|dark ice]]** blocks the way, stretching across the horizon. The glacier is the final working of the **[[38 - Rime Lords#Rime Lords|Rime Lords]]**. It seals a rift in the world, and keeps the worst of the **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Shantyara**|Howling Curse]]**) at bay. Mammoth herders come here to quarry dark ice, slowly chipping the glacier away.

sections.title: Always
sections.body:
- A landscape so wide and empty you can see the horizon curve
- The constant wind, whispering and howling, tugging and lashing
- Patterns forming in the windswept grass or snow, or the growth of flowers, like runes that fade just before you can read them
- The swell of anger, the urge to inflict violence on your companions, as your temper and patience fray

sections.title: Spring
sections.body:
- Yellow and brown grass, splashed with pale violet flowers
- Fields of standing water, just inches deep, reflecting the empty sky
- Squelch and crackle of half-frozen soil underfoot

sections.title: Summer
sections.body:
- Days that last forever, the sun never truly setting
- Mix of bright green grass, red lichen, white flowers
- Swarms of insects, spawned from muddy soil
- Birds and **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**1d6** instinct/moves|ice drakes]]** pecking at the soil or flocking to the sky

sections.title: Autumn
sections.body:
- The world turned red and orange and golden
- Long shadows in the low, low sun
- Sharp cold edge to the wind
- The restless foraging of small animals

sections.title: Winter
sections.body:
- Endless night, the sun barely rising at noon
- Thin carpet of frost and snow, shrubs and dead grass poking out
- The ceaseless, gnawing wind and cold
- The sense that you've made a terrible mistake in coming here

sections.title: Traversing the Wastes
sections.body:
The Frozen Wastes are inhospitable in a way that southerners can barely comprehend. On any expedition into the wastes:

- You need a knowledgeable guide, like a **[[19 - The Frozen Wastes#**Dangers**|mammoth herder]]**, who knows the ways of this place
- You need lots of firewood or dried dung, for you'll find no fuel and even in the summer the "nights" can be freezing
- The way will be grueling (from cold and mud and bugs); you risk exhausting yourselves/your supplies
- You need to watch out for predators, like **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Raselbaedd**|wolves]]** or mchebra-gris (**kleztigrs**, basically, see [[42 - The Steplands#1d6 natural beast or spirit|The Steplands]])
- You need to watch out for kettle lakes, easily mistaken for ice or shallow standing water
- You risk succumbing to the **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Shantyara**|Howling Curse]]**, which taints much of the Wastes

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- What precious flower grows only in the Frozen Wastes, blooming for a few days each summer? Why is it so valuable?
- What do you find most surprising about this place?
- What do you find yourself missing the most, out here in vast and empty space?
- What is \_\_\_ doing, that you find so damned annoying?
- If you had to kill \_\_\_, how would you do it?
- If it came down to it, which of your companions would you eat first?

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- A villain that the PCs want dead flees north, into the Frozen Wastes
- Only one thing can kill the **[[08 - The Crombil#The Crombil|Crombil]]**, and it lies at the bottom of a kettle lake
- The dark ice glacier that contains the **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Shantyara**|Howling Curse]]** has begun to melt and recede

sections.title: Terrain
sections.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

sections.title: 1d12 terrain
sections.body:
- 1 Kettle lake, small but dozens or hundreds of feet deep
- 2-3 Stretch of shallow standing water/ice
- 4-6 Wide open expanse, just grass and shrub for miles
- 7-8 Large patch of mud/frozen mud
- 9 Gentle slope/low ridge/small rise
- 10 Low moraine, a small hill of broken stones and loose soil
- 11 Rocky outcrop, a dozen feet tall at most
- 12 Single, notable tree

If you like, roll 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

sections.title: Discoveries
sections.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d6).

sections.title: 1d6 discovery
sections.body:
- 1 Change of terrain (previous column) and roll again
- 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.)
- 3 **[[50 - Useful or Valuable Flora#Useful or valuable flora|Useful or valuable flora]]**
- 4 **[[38 - Rime Lords#Dark ice|Dark ice]]**
- 5 A ruin or artifact of the **[[38 - Rime Lords#Rime Lords|Rime Lords]]**
- 6 An encounter (see below)

sections.title: 1d6 encounter
sections.body:
- 1 A **[[19 - The Frozen Wastes#**Dangers**|mammoth herder]]** encampment, now deserted
- 2 Small animals (fox, hares, rodents, birds, etc.)
- 3 A herd of wild **[[19 - The Frozen Wastes#**Dangers**|mammoths]]** or **[[19 - The Frozen Wastes#**Dangers**|caribou]]**, wary, easily spooked/provoked
- 4 A **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Spirits of the wild|spirit of the wild]]** or perhaps a **[[38 - Rime Lords#**Tulpa**|tulpa]]**, curious or indifferent
- 5 **[[19 - The Frozen Wastes#**Dangers**|Mammoth herders]]**, seen in the distance, avoiding you
- 6 **[[19 - The Frozen Wastes#**Dangers**|Mammoth herders]]**, approaching warily

sections.title: Mammoth herders
sections.body:
Here's a typical band:

**Size** *hamlet* (~15-30 souls) **Population** +0

sections.title: Prosperity -1
sections.body:
- Trade with (pick 1 or 2)...
  - ... other mammoth herders
  - ... a farming valley
  - ... a fortress-monastery

sections.title: Resources
sections.body:
- Mammoths (wool, ivory, bone, dung)
- Sleds/sledges of bone, hide, timber
- Hunting (wild mammoths, caribou, hare, fish)
- Gathering (timber, ivory, bone, flint, herbs, **[[38 - Rime Lords#Dark ice|dark ice]]**

sections.title: Defenses +0
sections.body:
- Mammoth-mounted warriors
- Hounds and hunting drakes
- Spears/javelins tipped with dark ice, flint, bone, or bronze (*crude*)

About half of each band are youngsters, with the rest being able-bodied adults. Elders rarely allow themselves to survive long enough to become a burden to the others. A band typically keeps a herd of 10 to 20 adult **[[19 - The Frozen Wastes#**Dangers**|mammoths]]**, plus a few young. They butcher their own beasts only when desperate, keeping them instead for wool, transport, and labor.

A band spends the warmer months on the Frozen Wastes, but spend the colder months sheltering in the caves, ruins, and forests of the **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#The Whitefang Mountains|Whitefang Mountains]]**. They'll trade with **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Questions**|valley farms]]** or **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Monk**|fortress-monasteries]]** in spring, before heading out into the Wastes, or in autumn, before they encamp for the winter.

**Something interesting:** Mammoth herders tend to be impeccably polite, with drawn-out, formal greetings and elaborate, almost artistic ways of doing everything from lacing up a parka to butchering a beast.

**Something useful:** These rituals help identify those afflicted with the **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Shantyara**|Howling Curse]]**, who lack in patience and good grace. Alas, this means that mammoth herders interpret frustration or bad manners as a sign of being accursed.

sections.title: Names
sections.body:
Tibetan-inspired: Bhokhi, Cheyez, Dangyi, Gamyang, Lhing, Jongash, Nayan, Ningo, Nyayelar, Paing, Ramang, Samanz, Sewas, Tawaro, Tsaru, Tsetse, Wasen, Yenphi

**The Frozen Wastes**

They do need to trade something of equal Value. Food, iron, glass, and wool are usually in demand. They have little to no use for finery.

<sup>\*</sup> +1 if sold/traded on to anyone other than mammoth herders

sections.title: Special items
sections.body:
The following might be acquired among mammoth herders, but the PCs must roll Trade & Barter to acquire them. This is in addition to the special items on the Moves & Gear handout.

When PCs *Trade & Barter among a band of mammoth herders*, they use the local Prosperity (-1 by default) with disadvantage because it's a *hamlet*. On a 6-, they don't mark XP—this band simply doesn't have or want what the PCs are after/selling.

| goods                             | value |
|-----------------------------------|-------|
| Pottery                           | 0     |
| Bronze or iron items, of any sort | 1     |

sections.title: Services and favors
sections.body:
PCs don't need to roll Trade & Barter for the following, but they might need to Persuade people—even if they offer something of suitable Value. If the PCs are well-regarded by a band, they might get these boons for free, or without needing to Persuade anyone, or at a reduced price.

| service/favor                              | value |
|--------------------------------------------|-------|
| A few nights of hospitalilty at their camp | 0     |
| Labor for a day or two                     | 1     |
| Accompanying the PCs to a place…           |       |
| they were heading towards anyhow           | 0     |
| a few days out of their way                | 1     |
| they have no business going                | 2     |
| that they know to be cursed                | 3     |

warmer climes

sections.title: Hazards
sections.body:
- Brutal cold and ceaseless wind, leading to **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Ice drake**|hypothermia & frostbite]]**
- Hail storms—annoying, pelting (d4 damage), smashing (d6 damage)
- Mud—sucking, cold, exhausting
- Slippery footing
- Still, reflective water hiding a sudden, plunging drop into a kettle
- In summer: swarms of midges/ mosquitos—annoying, distracting, painful, diseased
- Getting lost or misjudging time in the vast featureless terrain
- **[[46 - The Things Below#**Voice of the Eternal Maw**|Whispers and visions]]**, pushing people towards bloodshed, cruelty, and savagery
- Places tainted by the **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Shantyara**|Howling Curse]]**

sections.title: Monsters
sections.body:
- **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**1d6** instinct/moves|Ice drakes]]**, especially in warmer months
- **[[42 - The Steplands#1d6 natural beast or spirit|Kleztigrs]]**, called "mchebragris" in the local tongue
- **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Raselbaedd**|Wolves]]**
- A **[[14 - The Flats#**Frythanc**|frythanc]]**, nesting in the Whitefangs and preying on caribou or even mammoths
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Wraiths]]** of those who died of starvation and want, or corrupted by the **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Shantyara**|Howling Curse]]**
- **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Spirits of the wild|Spirits of the wild]]**
- **[[38 - Rime Lords#**Tulpa**|Tulpas]]**, free-roaming or bound to something nearby
- **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Shantyara**|Kyakaara]]**, perhaps what remains of a corrupted band of mammoth herders

- herd first ä Greet someone from a distance, politely and formally

sections.title: Gods and religion
sections.body:
The village of Stonetop maintains a pavilion of the gods, an open-air structure with shrines to four main gods:

- **Aratis**, the Lawkeeper [[03 - Aratis, the Lawkeeper|Aratis, the Lawkeeper]]
- **Danu**, the Earth Mother [[09 - Danu, the Earth Mother|Danu, the Earth Mother]]
- **Helior**, the Daybringer [[25 - Helior, the Daybringer|Helior, the Daybringer]]
- **Tor**, the Rain-maker [[49 - Tor, the Rain-maker|Tor, the Rain-maker]], the Thunderhead, Slayer-of-Beasts

Tor is the most popular god in Stonetop, and associated with the **[[02 - The Village of Stonetop#**The village of Stonetop**|Stone]]** itself, but all four gods receive their proper respect. Depending on the PCs in will feature more prominently in a game

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- Who tends the pavilion of the gods?
- Are Stonetop's gods part of a coherent pantheon? Or a mix of gods from different cultures?
- What little gods are revered by your family/in Stonetop/where you grew up?
- What other gods are known in Stonetop? Do any have shrines in the pavilion? Which god, if any, do you consider

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- Someone vandalizes one/all of the shrines in the pavilion.
- The devotee of a foreign god arrives in Stonetop, preaching strange rites.
- A saint or prophet of a new or forgotten god arises in **[[32 - Marshedge#Marshedge|Marshedge]]** or **[[22 - Gordin's Delve#Gordin's Delve|Gordin's Delve]]**, upsetting the balance of powers there.
- Someone offends their household god(s); misfortune ensues.

sections.title: True nature
Divine intervention
sections.body:
Gods shape the world in subtle ways: through synchronicity, dreams, and omens, or by acting in the spirit world. Even Tor, who sometimes walks the world, limits himself to testing mortals and bestowing gifts and curses.

Every so often, though, a god will touch a mortal and imbue them with divine power. They then send that mortal forth as their instrument in the world. Such mortals become prophets or saints, heroes or villains, and help shape the world for generations to come. If you have the Blessed, the Judge, the Lightbearer, or the Storm-marked Heavy among your PCs, they might well be one of these god-touched mortals.

Should the PCs wish a god to intervene on their behalf, they can **petition** them (see next page). Should the PCs offend a god, or oppose one, or otherwise draw its ire, write that god up as a threat (a *magical entity*).

sections.title: Religion
sections.body:
For most people in the World's End, religion is not about faith or morality. It's a practical matter. The gods *are*, and they influence the world. You perform rites and make offerings in order to gain their favor and avert their wrath. Even priests and the like aren't necessarily devoted to the gods they serve; they just know what the gods expect and how to read their signs.

There are, however, some mortals who feel a profound connection to their god(s). Such devotees give offerings gladly. They aspire to emulate their god(s), to heed their teachings and spread their glory.

Devotees might form fellowships with the like-minded, which in time can become institutions: formal priesthoods, mystery cults, temples, and the like. Such institutions can be powerful forces in the world. They might preserve knowledge, legitimize leaders, protect innocents, or otherwise stabilize societies. But they might also grow corrupt, fat on power and privilege, run by cynics rather than true devotees.

sections.title: Petitioning the gods
sections.body:
Religion is an expected part of daily life. You help in the fields, you clean your house, and you give the gods their due. There's no extra benefit for performing the usual rites or offerings. But if you fail to do them, you invite disapproval from your neighbors and misfortune from the gods.

PCs can seek the favor of the gods in order to **[[15 - Homefront#**Player moves**|Bolster]]**. This involves time and effort, rites and offerings above and beyond what's typical and expected. Be sure to ask the PC for details! What god(s) do they petition? What sort of aid do they seek? What offerings do they make? Consider, too, the consequences. What other work goes undone? How do NPCs react? Etc.

Should a PC seek a specific boon from the gods—a curse lifted, an injury healed, a curse laid upon a foe, an answer or an insight, etc.—then Make a Plan! Some example requirements include:

 You must learn the proper rites/offerings You must obtain a specific sacred object You'll need the help of a particular spirit You'll need to wait for an auspicious date/time You must travel to a particular sacred site The rites will take hours/day/weeks You must make an appropriate sacrifice (Value 2 at least) You risk angering the god(s)

Choose requirements based on the PC's request, the god(s) they petition and how they are typically worshipped, the PC's relationship to the god(s), and how active the gods are in your particular game.

sections.title: Little gods
sections.body:
These are the gods of households, of specific places or geographic features, of particular chores or crafts or trades. Many are just **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Elemental spirits**|elemental spirits]]** worshipped at a **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Sacred sites|sacred site]]**. But others are spirits closely tied to humanity.

Little gods mostly just do their thing, quietly watching over their people or places, keeping things running or causing trouble, as is their wont. A little god can:

- <sup>ä</sup> Reveal what it knows, from its own perspective, to those who can speak to it
- <sup>ä</sup> Calm/annoy/frighten animals or children
- <sup>ä</sup> Move/hide/reveal an unattended item
- <sup>ä</sup> Express displeasure through accidents, misfortunes, neglect
- <sup>ä</sup> Fend off other spirits

Little gods manifest only to small children or at the beck and call of someone like a Blessed or **[[26 - Hillfolk#Upland horse|spirit-talker]]**. When they do manifest, treat them more like NPCs than monsters.

Here are some example little gods. Feel free to modify them, expand on them, and ask the PCs questions about them.

sections.title: Ancestral spirits
sections.body: *Group*, *spirit*, *protective*, *conservative* **Instinct** to protect their family's honor. **Manifests** in dreams, as drafty whispers. **Expects** an altar, prayers, incense/libations. **Notes** Like grandparents who don't quite get you.

sections.title: Garden spirits
God of the Cistern
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *spirit*, *gloomy*, *patient*

**Instinct** to keep secrets.

**Manifests** as an echo in the dark, as a misty figure.

**Expects** water, snow, coins, wishes. **Notes** Desperately wants to know how

various tales end(ed).

sections.title: God of weaving
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *spirit*, *exacting*, *insightful* **Instinct** to reward diligence, to punish perfectionism.

**Manifests** as a spider, as idle thoughts. **Expects** one perfect bit of fabric to dwell in, at least one minor flaw in everything else. **Notes** A trickster (tangles yarn, snaps your warp), sometimes a wise councilor.

sections.title: God of whisky
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *spirit*, *secretive*, *grandiose* **Instinct** to take its sweet time.

**Manifests** as a vaporous figure. **Expects** the head & tail of each batch. **Notes** Passionate about flavors, smells.

sections.title: Hearth spirit
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *spirit*, *friendly*, *nurturing* **Instinct** to belong.

**Manifests** as pops & whispers, as an impish little person.

**Expects** warm greetings, friendly banter, little offerings cast into the flames.

**Notes** Natters on quietly even if no one's present.

sections.title: Household spirits
sections.body:
*Group*, *spirit*, *helpful*, *mischievous*

**Instinct** to easily take offense.

**Manifests** as gnomish little folk.

**Expects** bowls of goat's milk, bits of bread. **Notes** Complain loudly to each other, grumble about leaving if things don't improve.

sections.title: Threshold spirit
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *spirit*, *protective*, *vigilant*

**Instinct** to dislike outsiders.

**Manifests** as a grumpy little person. **Expects** sprinkles of salt, poured-out whisky, reverent touches.

**Notes** Crosses arms, puffs up, harrumphs.

See also the following **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Elemental spirits**|elemental spirits]]**361):

- Beast wardens (for livestock)
- Fertility spirits
- Meadow/prairie/grass spirits (for the fields)
- Rock/stone spirits (for the Stone or the Ringwall)
- Stream/creek spirit (for the Stream).

sections.title: Other gods
sections.body: To create a new god, choose or roll for 1 or 2 themes, who they are relevant to, their standing, and up to 3 observances.

sections.title: 1d12 theme
sections.body:
- 1 Thresholds/change/trickery/chaos
- 2 Competition/violence/murder/ warfare
- 3 Moon/stars/night/sleep/dreams/ revelation
- 4 Skill/crafts/learning/writing/wisdom
- 5 Travel/trade/navigation/enterprise
- 6 Luck/prosperity/fortune/fate
- 7 Hearth/home/marriage/family/ community/civilization
- 8 Fertility/birth/vigor/sickness/death
- 9 Agriculture/hunting/seasons/ weather
- 10 Music/art/beauty/passion/revelry
- 11 Bravery/justice/oaths/protection
- 12 Fire/rebirth/hope/light/sun

sections.title: 1d12 relevant to
sections.body:
- 1-2 A specific household/family
- 3-4 A particular village/band/clan
- 5-6 A minority within a larger people (e.g. Manmarcher smiths)
- 7-8 A whole people (e.g. Manmarchers)
- 9-10 A minority across multiple peoples (e.g. thieves everywhere)
- 11-12 Many different peoples

A deity who is relevant to multiple peoples might have different standing and observances among each.

sections.title: 1d12 standing
sections.body:
- 1-2 Obscure, forgotten, misunderstood
- 3-5 Avoided, propitiated, appeased
- 6 Revered in secret
- 7-9 Invoked only when needed
- 10-11 Given due respect and honor
- 12 Adored, praised, heaped with gratitude

sections.title: 1d12 observance
sections.body:
- 1 Charity/the care of \_\_/ the cultivation of \_\_
- 2 Dancing/intoxication/ ecstatic practice
- 3 Divination/augury/prophecy
- 4 Elaborate prayers/ceremonies/ gatherings
- 5 Fashioning of icons/symbols/ monuments
- 6 Fasting/ascetic denial/extreme endurance
- 7 Libations/offerings of material wealth (burnt, buried, etc.)
- 8 Pilgrimage/hermitage
- 9 Sacrifice (animal/blood/human)
- 10 Taboo/proscribed behaviors
- 11-12 Roll 1d10 again, twice, but one of them is done in secret

From there, envision the god's specific sphere of influence, how it is depicted, its rites, its myths, and its **[[20 - Gods and Religion#**1d12** observance|true nature]]**.

To name your god, use one of these methods:

- Pick a word associated with the god, translate it into an appropriate language, then change it a little ("threshold" >> "trothwy" in Welsh >> "Troth")
- Pick a deity from actual mythology or popular culture, and change a letter or two ("Thor" >> "Tor").

Then give them one or more titles, kennings, or honorifics to reflect their portfolio. Minor gods might have only a title; "She Who Threads the Needle" might go by no other name than that.

If this god will cause ongoing trouble for the PCs or the village, write it up as a threat (a *magical entity*).

*[[47 - Three Coven Lake#Three Coven Lake|The PCs are approaching Three Coven Lake]]**[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk|, and I've decided that a group of Hillfolk]]* *called The White Hand patrol the area. Their god tells them to keep outsiders away and to keep the horrors of the lake at bay. I consider having them worship Helior, but decide to make up a new god instead.*

*I start by picking a theme of "bravery/justice/oaths/protection," but also roll a second theme for flavor. I get a 12, focusing on "fire/rebirth". Then I roll a 6 for relevance ("minority within a larger people"), an 8 for standing ('invoked only when needed"), and 8 for observances ("pilgrimage/hermitage"). I could use a bit more, so I roll another observance: 5 ("fashioning of monuments"). I look at the possible true natures, ponder a bit, and settle on this:*

*The White Hand worship Gyndorn of the Cleansing Flame, a hero of the Hillfolk who led an uprising against the Barrow Builders* [[05 - Barrow Builders|Barrow Builders]]*, and whose name the Hillfolk invoke when facing unholy evil.* 

*Gyndorn charged her followers with eternal vigilance. She calls warriors and spirit-talkers from all tribes to pass through a sacred flame and be reborn as members of the White Hand. Members of the sect follow a circuit around the lakes that Gyndorn took herself, marking certain landmarks with white handprints each time they pass. And each band in the sect carries a consecrated flame with them as they go.*

sections.title: The Golden Oak
sections.body:
**The Golden Oak**

On a clear day, sharp-eyed folk in Stonetop can spot the Golden Oak, gleaming at the horizon and dwarfing the trees around it. According to the tales of the **[[17 - Forest Folk#Forest Folk|Forest Folk]]**, a hero buried at the roots of the Oak can find their way back from beyond the **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Questions**|Last Door]]**. But no one from Stonetop has ever made it there and back.

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- When was Stonetop's last expedition to the Golden Oak? Who were they trying to save?
- What other tales have you heard about the Oak? How much of them do you believe?
- Who would have to die for you to risk a journey to the Golden Oak?

sections.title: Getting there
sections.body:
**Something interesting:** The Golden Oak doesn't quite exist in this world, at least not anymore. It can only be reached through Fae pathways, at great risk and personal cost.

When you *travel towards the Golden Oak in the mundane world*, you never reach it. Even if you were to fly above the trees, it would remain always out of reach, its bearing shifting.

**Something useful:** To reach the Golden Oak, you must…

- learn to walk the Fae paths (see **[[53 - Waystones#Waystones|way stones]]**);
- follow the Fae paths to a specific set of waystones, those closest to the Oak;
- find a secret crossing and face its guardian, set there by the **Lady of Crows** herself [[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death's Door**|Death and the Undying]]; and
- pay the Lady's due to make the crossing.

When you *pay the Lady's due*, choose 1 as a group:

- Present a marker of the **Pale Hunter**'s favor [[35 - The Pale Hunter|The Pale Hunter]], honestly earned
- Each of you must make a sacrifice. Cross off a move you possess; it is gone and you cannot regain it. (Followers cross off a tag of the GM's choice instead.)
- One of your number passes willingly through the Last Door, their body gone, never to be seen again.

When you *make the crossing*, you glimpse the Last Door and what lies beyond. Tell

**The Golden Oak**

that tells you that something is making you more angry, savage, and violent?

sections.title: The savage curse
sections.body:
This was once a hub of the **Green Lords**' civilization [[24 - Green Lords#Names|Names]]. When the **[[12 - Fae#Fae|Fae]]** rebelled and turned on their masters, they claimed it for themselves. The surviving Lords laid a curse of primal savagery on the place. The rebel Fae abandoned it and the **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death's Door**|Lady of Crows]]** sealed it away.

When you *are under the vast canopy of the Golden Oak*…

- … and you **Persuade using anything but violence or threats**, the best you can get is a 7-9.
- … and you **give in to the savagery in your heart**, regain 2d8 HP and become larger, more muscular, more bestial. Gain advantage when you roll STR and disadvantage when you roll WIS until you leave this place.
- … and you **fight with no mercy, hesitation, or remorse**, roll damage with advantage.
- … and you **seek to make something, heal someone, or repair something**, roll +WIS: **on a 10+**, you can work as long

us what you see.

sections.title: Setting the scene
sections.body:
The Golden Oak itself is enormous, so big as to defy any sense of scale. The bark is pale and luminous, and the glittering canopy is distant as the sky.

- What detail do you notice that drives home the fact that everything here is huge and massive?
- What here tells you this is a place of primal, unchecked vitality?
- What do you notice about yourself,

as you keep focused on your task; **on a 7-9**, you make some progress, but are easily distracted, frustrated, or enraged.

sections.title: Dangers
sections.body: Pick 1, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 danger
sections.body:
- 1 Enormous vermin—dog-sized, mansized, horse-sized, or worse—each with some uncanny trait or mutation
- 2 Gargantuan beasts, crashing about you, disinterested and unconcerned
- 3 **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Specters]]** of betrayed Green Lords, like roving storms of hate
- 4 **[[12 - Fae#**The Bloody Boots**|Hollow Fae]]**, trapped here for ages, mad with soul-hunger
- 5 Pockets of dangerous old magic, ready to ensnare, rend, or mutate

sections.title: 1d6 danger
sections.body: 6 An overwhelming urge to fight, to hurt, to kill. If they act on the impulse, they mark XP. If they resist, they are Defying Danger (probably with WIS).

sections.title: Discoveries
sections.body: Pick 1, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 discovery
sections.body:
- 1 A small pocket of safety: a cave, an old ruin, a hollow in a massive tree
- 2 The remains of a Green Lord or a rebel warrior, strangely well preserved
- 3 A dead beast: enormous, half-eaten, teeming with vermin
- 4 **[[50 - Useful or Valuable Flora#Useful or valuable flora|Useful or valuable flora]]**, growing in preposterous proportions
- 5 A mad hermit, trapped here and lost in time (perhaps from a previous expedition?)
- 6 An artifact of... (roll d12)
  - 1-7 The **[[24 - Green Lords#Names|Green Lords]]**
  - 8-11 The **[[12 - Fae#Fae|Fae]]**
  - 12 The **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**

sections.title: Treasures of the Golden Oak
sections.body:
- A living leaf from the Golden Oak can be made into a **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#Goldleaf Cloak|Goldleaf Cloak]]**.
- With proper tools and knowledge, the bark can be harvested to craft weapons or armor (2 armor, *warm*, *cumbersome*) that is hard as iron and bane to undead or things from beyond the Last Door.
- A golden acorn, harvested in autumn, can be planted elsewhere to grow a **Golden Sapling** (see sidebar).

sections.title: Buried at the roots
sections.body:
When you *bury a corpse at the roots of the Golden Oak*, close to the trunk, roll and add…

- … +1 if the corpse has not yet started to rot;
- … +1 if they died bravely in their prime; and
- … +1 if their name is still well-known.

**On a 10+**, they return from beyond the Last Door when the sun next rises, whole and healthy though likely shaken; **on a 7-9**, they do not yet rise, but maybe if you give it another night? **On a 6-**, they return, but something else comes with them, something dangerous and dark, something now tied to them in a deep and primal way.

sections.title: GOLDEN SAPLING
sections.body:
Requires all of the following, in order:

- Retrieve an acorn from the boughs of the Golden Oak in late autumn
- Plant the acorn in the fields near Stonetop, watering it with the lifeblood of a great virile beast, like an aurochs bull
- Nurture and protect the sapling through 4 years of growth

When you *mark all the requirements*, add "Golden Sapling" to the Resources list and automatically mark the **Greater Harvest** improvement.

Henceforth, when the steading generates Surplus, even just 1, it generates +1 Surplus.

sections.title: Gordin's Delve
sections.body: **Size** *town* (~700 to 800 souls)

sections.title: Prosperity +0
sections.body:
- Trade with... ... Marshedge (wheat, flax, rope, textiles, herbs, glass)
    - ... Stonetop (barley, potatoes, furs, whisky)

sections.title: Resources
sections.body:
- Mining (iron, copper, lead, tin, silver)
- Trades (smithing, tinkering)
- Timber
- Goats
- Aqueduct

sections.title: Defenses -1
sections.body:
- Maker-ramparts
- Unassailable position

For centuries, it was just a pile of **Forge Lord** ruins [[18 - Forge Lords|Forge Lords]] at the end of the **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#The Makers' Roads|West Road]]**, up in the **[[27 - Huffel Peaks#Huffel Peaks|Huffel Peaks]]**. A few generations back, ambitious folk started hauling ore out of the Delves. Foundry got built, merchants showed up, and coin flowed in—along with a constant trickle of folks seeking opportunity, or with nowhere else to go.

There's no town council, not even a militia to speak of. And unlike Stonetop, there ain't much sense of community. Some folks got friends, and allies, and reputations to lean on. But when it comes right down to it, sure seems like everyone's only looking out for themselves.

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- When was the last round of violence between the Delvers and the Hillfolk? What did it cost you, personally?
- Which of you have been here before? When? Why?
- Who up here should you never, ever cross?
- Who's the merchant who trades with Stonetop the most?
- Who left Stonetop to come up here? What's the last you've heard of them?
- What are they said to eat up here, besides goats and imported grain?
- According to the stories you've heard, who the hell was Gordin?

sections.title: Places
sections.body:
- **The slopes near town**, all sludge and mud and tree stumps, mining camps and goat herds
- **Sorrow's Gate**, where the West Road ends, a sprawl of tents, no permanent structures on the pave stones
- **The Arms**, two ridges that cradle the town, Maker-ruins clinging to their sides
- **Foundry**, a brick slab squatted at the town's foot, belching smoke and slurry
- **The ancient aqueduct**, still in use, feeding Foundry and turning its waterwheel
- **The Terraces**, seven of them, crowded with shops, stables, tenements, alleys, and people. Make-do construction lashed to humbling ruins
- **The Swap**, an open-air market for labor, second-hand goods, questionable food or pleasures, and the odd Delve-finds
- **A half-dozen walled-off compounds**, claiming the best spots, built into Maker-ruins
- **The three Delves**, gaping tunnels into the dark, miners coming and going

sections.title: People
sections.body: No one is from here originally. Even folks born here, their parents or grandparents came from somewhere else. For any NPC, pick or roll where they're from and what brought them. Pick a name based on their origins, maybe with an epithet like "the Knife," "Gaptooth," "Limper," "Boils," "the Yellow," or "Go-Between."

sections.title: 1d12 where they're from
sections.body:
- 1-2 Lygos, or other points south
- 3-5 Marshedge
- 6-7 The Manmarch
- 8 Stonetop
- 9 The Steplands (Hillfolk)
- 10 Barrier Pass (or beyond)
- 11-12 Grew up here; reroll for parents

sections.title: 1d12 what brought them
sections.body:
- 1-3 Seeking their fortune
- 4-5 To join family/friends
- 6-9 A bad turn, nowhere else to go
- 10 On the run
- 11 Happenstance, fate, dumb luck
- 12 Dragged here against their will

**The Bosses** each run big parts of the local economy; everyone in town relies on what they control. Their positions are far from stable, and when one topples, there's turmoil. Each is attended by 10-15 family, servants, and retainers.

- Mutra the Teeth (mines, timber)
- Odo Thriceborn (mines, housing)
- Honest Draigh (trade with Marshedge, food)
- Jahalim of the Keys (Foundry)
- Smiling Ffransis (water, booze)

**Merchants** bring goods and raw material in and out of Gordin's Delve. Most are backed by one of the Bosses, but one or two manage the careful dance of independence. (~10 operations, or ~25 souls total)

**The Keeps** keep the town running, such that it does. They run the trades, the shops, the inns, the boarding houses, the hostelries, the dens of vice. They don't mine the Delves, they mine the miners. And the Bosses mine *them*. (~50 souls, including family and apprentices)

**Miners** make up the bulk of the town. Small crews work what claims they can, usually veins far down the Delves or dotting the slopes. They'll be gone for days on end. The Bosses' crews are bigger, working the best veins on day/night shifts. It's hard work, that barely pays enough to get by. (~250 souls)

**Prospectors** work alone or in small teams, seeking new claims and selling them to mining crews. They also sell Maker artifacts to the Bosses when they can, or else at the Swap for whatever they can get. Prospecting is terribly dangerous, but a way to make good coin fast. (~5-10 souls at any given time)

**Smelters** work at Foundry, processing raw ore into iron, copper, silver, lead, and tin. There's a handful of skilled smiths, too, but mostly it's hard, hot, dangerous work. Pay's decent, though, and you don't have to go down the Delves. (~50 souls, working in shifts)

**Loggers** ply the mountainsides for timber and haul it back to town, for construction or to fuel hearths and Foundry's furnace. The nearby slopes were stripped clean long ago, so nowadays loggers range farther out into the mountains. Hard, dangerous work that takes folks away from town for weeks at a time. (~50 souls)

**The desperate** can't find work or can't keep it, due to injury, infirmity, or temperament. They take what gigs they can, or they scavenge, or they die slowly out near Sorrow's Gate. Some turn to robbery and theft, but unless they're very good, they're beaten or killed by victims, mobs, or other cutthroats. And if you are very good at thuggery? Well, the Bosses probably have work for you. (~100-200 souls, always in flux)

**Beyond that**, another ~100-150 souls do odd jobs: goat herding, butchering, hauling water, cleaning and cooking, working for the Keeps or the Bosses as staff or muscle or entertainment or a combination of all three. They often live with their employers, or their stock.

There are very few full-time homemakers in Gordin's Delve; almost everybody works or hustles a little. Even kids are put to work as soon as possible. It's not a nice place, not at all.

sections.title: Commonly available
sections.body:
The following are special items in Stonetop, but commonly available in Gordin's Delve-PCs can acquire them here without having to roll. They do need to trade something of equal Value. Stonetop trade goods (whisky,  furs, barley, potatoes) are always in demand.

sections.title: Special items
sections.body:
The following might be acquired in Gordin's Delve, in addition to special items from the Moves & Gear handout. PCs must roll Trade & Barter to acquire them.

When PCs Trade & Barter in Gordin's Delve , they use the local Prosperity (+0 by default) with advantage because it's a town . On a 6, they don't mark XP; they must talk to one of the Bosses if they want to acquire/sell it.

| goods | value |
|---|---|
| A lock and key | 1 |
| Silver jewelry | 1 |
| A small silver mirror | 2 |
| An object of black iron [[36 - Primordial Powers#**Primordial powers**\|Primordial Powers]] | +2 |
| An object of orichalcum (see [[18 - Forge Lords#**1d6** architectural elements|Forge Lords]]) | +2 |

| food & lodging | value |
|---|---|
| Humble, cramped apartment to call your own | 2 |
| Modest house or shop | 3 |

sections.title: .   trade opportunities   .
sections.body:
If the PCs get a trade opportunity (from the Seasons Change move), Gordin's Delve might offer the following:

- A good exchange rate, two or even three Value 2 items for 1 Surplus.
- A cartload of timber or metal (Value 2), in exchange for help with a problem.
- A cartload of timber (Value 2) every season for a year, in exchange for 1 Surplus now
- A tradesperson (engineer, foreman, smith, etc.) willing to move to Stonetop for a price
- An arcanum of the Stone Lords [[43 - Stone Lords|Stone Lords]], Forge Lords [[18 - Forge Lords|Forge Lords]], or Things Below [[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|The Things Below]].

sections.title: ASK AROUND
sections.body:
When you spend a purse of coppers and a few hours asking around town , say what you hope to learn and roll +CHA: on a 10+ , you get a clear and useful answer; on a 7-9 , you get an answer but pick 1:

- It's incomplete, contradictory, or unclear, but the GM will say how you could learn more
- It'll cost you another purse of coppers
- You've drawn unwanted attention or gotten yourself embroiled in a situation of the GM's choosing

sections.title: CAROUSE
sections.body:
When you spend a handful of silvers having a good time , roll +CHA: on a 10+ , pick 2; on a 7-9 , pick 1:

- You spread a rumor or tale far and wide
- You make a loyal new friend
- You learn something useful or juicy
- You avoid getting swindled, tricked, or entangled in someone else's trouble

sections.title: RECRUIT
sections.body:
When you put out word that you're hiring , say who you're looking for, what the job is, and what you're offering (the GM will veto if you're being unreasonable). Then roll and add…

- …   +1 if your offer is generous;
- …   +1 if the work conditions aren't terrible;
- …   +1 if you've got a solid reputation hereabouts.

On a 10+ , you find someone, but pick 1; on a 7-9 , you find someone but pick 2.

- They lack some of the skills you wanted
- They suffer from some impairment or vice (GM's choice)
- You draw unwanted attention in the process
- They have debts or obligations that you need to clear up first

| services                                                              | value |
| --------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----- |
| Someone to keep an eye on things for a week                           | 0     |
| A bit of crude, one-sided violence                                    | 0     |
| A guide into the slopes/Delves, for a day                             | 0     |
| A crew of miners/woodcutters, for a week                              | 1     |
| A prospector, to take you to a secret or dangerous part of the Delves | 1     |
| Atrustworthy live-in servant, for a season                            | 1     |
| A bodyguard/muscle, for a few days                                    | 1     |
| A targeted killing, discrete                                          | 1     |
| An engineer/foreman, for a big project                                | 2     |

An assassination, someone important

sections.title: Secrets
sections.body:
- **The Red Door** is a well-known feature down in the **[[22 - Gordin's Delve#**The Delves**|First Delve]]**, a 20-foot tall slab of **[[43 - Stone Lords#Various treasures|makerglass]]** , inlaid with hundreds of patterned tiles. Some are missing, allowing the tiles to be slid around. When the tiles are in the exact right spots, the Red Door will open. Hints to the correct arrangement are hidden in old mosaics throughout Gordin's Delve.
- **An established shopkeep** (like a chandler, a publican, an innkeeper, maybe even a barber?) has a habit of killing folks who won't be missed. They're otherwise a pillar of the community.
- Everyone knows there are **grates in the terraces**, carrying water through chutes and culverts to pour out below Sorrow's Gate. But there's more, a series of cramped tunnels and chutes that connect to some of the old ruins and even the Delves. The sewers are gross, infested with rats or worse, and just big enough to get lost in.
- **A cult of locals** (some influential) have taken to tossing sacrifices into the underground lake at the bottom of the **[[22 - Gordin's Delve#**The Delves**|Second Delve]]**: coin, flesh, living victims. They've received blessings from **[[46 - The Things Below#**For We Are Many**|Daagon]]** as a result.
- Every few years, miners in the **[[22 - Gordin's Delve#**The Delves**|Third Delve]]** will unearth a "door" made of **[[18 - Forge Lords#**1d6** architectural elements|orichalcum]]**. Rarely, they rebury it. Usually, they pry it open, to find nothing but more rock beyond. A powerful earthquake always follows a few days after one of these doors is opened, but few have made the connection.

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- The PCs' favorite merchant is in trouble with one or more Bosses
- An ambitious Keep is angling to become a new Boss
- Miners, smelters, and loggers seek to organize and throw off the Bosses' yoke
- A small mining crew has gone missing; a worried loved one asks for help
- One of the Bosses was just killed by an underling, who's desperately trying to assume their position
- Ustrina offer gold and silver for something unsavory (fresh blood, corpses, slaves, etc.)
- Logging crews have gone missing, and the folks who went to check on them never came back. Are Hillfolk raiders to blame? Bandits? Or something worse?

sections.title: Living conditions
sections.body:
- Hunger, if you can't afford food
- Exposure to **[[27 - Huffel Peaks#Harsh mountain weather|harsh mountain weather]]**, if you can't afford shelter
- Sickness and disease, from close quarters and poor hygiene
- **[[42 - The Steplands#1d6 natural beast or spirit|Mudslides]]**, especially on the slopes
- Workplace accidents (or "accidents")
- General apathy from those who could help

sections.title: Folks from elsewhere
sections.body:
- **[[02 - The Village of Stonetop#The village of Stonetop|Stonetop]]**
- **[[32 - Marshedge#Marshedge|Marshedge]]**
- The **[[34 - North Manmarch#North Manmarch|Manmarch]]**, [[40 - South Manmarch#South Manmarch|350\)]]
- **[[29 - Lygos and the South|Lygos]]** or other points south 
- **[[04 - Barrier Pass#Barrier Pass|Barrier Pass]]** or beyond
- **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk|Hillfolk]]**, cast out by

sections.title: Alley cutthroats
sections.body:
*Group*, *stealthy*, *cautious*, *organized*

**HP** 6; **Armor** 0

**Damage** wicked knife d8 (*hand*)

**Instinct** to prey on the unwary

- ä Pick out a likely mark
- ä Jump someone
- ä Fight dirty
- ä Leave no witnesses

sections.title: Desperate souls
sections.body:
*Group*, *craven*, *meek*, *opportunistic*

**HP** 6; **Armor** 0

**Damage** brick, makeshift club, rusty knife d6 (*hand*, *crude*)

**Instinct** to do what they must

- ä Do something unsavory, if there's food or coin in it
- ä Tell someone what they think they want to hear
- ä Demonstrate their humanity in a small—or sometimes enormous—way

sections.title: Dawa Eyegouger
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *brutal*, *fearless*, *drunkard*

**HP** 16; **Armor** 0

**Damage** knife or fingers d8 (*hand*, *messy*, 1 piercing, advantage)

**Instinct** to get this over with

- ä Ignore pain, doubt
- ä Explode into sudden violence
- ä Fight dirty, mean, without remorse

A goatherder from north of Barrier Pass, with orange irises and too-long fingers (marks of the **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Shantyara**|Howling Curse]]**). She turned up in Gordin's Delve years back, and one of the Bosses put her to work as an enforcer. They keep her sauced and happy, until there's work to be done. Then they dry her out, point out the target, and stand back.

sections.title: Laborers
sections.body:
*Horde*, *hardy*

**HP** 7; **Armor** 0

**Damage** fists, boots d4 (*hand*) or pickax d6 (*close*, *messy*, 1 piercing)

**Instinct** to cling to what they've got

- ä Make a show of toughness, solidarity
- ä Dogpile someone (*grabby*, *forceful*)
- ä Break and run when things go sideways

sections.title: Shoddy construction
sections.body:
Gordin's Delve is a weird mix of piled stone and crude carpentry, lashed onto Maker-ruins that have stood for hundreds of years. It's all very slapdash and precarious.

- <sup>ä</sup> Things creak, sway, and teeter ominously
- <sup>ä</sup> A wall/support/floor gives way, putting someone in danger
- <sup>ä</sup> Someone falls from a height/is buried

sections.title: The Delves
sections.body:
The Arms feature three archways, each over 30 feet tall. Each arch leads to a tunnel of smooth stone that plunges into the mountain, eventually connecting to the **[[28 - The Labyrinth#The Labyrinth|Labyrinth]]**. Side passages, chambers, and complexes branch off the main tunnels—some ancient, some excavated in the last 50 years.

**The First Delve** opens where the two Arms meet. It stretches half a mile from the surface, then becomes a series of switchback tunnels, one after the other, down and down and down. The main tunnel ends with a cave-in, but side passages and chambers connect to the Labyrinth. **Forge Lord** carvings [[18 - Forge Lords|Forge Lords]] decorate the walls, and rich veins of copper and hematite (iron ore) have been found.

**The Second Delve** opens on the righthand slope (facing uphill). The delve goes straight, sloping down for about 300 feet, then turns left at a landing. Another 300 feet, there's another landing, another left turn. The pattern repeats dozens of

times, ending at a ledge that overlooks a subterranean lake. Miners brick up the end of the passage, but their walls rarely stand for long. Ancient side passages spoke outward from this delve; many have led to rich (though inconvenient) copper veins.

**The Third Delve** opens on the lefthand Arm. The main tunnel forks and turns frequently, soon becoming one great big twisting mess of tunnels and chambers. Many of these lower tunnels connect with the Labyrinth. Hematite veins have been found at the end of many such tunnels, and turned into active mines.

sections.title: Impressions
sections.body:
- Vast, arched tunnels, stretching well beyond your torchlight
- Echoing sounds of picks on rock, carts rumbling, voices grumbling, your own footsteps
- That sense of immense, merciless weight above you
- A smooth, rippling texture to the main tunnel walls, less like they were carved and more like they were… melted?
- Constant, warm breeze flowing up, like gentle breath
- Crude, cramped scale of new excavations, propped up with timbers

sections.title: Chance encounters
sections.body: Choose or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 encounter
sections.body:
- 1 Cultist or worshiper of the Things Below [[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|The Things Below]]
- 2 A shady, strange, or desperate character, out of place
- 3-4 Mining crew, or part of one
- 5 Someone alone, taking something to/from a mine
- 6 Prospector, or (1 in 6 chance) some Ustrina [[51 - Ustrina|Ustrina]]

For disposition, choose or have someone roll:

sections.title: 1d6 disposition
sections.body:
- 1 Planning treachery or betrayal
- 2 Aggressively unfriendly, even hostile
- 3 In dire need of assistance
- 4-5 Wary, tight-lipped, suspicious
- 6 Friendly enough

sections.title: Discoveries
sections.body: Pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 discovery
sections.body:
- 1 signs of violence/disaster (below)
- 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.)
- 3 A mine, abandoned or destroyed (perhaps mysteriously)
- 4 Possessions/gear, lost or discarded
- 5 A vein of copper, hematite (iron ore), or galena (silver/lead ore)
- 6 A ruin or artifact of the Forge Lords [[18 - Forge Lords|Forge Lords]]

sections.title: 1d6 signs of violence/disaster
sections.body:
- 1 A scene of wanton carnage
- 2 A dead monster from the Labyrinth [[28 - The Labyrinth|The Labyrinth]], bloody trail leading off
- 3 One or more dead bodies
- 4-5 A recent collapse, possibly with survivors
- 6 Sounds of a fight, or a call for help

sections.title: Dangers
sections.body:
- Utter darkness
- Getting lost
- Pockets of bad air: stale, poisonous, smoke- or dust-filled
- Cave-ins, especially in more recent excavations
- Laborers [[22 - Gordin's Delve#Dangers|Gordin's Delve]], with something to hide or protect
- Ghosts (see [[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Death and the Undying]]), victims of   cave-ins or previous violence
- Traps or wards protecting Forge Lord compounds [[18 - Forge Lords|Forge Lords]]
- Monsters come up from the Labyrinth [[28 - The Labyrinth|The Labyrinth]]

sections.title: The Great Wood
sections.body:
the Great Wood, which stretches hundreds of miles to the east. Few villagers dare to even cross the **[[44 - The Stream#The Stream|Stream]]** that flows near the base of the bluff. Hunters, trappers, and foragers go deeper, but even they venture a day or two into the forest at most.

small streams and dotted with ponds. The trees are a mix of deciduous and evergreen. In some spots, the trees grow tightly, surrounded by thick brush so that one can barely walk. In others, massive old growth trees dominate, the space between them sparse and open.

sections.title: Always
sections.body:
- Massive trunks, reaching up up up, some bigger around than houses
- Pale sky glimpsed through the canopy
- Trees bending, creaking, rustling in the wind
- A skittering in the distance
- A sense of smallness, of being swallowed up by something ancient and vast

sections.title: Spring
sections.body:
- Feet slipping on mud, rotting leaves
- Drip drip dripping of snowmelt, rain, mist
- Pale buds and early blooms, blue and yellow and violet
- Hopeful swell of birdsong

sections.title: Summer
sections.body:
- Brilliant, overwhelming green
- Drone of insects buzzing, birds chirping
- Oppressive heat and humidity

sections.title: Autumn
sections.body:
- A world aflame with reds and golds
- Crunch of needles and leaves underfoot
- Stark long shadows cast by a low sun Sharp smell of cold, damp air

sections.title: Winter
sections.body:
- A frosty coating, a deathly stillness
- Shocking white of snow drifts
- Trees like boney fingers
- Surprising chirp and rustle of finches

sections.title: The Compact
sections.body:
For generations, the people of Stonetop have kept their promise to the **[[17 - Forest Folk#Forest Folk|Forest Folk]]**: they never cut down a living tree in the Great Wood. The villagers gather deadfall, but timber comes from elsewhere.

- According to the elders, when did someone last break the compact? What happened to them?
- Who's been pushing to start harvesting trees, since the Forest Folk seem to have vanished?

What will happen if someone fells a living tree? That's for you to decide. Maybe…

- … they suffer the **[[37 - Red Groves#**Hooks**|Forest's Wrath]]**;
- … they risk releasing an evil spirit, held captive by the tree's roots;
- … the trees house the spirits of dead Forest Folk, and they've just created a **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|specter]]**; or
- … nothing—unless the Forest Folk return.

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- Who in your family used to ply the Wood? How did they lose their nerve?
- What did you see deep in the Wood
- that you've never told to another? Who did you lose to the Wood? How?
- Of all the stories you've heard of the Wood, which one scares you most?

sections.title: Places
sections.body:
- The **[[21 - The Golden Oak#The Golden Oak|Golden Oak]]**
- The **[[33 - The Maw#The Maw|Maw]]**
- **[[37 - Red Groves#Red Groves|Red groves]]**
- The **[[44 - The Stream#The Stream|Stream]]**
- **[[52 - Vor Svetelik#Vor Svetelik|Vor Svetelik]]**

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- A villager wanders off into the Wood for unknown reasons
- A hunter stumbles home, having narrowly escaped some new danger
- A southern scholar arrives in Stonetop, seeking a guide into the Great Wood
- A hunter or trapper who the PCs care

sections.title: GREAT WOOD TIMBER
sections.body:
Requires all of the following:

- Proof that trees can be felled without dire consequence
- A crew of brave lumberjacks
- A skilled carpenter, working full time
- Pulling Together once, over a course of a few weeks, to generate the first load of timber.

When you *mark all the requirements*, increase Fortunes by 1 and add "Timber from the Great Wood" to the Resources list.

Henceforth, the steading has a ready supply of timber for improvements and other projects, and it consumes 1 less Surplus every winter.

sections.title: Terrain
sections.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

sections.title: 1d12 terrain
sections.body:
- 1 Pond, wetland, or lake
- 2 Creek, stream, or river
- 3 Rocky outcropping, cave(s)
- 4-5 Clearing, meadow, sparse trees
- 6-7 Dense thicket
- 8-9 Swath of one type of tree
- 10 Briars, thorns, nettles
- 11 Slope, hill, ridge, ravine
- 12 Single, notable tree

If you like, roll 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

sections.title: Discoveries
sections.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 discovery
sections.body:
- 1 A change of terrain (previous page) and roll again
- 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.)
- 3 game (see below)
- 4 **[[50 - Useful or Valuable Flora#Useful or valuable flora|Useful or valuable flora]]**
- 5 A **[[07 - Crinwin#Crinwin|crinwin]]**, **[[12 - Fae#Fae|Fae]]** or **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|spirit of the wild]]**, curious or indifferent
- 6 A point of interest (next column)

sections.title: 1d6 game
sections.body:
- 1-3 Mostly harmless but prone to bolt: deer, fowl, rabbits, etc.
- 4-5 Big and dangerous if provoked: **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Dangers**|boars]]**, **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Raselbaedd**|wisents]]**, **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Raselbaedd**|tal gerdwyr]]**, etc.
- 6 Deadly and easily provoked: **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Cave bear**|cave bears]]**, **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Cave bear**|ceirwmawr]]**, **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Raselbaedd**|raselbaedd]]**, etc.

sections.title: 1d6 point of interest
sections.body:
- 1 A place corrupted by the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]** or the **[[15 - Fomoraij#Names|Fomoraij]]**
- 2 A place **haunted** by death and the undying [[10 - Death and the Undying#Death and the undying|Death and the undying]]
- 3 A place bearing traces of the **[[17 - Forest Folk#Forest Folk|Forest Folk]]**
- 4 A place sacred to the **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|spirits of the wild]]**
- 5 A **[[12 - Fae#**Getting there**|Fae domain]]** or **[[53 - Waystones#Waystones|waystone]]**
- 6 A ruin or artifact of the **[[24 - Green Lords#Names|Green Lords]]**

If it's been there for at least 10 years, it's likely marked by Forest Folk **[[17 - Forest Folk#**Hooks**|glyphs]]**.

sections.title: Hazards
sections.body:
- Getting lost
- Treacherous terrain (loose footing, mudslides, sinkholes, deadfall, etc.)
- Swarm (bees, wasps, midges, bats, etc.)
- Tall tree to climb, and a reason to climb it

sections.title: Monsters
sections.body:
- **[[07 - Crinwin#Crinwin|Crinwin]]**
- **[[12 - Fae#Fae|Fae]]**
- **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|Spirits of the Wild]]**
- Chimerae and other remnants of **[[24 - Green Lords#Names|the Green Lords]]**
- Servants of the **[[15 - Fomoraij#Names|Fomoraij]]**
- The **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|ghosts]]** or **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|revenants]]** of those who died in the Wood, like the **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|Gwraig Wen]]**
- A copse of **dool trees** and its resident spirits [[10 - Death and the Undying#Dool trees|Dool trees]]
- The **Pale Hunter** and his hounds [[35 - The Pale Hunter|The Pale Hunter]]
- The **[[08 - The Crombil#Hooks|Crombil]]**, should it wake
- One of the many beasts that inhabit the Wood

sections.title: Boar
sections.body:
*Group*, *gluttonous*

**HP** 10; **Armor** 0

**Damage** gore d8 (*hand*, *messy*, 1 piercing) **Instinct** to protect their territory

- ä Relentlessly charge their victim, over and over
- ä Eat just about anything
- ä Ignore pain or injury

sections.title: Butcherbird
sections.body:
*Horde*, *tiny*, *organized*

**HP** 1; **Armor** 1 (*tiny*)

**Damage** peck d6-2 (*hand*, *messy*, 2 piercing) **Instinct** to feast on bloody, bloody meat

- ä Grab on and stab stab stab
- ä Fend off any who would steal the flock's meal
- ä Scent blood from miles away

About the size of a woodpecker, with black and wine-red plumage, flitting about in small flocks (2d6+4). Their laugh will make your blood run cold. But don't worry. They'll get your blood flowing again, real soon.

**Something interesting:** Butcherbirds can smell fresh blood from miles away, and might descend on a hunter's kill to gorge.

sections.title: Cave bear
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *cautious*

**HP** 16; **Armor** 1 (fur, hide)

**Damage** claws, bite, hug d10+4 (*hand*, *close*, *forceful*, *messy*, 1 piercing)

**Instinct** to fill its belly, to protect its young

- ä Rend, maul, crush
- ä Move with surprising speed and grace
- ä Sniff out food, or trouble

Great big silver-brown things, eating anything they can dig up or catch. They mostly hibernate through winter, and get especially bold and ornery in autumn as they try to fatten up. They've been known to wake up and forage even in winter, though.

They lair in big caves, usually two mates and maybe their cubs. Out and about, the bulls are usually found on their own; the sows on their own, or with their cubs. They're not usually aggressive towards people—unless surprised, extra hungry, or protecting the cubs. Then? Watch out.

sections.title: Ceirwmawr
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *terrifying*, *fearless*

**HP** 16; **Armor** 0

**Damage** hoof d10+1 (*close*) or antlers d10+3 (*reach*, *forceful*)

**Special quality** awe-inspiring antlers **Instinct** to tolerate no disrespect

- ä Toss aside a barrier or foe
- ä Suffer harm without flinching
- ä Make a show of strength

Majestic. Proud. Antlers so large you can scarce believe how deftly it moves through the woods. They are the royalty of deer. It is said that only the Pale Hunter can give them chase, and that any mortal who dares to stalk them quickly finds the tables turned.

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- What special property does folklore say the ceirwmawr's antlers possess?
- What dire fate is said to befall those who slay a ceirwmawr without the Pale Hunter's permission?

sections.title: Cougar
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *stealthy*, *cautious*, *athletic*

**HP** 12, **Armor** 0

**Damage** bite d10 (*hand*, *grabby*) **Special qualities** keen night vision and sense of smell

**Instinct** to catch prey unawares

- ä Move into position, silently and unseen
- ä Pounce on prey, dragging them down (*forceful*)
- ä Bite down and rake (*messy*)

They don't usually hunt people. But when game is scarce, or some fool provokes them, they sometimes get a taste for human blood. Even then, they mostly leave groups alone, waiting for someone to fall behind or wander off. Mostly.

sections.title: Cwisarff
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *small*, *stealthy*, *cautious*

**HP** 10; **Armor** 0

**Damage** venomous bite d10 (*close*) **Instinct** to defend its current spot

- ä Wait patiently to spring on prey
- ä Hiss menacingly at a threat
- ä Bite quickly and ready to strike again

The vaunted "tree adder," most lethal serpent in the Wood, up to 6 feet long and dripping with venom. In warm months, they hunt and bask in the canopy or sunny clearings. In colder months, they hide in hollows and under logs, going into torpor in winter. They don't hunt people, but they don't fear them either.

When you are bitten by a cwisarff, mark a debility in addition to taking damage. Until the poison is treated, you can't regain HP and are in danger of further damage and debility.

sections.title: Cynddaraig
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *terrifying*, *fearless* **HP** 20; **Armor** 2 (scales)

**Damage** three-inch long teeth d10+3 (*close*, *reach*, *grabby*, *messy*, 3 piercing, advantage)

- **Instinct** to dominate its territory ä Roar and unfurl its quills, making even brave warriors quail
- ä Bite a limb clean off
- ä To lose itself in primal fury

The mighty "rage drake," almost 10 feet tall and 20 feet from nose to tip of tail. Its jaws are full of knives. Blood-red patterns surround its eyes. A frill of feathered quills sprouts from its neck and runs down its back. Maybe a half-dozen of them stalk the whole Great Wood, and one hasn't been spotted near Stonetop for generations (Tor be praised).

**Something interesting:** They prey mostly on large game (wisents, tal gerdwyr, etc.). They'll attack people, but rarely hunt them.

**Something useful:** They attack people to establish dominance, seeing us as rivals. An appropriate offering or show of obeisance might calm one enough for you to get the hell out of there.

sections.title: Feathered drake
sections.body:
*Group*, *stealthy*, *organized*

**HP** 6; **Armor** 1 (feathers, scales)

**Damage** boney crest d8+2 (*close*, *forceful*), talon & teeth d8+2 (*hand*, *messy*, 1 piercing)

**Special qualities** flightless **Instinct** to spring on unwary prey

- ä Burst out of the brush
- ä Bash prey to the ground, senseless
- ä Surround downed prey, and rend

Bigger than a grown man, with a talon yea-long on each foot and that ridge on their heads they use to go crashing through the undergrowth. They might follow a party for days, waiting for them to let their guard down or for someone to fall behind. Then? Bam.

Their feathers are much-sought by merchants. You can harvest a bundle of drake feathers (*fragile*, Value 3) from a flock of 3-4 drakes.

sections.title: Gylglyd vines
sections.body: **Damage** strangling vines d6 (*hand*, *grabby*)

sections.title: Instinct to lure prey
sections.body:
- ä Ensnare their senses with beauty
- ä Lull their minds with euphoric scents
- ä Slowly entwine them, unnoticed, and hold them fast

Thick, velvety green vines that drape entire groves or clearings, speckled with flowers of iridescent blue and pink and yellow. The mind reels to see them, wanting to move closer. The flowers give off the sweetest scent, erasing all cares. But the wary might notice strange bulges in the hanging vines, or white bone beneath the carpet.

**Something interesting**: Gylglyd flowers are prized by herbalists and apothecaries, but nearly impossible to cultivate.

**Something useful**: A kerchief soaked in musk can counter the euphoric effect.

**Hagr**

 *Solitary*, *large*, *terrifying*

**HP** 16; **Armor** 1 (blubbery hide) **Damage** meaty hands d10+3 (*hand*, *close*, *grabby*, *forceful*) or tree trunk d10+5 (*reach*, *area*, *crude*, *forceful*) or hurled heavy thing d10+3 (*thrown*, *forceful*)

**Instinct** to compulsively shape its environment

- ä Fix someone with its evil eye, over whelming them with fear
- ä Pluck things from the ground as if weightless
- ä Obsessively stack things to exacting standards
- ä Throw a tantrum when things aren't just right

Misshapen brutes with sagging flesh and one bulging eye so filled with hate it'll stop you dead in your tracks. They spend their days collecting fallen branches, looking for just the right one to add to their home of rotting logs. The others they pile neatly, reverently. Perhaps they were servants of the Green Lords, once bred to build, and now, long ages hence, building still.

sections.title: Nailadd
sections.body:
*Group*, *cunning*, *stealthy*

**HP** 6; **Armor** 2 (carapace)

**Damage** venomous bite d8 (*hand*) **Special qualities** webs, prodigious leaps **Instinct** to capture prey and slowly consume it

- ä Leap on prey and knock it prone
- ä Inject a paralytic venom
- ä Drag helpless prey into the canopy and cocoon it

So far, these hound-sized spiders have thankfully avoided hunters and trappers in the Wood. They certainly haven't come near town. But they're out there, rest assured, and growing in numbers.

**Something interesting:** Nailadd venom paralyzes prey, rather than killing it.

**Something useful:** You can harvest the venom (d4 uses, *dangerous*, *reload*, Value 1) from an intact corpse. When you *coat a blade in nailadd venom*, it deals +1d4 extra damage and numbs the wounded area.

sections.title: Raselbaedd
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*

**HP** 20; **Armor** 4 (hide, unnatural resilience) **Damage** gore d10+5 (*hand*, *close*, *forceful*, *messy*, 3 piercing)

**Special qualities** iron hide, always hungry, easily provoked, afraid of fire

**Instinct** to hold a grudge

- ä Rip things apart: armor, weapons, flesh
- ä Eat almost anything
- ä Refuse to back off or let go, even when dying
- ä Recover from even mortal wounds, over time

When a boar lives long enough, and survives enough close calls, and dines year after year on the roots and nuts of ferro trees, it eventually becomes a raselbaedd. The size of a horse, with tusks like short spears and a hide that's crisscrossed with scars and that bristles with broken blades.

When you *deal 2 damage or less to a raselbaedd*, *before armor,* your weapon chips or shatters on its hide.

A raselbaedd's hide (*immobile*, Value 2) can be harvested by a competent hunter with a fine steel blade (2 piercing, Value 2). It can then be fashioned into 1d4 raselbaedd cuirasses (2 armor, Value 3). Doing so requires:

- An exceptional tanner
- An upgraded workshop with fine steel tools (Value 3)
- A season of labor per cuirass

sections.title: Tal gerdwyr
sections.body:
*Group*, *huge*

**HP** 14; **Armor** 2 (feathers, scales)

**Damage** trample, kick d8+5 (*close*, *forceful*), tail sweep d8+5 (*reach*, *area*, *forceful*)

**Special quality** extremely long neck

**Instinct** to get annoyed

- ä Bellow loudly
- ä Smash down or through a barrier
- ä Reach very, very high up

The Forest Folk would sometimes tell stories of the "tall walkers," drakes with necks like trees and bodies like small hills, draped in feathers that look like vines and leaves. Supposedly, they roam deep in the forest, blithely chewing leaves from the canopy and singing mournful songs to each other.

sections.title: Wisent
sections.body:
*Group*, *large*, *cautious*

**HP** 10; **Armor** 0

**Damage** headbutt, gore, trample d8+3 (*hand*, *close*, *forceful*)

**Instinct** to protect the herd

- ä Put itself between a threat and the young
- ä Stampede (*area*, *forceful*, *messy*)
- ä Leap a surprising height and distance, even from a standstill

Forest bison travel in herds of up to twenty, but only the bulls typically fight. When cornered, though, even the sows can be dangerous due to their mass and strength.

sections.title: Wolf
sections.body:
*Group*, *organized*, *cautious*

**HP** 6; **Armor** 0

**Damage** bite d8 (*hand*, *grabby*)

**Qualities** keen sense of smell

**Instinct** to bring down the weakest prey

- ä Follow prey for miles on end
- ä Surround, flank, and harry
- ä Clamp down, eventually crushing bone

sections.title: Green Lords
sections.body:
In the time of the **[[30 - The Makers#The Makers|Makers]]**, the Green Lords ruled the **[[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|Great Wood]]**) and what is now **[[13 - Ferrier's Fen#Ferrier's Fen|Ferrier's Fen]]**). They grew things, bred and cultivated. They bent the **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|spirits of wild]]** to their will, and brought forth new forms of life—some of which would eventually become the **[[17 - Forest Folk#Names|Forest Folk]]**.

Yet while they mastered life itself, they feared death. They dreaded the bleak eternity waiting behind the **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Questions**|Last Door]]**. They bred the **[[12 - Fae#Fae|Fae]]** out of spirit and flesh, and harnessed their power to craft false afterlives. And: it worked. Dozens of Green Lords had themselves mummified and entombed, dreaming forever of decadence and wonder.

Some of the Fae, however, grew to resent their servitude. They plotted in secret and rose up against their masters. They gutted tombs, put the living to the sword, and sent the survivors running.

The Green Lords, in panic and desperation, called on the**[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]** and used their power to quash the rebellion. But this in turn drew the ire of other Makers. They worked a mighty magic that collapsed a vast swath of Green Lord territory, sinking it into a mire and killing thousands. The Fae rallied and besieged the survivors at **[[52 - Vor Svetelik#Vor Svetelik|Vor Svetelik]]**. Their cause lost, the Green Lords unleashed a last great wave of horror before retreating into the bowels of the earth. There they have remained, growing ever more twisted and vile, becoming the **[[15 - Fomoraij#Names|Fomoraij]]**.

sections.title: Names
sections.body: Slavic-sounding: Athanasi, Bogdana, Chedomir, Dozhena, Darobor, Jigorii, Kresimira, Mstislava, Nikalor, Radomira, Tikomar, Tomila, Vecheslav, Voitsek, Yaroslana, Yevena.

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- An earthquake or wildfire reveals a long-buried Green Lord ruin close to Stonetop.
- A **[[24 - Green Lords#**1d12** ability|hazardous growth]]** is aggressively spreading through the Great Wood
- An old scholar or manuscript shows up, telling of a Green Lord relic that can greatly improve crops.
- Some fool braves a Green Lord's tomb and returns with a valuable treasure, but they're pursued by mummified servants and infected with a horrible rotting disease.

sections.title: Sites
sections.body: Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 site
sections.body:
- 1-4 **Lingering signs** of their presence and influence (at right)
- 5-6 A **[[24 - Green Lords#Ruins|ruin]]**

sections.title: Lingering signs
sections.body: Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 sign
sections.body:
- 1 **An entity, imprisoned** in a strange tree, rune-carved stone, or natural feature (next page)
- 2 **Strange plants** (next page)
- 3 A section of the **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#**The Crossroads**|Broken Roads]]**, unearthed or still

sections.title: An entity, imprisoned For example:
sections.body:
- A tree with a metallic sheen, its wood infused with iron. Its trunk spirals around itself, like a spool of yarn, a rebellious Fae trapped within.
- A man-sized mass of blood-sucking insects—an entity of pain and disease—imprisoned in amber.
- A mighty old elder tree, its bark like bone, oozing black sap from the deathless horror it holds bound in its roots.
- A great hunk of stone, reminiscent of a ravenous, howling beast, wrapped in petrified vines and roots and mostly buried/submerged.
- A Fae locked in the form of a tree, a snarl of defiance barely visible.

sections.title: Strange plants
sections.body:
For example:

- A grove of trees, planted in strangely regular patterns (neat rows, spirals, etc.)
- Trees/vines, their trunks or stalks woven around each other to form walls, curtains, ceilings, etc.
- Shrubs/briars in crude topiary shapes with no obvious tending
- Grand sculptures of living wood that memorialize long-dead Green Lords
- Moss, lichen, mold, or fungus that glows, or hums, or gives off heat, etc.
- Plants made partially of impossible material (flowers/leaves of glass, bark infused with metal, a vine with thorns of bone, a petrified tree that blooms with normal flowers, etc.)
- An enormous and ancient tree, festooned with **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Feathered drake**|gylglyd vines]]** that protect some Green Lord artifact.

sections.title: A spirit of the wild, bound For example:
sections.body:
- A spirit of winter/hibernation bound to a grotto by vines growing in runic patterns. Other runes fill those who approach with dread. An ancient being lies in torpor within.
- A clearing or meadow lush with an edible grain, tended by a **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Elemental spirits**|fertility spirit]]** bound to a menhir standing in the field's center.
