- An orchard of fruit trees, guarded by a **birdwaig** and her **glasbren** daughters [[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**The Brennauwed**|Spirits of the Wild]], a long family of spirits and trees bound by an ancient oath to the Green Lords.
- A ring of five ancient willow trees, each with a rune-carved hollow, surrounding a stretch of mire. A spirit of reclamation and decay is bound within. Anything that enters the mire will soon be sucked into the muck, where it will decompose and dissolve at an alarming rate.
- A huge stone altar on a treeless hilltop, exposed to the sun. A spirit of growth and vitality is bound within, fed by blood and bestowing gifts of strength.
- An obelisk of milky white crystal, pushing out of the loamy soil, shot through with tendrils of vine that form looping runes of binding. The spirit bound within can pull forth maladies and afflictions from those who touch it, but the spirit is willful and the process is excruciating.

sections.title: Ruins
path: A spirit of the wild, bound For example:
sections.body: Pick or roll for theme [[24 - Green Lords#**Ruins**|Green Lords]], structure, purpose, architectural elements, and condition. Place it in an appropriate terrain (see [[23 - The Great Wood#GREAT WOOD TIMBER|Great Wood Timber]] for the **Great Wood** or [[13 - Ferrier's Fen#**Questions**|Ferrier's Fen]] for **Ferrier's Fen**). Populate it with **[[24 - Green Lords#**Ruins**|lingering signs]]**, **[[24 - Green Lords#**Artifacts**|artifacts]]** and/or **[[24 - Green Lords#**Dangers**|dangers]]** of the Green Lords or the appropriate region.

sections.title: 1d6 structure
sections.body:
- 1 Sprawling compound of "smaller" buildings
- 2 Single, smaller building
- 3 Earth-covered mound
- 4 Ancient trees, rooms and structures built into hollows and on crooks
- 5 Underground vault(s)
- 6 Ziggurat/pyramid/dome

sections.title: 1d6 purpose
sections.body:
- 1 Dwelling (home, barracks, dormitory, etc.)
- 2 Containment (fortress, prison, kennel, menagerie, etc.)
- 3 Storage (cellar, vault, warehouse, library, collection, etc.)
- 4 Production (workshop, nursery, factory, kitchen, abattoir, etc.)
- 5 Esoteric (laboratory, shrine, surgery, etc.)
- 6 Tomb (of a Green Lord, their ancestors, their servants, their creations, etc.)

sections.title: 1d6 architectural elements
sections.body:
- 1 Huge slabs of smooth stone (white marble, granite, serpentine) carved with scrollwork and bas reliefs
- 2 Moss and lichen, of striking colors, growing in decorative patterns
- 3 Amber, agate, geodes, even unworked boulders of interesting shape
- 4 Ivory, bone, horn, antler, fur, silk sometimes fused with or growing from wood or stone
- 5 Pillars, arches, or even walls of petrified wood, seemingly grown into shape
- 6 Vines and tendrils (often petrified or metal-infused) clinging to surfaces in branching whorls, sigils, and runes

Most Maker-ruins are sized for giants people two or even three times as tall as humans—but often with smaller-scale rooms, passages, and so forth for their servants.

sections.title: 1d6 condition
sections.body:
- 1-2 Shattered and laid waste by time, war, or vandalism; mostly buried, submerged, and/or overgrown; its purpose unrecognizable
- 3-4 Crumbling, largely buried, submerged, and/or overgrown
- 5 Buried, submerged, and/or overgrown, but mostly intact
- 6 Fully visible and strangely well-preserved

See **Vor Svetelik** (the ruined city of the Green Lords, [[52 - Vor Svetelik|Vor Svetelik]]) for additional examples.

Many Green Lord ruins feature (or are preserved by) **[[12 - Fae#**Getting there**|Fae domains]]**.

*The PCs are tracking nailadd (giant jumping spiders) through the Great Wood, and I think that they're heading for a Green Lord ruin. I roll a 6 for theme (the spirits of the wild, bound or put to use), a 6 for structure (ziggurat/pyramid/dome), a 4 for purpose (production), a 4 for architectural elements (ivory, bone, horn, antler, fur, silk), and 3 for condition (crumbling, largely buried and overgrown). I ponder for a little, then come up with this:*

*A crumbling ziggurat, half-collapsed and mostly covered with dirt, trees, and webs. Inside is a smaller ziggurat that bears a silk tapestry of the Mother-of-Spiders, a powerful elemental spirit. In days of yore, this place was filled with spiders of all sizes, spinning silk and weaving impossible fabrics for the Green Lords. Now, the spiders merely grow large, and hungry, and strangely intelligent.*

sections.title: Various treasures
sections.body:
- **A mummified bird in a clay pot** (*fragile*, Value 0)
- **A number of corroded bronze hatchets** of ancient make, with beautifully preserved wooden grips (*crude* and Value 0 as-is; *hand* and Value 1 if restored)
- **A beautiful silk shirt**, embroidered with vine-like patterns (Value 1)
- **Stacks of raw, unworked timber** with a metallic sheen to the grain; incredibly strong but incredibly difficult to work (*immobile*, Value 2)
- **A coat of scale armor** for someone 20 feet tall. The scales are strange, organic, semi-translucent and iridescent around the edges. Thick, twisting vines grow from the armor where a body should be; nothing else grows nearby. The armor is impractical and creepy, but if extracted intact, it could fetch a decent price (*immobile*, Value 2) to a collector.
- **An oversized drinking horn**, gold and amber fused into the horn in impossible ways (Value 3)
- **A tall, thick, gnarled tree** bearing large, delicious fruits in early autumn. Provides as many provisions (4 uses) as the PCs care to take, and could be cultivated into a rhoillyg orchard.

sections.title: A large urn
sections.body:
*immobile*, *magical*

When the *urn is unoccupied and a Fae looks inside*, it is compelled to crawl in (it can always fit). If you *then close the lid*, the Fae is trapped and the urn slowly and painfully peels away its knowledge, essence, and sense of self. In time, nothing remains but a pile of pearls (Value 2).

sections.title: RHOILLYG ORCHARD Requires both:
sections.body:
A sack full of rhoillyg seeds An herbalist of considerable skill and patience

And either of these, to germinate the seeds:

- A year or so of experimentation
- Advice from a knowledgeable source

And then each of these:

- A full spring, to sprout and cultivate saplings
- Pulling Together once, over the course of a summer, to clear and plant the orchard
- Protecting the orchard through two more summers

When you *mark all the requirements*, increase Fortunes by 1 and add "Rhoillyg Orchard" to both the Resources list and the steading map. Henceforth, the steading generates +1 Surplus in summer and another +1 Surplus when the autumn harvest is complete.

sections.title: A vine in a rune-carved pot , magical
sections.body:
It's about as thick as your finger and maybe 30 feet long stretched out, with skinny little tendrils. It looks rather dried out, but moves slightly if you touch it or its pot. If you water it, it twitches excitedly.

A plant spirit is bound to the vine and pot. If you befriend it and carry its pot, it will animate and act as a follower: *grabby*, *clever*, *playful*; **HP** 3; **Damage** 0; **Instinct** to not let go; **Cost** affection, play time.

sections.title: A fang-dagger
path: A vine in a rune-carved pot , magical
sections.body:
*hand*, *magical*, +1 damage An 8-inch fang, bound to a wooden hilt by fiber that never rots. A cruel-looking thing, with strange carvings along the blade.

**Something interesting:** The fang looks like it came from a **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Cougar**|cynddaraig]]**, and the markings are definitely some sort of spell related to blood and soil.

**Something useful:** When you *bury the fang-dagger and water the soil with blood*, the ground buckles and bulges. A minute later, a full-grown cynddaraig bursts from the soil—angry, confused, and hungry. The spell grants no control over the beast and provides no way to dismiss it. The dagger is gone, now a tooth in the rage-drake's maw.

sections.title: A big stone coffer
sections.body:
*immobile*, *magical*

Carved from serpentine, 4 feet by 2 feet by 2, with a heavy stone lid. When found, it's likely half-full with soil. To those sensitive to such things, it thrums with the power of fertility and growth.

When you *add fresh soil to the coffer, plant seeds in it, and close the lid*, the seeds will germinate and sprout overnight. Each seed will sprout only once, and the sprouts henceforth grow at a regular rate, regardless of whether they stay in the coffer or are planted elsewhere.

With coordination, this could lead to a boom in Stonetop's gardens and +1 Surplus each summer. In the hands of a skilled herbalist, this can greatly improve an herb garden and allow the cultivation of rare or difficult plants. The herbalists of Marshedge would kill over this—or even start a war.

sections.title: Small clay jars
sections.body:
*fragile*, *magical*

A number of earthenware jars, about the size of a pinecone, sealed with wax and seemingly empty. When opened (or broken) a fine cloud of dust puffs out. The dust is, in fact, chogor spores.

When *someone is exposed to chogor spores*, the spores quickly start to grow on them in masses of vine-like ferns. They will—in a matter of seconds—ensnare and restrain their victim. After about an hour, the ferns die and rapidly dry out, but until then, it's like being bound up in twine.

sections.title: Minor arcana
sections.body: Pick 1 or have someone roll.

sections.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
sections.body:
- 1 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A crumbling arch|A strange skull and antlers]]**
- 2 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#Goldleaf Cloak|A giant oak leaf]]**
- 3 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#Ffyrnig Tonic|A huge wooden sphere]]**
- 4 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A patch of rainbow moss|A stretched vellum]]**
- 5 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A patch of rainbow moss|A patch of rainbow moss]]**
- 6 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A crumbling arch|A crumbling arch]]**

sections.title: Major arcana
sections.body:
These might very well be associated with the Green Lords.

- The **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Twisted Spear , reach, magical|Twisted Spear]]**
- The **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Shield of the Wisent Witch|Shield of the Wisent Witch]]**

sections.title: Bound Entities
sections.body:
- **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|Spirits of the wild]]**, forced or enticed to perform a particular task or to protect a particular place
- **[[12 - Fae#Fae|Fae]]**, loyal and still serving their embalmed masters (possibly bitter and questioning their fate) or rebellious and imprisoned for their crimes
- Spirits and beasts **corrupted** by the Things Below [[46 - The Things Below#**1d12** origin|The Things Below]], either locked away by the Green Lords, or weaponized against the Fae
- **[[52 - Vor Svetelik#**Plasmic horror**|Plasmic horrors]]**, which once served as blank canvases for fleshweaving

sections.title: Chimerae
sections.body:
The Green Lords meddled wantonly with life, mixing creatures in impossible ways to suit need or whim. Some such creatures linger, breeding true or having survived for hundreds of years.

Pick or roll for purpose, ability, size, and composition. Allow purpose, ability, and size to inform the elements of its composition, and what traits it has from each element.

sections.title: 1d12 purpose
sections.body:
- 1-2 War/destruction/murder/hunting
- 3-4 Defense/protection/companionship
- 5-6 Experimentation/insight/curiosity
- 7-8 Labor/industry/mundane tasks/ convenience
- 9-10 Something obscure, or very specific
- 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice and combine

sections.title: 1d12 ability
sections.body:
- 1 Burn/dissolve/destroy/drain
- 2 Transform/mutate/change
- 3 Control/influence minds or emotions
- 4 Wield/produce/ignore elements or energy
- 5 Poison/disease/infect
- 6 Hide/mimic/disguise
- 7 Entangle/trap/engulf/subdue
- 8 Attack at range/over an area
- 9 Prowess/mobility/speed
- 10 Strength/toughness/durability
- 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice

sections.title: 1d12 size
sections.body:
- 1-2 Cat-sized or smaller (*tiny*)
- 3-4 Like a human child (*small*)
- 5-8 Human-sized
- 9-11 Like a horse, a cart, etc. (*large*)
- 12 Like an elephant, or bigger (*huge*)

sections.title: 1d12 composition
sections.body:
- 1-4 Beast + beast
- 5-6 Beast + plant
- 7-8 Beast + mineral
- 9-11 Beast + roll again with 1d8
- 12 Person + roll again with 1d8

sections.title: Hazardous growth
sections.body: Pick or roll for form and peril, possibly rolling the Die of Fate for size/scope/scale. Maybe consider how it's **[[50 - Useful or Valuable Flora#**Useful or valuable flora**|useful/ valuable]]** as well.

sections.title: 1d12 form
sections.body:
- 1-2 Invisible to the naked eye/spores/dust
- 3-4 Mold/moss/lichen/slime
- 5-6 Mushrooms/puffballs/stinkhorns/fungus
- 7-8 Vines/briars/brambles/shoots
- 9-10 Flowers/plants/ferns/grasses
- 11-12 Tree(s)/shrubs/bushes/hedges

sections.title: 1d12 peril
sections.body:
- 1 Crushes, strangles, cuts, stabs (possibly animate)
- 2 Eats away, dissolves, absorbs (tissue or specific material)
- 3 Grows incredibly quickly, overwhelms, ensnares
- 4 Infects, colonizes, sickens, changes
- 5 Poisons, weakens, subdues
- 6 Causes dizziness, hallucinations, delusions
- 7 Releases spores/pollen/etc. that irritate, obscure, agitate
- 8 Draws attention (via noise, phosphorescence, scent, etc.)
- 9-10 Roll 1d10 twice and combine
- 11-12 Roll again with 1d10 but make it weird—elemental, psychic, spiritual, extremely specific, etc.

See **Vor Svetelik** (ruined city of the Green Lords, [[52 - Vor Svetelik|Vor Svetelik]]) for specific examples.

sections.title: The Unquiet Dead
sections.body:
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Ghosts]]** of the Green Lords and their many servants, who died in battle or sudden cataclysm.
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Specters]]** of Green Lords who were murdered by trusted servants.
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Wraiths]]** of Green Lords who were mummified and entombed, only to have their false afterlives ruined by treachery and war.
- **[[52 - Vor Svetelik#**Chimerae**|Nemurvojak]]**, the corpses of mortal servants, reanimated by demon-seed

sections.title: Llamudwr
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *construct*, *corrupted*, *cautious*, *stealthy*

**HP** 16; **Armor** 4 (resilience), 0 vs. bronze **Damage** stinging tail d10+1 (*reach*), crunching bite d10+1 (*hand*, *grabby*, *messy*, 1 piercing)

**Special qualities** clumsy when grounded, hates the sun

**Instinct** to fill its belly and retreat beneath the water

- ä Lurk unseen, pick a target, and wait until sunset
- ä Leap out of the water with shocking speed
- ä Follow prey from the sky, or hanging

 from trees ä Strafe its prey and deliver a painful, debilitating sting ä Chomp down, and then swallow whole Everyone knows to shun deep, still water, and the llamudwr (or "water leapers") are surely one reason why. Take the body of a frog, 6 or 7 feet long. Add another 8 or 9 feet of whipping tail, tipped with a venomous barb. Get rid of its legs, not even stumps, but give it bat wings, a span of nearly 20 feet unfurled. Hide it in a lake or fen, away from the sun's cruel light. And finally, make it hungry. Very, very hungry.

sections.title: Mummified Green Lord
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *spirit*, *undead*, *magical*, *cunning*, *cautious*, *organized*, *terrifying*

**HP** 24; **Armor** 4 (potent wards), 0 vs. fire **Damage** mighty blows d10+2 (*hand*, *close*, *forceful*)

**Special qualities** tethered to canopic jars, can sense all that happens in its tomb

- **Instinct** to preserve its glorious afterlife ä Animate its preserved corpse
- ä Overawe anyone in its presence
- ä Twist, mutate, or transform living things with a gesture
- ä Wield nearby plants and beasts as extensions of itself
- ä Regenerate its corpse, even if burned, unless its canopic jars destroyed

They lie entombed, their corpses embalmed, their souls bound to the heavily-warded canopic jars that store their most-vital organs. Yet those souls linger forever in Fae-woven realms of glamour and delight. In these realms, the Green Lords are as gods. They are loathe to leave them, and rarely need to. But should their existence be truly threatened, they will return to this world of base flesh, inhabit their long-dead bodies, and punish any intruders with the savagery of the cruelest beasts.

sections.title: Mummified servant
sections.body:
*Horde*, *spirit*, *undead*, *organized*, *relentless*

**HP** 11; **Armor** 0

**Damage** choking grip d6 (*hand*, *grabby*), spear d6 (*close*, 1 piercing)

**Special qualities** takes +1d4 damage from fire, tethered to canopic jars

**Instinct** to destroy intruders

- ä Animate its preserved corpse
- ä Surround intruders and hem them in, cutting off escape
- ä Keep fighting with limbs that have been severed
- ä Regenerate its corpse, unless burned or its canopic jars destroyed

Some of the Green Lords were mummified and interred with their most faithful mortal servants. These servants get to enjoy their master's false afterlife, but when threats to the tomb arise, they are sent back to their corpses, to rise and crush any intruders they find.

sections.title: Rhagedn
sections.body:
*Horde*, *construct*, *stealthy* **HP** 3; **Armor** 2 (woody hide)

**Damage** talons, antlers d6+2 (*hand*, *messy*, 1 piercing)

**Special qualities** clumsy fliers **Instinct** to protect their nests

- ä Shift into a better position while unobserved
- ä Strafe their prey (*forceful*) and return to the skies
- ä Roost on earth or stone or wood, sinking in talons, furling its wings, and becoming like woody stumps or boughs

Hunched humanoid forms carved of wood, with great wings, taloned feet, and heads crowned with branch-like antlers. They were made by the Green Lords to be sentinels and guardians, to watch over their borders and their most important sites. Now, ages and generations later, they still watch, and wait, and protect their nests.

**Something interesting:** The tales say that the song of a redbreasted bluebird will keep the rhagedn still, letting folk pass unmolested.

**Something useful:** It just so happens that you know how to whistle the tune (whether or not you can whistle it well enough is questionable).

sections.title: Swyn
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *construct*, *corrupted*, *stealthy*, *magical*, *devious*, *cautious*, *hoarder* **HP** 20; **Armor** 4 (resilience), 2 vs. bronze

(scales)

**Damage** bite & constriction d10+3 (*hand*, *close*, *forceful*, *grabby*)

**Instinct** to indulge its ego

- ä Appear out of nowhere and lock eyes
- ä Hold its prey fast with gaze and soothing words
- ä Plant a suggestion in its victim's mind
- ä Turn the weak-willed into fawning servants

Swyn are great serpents with simian heads and hypnotic eyes. The Green Lords bred them out of desperation, borrowing secrets from the Things Below. They wanted spymasters and saboteurs. What they got was a race of petty, craven narcissists.

Each swyn fancies itself the greatest, the most beautiful, the most magnificent of beings. They have exacting tastes, far more subtle and refined than any of their peers. But should you embarrass, defy, or simply bore one, expect to be brutally devoured.

Few of these creatures survive, thankfully. The Fae hunt them and they shun others of their kind, jealous of territory and wealth. But every dozen years or so, some urge drives them all to the ruins of **Vor Svetelik** (page [[52 - Vor Svetelik#Vor Svetelik|468]]), entourage in tow, where they engage in ostentatious displays and mating rituals. There, amidst the sick white trees, the next brood of swyn are seeded. There, perhaps, these foul things might be wiped out once and for all.

When *the swyn compels you to act against your will*, mark XP if you do what it wants. If you resist, you are Defying Danger.

sections.title: Zrajedak
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *construct*, *corrupted*, *devious*, *terrifying*, *stealthy*

**HP** 16; **Armor** 4 (resilience), 1 vs. bronze (hide)

**Damage** maw full of iron teeth d10+3 (*hand*, *messy*, *grabby*, 1 piercing, advantage), iron tail-quills d10+1 (*near*, *area*, *reload*)

**Special qualities** keen sense of smell **Instinct** to torment its prey

- ä Assume a form similar to that of its prey, but just a little off
- ä Reveal its horrible, smiling maw
- ä Cripple a victim and let it suffer
- ä Chew right through a limb

When the Fae rebelled, the Green Lords made living weapons to use against them. The zrajedak—or "treachery-eaters"—were one such weapon. They merged the body of a lion, the tail of a spiney drake, and the face of their mortal servants, and then infused it all with iron, and malice, and the cruel power of the Things Below.

Some of these horrors are trapped by Fae magic. Others remain in stasis, yet to be unleashed on the world. But surely some few stalk the world still, for the Forest Folk would tell hushed tales of them. "Never trust someone who will not show you their teeth," they'd say, "for Zrajedak smiles only before he bites."

sections.title: Helior, the Daybringer
sections.body:
Helior is one of the four main **[[20 - Gods and Religion#Gods and religion|gods]]** of Stonetop. He is the god of the sun and light, beacon of hope and mercy, the fiery foe of the dark.

If one of your initial PCs has the Lightbearer playbook, then they will answer many of the questions in this section via their presence, their background, and the decisions they make during character creation and introductions. Defer to that PC as the expert on Helior in your game, but don't forget to ask other PCs for their opinions and experiences, too.

sections.title: Themes
sections.body: Pick, or roll 1d12.

sections.title: 1d12 theme
sections.body:
| 1-2 | The sun/day/light         |
|-----|---------------------------|
| 3-4 | Hope/courage/aspiration   |
| 5-6 | Mercy/charity/kindness    |
| 7   | Truth/revelation          |
| 8   | Fire/burning/warmth       |
| 9   | Glory/awe/majesty         |
| 10  | Radiance/beauty/intensity |
| 11  | Refuge/sanctity/defense   |
|     |                           |

12 Purification/cleansing/redemption

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
According to the tales:

- How did Helior come to be (a god)? What's his origin story?
- How did he obtain or create the White Flame?
- Which god is his rival/ally/lover/kin?
- Where does the sun/Helior go each night?
- What causes eclipses? What do they mean or portend?
- Who was famously martyred in Helior's name? Who killed them, and why?

Use the answers to help decide what Helior's **true nature** is [[20 - Gods and Religion#**1d12** observance|Gods and Religion]].

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- A devotee of Helior ministers in a nearby town, fomenting dissent.
- A child is born with a sun-shaped birthmark during a solar eclipse.
- The spirit of the prior Lightbearer appears to a PC and assigns them a quest.
- A Manmarcher **[[34 - North Manmarch#**Chief**|chief]]** acquires a relic of the prior Lightbearer and seeks to auction it off.
- A charlatan or **[[46 - The Things Below#**Cultist**|sorcerer]]** claims the mantle of Lightbearer.
- Spring fails to come; omens point to Helior being trapped or wounded.

sections.title: Veneration
Consider or ask:
sections.body:
- Why does the worship of Helior involve the things it does? According to his devotees, why do these things please him so?
- How do these practices change from community to community?
- How are the solstices celebrated or observed?
- If the worship of Helior is foreign/ new/forgotten/persecuted: what other god or gods of the sun are worshipped around here? (See [[20 - Gods and Religion#**Other gods**|Gods and Religion]] for guidance)

sections.title: Places of worship
The Lightbearer
sections.body:
The Lightbearer is or was the appointed servant of Helior, wielder of his power and grace. There has only ever been one active at a time. If one of your PCs has the Lightbearer playbook, they're it.

If no PC has the Lightbearer playbook, then consider or ask:

- How many Lightbearers have there been?
- Is a Lightbearer currently active in the world?
- If so, where do they dwell and how well known are they?
- If not, when did the last Lightbearer live?
- What are/were they like, personally?
- What great deeds are/were they known for?
- Who are/were their greatest allies? Their greatest foes?
- How is the mantle of Lightbearer said to pass or manifest?

Should an NPC Lightbearer (or their spirit) oppose or cause trouble for the PCs, write them up as a threat (a *wildcard* if they still live, a *magical entity* if they cause trouble from beyond).

Remember: NPCs don't have playbooks. An NPC Lightbearer might have tags or moves reminiscent of a PC Lightbearer, or not.

sections.title: Devotees
sections.body:
Helior is a generous god, one who brings light and warmth to the world. He calls on humanity to do the same, to live lives of mercy, charity, and hope. Only a few devotees truly heed his call.

In any given community, ask or consider:

- How many devotees of Helior reside here?
- Is there a formal cult or order, or just likeminded individuals?
- Who is most notable among them?
- How much influence do they have?
- What charitable work(s) do they pursue? Who in the community resents them?
- What contact do they have with devotees from other settlements?

Should the Heliorite devotees of a community oppose or cause trouble for the PCs, write them up as a threat (like a *wildcard*, but maybe an *institution*).

sections.title: Entities of darkness
sections.body:
The Lightbearer playbook has moves and invocations that affect creatures or spirits "of darkness." The **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]** definitely count, as do any corrupted beings. Spirits of night and dark places (like caves) probably count, too. Undead are almost certainly entities of darkness, but maybe there are exceptions?

Ask the Lightbearer what they think makes something a creature or spirit of darkness. Consider what you know about the entity in question, and make a ruling. When in doubt, defer to the Die of Fate or the result of an appropriate move.

sections.title: Various treasures
sections.body:
- A vial of lemon-infused oil (Value 0)
- A copper torc decorated with a stylized sun (Value 1)
- A circle of bronze sigils two paces wide, inlaid into a stone floor; blessed by Helior, a ward against spirits of darkness (*magical*, *immobile*, Value 2)
- A lit oil lamp that consumes no oil; it will burn forever unless snuffed, blown out, or spilled (*magical*, *fragile*, Value 2)
- Holy relics ( uses, Value 3 to a collector): if you have one in your inventory when you Invoke the Sun God, you can mark a use in lieu of choosing a consequence
- A crown of white gold, that shines with holy light (*reach*, *area*) when worn by one with a pure soul (*magical*, Value 4)

sections.title: Minor arcana
sections.body: Pick 1 or have someone roll.

sections.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
sections.body:
- 1-2 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A gold ring magical|A gold ring]]**
- 3-4 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A gold butter lamp|A gold butter lamp]]**
- 5-6 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A rusty steel blade|A rusty steel blade]]**

sections.title: Associated entities
sections.body:
- An **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Other primordial entities**|archon]]**, set to guard a holy site (like the Lightbearer's tomb)
- A southern **[[29 - Lygos and the South#**Assassin**|assassin]]**, paid to murder the Lightbearer or a problematic devotee
- An elemental **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Elemental spirits**|spirit of fire]]**, consecrated by Helior, passionate and righteous

sections.title: Pilgrims
sections.body:
Threat (*rabble*)

**Instinct** to find succor and purpose

- ä Grow in numbers: a steady trickle, a constant stream, a tide
- ä Demand time, attention, consideration
- ä Strain or consume resources
- ä Get into trouble with the locals
- ä Misinterpret teachings
- ä Split into factions

As a Lightbearer grows in power, word of their miracles will likely spread. Pilgrims are likely to follow. They seek out the Lightbearer for healing, direction, or purpose.

sections.title: Fanatic
sections.body:
*Group*, *organized*, *devoted*

**HP** 10; **Armor** 0

**Damage** weapons d8 (tags by weapon) **Instinct** to protect their beliefs

- ä Make a passionate argument
- ä Suffer for the cause
- ä Do evil for the greater good

A Lightbearer often attracts faithful followers, as do some of Helior's more charismatic devotees. Some of these followers might take things a little far.

sections.title: Hillfolk
sections.body:
The **[[42 - The Steplands#The Steplands|Steplands]]** are home to dozens of nomadic bands, collectively called the Hillfolk. The bands are tied together into a bewildering tapestry of kinship and intermarriage and treaty, rivalry and blood feud and long-standing grudge.

Some Hillfolk roam the **[[14 - The Flats#The Flats|Flats]]** in warmer months. Some bands trade with Stonetop, at least on occasion, signaling to them at **[[48 - Titan Bones#Titan Bones|Titan Bones]]**. Others trade with **[[32 - Marshedge#Marshedge|Marshedge]]**, and still others with **[[29 - Lygos and the South#**Merchants**|southern merchants]]**. They have little contact with the **[[34 - North Manmarch#North Manmarch|Manmarchers]]**, and tend to treat them with suspicion.

Their wariness towards the Manmarchers is nothing, though, compared to their contempt for **[[22 - Gordin's Delve#Gordin's Delve|Gordin's Delve]]**. The Hillfolk view mining metal ores as sacrilege. They'll gladly steal from the Delvers or loot their corpses, but only the most debased Hillfolk trade peacefully with Gordin's Delve. And only the most shunned and shamed will actually move there and call the mining town home.

sections.title: Names
sections.body: Breton-inspired, but missing vowels and clipped-sounding: Adm, Blej, Cirl, Davth, Elst, Gwilm, Gwenl, Henri, Ines, Jenfir, Jown, Juda, Kiln, Laurl, Loic, Merrn, Maikl, Nanzl, Nolwn, Quent, Reegn, Ropr, Sabi, Stren, Yanz.

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- Which of you knows the Hillfolk best? How so?
- What clothing/markings/traits are distinctive to (these) Hillfolk?
- How is Hillfolk language different from Stonetop's?
- What do the Hillfolk call themselves? What do they call each other?
- What are groups of Hillfolk called? Bands? Rides? Herds? Families? Etc.
- What affiliations do they have beyond their bands? Clans, nations, sects, etc?
- What sparked the last open conflict between them and Gordin's Delve?
- What, do you think, is the source of their taboo against mining?
- What deity (if any) do they hold in highest regard?
- How is their view of the gods different than Stonetop's?
- What festival do (these) Hillfolk celebrate this time of year?
- What game/pastime/food are (these) Hillfolk known for?

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- A band of Hillfolk has come under the sway of a **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk rider|hdour]]** and moved into the **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#The Ruined Tower|Ruined Tower]]**.
- Ne'er-do-wells from Gordin's Delve kill some Hillfolk in the Flats; war looms.
- Someone in town owes a blood debt to a Hillfolk band; they come calling to collect, with friends.
- The ruin that the PCs seek lies in the territory of a particularly unfriendly Hillfolk band.

sections.title: Lore
sections.body:
Hillfolk rarely discuss their ancestry with outsiders. They claim an ancient right to the Steplands, but fear the **[[05 - Barrow Builders#Barrow|barrow mounds]]** and many of the **[[43 - Stone Lords#Stone Lords|Stone Lord]]** ruins.

**Something interesting:** The Hillfolk's ancestors were human servants (slaves?) of the Stone Lords. They rebelled, calling on the power of the**[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**. Petty warlords and would-be sorcerer-kings—the **[[05 - Barrow Builders#Barrow Builders|Barrow Builders]]**—dominated the Hillfolk for many years after the Stone Lords fell.

**Something useful:** Many Hillfolk traditions stem from their time suffering under the Stone Lords and the Barrow Builders, and the efforts required to throw off the yoke of those rulers. What's one such tradition that you know of, and how can you use it to your advantage?

sections.title: Nomads
sections.body:
Most Hillfolk bands are nomadic, following food and good grazing for their herds as the seasons change. Each band has traditional territory, including a winter camp with permanent shelter (caves, ruins, stone huts, wooden longhouses, etc.). A number of bands have territory that extends into the Flats or **[[40 - South Manmarch#South Manmarch|South Manmarch]]**, but winter camps are always nestled in the Steplands.

The **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#The Makers' Roads|Makers' Roads]]** are considered communal territory, but otherwise infringing on a band's territory is cause for suspicion or bloodshed. Wise travelers hide their passage, or else bring tribute and actively seek to avoid offence.

- How do (these) Hillfolk mark the boundaries of their territory?
- What sort of temporary structures do (these) Hillfolk dwell in? (Tents? Yurts? Log houses? Something else?)
- The Steplands are fertile enough—why don't (more) Hillfolk take up agriculture, do you think?

sections.title: Hillfolk bands
sections.body:
Here's a typical band:

**Size** *hamlet* (~25 to 75 souls)

sections.title: Prosperity -1
sections.body:
- Trade with other bands
- Trade with (pick 1)...
  - ... Marshedge (rope, textiles, herbs, pottery)
  - ... Stonetop (whisky, potatoes, oats)
  - ... Lygos, etc. (salt, tools)

sections.title: Resources
sections.body:
- Horses, sheep, goats, hounds
- Wool, milk
- Forage (fruits, nuts, veggies, honey)
- Hunting (meat, fur, hides)
- Gathering (timber, sap, flint)

sections.title: Defenses +0
sections.body:
- Mounted warriors
- Flint-tipped weapons (*crude*)

About a third of each band are able-bodied adults; the rest are children or elders. Maybe half of the adults are riders, and the rest manage life back at camp. Older children typically manage herds, often with the help of hounds. Specialization is rare, and even homemakers and older children are capable fighters.

- Smoke from multiple fires seen over the horizon
- Bleating of sheep or goats, cluck of chickens, bark of dogs
- Thickets and pens for keeping herds
- Stern, suspicious faces and hands hovering near weapons

If a PC is familiar with the(se) Hillfolk, ask them:

- What is this group called?
- What sets them apart from other Hillfolk?
- Who are their staunchest allies?
- Who are their bitterest rivals/enemies?
- What resource do they have in (relative) abundance?

sections.title: Naming bands
sections.body:
If you need inspiration for naming a Hillfolk band (or other group), pick a word from below (e.g. "Red band") or mix 2-3 (e.g. "Ash Pickers" or "Ochre Wolf Fang").

Arrow, Ash, Badger, Black, Blanket, Blue, Cloak, Crow, Drake, Eastern, Eye, Face, Fang, Feather, Ferret, Fist, Foot, Fur, Goat, Grass, Green, Hair, Hand, Herd(er), Hill, Hoof, Hound, Leaf, Lower, Moon, Northern, Ochre, Paw, Picker, Planter, Rain, Ram, Raven, Red, Rock, Run(ner), Seed, Shield, Shrew, Slayer, Snake, Snow, Soil, Southern, Spear, Staff, Sun, Tooth, Tree, Upper, Vulture, Watch(er), Weed, Western, Whisper(er), White, Wing, Woad, Wolf, Yellow.

sections.title: Traits
sections.body: Ask the PCs, or pick 1 or 2, or roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d12 trait
sections.body:
- 1 Particularly large or small, prosperous or poor
- 2 "Picked band," made of unrelated adults, few children among them
- 3 Worship a particular deity, revere a particular spirit
- 4 Famous member, now or in the past
- 5 Keep dangerous beasts (boars, drakes, etc.)
- 6 Ride something other than horses (aurochs, Huffel rams, big deer)
- 7 Tend to be large/slender/tall/ short/etc.
- 8 Especially fierce/friendly/territorial/aggressive/political/etc.
- 9 Extraordinarily large/fast/clever/ fearless/etc. horses
- 10 Produce more than their share of spirit-talkers/hdours/**[[42 - The Steplands#**Gouzadn**|gouzadn]]**
- 11 Guard a greatly feared and cursed ruin/barrow/area
- 12 Not actually nomadic; they stay in cliff-dwellings all year

sections.title: Commonly available
sections.body:
The following are special items in Stonetop, but commonly available among the Hillfolk—PCs can acquire them here without having to roll. They do need to trade something of equal Value. Stonetop trade goods (whisky, potatoes, oats) are always in demand.

Note that items printed on the Inventory insert are *crude* if acquired from the Hillfolk, and any iron items will instead be stone, bone, wooden, or maybe hammered copper. Iron items are considered **special items** (see below).

| goods                                   | value |
|-----------------------------------------|-------|
| Salt (a little box)                     | 0     |
| Adz (hand, crude)                       | 0     |
| Cudgel, wood (close, crude)             | 0     |
| Cartload of timber (immobile)           | 2     |
| livestock                               | value |
| Goat (sure-footed, curious, hungry);    | 1     |
| HP 3; Damage d4 (hand);                 |       |
| Instinct to explore; can butcher        |       |
| for 4 provisions (6 uses)               |       |
| Sheep (timid, hardy, wooly)             | 1     |
| HP 3; Damage d4 (hand);                 |       |
| Instinct to follow the herd; can        |       |
| butcher for 4 provisions (6 uses)       |       |
| Sheep hound, follower (keen-nosed,      | 1     |
| herder, fierce) HP 6; Damage d6         |       |
| (hand, grabby); Instinct to bully,      |       |
| threaten; Cost training                 |       |
| Horse, follower (powerful, keen-nosed,  | 3     |
| swift or hardy, large); HP 10;          |       |
| Damage d6+3 (hand, close, forceful);    |       |
| Instinct to panic; Cost care & grooming |       |

sections.title: . trade opportunities .
sections.body:
If the PCs get a trade opportunity (from the Seasons Change move), a band of Hillfolk might offer the following:

- A good exchange rate, two or even three Value 2 items for 1 Surplus.
- A cartload of timber or a dozen sheep/goats (Value 2), in exchange for help with a problem.
- Horses (Value 3) traded for a similar amount of Surplus (Value 2), plus help with a problem.
- Someone versed in training and caring for horses, willing to move to Stonetop
- An arcanum of the **[[43 - Stone Lords#Stone Lords|Stone Lords]]**, the **[[05 - Barrow Builders#Barrow Builders|Barrow Builders]]**, the **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|spirits of the wild]]**, or the**[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**.

sections.title: Special items
sections.body:
The following might be acquired among the Hillfolk, but the PCs must roll Trade & Barter to acquire them. This is in addition to the special items on the Moves & Gear handout.

When the PCs *Trade & Barter among the Hillfolk*, they use the local Prosperity (-1 by default) with disadvantage for being a *hamlet*. **On a 6-**, they don't mark XP and this band doesn't have/want it—maybe they know of another band who might?

| goods                              | value |
|------------------------------------|-------|
| Iron items from inventory insert   | 0     |
| Makerglass (misshapen shard/chunk) | 1     |
| Makerglass (an item made of it)    | +2    |

| livestock                               | value |
|-----------------------------------------|-------|
| Huffel ram (hardy, wooly, powerful,     | 2     |
| grumpy); HP 6; Damage d6+2              |       |
| (hand, forceful); Instinct to protect   |       |
| the herd; can butcher for               |       |
| 4 provisions (9 uses)                   |       |
| Upland horse, follower? (powerful,      | 3     |
| keen-nosed, agile, brave, large);       |       |
| HP 14; Damage d6+3 (hand, close,        |       |
| forceful); Instinct to suffer no fools; |       |
| Cost firm training                      |       |

sections.title: Services and favors
sections.body:
PCs don't need to roll Trade & Barter for the following, but they might need to Persuade the Hillfolk—even if they offer something of suitable Value. A group that's well-disposed to the PCs might grant these boons for free, or without needing to be Persuaded, or at a reduced price.

| service/favor                              | value |
|--------------------------------------------|-------|
| Safe passage through their territory       | 1     |
| A few nights' hospitality in their camp    | 1     |
| A guide through their territory            | 2     |
| A guide through another band's territory   | 3     |
| A guide to a cursed/sacred/forbidden place | 3     |

sections.title: Hdour
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *cautious*, *devious*, *hoarder*, *magical*, *spirit-wise*

**HP** 14; **Armor** 2 (thick hides, minor wards) **Damage** bronze knife d8 (*hand*) **Instinct** to ignore rules, boundaries, and taboos

- ä Call up a spirit to attend them
- ä Send spirits or beasts to harass, spy, or attack
- ä Reveal some spell or trick prepared in advance
- ä Unleash power they cannot control

**Spirit-talkers** (next page) have great power, but use it with care and only for the benefit of their people. There are many rules that they follow, many things they will refuse to do. Because to step over the line, and to seek power for power's sake, and to use their power selfishly—that is the way of the sorcerer, the hdour.

When the Hillfolk suspect a spirit-talker of sorcery, they exile them. In the worst cases, they bury them alive. Few are willing to deal a death-blow to a hdour, for that's a sure way to get oneself cursed.

Hdour are often in possession of one or more **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#An old scroll case , fragile|arcana]]**, which they do not hesitate to use. They are often attended by beasts, **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|spirits of the wild]]**, spirits of the **[[10 - Death and the Undying#Death and the undying|dead and undying]]**, and minions of the**[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**.

sections.title: Hillfolk rider
sections.body:
*Group*, *stealthy*, *organized*, *horse-wise* **HP** 6; **Armor** 0 to 2 (thick hides, shield) **Damage** flint-tipped spear d8 (*close*, *thrown*, *crude*), cudgel d8 (*close*, *crude*), adz d8 (*hand*, *crude*), or bow d8 (*near*)

**Instinct** to distrust outsiders

- <sup>ä</sup> Ride a steed like an extension of themselves
- <sup>ä</sup> Outmaneuver their prey
- <sup>ä</sup> Take advantage of the terrain

A typical band of Hillfolk has between 6 to 12 riders: adult hunters and warriors. They patrol their territories in twos and threes, seeking game and keeping an eye out for trespassers. The brash and the bold will challenge anyone they don't recognize. More cautious riders will follow unseen and take the outsiders' measure; whether they let them pass, or spook them into leaving, or murder them in their sleep… well that remains to be seen.

When *a Hillfolk rider and their horse attack a foe together*, they deal 1d6+4 damage (the horse's 1d6+3, with +1 for the rider), combining tags from the attacks as makes sense.

sections.title: Spirit-talker
sections.body:
*Group*, *cautious*, *cunning*, *magical*,  *spirit-wise*

**HP** 8; **Armor** 0 to 2 (thick hides, shield) **Damage** flint-tipped spear d8 (*close*, *thrown*, *crude*), club or adz d8 (*hand*, *crude*), or bow d8 (*near*)

**Special quality** sensitive to the presence of active spirits

**Instinct** to protect their people

- <sup>ä</sup> Sharply rebuke a spirit or mortal
- <sup>ä</sup> Commune with beasts, or the spirits of a place or thing
- <sup>ä</sup> Take advantage of the terrain
- <sup>ä</sup> Borrow and wield a friendly spirit's power

Now and then, a young Hillfolk shows signs of being touched by the spirits. The signs often come after a sickness or brush with death. They hear voices that aren't there. They find themselves more comfortable with horse and hound than their fellow people. Sometimes, they lose the human tongue altogether and go feral, becoming a **[[42 - The Steplands#**Gouzadn**|gouzadn]]**. But when things go right—when they are guided by an elder, and possessed of great self-control they become a spirit-talker for their band.

sections.title: Upland horse
sections.body: *Group*, *large*, *powerful*, *keen-nosed*, *agile*, *brave*

sections.title: HP 14; Armor 0
sections.body:
**Damage** kick, trample d6+3 (*hand*, *close*, *forceful*)

**Instinct** to suffer no fools

- ä Sniff trouble on the wind
- ä Navigate tricky terrain or obstacles
- ä Remain calm, unmoved
- ä Find their own way

Hillfolk bands breed their horses for strength, agility, and courage. The best become steeds for Hillfolk riders. It takes a firm hand and a steady will to handle an Upland, but win one over and you've got a friend for life.

sections.title: Huffel Peaks
sections.body: This range runs along the western edge of the **[[14 - The Flats#The Flats|Flats]]** and the **[[42 - The Steplands#The Steplands|Steplands]]**, connecting with the **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#The Whitefang Mountains|Whitefang Mountains]]** in the north. They're not as tall and menacing as the Whitefangs, but they are jagged things of granite and basalt, home to a number of volcanoes, and rich in minerals and ore.

sections.title: Always
sections.body:
- Slabs of jagged stone: impassive, looming, somehow still surprising
- Tall thin trees (aspen, pines, firs) in the lower slopes and valleys; windswept junipers higher up
- The stone-on-stone clink and thunk as rocks tumble down a slope
- A sense of being surrounded, cut off

sections.title: Spring
sections.body:
- Snow clinging to slopes and peaks, slowly melting
- Pale green on the tips of branches; meadows bursting with wildflowers

sections.title: Summer
sections.body:
- Too-bright sky as you step out of the shade
- Lips cracking in a hot, dry breeze
- Hot sun beating down on you while, somehow, you're getting rained on

sections.title: Autumn
sections.body:
- Slopes and meadows of yellow and gold
- A bitter chill in the now-damp air
- Air pressure dropping as a storm rolls in

sections.title: Winter
sections.body:
- Black stone peeking out from white
- Trees drooping under snow
- A sense of stillness, despite the constant wind

sections.title: The World's End
Places
sections.body:
- **[[22 - Gordin's Delve#Gordin's Delve|Gordin's Delve]]**
- The **[[28 - The Labyrinth#The Labyrinth|Labyrinth]]**

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- Prospectors find veins of cinnabar near Gordin's Delve, and set about mining the lucrative, toxic stuff
- A **[[18 - Forge Lords#**Draventao**|draventao]]** chases a **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk|Hillfolk]]** band from their winter camp in the mountains
- Scouts and warbands of an unknown people start to appear in the Huffel Peaks
- A massive volcanic eruption throws ash across the region, causes strange sunsets and weather, spreads panic and superstition, and perhaps sets loose a powerful **emanation** of the Things Below [[46 - The Things Below#**Wretch**|The Things Below]]

sections.title: Terrain
sections.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

sections.title: 1d12 terrain
sections.body:
- 1 Lava/hot springs/geysers/mudpots/ fumaroles
- 2 Crater/caldera/lava tubes/volcanic formations
- 3 Stream/river/waterfall/lake/ boggy soil
- 4 Travertines/basalt columns or terraces/fairy chimneys/arches
- 5-7 Sheer rock face/bluff/summit/peak
- 8-9 Steep slope/switchback trails/ ridgeline
- 10 Ravine/canyon/gorge
- 11 Gentle slope/meadow/valley/pass
- 12 Cave, cavern, grotto

If you like, roll 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

sections.title: Discoveries
sections.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 discovery
sections.body:
- 1 A change of terrain (previous column) and roll again.
- 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.)
- 3 An entrance/exit to the **[[28 - The Labyrinth#The Labyrinth|Labyrinth]]**
- 4 An encounter (next page)
- 5 A point of interest (next page)
- 6 A natural resource (next page)

sections.title: 1d6 encounter
sections.body:
- 1 **[[28 - The Labyrinth#Thraulgwyn|Thraulgwyn]]**: come out of the Labyrinth for some reason
- 2 **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Spirits of the wild|Spirit of the wild]]**: curious or indifferent
- 3-4 **[[51 - Ustrina#Ustrina|Ustrina]]**: travelling, scrounging, on patrol
- 5 Miners/prospectors/loggers, likely based out of **[[22 - Gordin's Delve#Gordin's Delve|Gordin's Delve]]**): wary, on edge
- 6 **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk|Hillfolk]]**: herding, hunting, encamped, or on patrol

sections.title: 1d6 point of interest
sections.body:
- 1 A place corrupted by the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**
- 2 A place **haunted** by death and the undying [[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|Death and the Undying]]
- 3 A ruin or artifact of the **[[43 - Stone Lords#Stone Lords|Stone Lords]]**, maybe claimed by the **[[05 - Barrow Builders#Barrow Builders|Barrow Builders]]**
- 4-5 A ruin or artifact of the **[[18 - Forge Lords#Forge Lords|Forge Lords]]**, perhaps inhabited by **[[51 - Ustrina#Ustrina|Ustrina]]**
- 6 A place sacred to the **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Spirits of the wild|spirits of the wild]]**

sections.title: 1d6 resource
sections.body:
- 1 Game, but dangerous to hunt, like a **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Cave bear**|cave bear]]**, **[[27 - Huffel Peaks#**Fire drake**|fire drakes]]**, or goats on an impossible slope
- 2-3 Game, not dangerous but prone to bolt: a goat or deer, quail, rabbits, etc.
- 4 **[[50 - Useful or Valuable Flora#Useful or valuable flora|Useful or valuable flora]]**
- 5 A safe place to shelter/a shortcut/ an unexpected path
- 6 A vein of ore/mineral deposit

sections.title: Hazards
sections.body:
- Getting lost (maze of mountains, canyons, passes, and valleys)
- Sheer rock face to climb, and a reason to climb it
- Steep slopes, unstable footing (narrow paths, wet stone, scree, thin waterlogged soil, mud, crumbling rock)
- Flash flooding in valleys/on slopes
- An unexpected obstacle/hazard: go forward and risk it, or backtrack and lose half a day?
- **[[42 - The Steplands#1d6 natural beast or spirit|Mudslides and rockslides]]**
- **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Ice drake**|Altitude sickness]]**, especially near the peaks

sections.title: Harsh mountain weather
sections.body:
- Baking sun, dry air, lack of water
- Fog banks/low clouds, blocking vision and soaking everything
- Buffeting wind, drowning out voices and keeping you off-balance
- Powerful gusts, threatening to toss you aside
- Rain and wind rolling up from below
- Rain and sleet pelting you, soaking you, threatening to wash you away
- Thunder or lightning: making you flinch/jerk/jolt, triggering rockslides
- Brutal cold and ceaseless wind, leading to **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Ice drake**|hypothermia & frostbite]]**
- Snow and ice burying a pass or trail, making narrow paths impossible
- Snow and ice building up, forming an **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Ice drake**|avalanche]]**

sections.title: Volcanic activity
sections.body:
- Superheated air (d4 damage) or poisonous vapors (d4 damage, ignores armor), especially in enclosed spaces
- Searing hot stones (d6 damage)
- Steam vents/geysers (d8 damage, may be *forceful*)
- Scalding hot mud/water (d8 damage, ignores armor)
- Lava flows (d10+2 damage, *grabby*, *messy*, 3 piercing)

sections.title: Earthquake
sections.body:
- <sup>ä</sup> The ground shakes briefly, alarming but not dangerous
- <sup>ä</sup> Powerful tremors make footing unstable, knock rocks loose, cause animals to panic
- <sup>ä</sup> Tremors persist: people lose their footing, large rocks tumble (d8 damage, *forceful*), trails and paths are damaged
- <sup>ä</sup> It's a big one: **mudslides** and **[[42 - The Steplands#1d6 natural beast or spirit|rockslides]]**, **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Ice drake**|avalanches]]**, whole rockfaces come tumbling down

sections.title: People
sections.body:
- **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk|Hillfolk]]**
- **[[51 - Ustrina#Ustrina|Ustrina]]**
- Laborers (miners, woodcutters) from **[[22 - Gordin's Delve#Gordin's Delve|Gordin's Delve]]**
- Folk from **[[04 - Barrier Pass#Barrier Pass|Barrier Pass]]** or the **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#The Whitefang Mountains|Whitefang Mountains]]**
- Some southern adventurer or **[[29 - Lygos and the South#**Dangers**|antiquarian]]**, ruin-hunting
- **[[28 - The Labyrinth#Thraulgwyn|Thraulgwyn]]**, come up from the **[[28 - The Labyrinth#The Labyrinth|Labyrinth]]**

sections.title: Monsters
sections.body:
- Predators: **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Cave bear**|cave bears]]**, **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Cougar**|cougars]]**, **[[42 - The Steplands#1d6 natural beast or spirit|kleztigr]]**, **[[42 - The Steplands#1d6 natural beast or spirit|spitting drakes]]**
- **[[14 - The Flats#**Frythanc**|Frythanc]]**, nesting or hunting over a wide valley/plateau
- A **[[42 - The Steplands#**Gouzadn**|gouzadn]]**, living far from people
- The **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|ghosts]]** and **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|revenants]]** of those who died up here, recently or in ages past
- **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Spirits of the wild|Spirits of the wild]]**
- Constructs left by the **[[18 - Forge Lords#Forge Lords|Forge Lords]]** or the **[[43 - Stone Lords#Stone Lords|Stone Lords]]**
- **[[28 - The Labyrinth#**Magma worm**|Magma worms]]** near volcanic features
- A **[[18 - Forge Lords#**Draventao**|draventao]]**, out for a rare hunt or squatting in some ruin

sections.title: Caralandrao
sections.body:
*Horde*, *small*, *stealthy*, *hoarder*

**HP** 3; **Armor** 2 (shell)

**Damage** pincers d4 with disadvantage (*hand*, *grabby*)

**Special qualities** spider-climb, camouflaged shell

**Instinct** to feed on magic

- ä Sense mystic emanations from afar
- ä Get close without being noticed
- ä Skitter-skatter, dart and weave
- ä Make off with something magical

Are they crabs? Spiders? Hard to say. Whatever they are, they've got too many too-long limbs, shells that blend in with the rocky terrain, and an appetite for magical artifacts. They'll drag their prizes back to their nests to feed. They can suck the power right out of minor trinkets, but powerful arcana can feed a small colony indefinitely.

And yet, they keep venturing out to find more.

sections.title: Fire drake
sections.body:
*Group*, *aggressive*, *tough*

**HP** 10; **Armor** 2 (scales)

**Damage** bite d8 (*hand*, *grabby*)

**Special qualities** fireproof; caustic, burning blood

**Instinct** to get riled up easily

- ä Sound off in a deep, threatening chorus
- ä Blast a jet of burning, caustic blood from its eyes, d8 damage (*near*, *messy*, *reload*, ignores armor), but lose 1d4 HP
- ä Shake and worry and toss aside a foe it has bitten, d8+2 damage (*hand*, *forceful*)

Picture a horned lizard, but big as a large boar and with a mouth full of short jagged teeth. They're often encountered basking near hot springs or mudpots or other geothermal vents, soaking up the heat until they swell and glow like embers. Of course, once they notice you, they stop basking and start attacking.

**Something interesting:** Fire drake blood is caustic and catches fire when exposed to air; a jar of it deals d6 damage (*thrown*, *area*, *dangerous*, ignores armor, Value 1), but good luck harvesting it.

**Something useful:** The trick is to tap a vein with a metal tube, and drain the blood directly into a wax-sealed jar. Just… be careful.

sections.title: Frythanc fledgling
sections.body:
*Group*, *stealthy*

**HP** 6; **Armor** 0 (scales)

**Damage** talons d8+2 (hand, forceful), beak d8 with disadvantage (hand)

**Special qualities** exceptional eyesight; terrible hearing

**Instinct** to eat and grow

- ä Cling to rock faces, camouflaged
- ä Strafe their prey
- ä Knock prey off a ledge

The full-grown frythancs that hunt the **[[14 - The Flats#The Flats|Flats]]** are terrors. Their young, thankfully, keep to the mountains. There, they hunt goats and sheep and the occasional unwary traveler. The fledglings aren't nearly as dangerous as their full-grown parents, but still, let's see how you do when birds with 12-foot wingspans try to tear you off some switchback trail.

sections.title: The Labyrinth
sections.body:
These tunnels stretch out beneath the **[[27 - Huffel Peaks#Huffel Peaks|Huffel Peaks]]**, occasionally connecting to the surface. Perhaps they stretch further, to the **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#The Whitefang Mountains|Whitefangs]]** or the **[[42 - The Steplands#Spring|Steplands]]** or even to the **[[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|Great Wood]]**.

The **[[51 - Ustrina#Ustrina|Ustrina]]** come out of the Labyrinth when they visit Gordin's Delve to trade. But when asked, they claim it is not their home.

Other things come out of those tunnels, too, to prey on miners in the Delves. Survivors tell tales full of outlandish horror. Chief among those tales are the **[[28 - The Labyrinth#Thraulgwyn|thraulgwyn]]**, the "pale demons," white-haired raiders wielding weapons of bone and volcanic glass.

But the Labyrinth holds more than horrors. It holds riches, too. Ore and gems, seams of coal, and ancient wonders of the Forge Lords. Riches enough to spur the occasional expedition, ill-fated though it may be.

sections.title: Impressions
sections.body:
- The vast and hostile darkness, a quiet that sucks at the soul
- The humbling scale of the tunnels
- Air heavy with the smell of earth, stone, and sulfur
- Smooth walls, ribbed walls, knobby walls, cracked walls
- Distant dripping, clinking stone, footsteps, whispers, screams

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- Which of you has seen the Labyrinth in your dreams? What do you recall?
- Who entered the Labyrinth years ago, never to be seen again?
- What legendary Forge Lord site is said to be down there?
- Of all the tales that Delvers tell, of things coming out of the Labyrinth, which disturbs you most?
- What do you think the thraulgwyn, the pale demons, really are?

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- Thraulgwyn raid a mining crew and drag off captives.
- A prospector can lead the PCs to the **[[28 - The Labyrinth#The Portal Vault|Portal Vault]]**, but plans to feed them to her sister's wraith once there.
- Ustrina offer a reward for retrieving a relic from a ruin in the Labyrinth.
- An old map of the Labyrinth shows a path from Gordin's Delve to Moratia Rubra (the Forge Lords' old capitol).
- More and more Delvers hear a voiceless call from the Labyrinth. Some heed it.
- A **[[18 - Forge Lords#**Draventao**|draventao]]** emerges from its lair in the Labyrinth and preys on loggers, miners, and such.

sections.title: Navigating the Labyrinth
sections.body:
The Labyrinth is vast and sprawling. In theory, one could travel these tunnels from Gordin's Delve to the far end of the Huffel Peaks and the sea that lies beyond. It'd be madness, but one could try.

On any expedition into the Labyrinth:

- You'll need plenty of light sources and more supplies than you'd think
- You need a knowledgeable guide or an accurate map
- Even then, you risk getting lost
  - You must surmount any number of tight squeezes and difficult climbs
- The way is perilous, plagued with danger

sections.title: LOST IN THE DARK
sections.body:
When you *lose your way in the Labyrinth*, each member of your party consumes 1d4-1 supplies. Then, roll 2d6 once for the party and if…

- … you all had enough supplies, add +1;
- … you are led by someone wise to the Labyrinth's ways, add +1;
- … you are stumbling around blind, roll with disadvantage; and/or
- … one of you has the Mental Map move (from the Ranger playbook), roll with advantage.

**On a 10**+, you find your way to a portable light source, an exit from the Labyrinth, or a familiar path—GM's choice as to which; **on a 7-9**, you encounter **[[28 - The Labyrinth#Thraulgwyn|thraulgwyn]]**, a Forge Lord **[[18 - Forge Lords#1d6 site|ruin]]**, or an obstruction [[28 - The Labyrinth#**Nightmare tableaux**|The Labyrinth]]—GM's choice, and afterwards you might need to make this move again; **on a 6-**, decide as a group whether you find yourselves in the **[[28 - The Labyrinth#The Deeps|Deeps]]**, or stumble out of the Labyrinth days or weeks later, with all your debilities marked and only 1d8 HP remaining. If you choose the latter, each PC must pick 1:

- You have suffered a permanent injury or impairment—what and how?
- A follower of yours didn't make it out—who and why?
- You lost a treasured (or at least vital) item—what and how?
- Actually, you didn't make it out after all —why not?

sections.title: Terrain
sections.body:
Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

| 1d12 | terrain |
|---|---|
| 1-2 | Lave tube or magma worm tunnel |
| 3-4 | Braided/branching tunnels |
| 5-6 | Chamber, cavern, or alcove |
| 7-8 | Steps, terraces, ledges |
| 9-10 | Forge Lord construction, worked stone passage, or **[[18 - Forge Lords#**Ruin**\|ruin]]** |
| 11-12 | obstruction (see below), and roll 1d10 again |

| 1d12 | obstruction |
|---|---|
| 1-2 | A cave-in (dig through?) |
| 3-4 | A squeeze (wriggle through?) |
| 5-6 | A sheer rock face, like the earth just shifted (climb?) |
| 7-8 | A shaft/chimney (climb/squeeze?) |
| 9-10 | A flooded chamber (wade, swim, find an exit underwater?) |
| 11 | A chasm/rift, your path crossing it (jump, belay, or find a bridge?) |
| 12 | Intense heat, foul air, brittle crust, magma flows (good luck) |

For any given tunnel or chamber, pick or have someone roll for 1-3 features . Consider rolling 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

sections.title: 1d12 feature
sections.body:
| 1d12 | feature |
|---|---|
| 1 | Stream/spring/pool/lake |
| 2 | Stalactites/sharkteeth/knobby, ball-like stone protrusions |
| 3 | Rubble/debris/boulders (possibly fused into other stone) |
| 4 | Dome shaped cupola overhead |
| 5 | Hexagonal columns, packed tight |
| 6 | Apron/tongue/slipbank/gutters |
| 7 | Flat, cracked, a hardened lava lake |
| 8 | Steep slope/awkward ridges/undulating or ropy floor |
| 9 | Intense heat/cold |
| 10 | Cramped/narrow/low ceilings |
| 11 | Slippery mud/scree/oil/ooze |
| 12 | Glowmoths, glimmercaps, **[[46 - The Things Below#**Unnatural phenomena**\|red crystal]]**, other light sources |

sections.title: Discoveries
sections.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 discovery
sections.body:
- 1 A change of terrain (previous page) and roll again.
- 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.)
- 3 An encounter with thraulgwyn (see below)
- 4 Some other encounter (next column)
- 5 An opportunity (next column)
- 6 A point of interest [[28 - The Labyrinth#1d6 discovery|1d6]]

sections.title: 1d6 thraulgwyn
sections.body:
- 1 A whole tribal band, on the move
- 2-3 A gang on patrol, wary but curious
- 4 One or two, under attack by a rival band
- 5 Foragers, eager to invite guests home
- 6 Two groups in a tense parley

See [[28 - The Labyrinth#**Thraulgwyn**|The Labyrinth]] for more on the thraulgwyn.

sections.title: 1d6 other encounter
sections.body:
- 1 A thrall [[46 - The Things Below#**Cultist**|The Things Below]], terribly marked, living down here all alone
- 2 A swarm of small, cave-dwelling vermin-cave spiders, rockrats, or bats [[42 - The Steplands#1d6 natural beast or spirit|The Steplands]]-alarming and distracting, but essentially harmless
- 3 A ghost [[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Death and the Undying]], revenant [[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|Death and the Undying]], or spirit of the wild [[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|Spirits of the Wild]]: curious or indifferent
- 4 A metal construct [[18 - Forge Lords#**Metal constructs**|Forge Lords]], following ancient orders or awaiting activation
- 5 Adventurers/prospectors from Gordin's Delve, wary, maybe lost
- 6 Ustrina [[51 - Ustrina|Ustrina]]: surface-bound, returning to the Portal Vault [[28 - The Labyrinth#1d6 discovery|1d6]], or seeking something

sections.title: 1d6 opportunity
sections.body:
- 1 A good place to rest/hide/ ambush foes
- 2 Potable water, edible fungi, cave fish, rockrats, etc.
- 3 Portable light source: glimmercaps, red crystal [[46 - The Things Below#**Unnatural phenomena**|The Things Below]], glowing makerglass [[43 - Stone Lords#Various treasures|Various treasures]], etc.
- 4 A vein of coal/ore/copper/gold/gems
- 5 An artifact of the Forge Lords [[18 - Forge Lords#**Artifacts**|Forge Lords]], Barrow Builders [[05 - Barrow Builders#**Artifacts**|Barrow Builders]], or Things Below [[46 - The Things Below#**Unnatural phenomena**|The Things Below]]
- 6 Sign of their objective/quarry

sections.title: 1d6 point of interest
sections.body:
- 1 A place corrupted by the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**, perhaps a shrine tended by **[[28 - The Labyrinth#Thraulgwyn|thraulgwyn]]**) or **[[46 - The Things Below#**Cultist**|cultists]]**
- 2 A **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|haunted site]]**
- 3 A **notable site** (see below)
- 4 A **[[28 - The Labyrinth#1d6 thraulgwyn|thraulgwyn settlement]]**
- 5 A **[[18 - Forge Lords#**Ruin**|Forge Lord site]]**, perhaps inhabited by Ustrina
- 6 A path up and out into the **[[27 - Huffel Peaks#Huffel Peaks|Huffel Peaks]]**, or down into the **[[28 - The Labyrinth#The Deeps|Deeps]]**

sections.title: Notable sites
sections.body:
- Heat, choking air, and a resonant rumble give way to a magma lake, air a-shimmer with heat, toxic gas.
- A vast subterranean lake, perhaps the same one that connects to the **[[22 - Gordin's Delve#**The Delves**|Second Delve]]**.
- The **Hanging Forest**, a long, wide tunnel full of skinny roots dangling from the surface. Full of crickets and things that eat them.
- The **Mirrorkeep**, a Forge Lord outpost, largely intact, lined with reflective mosaics. Sits over a bottomless (?) pit.
- The **Dreaming Breath**, a hot spring pool. Mold and fungi cake nearby stone. The waters restore vigor but cause stupefying euphoria.
- The **Red Glutton,** a horned, 40' tall, toadlike statue with an ever-burning brazier and fist-sized rubies (Value 4) for eyes. Revered by thraulgwyn.
- The locked doors of the **Portal Vault** (next column), cold and imposing.

sections.title: The Portal Vault
sections.body:
Five hours from the Delves (if you know the shortest route) stands a pair of double doors: 30' tall, bronze, covered in runic wards and reliefs of Forge Lord glories. A cunning lock seals them. Only six keys exist; the **[[51 - Ustrina#Ustrina|Ustrina]]** hold five.

Inside is a vast octagonal space, 60' from floor to angled ceiling. Six of its corners feature 20' tall niches, each housing a large **[[18 - Forge Lords#**Metal constructs**|metal construct]]** that attacks any party that doesn't bear one of the keys.

A slab of white stone, 30' tall and 3' thick, stands upright in the center of the vault. Each side is etched with a magic circle. It's a **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Primordial powers**|portal]]**, connected to those in the **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#The Ruined Tower|Ruined Tower]]**, **[[47 - Three Coven Lake#Three Coven Lake|Three Coven Lake]]**, **[[52 - Vor Svetelik#Vor Svetelik|Vor Svetelik]]**, and so forth. It is fully functional, and can be used via **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A time-worn missive|Opening the Way]]**.

The Ustrina use this place to travel to Gordin's Delve, and to return to their far-flung homes. No one sect controls the Vault, and they treat it as neutral ground. But that doesn't apply to non-Ustrina.

sections.title: Thraulgwyn
sections.body:
Their hair is white, their skin pallid and painted with lime and mud and blood. Their pupils are wide, like gaping holes. They file their teeth sharp, and craft their weapons from obsidian, stone, and bone. They tan human flesh for leather.

The folks of Gordin's Delve know them as thraulgwyn, "pale demons," terrors that emerge from the Labyrinth and drag off friends and loved ones.

**Something interesting:** The thraulgwyn descended from **[[05 - Barrow Builders#Barrow Builders|Barrow Builders]]**, from the followers of certain warlords and sorcerer-kings who fled into the Labyrinth when their subjects rose against them. They were already depraved disciples of the Things Below, and after generations of inbreeding, cave-dwelling, and cannibalism, they're barely human.

**Something useful:** Except, that's not really true. They have language (a dialect of the Hillfolk tongue) and outsiders have made deals with them. They hate other tribes more than they hate surface-dwellers. And while they do eat the dead, most of their food is foraged, even cultivated.

**Tip:** Cock your head. Dart your eyes. Hold a hand up to block the light. Try not to touch your teeth with your tongue.

sections.title: Names
sections.body:
Breton- or Cornish-inspired (after the Barrow Builders), but clipped: Aln, Az, Benez, Clem, Con, Dav, Demel, Gryf, Gwun, Humra, Iarn, Ilcar, Ilcu, Kyt, Lum, Mar, Mera, Nez, Rud, San, Tek, Wurg.

Tribal bands are named "Uskl-" (the People of ) and a truncated **Barrow Builder** name [[05 - Barrow Builders|Barrow Builders]]: Uskl-Azel, Uskl-Judak, Uskl-Enor, Uskl-Renza, Uskl-Wioc, etc.

sections.title: Tribal bands
sections.body:
Here's a typical tribal band:

**Size** *hamlet* (~25 to 75 souls)

sections.title: Resources
sections.body:
- Gathering (fungus, fish, water)
- Farming (fungus, grubs, bugs)
- Scavenging (Forge Lord sites)
- Raiding (neighboring tribes)
- Cannibalism

sections.title: Defenses +0
sections.body:
- Warlike culture
- Bone clubs/spears tipped with obsidian
- Corrupted gifts
- Magical artifacts

Each tribal band descends from a particular warlord or sorcerer-king of the Barrow Builders and the disciples who followed them into exile. And each band is likewise corrupted by a different one of the Things Below.

For each tribal band, pick or create the **[[46 - The Things Below#**Emanation**|entity they worship]]**. Pick or roll for a gift and a mark that entity bestows on their people. Then pick or roll an asset and a quirk. Finally, identify 1d6 **artifacts** of the Barrow Builders, **[[18 - Forge Lords#**Artifacts**|Forge Lords]]**, or **[[46 - The Things Below#**Unnatural phenomena**|Things Below]]** that they hold as sacred treasures.

| 1d6 | gift |
|---|---|
| 1 | Eat flesh to regenerate wounds |
| 2 | Soundless movement |
| 3 | Immunity to poison, fire, and cold |
| 4 | Brutal strength (+2 damage, *forceful*) |
| 5 | Throat sac, exhales poison gas (d4, *close*, *area*, *reload*, *debilitating*) |
| 6 | Wordless communication |

sections.title: 1d6 mark
sections.body:
- 1 No eyes (they echolocate)
- 2 Too-long limbs, fingers, necks
- 3 Covered in weeping sores
- 4 Grotesque/horrific behaviors (even for thraulgwyn)
- 5 Subject to hallucinations
- 6 Fear open flame

See [[46 - The Things Below#**1d12** origin|The Things Below]] for more gifts and marks.

sections.title: 1d6 asset
sections.body:
- 1 Metal weapons, lovingly cared for
- 2 Means to brew a paralytic poison
- 3 An additional gift
- 4 Greater numbers (a *village*, ~150 souls)
- 5 A pet **[[46 - The Things Below#**Wretch**|emanation]]**
- 6 Still led by their **[[05 - Barrow Builders#**Deathless ones**|deathless]]** founder, and roll again

sections.title: 1d6 quirk
sections.body:
- 1 Hubristic and overconfident
- 2 Bear an additional mark
- 3 Build structures out of bones
- 4 Venture out to the surface at night
- 5 Revere **[[28 - The Labyrinth#**Magma worm**|magma worms]]**
- 6 Intrigued by surface-dwellers

sections.title: Trade & Barter
sections.body: The PCs must be on civil terms with a group of thraulgwyn before any of this matters.

sections.title: Commonly available
sections.body:
The following would be special items in Stonetop, but they are commonly available among the thraulgwyn. The PCs can acquire them here without rolling, but need to trade something of equal Value. Meat, clean water, and whisky are always in demand, as are durable goods and tools. But thraulgwyn have little use for coin or luxuries, and are suspicious of most other surface food.

| goods                                    | value   |
|------------------------------------------|---------|
| Set of bone tools (needles, hooks, etc.) | 0       |
| Pouch of hallucinogenic spores ( use)    | 0       |
| Supplies ( uses, crude , unsavory )      | 0       |
| Corpse-wax candle (last ~1 hour, close ) | 1*      |
| Bone or stone weapon ( crude )           | 1*      |
| Glimmercap oil ( hours, hand , crude )   | 1*      |
| Elaborately scrimshawed bone item        | +1      |

- *-1 if sold/traded outside the Labyrinth.

sections.title: Special items
sections.body:
The following might be acquired from or traded to the thraulgwyn, but the PCs must roll Trade & Barter to do so.

When PCs Trade & Barter among a band of thraulgwyn , they use the local Prosperity (-1 by default) with disadvantage because it's a hamlet . On a 6, they don't mark XP-this band simply doesn't have or want what the PCs are after/selling.

| goods | value |
|---|---|
| Red crystal 'candle' ( hand , magical ) | 1 |
| Vial of paralytic powder ( use, close ) | 1 |
| Meat provisions, don't ask ( uses) | 1* |
| Human-hide armor (1 armor) | 1* |
| pouch of [[18 - Forge Lords#**Anthracite**\|anthracite]] (~10 lumps, each lasts ~8 hours, *close*, *area*) | 2* |
| Red crystal 'torch'( reach , area , magical) | 3* |
| Pottery, wool, textiles, wooden items | +1* |
| Bronze, iron, or steel items/weapons | +1* |
| An object of black iron [[36 - Primordial Powers#**Primordial powers**\|Primordial Powers]] | +2 |
| Scavenged makerglass [[43 - Stone Lords#Various treasures\|Various treasures]] | +2 |

* -1 if sold/traded outside the Labyrinth.

Thraulgwyn are always willing to accept people or corpses in trade: a living wretch or a dodgy corpse is Value 1, a healthy specimen is Value 2, a big healthy surface dweller might be Value 3.

sections.title: Services and Favors
sections.body:
PCs don't need to roll Trade & Barter for the following, but they will likely need to Persuade people-even if they offer something of suitable Value.

| service/favor                            |   value |
|------------------------------------------|---------|
| Araid against a rival tribe              |       0 |
| Directions to a spot in the Labyrinth    |       1 |
| A day's food and shelter                 |       1 |
| A guide for a few days                   |       2 |
| A temporary truce with a rival tribe     |       2 |
| Directions to a forbidden or sacred site |       3 |
| Ritual to commune with a Thing Below     |       3 |

sections.title: The Deeps
Impressions
sections.body:
- Endless tunnels, going and going
- Thoughts drifting as you trudge along, jerking awake
- The darkness, deeper and absolute
- When did you last eat, drink, or rest?
- Wait, who are these people again?
- Dreamlike acceptance of whatever weirdness you encounter

sections.title: Arriving
sections.body:
When the PCs first arrive, describe their endless slog using the impressions above. Intersperse your description with questions like these:

- What pleasant detail about the surface are you struggling to recall?
- What do you fear you'll find here?
- What nightmare keeps replaying in your mind?
- Who'd be better off if you never made it home?
- When did you start suspecting that \_\_ isn't really who they say they are?

After setting the mood, present a **nightmare tableau** (below). If the PCs escape it, they are then **traversing the Deeps** (next page).

sections.title: Nightmare tableaux
sections.body: Pick or roll for the **[[28 - The Labyrinth#**Nightmare tableaux**|terrain]]**, including features, and combine with a **theme** of the **[[46 - The Things Below#**Emanation**|Things Below]]**. Pick or roll for the crux of the tableau and its instinct. Create a scene or situation that combines these elements, riffing on whatever internal conflicts the PCs are dealing with.

sections.title: 1d6 crux
sections.body:
- 1 Another traveler, trapped here
- 2 Details pulled from the PCs' minds
- 3 Simulacra of known NPCs, convinced that they are real
- 4 Simulacra of the PCs, convinced they are real
- 5 An **[[46 - The Things Below#**Unnatural phenomena**|artifact]]** of the Things Below, the tableau's heart
- 6 Roll twice, combine

sections.title: 1d6 instinct
sections.body:
- 1 To traumatize/punish them
- 2 To weaken/hurt/degrade them
- 3 To **corrupt** them [[46 - The Things Below#**Corrupted being**|The Things Below]]
- 4 To turn them against each other
- 5 To trap or ensnare them
- 6 To coalesce into a full-fledged

**[[46 - The Things Below#**Wretch**|emanation]]**

Play out the scene with dreamlike logic but very real potential consequences for the PCs. Make liberal use of **[[46 - The Things Below#**Voice of the Eternal Maw**|whispers and visions]]** and **[[46 - The Things Below#**On a 6-**, pick 1:|unnatural phenomena]]**. Should a simulacrum leave the tableau with the PCs, decide for yourself what happens; don't be afraid to get weird.

sections.title: Traversing the Deeps
sections.body: Time flows strangely down here. Visitors don't really need food or drink. Sleep is fleeting. Light sources might last indefinitely, only to snuff out at the worst moment. Distances are fluid, dreamlike. Those who wander without a purpose are sure to be trapped, or worse.

sections.title: SEEK OUT
sections.body:
When you *seek something in the Deeps*, whoever leads the way rolls +WIS. Unless they bear a holy light, they roll with disadvantage. **On a 10+**, pick 1 from the list below; **on a 7-9**, encounter a danger, but gain +1 Progress; **on a 6-**, lose all Progress and encounter a danger.

- Encounter a danger, but gain +2 Progress
- You find a reprieve, a pocket of normalcy and a safe place to Make Camp

When you *encounter a danger*, roll the Die of Fate to see what it is.

*The PCs get lost in the Labyrinth, one of their followers dying in the process. Now they've wandered into the Deeps, full of guilt and recrimination.*

*For their first tableau, I roll a 7 for terrain ("steps, terraces, ledges") and an 11 for a feature ("slippery mud/scree/oil/ ooze"). For a theme, I get 12: "Death/undeath/loss/grief… (skulls, tombs, pyres)." Looking at the crux list, I just pick "simulacra of known NPCs," and I roll a 5 for instinct ("to trap or ensnare them").* 

*The tunnel opens to a cavern of everwider terraces, sloping down out of sight, covered with scree and rubble. But the scree isn't stone, it's bones and skulls, who knows how deep? If they slip, the bones cut them, drag them down, try to bury them. As they cross, that dead follower starts calling for help, trapped in the bone-rubble, and don't you dare leave me again, you bastards!*

sections.title: 1d6 danger
sections.body:
- 1 **[[28 - The Labyrinth#**Soithech**|Tcaventes]]**, wandering
- 2 An **[[46 - The Things Below#**Wretch**|emanation]]**
- 3 A huge **[[28 - The Labyrinth#**Magma worm**|magma worm]]**
- 4 A **nightmare tableau** (previous page)
- 5 An ordeal: heat, cold, hunger, intrusive thoughts, false memories, etc.
- 6 Someone else lost down here, maybe from a different time, paranoid or at least extremely wary
