When you *escape or otherwise survive the danger and you hold 3+ Progress,* you find your goal. If you continue to seek your current goal, keep your Progress and make this move again. If you choose a new goal, lose all Progress.

sections.title: The Utterwell
sections.body:
At the very bottom of the Deeps stands a throne of **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Primordial powers**|black iron]]**, the seat of **[[28 - The Labyrinth#**Soithech**|Tcaventes, Shackle and Key]]**. Behind the throne looms a black iron door. An **[[18 - Forge Lords#**1d6** architectural elements|orichalcum]]** bolt holds the door fast. The bolt is bound with the **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Ineffable Words|Ineffable Word]]** for "seal."

The black iron door opens onto the shore of the Utterwell: a sunless sea, still and black as night. No light penetrates its depths, not even the light of Helior. Anyone or anything who enters the sea is gone, consumed by the Things Below.

When you *stand before the Utterwell and call one of the Things Below by name*, it will emerge in all its terrible glory. Woe be upon you. And if the door is still open, woe be upon the world.

sections.title: Hazards
sections.body:
- Getting lost
- Running out of light
- Cold, especially if they get wet
- Heat, gas, **[[27 - Huffel Peaks#**Volcanic activity**|volcanic activity]]**
- Treacherous footing, slopes, climbs
- Getting stuck in a squeeze
- An unexpected drop-off
- Cave-ins, rock-falls
- **[[27 - Huffel Peaks#**Volcanic activity**|Earthquakes!]]**
- Panic in the face of the above

sections.title: Monsters and such
sections.body:
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Ghosts]]** and **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|revenants]]** of those who never got out
- **[[18 - Forge Lords#**Metal constructs**|Metal constructs]]**, guarding something or on some task
- **[[27 - Huffel Peaks#**Volcanic activity**|Caralandrao]]**, seeking magic
- **[[27 - Huffel Peaks#**Fire drake**|Fire drakes]]**, near magma
- A **[[42 - The Steplands#1d6 natural beast or spirit|grochslon]]**, near a stream, lake, or cave mouth
- Explorers: **[[29 - Lygos and the South#Adventurer|adventurers]]**, an **[[29 - Lygos and the South#Adventurer|antiquarian]]**, a **[[46 - The Things Below#**Cultist**|sorcerer]]**, etc.
- **[[51 - Ustrina#Ustrina|Ustrina]]**, en route or in search of something
- A **[[18 - Forge Lords#**Draventao**|draventao]]**, making its lair down here
- **[[46 - The Things Below#**1d12** origin|Corrupted beings]]**: bats, rockrats, spirits, any of the above
- **[[46 - The Things Below#**Wretch**|Emanations]]** of the Things Below, seeping out of the Deeps

sections.title: Corruption, as one goes deeper
sections.body:
- **[[46 - The Things Below#**Voice of the Eternal Maw**|Disquiet and unease]]**
- **[[46 - The Things Below#**Voice of the Eternal Maw**|Whispers and visions]]**, coming from below
- **[[46 - The Things Below#**On a 6-**, pick 1:|Unnatural phenomena]]**, in or near the Deeps

sections.title: Magma worm
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *primordial*, *large*, *terrifying* **HP** 19; **Armor** 4 (rocky plates, lacks vitals) **Damage** bite d10+3 (*hand*, *close*, *grabby*, *messy*, 1 piercing) or searing touch d10 (*hand*, *area*)

**Special qualities** blind; tremorsense; fireproof; superheated body; can move only forward, can't back up

**Instinct** to devour minerals and metal

- ä Eat through solid rock
- ä Burst forth from a tunnel wall
- ä Exude choking, sulfurous gas
- ä Melt metal that pierces it

Creatures of the first age, things of fire and stone. They're segmented, 3' thick and 20' long, covered in overlapping plates that glow red at the seams. The air heat-shimmers around them. They have no eyes, just a great mouth-opening and concentric rows of grinding teeth that melt and burrow through rock. Winding tunnels stretch behind them, lined with slag hardened into ropy stone.

The worms described here are mere centuries old, the ones most likely to be encountered. Older and larger ones survive in the Deeps, up to 10' wide and over a 100' long (*huge*, +6 HP, +2 damage and roll 1d12 instead of 1d10). In eons past, even larger worms carved out much of the Labyrinth.

sections.title: Thraulgwyn raider
sections.body:
*Group*, *corrupted*, *stealthy*, *organized*, *cautious*

**HP** 6; **Armor** 0 or 1 (human hide) **Damage** obsidian-tipped bone spear d8 (*close*, *thrown*, *crude*), femur d8 (*close*, *crude*), hair-rope net (*thrown*, *crude*, *grabby*), bite d6 (*hand*)

**Special qualities** gifted and marked according to their tribe [[28 - The Labyrinth#1d6 discovery|1d6]]

**Instinct** to glorify their tribe

- ä Use their gift
- ä Move quietly through the dark
- ä Lure prey into an ambush
- ä Pounce with sudden ferocity
- ä Flee if things go south

They roam the tunnels in gangs of 3-5, playing cat and mouse with rival bands. Should they notice outsiders, they might try to ambush them, capture them, and eat them. But they might also greet them, offer to trade, try to use them against their foes.

Some raiding parties are led by a champion (*leader*, +2 HP, deals damage with advantage), who bears one of their tribal band's sacred artifacts.

sections.title: Soithech
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *corrupted*, *devious*, *magical*, *cautious*

**HP** 18; **Armor** 5 (resilience, human hide), 1 vs. bronze

**Damage** obsidian knife d10 (*hand*, *crude*) **Special qualities** powerfully gifted and marked, as per their tribe [[28 - The Labyrinth#1d6 discovery|1d6]]

**Instinct** to maintain their status

- ä Foresee an opportunity or dire event
- ä Play parties against each other
- ä Use their gift, or one of their tribe's sacred artifacts
- ä Embody an aspect of their master

Most thraulgwyn settlements are ruled by a soithech, a "vessel," who claims descent from the tribe's founder. They preach old ways and old hatreds, keeping their people in line with prophecies of glory and doom—some of which are even true.

A soitech's power is not just political. They manifest their tribe's gift and mark with great intensity, and they can let the power of their patron Thing Below flow through them. When they do, they change to embody one of its aspects [[46 - The Things Below#**Emanation**|The Things Below]] and gain up to 3 additional gifts [[46 - The Things Below#**1d12** origin|The Things Below]]. Adjust their stat block accordingly.

sections.title: Tcaventes, Shackle and Key
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *primordial*, *construct*, *magical*, *terrifying*, *corrupted*

**HP** 25; **Armor** 6 (hard, warded, lacks vitals), 4 vs. black iron

**Damage** word of force d12+3 (*near*, *area*, *forceful*) or four fists d12+3 with advantage (*close*, *reach*, *forceful*)

**Special qualities** ageless; immune to the elements; perceives thought and matter; omnilingual

**Instinct** to hide evidence of their failure, even from themselves

- ä Sense and neutralize threats with cold precision
- ä Shift the environment to create or close an opening
- ä Seal or unseal: a door, a binding, a mouth, a secret, etc.
- ä Blaze suddenly with the blinding light of creation

They are an **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Other primordial entities**|archon]]**, forged by the First Creators to stand vigil over the **[[28 - The Labyrinth#**Tcaventes, Shackle and Key**|Utterwell]]** and to act as a bulwark against the Things Below. They are Tcaventes, Shackle and Key. A flawless guardian.

Or at least, they were. Over eons, even they succumbed to the whispers of the Things Below. The corruption leads to lapses of discipline that let their charges' influence seep out through the Deeps. It amplifies their guilt, which leads to denial strong enough to drive Tcaventes mad.

Should the PCs encounter Tcaventes on their throne, they will find the archon focused, vigilant, and stern, with only a hint of their unravelling mind.

sections.title: Lygos and the South
sections.body:
Picture a sun-drenched land, a semi-arid climate. Roads stretch out between cities and oases. Farmers tend to orchards of figs, dates, and olives. Boundless fields of grain sway in gentle winds.

Picture palaces, temples, and handsome estates. Priests, scholars, and bureaucrats bustle about. Politicians squabble over laws and votes. Merchants hawk goods from the far corners of the world. Dozens of languages can be heard in streets and taverns.

Picture armies on the march in tight formation, spears and shields and helms a-glinting. Plumes of dust rise from thousands of stamping feet. Horses neigh and harnesses clatter. Camels bray. Elephants trumpet.

Picture opulence and splendor, silks and feathers, spices piled high in lurid color. Picture hunger and desperation, urchins risking a hand to steal a loaf of bread. Picture the rich and powerful above mundane concern. Picture the poor and downtrodden consumed by nothing but.

sections.title: Names
sections.body: Inspired by Greek, Hebrew, Persian, and Arabic: Agatte, Aref, Alix, Baraz, Canan, Darya, Demetra, Elene, Elios, Fotios, Faruza, Golza, Iasos, Iona, Kyriakos, Marika, Maayan, Osher, Natasa, Nivola, Rinat, Stamat, Thecla, Zhaleh.

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- Do they fear deep water down south?
- Do they ply the seas or shun them?
- What are the most common languages down there?
- What clothing/markings/traits are distinctive to Lygosi? To southerners in general?
- What festival or holiday do they celebrate this time of year?
- What game/pastime/food are Lygosi/ southerners known for?
- Do they worship \_\_? How does that worship differ from up here in Stonetop?
- Who (or what) do they worship that isn't well-known up here?

sections.title: Personal questions
sections.body:
If a PC hails from the south, ask some or all of the following.

- Are you from Lygos proper? Or some where else down south?
- What little thing do you miss about life down there?
- What do you miss the least?
- What drove you to leave?
- Who did you leave behind?
- What southern practice do you still observe?
- What was most surprising to you about life in these parts?
- Are there other southerners in Stonetop? Who?

sections.title: A storied past
Hooks
sections.body:
- Catastrophe (war, plague, famine, etc.) sends refugees flooding north.
- A deposed royal flees to the north incognito, pursued by assassins.
- An old foe/rival/creditor of a PC (or an important NPC) comes calling.
- A southern antiquarian arrives, looking to excavate a specific Maker-ruin (possibly one in Stonetop).
- Members of a persecuted mystery cult arrive from down South; if Stonetop won't accept them, they take up residence in the **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#The Ruined Tower|Ruined Tower]]**.

sections.title: There and back again
sections.body:
A typical trip to and from Lygos involves:

- An entire season of travel (80-90 days), there and back.
- Joining a merchant caravan for the trip there, and another one for the trip back (Value 2 each way).
- While in Lygos, either…
- … a purse of silvers (Value 2) to pay for room, board, and bribes; or
- … a resident of Lygos willing to put you up and cover your costs.
- The risk of misadventure, robbery, betrayal, or sundry other setbacks at every turn.

Don't play out trips to Lygos in detail! Doing so would take dozens of sessions and could easily derail your campaign. Unless you and your group actively decide to leave Stonetop behind and play a very different sort of game, then you should handle PC trips to Lygos off-camera.

Use **[[16 - Writing Moves and Love Letters#**Writing love letters**|love letters]]** to gloss over such a journey. Work with the player(s) to identify:

- What they hope to accomplish
- How they plan to go about it
- What valuables/resources they bring with them

Think about what might go wrong, what might go well, and what obligations or simmering troubles they're leaving behind. Think about elements of the setting or character that you want to explore or highlight. Weave these together into a love letter for each PC who makes the journey, and present it to each player when their PC returns to Stonetop.

If some PCs remained behind while the others went to Lygos, you can write love letters for the PCs who stayed behind, summarizing events back home. Or, you can play one or more sessions with just those players. Or you can replace the travelling PCs with temporary PCs for a few sessions. Work with your group to decide on the best approach.

sections.title: Merchants
sections.body:
A few dozen caravans make their way up from Lygos every year. A typical merchant caravan is made up of:

- 3-12 separate merchants, each with at least one wagon or cart
- 2-4 helpers per merchant (often family members)
- Horses, mules, donkeys, or other beasts of burden
- **[[29 - Lygos and the South#**Assassin**|Caravan guards]]**, ~6 per merchant

Most caravans stop in in Marshedge to trade, but some enterprising souls will veer into the **[[42 - The Steplands#The Steplands|Steplands]]** and trade with the **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk|Hillfolk]]** directly. Others keep going north to one of the **[[34 - North Manmarch#**Hamlet**|Manmarcher's hillforts]]**. A rare few merchants make their way to **[[22 - Gordin's Delve#Gordin's Delve|Gordin's Delve]]**, and some of those stop in Stonetop.

sections.title: . trade opportunities .
sections.body:
If the PCs get a trade opportunity (from the Seasons Change move), a southern merchant might offer the following:

- A good exchange rate, two or even three Value 2 items for 1 Surplus.
- 1-4 Value 2 items or even a Value 3 item, in exchange for some creepy arcanum or relic of the past (might have repercussions)
- A unit of exotic trade goods (Value 3) in exchange for a bundle of **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Feathered drake**|drake feathers]]**, **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Feathered drake**|gylglyd flowers]]**, a **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Raselbaedd**|raselbaedd hide]]**, etc.
- A suit of armor or weapon of war, forged from **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Primordial powers**|black iron]]**, as payment for helping them recover some lost or stolen goods (Value 3).
- An arcanum of **[[36 - Primordial Powers#Artifacts|primordial power]]**, from one of **[[30 - The Makers#The Makers|the Makers]]**, or related to **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|the Things Below]]**.

sections.title: Adventurer
sections.body:
*Group*, *hoarder*, *devious*, [pick 1 more] **HP** 8; **Armor** 1 to 3 (skill, armor, and/or shield)

**Damage** whatever's at hand d8 (*hand*, *close*, maybe *forceful* or *messy*)

**Instinct** to stir up trouble

- ä Sniff out an opportunity, or reveal one
- ä Escape danger in a way that endangers others
- ä Act without scruple
- ä Use a particular knack or skill

They make their way north now and then: scoundrels, mercenaries, treasure-hunters… folk who just can't make a go of a normal life. They'll show up in Marshedge or Stonetop or Gordin's Delve, in search of some big score. Or running from the consequences of the last one. Or both.

sections.title: Antiquarian
sections.body: *Solitary*, *hoarder*, *cautious*, *cunning*, *magical*

sections.title: HP 12; Armor 0
sections.body:
**Damage** iron mattock d8 w/disadvantage (*close*, *messy*, *awkward*) or knife d6 w/disadvantage (*hand*)

**Special qualities** often in possession of **arcana** (page [[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#An old scroll case , fragile|508\)]] and willing to use them

**Instinct** to indulge their curiosity or to secure the next big score

- ä Expound at length upon a topic
- ä Open, touch, or tinker with something
- ä Use a minor spell or artifact
- ä Hang back and let the help face the danger

Among the southern well-to-do, the glorious past is a point of pride. Wealthy households keep pet scholars on retainer, and sponsor the study of ruins and antiquities. There's a brisk trade in artifacts and relics. Collectors pay handsomely for items of true power.

The south is full of ruins, but it's also teeming with people. The best sites have already been dug up. But up north, at the World's End? Why, one can barely swing a shovel without hitting some relic of the past, just waiting to be excavated. And studied. And sold.

sections.title: Assassin
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *stealthy*, *devious*

**HP** 12; **Armor** 1 (skill)

**Damage** dagger 1d10 (*hand*, 1 piercing) or garrote d8 (*hand*, *grabby*, ignores armor)

**Instinct** to finish the job (nothing personal)

- ä Stalk their target at length ä Pass unnoticed, in shadow or disguise
- ä Poison someone
- ä Slip away, try again later

Only in "civilized" lands is there such demand for murder that it's an actual profession.

 *Horde*, *organized*, *cautious*, *alert* **HP** 3; **Armor** 2 (shield, leather cuirass)

**Damage** iron spear d6 (close, thrown) or dagger d6 (hand)

**Professional instinct** to keep their ward(s) safe

- ä Spot or suspect trouble
- ä Take sensible precautions
- ä Put themselves in harm's way

**Craven instinct** to avoid real danger

- ä Suggest a safer course of action
- ä Make a show of force
- ä Flee, surrender, or betray their client

sections.title: Conquering army
Invasion Threat (affliction) Instinct to subjugate
sections.body:
l A company (200-400 troops) establishes a foothold in the **[[40 - South Manmarch#South Manmarch|South Manmarch]]**, proceeds to subjugate local **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk|Hillfolk]]** l 2,000 soldiers march on **[[32 - Marshedge#Marshedge|Marshedge]]**, their council surrenders, Brennan and the Claws flee l The army invades the **[[34 - North Manmarch#North Manmarch|North Manmarch]]**, conquering a number of settlements, hillforts l Remaining Manmarch chiefs surrender; a company of soldiers besieges **[[22 - Gordin's Delve#Gordin's Delve|Gordin's Delve]]**; another marches on Stonetop and demands surrender l **Impending doom:** Stonetop is conquered and a garrison established

**Stakes** Can the Hillfolk be united against the southerners? What about the Manmarchers? Will the PCs ally with Brennan?

**Cavalry**

*Horde*, *large*, *organized*, *terrifying* **HP** 10; **Armor** 2 (shield, iron scale) **Damage** iron-tipped long spear d6+4 (*reach*, *forceful*), or sword d6+4 (*close*, *forceful*), or bow d6 (*far*)

**Instinct** to attack where the foe is weak

- ä Circle around their enemy
- ä Attack on the move
- ä Form a wedge and charge, d6+4 w/ advantage (*reach*, *close*, *hand*, *area*, *forceful*, *messy*, 1 piercing)

**Infantry**

*Horde*, *organized*

**HP** 3; **Armor** 2 (shield, linen cuirass) **Damage** iron-tipped long spear d6 (*reach*), or short sword d6 (*hand*, *close*)

**Instinct** to follow orders

- ä Form a phalanx
- ä Pin down or hem in their foe

**Legionary**

 *Horde*, *organized*, *hardy*, *disciplined*

**HP** 7; **Armor** 3 (shield, iron scale)

**Damage** iron short sword d6 (*hand*, *close*), or spear d6 (*close*, *thrown*)

**Instinct** to conquer

- ä Fight in tight-knit formation
- ä Hold the line
- ä Advance implacably

**Skirmisher**

 *Horde*, *stealthy*, *organized*

**HP** 3; **Armor** 0

**Damage** long bow d6 (*far*), or sling d6 (*near*, *reload*), or short swords (*hand*, *close*)

**Instinct** to avoid a fair fight

- ä Unleash a volley, then retreat
- ä Cover distance quickly
- ä Attack a flank

sections.title: The Makers
sections.body:
These lands were once ruled by giants, who stood two or three times the height of a full-grown human. They were powerful not only in stature but also in learning, wisdom, and magic. They established mighty civilizations and left many wonders in their wake.

Five different groups of Makers helped to shape the region now called World's End. These included…

- … the **[[18 - Forge Lords#Forge Lords|Forge Lords]]**, passionate artisans who bent fire and metal to their will;
- … the **[[24 - Green Lords#Green Lords|Green Lords]]**, who bred and shaped all forms of life and bound the spirits of the wild to their will;
- … the **[[38 - Rime Lords#Rime Lords|Rime Lords]]**, who embraced the cold of the far north and practiced all manner of ascetic disciplines and esoteric arts;
- … the **[[43 - Stone Lords#Stone Lords|Stone Lords]]**, who carved cities out of bedrock, raised towering monuments, and sang stone and crystal and glass into impossible shapes; and
- … the **[[45 - Tempest Lords#Tempest Lords|Tempest Lords]]**, who harnessed the primal forces of sky and sea, who sailed forth to conquer much of the world.

The Tempest Lords' empire was the oldest and greatest nation of Makers, and for a long time the others lived in their shadow. But hubris led to the empire's ruin, and allowed the other Makers to rise in power. There were rivalries between them, certainly, and border conflicts, but for centuries they largely lived in peace. They traded goods and ideas, even cooperated on grand projects like the **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#The Makers' Roads|Makers' Roads]]**.

The peace did not last forever. The Green Lords called on the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]** to crush their rebellious servants, a crime for which the Stone Lords would not stand. They led their brethren against the now-corrupted Green Lords, shattered their lands and tried to wipe them from the earth. They succeeded, but at great cost.

For the whispers of the Things Below had become a song. And over the decades, the Stone Lords' own human servants were themselves corrupted. In a flurry of violence, they broke the seals and turned against their masters, and ushered in the **[[55 - Appendix A - Ages of the World#Time of Cataclysm|Time of Cataclysm]]**. The power of the Things Below was loosed upon the world, and one-by-one the remaining Makers fell.

That was a few centuries ago, at least. But the bones of those civilizations remain.

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- A still-living Maker is found, **[[45 - Tempest Lords#Vitrification|vitrified]]** or caught in some **[[12 - Fae#**Getting there**|Fae domain]]** or otherwise held in stasis.
- Someone unearths a stele, with a lengthy decree written in multiple Maker-scripts as well as an archaic but well-known—southern alphabet.
- Two rival scholars are willing to kill in order to prove their theories about the Makers are correct.
- Adventurers discover a ruin or tomb, clearly of the Makers, but bearing symbols and decorations unlike those of any of the known Makers. Perhaps there was a sixth type or nation of Makers?

sections.title: Everyone knows:
sections.body:
- Giants built most of the ruins that dot the landscape and on which the settlements in the region were built
- The Highway and the **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#The Makers' Roads|West Road]]** were built by these Makers
- There were different groups of Makers, dwelling in different regions
- They worked powerful magic, some of which persists today

sections.title: One might know:
sections.body:
- The different types of Makers, where each dwelt, their most important themes
- What happened to each group, broadly
- The distinct features of each group's writing/architecture/artifacts
- The special materials—like **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**Aetherium**|aetherium]]** or **[[43 - Stone Lords#Various treasures|makerglass]]** —associated with each group, and their common properties
- The names of a few individuals
- Which peoples served/followed/were created by them
- How to use/replicate some of their magic
- How to read their writing [[30 - The Makers#**True nature**|The Makers]]

sections.title: Very few know:
sections.body:
- The nuances of their politics, rivalries, history, and schisms
- How to speak their languages
- How to recreate their special materials
- The underpinnings of their magic

When a PC Knows Things about the Makers and gets a 7+, be sure to ask, "How do you know this?" If they're stuck for plausible answers, then give them suggestions: a folk tale, a children's song, secrets imparted by a wise old mentor, a tale heard from a merchant or an old prospector, some previous adventure, etc.

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- In their art, how did these Makers depict themselves? How were they different than humans, beyond just their size?
- Can Maker-ruins be found in **[[29 - Lygos and the South#Lygos and the South|Lygos and the South]]**? If so, how are they different than those often found around here?
- Who is the authoritative scholar on the(se) Makers? When were they active? On what did they base their research? What did they clearly get wrong?
- Which of the Makers' scripts are most like the common alphabet(s) used today?
- How can you tell that these markings are magical, not mere writing?

sections.title: Sites
sections.body:
Signs of these ancient civilizations litter the region, many of them surprisingly well-preserved. Some of these ruins are familiar and widely used today, but many are forgotten, avoided, or shunned. For while they contain many wonders of the past, they also hold ancient dangers. Plenty of them are **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|haunted]]**, or corrupted by the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**.

XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX

Create a site based on the type(s) of Makers who left it. Pick the creators based on where the site is located, or roll if you're unsure.

sections.title: 1d6 MAKERS & SITE LOCATIONS
sections.body:
- 1 The **[[18 - Forge Lords#Forge Lords|Forge Lords]]**:
  - The Huffel Peaks [[27 - Huffel Peaks|Huffel Peaks]]
    - Gordin's Delve [[22 - Gordin's Delve#Gordin's Delve|Gordin’s Delve]]
    - Western edge of the Flats [[14 - The Flats|The Flats]]
- 2 The **[[24 - Green Lords#Green Lords|Green Lords]]**:
  - The Great Wood [[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|The Great Wood]]
    - Ferrier's Fen [[13 - Ferrier's Fen#Ferrier's Fen|Ferrier’s Fen]]
  - The Foothills [[16 - The Foothills|The Foothills]]

sections.title: xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
sections.body:
- 3 The **[[38 - Rime Lords#Rime Lords|Rime Lords]]**:
  - The Whitefang Mountains [[54 - The Whitefang Mountains|The Whitefang Mountains]]
  - The Foothills [[16 - The Foothills|The Foothills]]
- 4 The **[[43 - Stone Lords#Stone Lords|Stone Lords]]**:
  - The Steplands [[42 - The Steplands#Spring|Spring]]
  - The South Manmarch [[40 - South Manmarch|South Manmarch]]
  - South arm of the Huffel Peaks [[27 - Huffel Peaks|Huffel Peaks]]
  - Southern edge of the Flats [[14 - The Flats|The Flats]]

- 5 The **[[45 - Tempest Lords#Tempest Lords|Tempest Lords]]**:
  - Along the **[[11 - The Dread River#The Dread River|Dread River]]**
  - The Flats [[14 - The Flats|The Flats]]
  - The Ruined Tower [[39 - The Ruined Tower|The Ruined Tower]]
  - Anywhere you want (their skyislands flew far and wide)
- 6 Multiple Makers:
  - Along the Makers' Roads [[31 - The Makers' Roads#The Makers' Roads|The Makers’ Roads]]
  - Along the **[[11 - The Dread River#The Dread River|Dread River]]**
  - Along borders between regions

sections.title: True nature
Runes & glyphs
The Makers' Roads
sections.body:
The West Road stretches from Stonetop to **[[22 - Gordin's Delve#Gordin's Delve|Gordin's Delve]]**. About 15 miles from town, it crosses the Highway, which runs from **[[04 - Barrier Pass#Barrier Pass|Barrier Pass]]** all the way to **[[32 - Marshedge#Marshedge|Marshedge]]** and beyond.

The roads are wide (~20 feet), grand, and humbling, clearly the work of the **[[30 - The Makers#The Makers|Makers]]**. They have lasted for centuries, strangely well-preserved and free of growth.

- Big slabs of tiled basalt, gently beveled, stretching to the horizon
- Trickle of rainwater or snowmelt flowing down gutters that never clog
- The unnerving way that dust, seeds, leaves, etc. refuse to land on the pavestones

Tip: speak quietly, reverently, like you were in a church or temple among the faithful.

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- Which of you have travelled the Highway or the West Road before? When and to where?
- The magic of the roads—the wards against violence, evil, and decay—how do they make each of you feel?
- When was the last time that you felt small, insignificant, or just very lonely?

sections.title: The magic of the Roads
sections.body:
The roads have lasted for centuries without maintenance. Predators shun them. Beings of darkness, chaos, and death are repelled, and generally cannot cross or set foot on them.

When you *attempt to commit violence upon the road*, or to *harm the road or anything upon it*, you hesitate. If you *will yourself to continue*, lose 1d4 HP and roll +WIS: **on a 10+**, go for it; **on a 7-9**, you act but have disadvantage on any rolls to commit violence (including damage); **on a 6-**, you fail to act and mark *dazed*.

sections.title: The Crossroads
sections.body:
The West Road crosses the Highway about 15 miles from town. A copse of **[[10 - Death and the Undying#Dool trees|dool trees]]** grows nearby. Despite the wards, there's something unsettling about the Crossroads. Everyone feels it. No one likes to talk about it.

- What about this place tells you that the veil between life and death is thin here?
- Whose ghost is said to haunt the Flats near the Crossroads?

**Something interesting:** One can call up the shades of the dead if they stand at the Crossroads under the new moon and call the departed's name.

**Something useful:** When you *stand at the Crossroads under the new moon and call the departed by name*, roll and add…

- … +1 if you are tied to them by blood;
- … +1 if you bear a possession of theirs; and/or
- … +1 if you offer food or drink that they loved in life.

**On a 10+**, their shade appears but pick 1; **on a 7-9**, their shade appears but pick 2:

- They are deranged, confused, muddled, unreasonable
- They have no intention of returning peacefully to their rest
- Something slips through the Last Door with them—a **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|dool spirit]]**, a **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|gwyllgi]]**, an unbound **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|wraith]]**, etc.

For more on **shades**, see [[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|Death and the Undying]].

sections.title: The Broken Roads
sections.body:
Roads once stretched from the Crossroads to the **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#The Ruined Tower|Ruined Tower]]**, and from Stonetop out into the **[[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|Great Wood]]**, and all throughout the **[[42 - The Steplands#Spring|Steplands]]**. Terrible magic shattered the stones and scorched their runes. Most have long since been buried, but sometimes a cracked stone is unearthed by beast, rain, or quake.

When you *strike an entity of darkness, chaos, or death with a shattered road-stone*  (*hand*, *thrown*, *crude*), it takes 1d8 damage (ignores armor) but the stone then crumbles to dust.

sections.title: Waysides
sections.body:
Every 25 miles or so, the roads expand to a broad circle, wide enough for travelers to camp without leaving the warded stones. Each wayside includes a well, a firepit, an obelisk marked with a single rune, and maybe more. Many feature a shrine to **[[03 - Aratis, the Lawkeeper#Aratis, the Lawkeeper|Aratis]]**.

- What, by tradition, do travelers leave behind each time they stop at a wayside?
- What distinguishes this wayside from the others?

sections.title: Fellow travelers
sections.body:
**1d6** traveler

Choose or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

| 1 | Some kook or shady character(s),      |
|---|---------------------------------------|
|   | looking for ruins or tombs to plunder |
| 2 | 1d12 immigrants, likely en route to   |
|   | Gordin's Delve                        |
| 3 | Someone seeking help/escaping         |
|   | trouble/in pursuit of                 |
| 4 | One or more Hillfolk [[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk\|Hillfolk]]       |
|   |                                       |

For disposition, choose or have someone roll:

5-6 A merchant and crew, quite likely someone the PCs know

| 1d6 | disposition                   |
|-----|-------------------------------|
| 1   | As hostile as the Roads allow |
| 2   | In dire need of assistance    |
| 3-4 | Tight-lipped / unfriendly     |
| 5   | Friendly enough               |
| 6   | Bearing useful news or        |
|     | opportunities                 |

sections.title: The nosgalau
sections.body:
Veteran travelers warn of the nosgalau, the night lights that lure travelers from the roads. They don't always appear, and even when they do, not everyone hears their call.

- Which of you have seen the nosgalau on a prior journey?
- Who were you with? Which of you was lured off the road?
- What do you think the nosgalau really are? Why do you think that?

When you *hear the call of the nosgalau*, say whose voice you hear on the wind and why you feel compelled to join them. Then, roll +WIS: **on a 10+**, say how you see through the deception, and you'll never hear their call again; **on a 7-9**, either mark XP and follow the call, or tell us how you detect the lie this one time; **on a 6-**, you heed the call and go to the nosgalau, unless your allies restrain you.

What befalls their victims is for you to decide, but it isn't good. Perhaps they…

- … fall prey to some mundane danger, like a mire or **[[14 - The Flats#**Hlomren**|pack drake]]**;
- … vanish in the night (the grass twisted in strange patterns), only to reappear elsewhere, muddy and haunted-eyed, with patchy memories; or
- … find themselves surrounded by faces from their past, concealing the nosgalau's true forms (all wisps and teeth, hatred and hunger).

sections.title: Runic calligraphy
sections.body:
Should one overcome the magic of the roads and pry up a pavestone, they would find the bottom laced with impossibly intricate, dizzying runes. They impart the magic of the roads, but they are indecipherable unless one already knows the proper magic.

To unlock the mysteries of this runic calligraphy, you must:

- Master each of these minor arcana:
- **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#Peacebond|Peacebond]]**
- **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A timeless vault|Preserving Runes]]**
- **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A crumbling arch|Sanctifying Mark]]** Acquire an intact pavestone
- Spend 1 season and 1d4 handfuls of silver attempting to replicate the runes.

Henceforth, when you make magic marks or runes, you can imbue multiple magical effects that you know. Such combined runes take no longer to craft than the most complex of the individual spells. Beyond that, work out the details with the GM.

- . steading improvement .
- **ROADBUILDING**

Requires all of the following:

- Unlock the secrets of **runic calligraphy**
- Teach the runes to at least 2 full-time artisans of quick mind and steady hand
- Secure a nigh-unlimited source of basalt blocks
- Recruit a foreman of considerable skill, who has studied construction of the Roads
- Recruit and maintain a crew of ~50 skilled laborers and masons

When you *mark all the requirements*, you can expand or repair the Makers' Roads. Every ~25 miles of road requires **Pulling Together**, one season of labor, 2 Surplus to feed the laborers, and a purse of silvers for payment and supplies.

sections.title: Marshedge
sections.body: **Size** *town* (~800 to 900 souls)

sections.title: Population +0
sections.body:
- **Prosperity** +2 Trade with...
  - ... Lygos (fine goods, spices, etc.)
  - ... Manmarch (timber, salt, amber)
  - ... Gordin's Delve (metal, tools)
  - ... Stonetop (whisky, fur)

sections.title: Resources
sections.body:
- Farming (wheat, hemp, wild rice, herbs)
- Trades (textiles, pottery, glass, rope, medicine)
- Forage (herbs, peat, clay)
- Marketplace
- Windmill

sections.title: Defenses +1
sections.body:
- **[[32 - Marshedge#**Dangers**|The Guard]]**
- Wooden palisade
- Bendis root braziers

The town of Marshedge lies along the **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#The Makers' Roads|Highway]]**, on a rise overlooking **[[13 - Ferrier's Fen#Ferrier's Fen|Ferrier's Fen]]**. Many consider Marshedgers insane to live so close to such a dangerous place, but long ago, some of them learned the ways of the Fen and of bendis root, the burning of which keeps unclean things at bay.

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- Which of the Old Families (Ferrier, Hawtrey, Tricklebank, Eldershaw) have you dealt with most? How so?
- What trouble did Brennan's Claws cause you, back in their bandit days?
- Do they worship \_\_\_\_ here? How?
- Who do you look forward to seeing?
- Who are you hoping to avoid?
- What's the oddest rumor you've heard about the fen-walkers?
- As you enter, what's the most striking difference between here and Stonetop?

sections.title: Places
sections.body:
- **The Hill**, around which the town huddles, looming over Ferrier's Fen. Riddled with **Maker**-ruins [[30 - The Makers|The Makers]].
- **The Palisade** surrounds the town, with braziers of bendis root burning day and night. Four members of **[[32 - Marshedge#**Names**|the Guard]]** walk the ramparts.
- **The Sruth**, a wide, shallow river that drains lazily past Marshedge and into the Fen. Lined with reeds and willows, the bed made of loose, sucking mud.
- **The Bridge**, a wooden span across the Sruth where a stretch of road once lay, just wide enough for a wagon. A pair of Guards collect tolls at the gate house.
- **Low Town**, hovels of the working folk, all around the Hill and Edgemarket. Wattle and daub construction, cramped gardens, dirty kids, lots of livestock. Guards don't patrol here.
- **Edgemarket**, on a **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#**The Crossroads**|wayside]]** near the middle of town, crowded with tents, wagons, merchants, and visitors. Surrounded by shops, a pair of pubs, and a few traveler-houses. Patrolled by a pair of Guards.

- **The Mire**, a muddy slum north of the main palisade, poking into the Fen. Wood walkways stretch between bogs of rice and herbs. Two gates link it to Low Town, each manned by a Guard.
- **Dropoff**, middle-class homes and shops all around the slope of the Hill. A pair of Guards are always on patrol.
- **High Town**, a neighborhood of stone buildings with slate roofs atop the Hill. Where the **[[32 - Marshedge#**Names**|Old Families]]** live. Ringed by a second palisade, with a pair of Guards always watching the gate.
- **The Garrison**, the Guard's headquarters, just outside High Town. A dozen or so Guards are stationed here at any time, off-duty but on-call.
- **Eastgate**, where the Highway passes through the palisade and farmers come and go to the fields. A pair of Guards collect tolls from visitors.
- **Miller's Hill**, a Maker-ruin just east of town, repurposed into a windmill. Everyone mumbles about how the place is haunted. It is.
- **The Fields**, stretching away to the east and south, owned by the Old Families. Some outbuildings, plenty of livestock, but few inhabitants.

sections.title: Ruins of the past
Names
sections.body:
Irish-inspired: Abben, Ailen, Brin, Brogan, Catlin, Coln, Daedre, Dermos, Ennin, Finnen, Gilor, Isbeal, Kiran, Lile, Lim, Mathuin, Mirne, Noren, Owan, Ragan, Renan, Seadha, Seann, Tierney, Ulliam

Marshedge and Stonetop share a language but the dialects are quite distinct, implying some common but distant ancestry.

sections.title: People
sections.body:
**Brennan**, onetime bandit leader of the Claws, now the town's marshal. The Council thought it'd be cheaper and easier to pay him to guard the town than to hunt him down and defeat him.

**The Guard**, the town's well-drilled standing watch. Tensions exist between the ex-Claws, old-timer loyalists, and the new recruits. (~40 guards total)

**The Old Families** dwell in the stone manor houses of High Town and have a piece of every pie. (~30 family members and another ~40 servants)

- The Ferriers have lost much clout in recent years. Their matriarch, Ianthe, is ailing but still sharp (**Instinct** to maintain her family's dignity).
- The Hawtrey patriarch, Muiris, is one of Brennan's main allies (**Instinct** to put his family's fortunes above all else).
- The Tricklebanks resent the Ferrier's prestige. Led by cagey old Caineach (**Instinct** to sieze any opportunity).
- The Eldershaws have a reputation for being tough yet honorable, but an untimely death has left young Ailbe in charge (**Instinct** to put off decisions).

**The Guilds**, comprised of weavers, potters, glassblowers, and herbalists. They generate much of the town's wealth, and chafe at the Old Families' power. (~125 artisans, assistants, and families)

**The lesser trades**—smiths, bakers, carpenters, thatchers, the miller, and others who keep the town running. (~40 artisans, assistants, and families)

**[[13 - Ferrier's Fen#**Exploring the Fen**|Fen-walkers]]** ply the wetlands, gathering herbs, hunting game, cutting peat and clay, and keeping tabs on the **[[13 - Ferrier's Fen#**The Gliomor**|suarachan]]**. They recruit from orphans and those with no prospects. Some have (and hide) weird mutations. (~20 souls)

**The Council**, Marshedge's governing body. Includes the head of each Old Family, the master of each Guild, the eldest of the fen-walkers, and Brennan (who has no vote, but pushes them around).

**Merchants** are key to the town's prosperity. Mostly local, but a few have settled here from afar. (~40 souls)

**Beggars & thieves** gather in Edgemarket, along the Highway, and in Dropoff, seeking sympathy or easy marks. (~20-40 souls, varies by day)

**Farmers & laborers** make up the rest of town. Mostly tenant farmers, put-upon homemakers, and muscle for hire hoping to earn rent. (~500 souls)

**Travelers** come to trade at Edge market, to consult with herbalists, or to seek (or spend) their fortunes. They stay with local contacts, in their own wagons, or at one of a few traveler-houses. (~25-100 souls, depending on the season)

sections.title: Secrets
sections.body:
- The town charter gives fen-walkers the right to demand aid from any resident, and the responsibility to execute those found to be "corrupted by vile spirits, or congressing with such."
- Every seven years, one of the Old Families delivers one of their children to the **[[13 - Ferrier's Fen#Mind your manners|Willow Witches]]**. A fen-walker leaves the child at one of the grand willow trees, never looking back. Rarely, such a child returns, years later, and greatly changed.
- The miller's wife is a **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|necromancer]]**, with a number of ghosts bound in the basement. Brennan knows her secret and has taken to

in Marshedge; there's a guild to join, rules to follow, and fees to pay. Surely Brennan and the Guard are compensated for

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- A daughter of the Old Families tries to save her son from the Willow Witches.
- A fen-walker discovers a ruin, home to a fell beast but also great treasure.
- Idealists attempt to organize and get recognition for a Farmers Guild.
- A High Town merchant has what the PCs seek; but seeks exclusive rights to trading Stonetop whisky in exchange.
- A spurned Guild apprentice is sending dark spirits against their old master
- A well-regarded loyalist of the old Guard has gone missing. His granddaughter asks the PCs for help.

sections.body:

When PCs Trade & Barter in Marshedge , they use the local Prosperity (+2 by default), with advantage because it's a town . On a 6-, they don't mark XP but they'll need to Ask Around if they want to sell/acquire it.

sections.title: .   trade opportunities   .
sections.body:
If the PCs get a trade opportunity (from the Seasons Change move), Marshedge might offer the following:

- A good exchange rate, two or even three Value 2 items for 1 Surplus
- A wagon, mule, or horse, for 1 Surplus and help with a problem
- A tradesperson (healer, herbalist, potter, glassblower, etc) willing to move to Stonetop for a price
- An arcanum of the Stone Lords [[43 - Stone Lords|Stone Lords]], Green Lords [[24 - Green Lords#Names|Names]], or Fae [[12 - Fae|Fae]]

sections.title: ASK AROUND
sections.body:
When you *spend a purse of coppers and a few hours asking around town*, say what you hope to learn and roll +CHA: **on a 10+**, you get a clear and useful answer; **on a 7-9**, you get an answer but pick 1:

- It's incomplete, contradictory, or unclear, ask the GM how you could learn more
- It'll cost you another purse of coppers
- You've drawn unwanted attention or gotten yourself embroiled in a situation of the GM's choosing

sections.title: CAROUSE
sections.body:
When you *spend a handful of silvers having a good time*, roll +CHA: **on a 10+**, pick 2; **on a 7-9**, pick 1:

- You spread a rumor or tale far and wide
- You make a loyal new friend
- You learn something useful or juicy You avoid getting swindled, tricked, or entangled in someone else's trouble

sections.title: RECRUIT
sections.body:
When you *put out word that you're hiring*, say who you're looking for, what the job is, and what you're offering (the GM will veto if unreasonable). Then roll and add…

- … +1 if your offer is generous;
- … +1 if the work conditions are favorable; and
- … +1 if your reputation around here is solid.

**On a 10+**, you find someone but pick 1; **on a 7-9**, you find someone but pick 2.

- They lack some of the skills you wanted
- They're entangled, impaired, or annoying
- You draw unwanted attention
- They have debts or obligations that you need to clear up first

| services                                 | value |
|------------------------------------------|-------|
| A guide around town, for the day         | 0     |
| Someone to watch things, for a day       | 0     |
| A laborer's help, for a day              | 0     |
| A crew of laborers, for a week           | 1     |
| A bodyguard/muscle, for a few days       | 1     |
| A consultation with a skilled healer     | 1     |
| An engineer/foreman, for a big project   | 2     |
| A skilled healer's care, for a few weeks | 2     |
| A fen-walker [[13 - Ferrier's Fen#**Exploring the Fen**\|Ferrier's Fen]] to guide you     |       |
| to a known, nearby spot                  | 1     |
| to a dangerous/unknown spot              | 2     |
| to a place of almost certain death       | 3     |

sections.title: Brennan
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *organized*, *hoarder*, *cautious*,  *devious*

**HP** 12; **Armor** 3 (brigandine, shield) **Damage** steel sword d8+1 (*close,* 3 piercing)

- **Instinct** to maintain or increase his power ä Identify and exploit a weakness
- ä Choose the best person for the job
- ä Hide his true strength or position
- ä Out-think and out-maneuver

sections.title: The Guard
sections.body:
*Group*, *organized*

**HP** 6; **Armor** 2 (iron scales)

**Damage** steel-tipped spear d8 (*close*, *thrown*, 2 piercing)

**Ex-Claw instinct** to lord over others.

- ä Bully, threaten, intimidate
- ä Cover each other's backs
- ä Demand a bribe or accommodation

**Loyalist instinct** to protect the town, no matter what.

- ä Give fair warning before using force
- ä Stand firm in the face of danger

**New recruit instinct** to avoid danger or punishment.

- ä Shout a warning/order
- ä Hesitate
- ä Look the other way

sections.title: Fire
sections.body:
Much of Dropoff is susceptible to fire, and Low Town is basically a tinderbox. A building catches fire? Hoo boy.

**Instinct** to consume and spread.

- ä Push them back with a wave of heat (d4 damage)
- ä Fill the area with choking smoke (debilities)
- ä Collapse a burning structure (d8 damage, ignores armor, *forceful*)
- ä Engulf someone or thing in flames (d10 damage, ignores armor, *messy*)

When *a building catches fire and it's not quickly contained*, start the following:

l The building is engulfed; cries of alarm l A nearby building or two also catch; the original building starts to collapse l Many buildings are ablaze; folks panic l Fire spreads in multiple directions; palisade catches fire; bedlam l **Impending doom:** Fire burns itself out,

 scores dead, homes and shops ruined, large parts of the palisade destroyed

sections.title: Thieves
sections.body:
*Group*, *devious*, *stealthy*, *cautious*

**HP** 6; **Armor** 0

**Damage** knife d6 (hand, disadvantage) **Instinct** to get theirs and get gone.

- ä Grab something, slip away unnoticed
- ä Vanish into a crowd or an alley
- ä Spot an easy mark
- ä Wriggle free

When you *go unwary into the bustle of Edgemarket or the crowded streets of Dropoff*, roll +WIS: **on a 10+**, you spot the pickpocket coming or catch them in the act; **on a 7-9**, they make off with only trifles (1d4 uses of Supplies, a purse of coppers, a skin of whisky, etc.); **on a 6-**, whatever they got, you're going to miss it.

sections.title: The Maw
sections.body: There's a cave in the **[[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|Great Wood]]**, a few hours north of Stonetop. No underbrush grows within 30 feet of it, and bones litter the entrance. Trappers and hunters avoid it, and claim to have heard strange noises coming from its direction especially at night.

sections.title: Impressions
sections.body:
- An unnatural quiet, the forest holding its breath
- A blighted patch, spreading out from the cave, where only grey lichen grows
- Bones strewn about, broken & chewed
- The black void of the cave mouth
- Those strange noises from within

Tip: speak softly, hold very still. Pause a little longer between words, sentences.

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- Who among you have been here before? What brought you?
- What do the noises from the Maw (supposedly) sound like?
- What do you think is in there? Why?

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- A beloved animal goes missing; someone saw it near the Maw.
- A hunter reports strange figures (or tracks) coming and going.
- A sickness afflicts the town; the treatment is an herb that grows near the Maw, but that must be harvested under moonlight.

sections.title: What could be in there?
North Manmarch
sections.body:
The North Manmarch lies east of the **[[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|Great Wood]]**, north of the **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#The Makers' Roads|Highway]]** and northeast of **[[32 - Marshedge#Marshedge|Marshedge]]**. Its western edge is made up of rolling hills, giving way to a wide, sparsely-wooded prairie that slopes gently towards the east. Many springs bubble up from the hills and flow eastward in tiny streams that feed the **[[11 - The Dread River#The Dread River|Dread River]]**.

The region is heavily populated and small, well-armed hamlets dot the hills and plains.

sections.title: Always
sections.body:
- A wide-open sky, a sense that you can see forever
- Wagon-rut trails and well-worn foot paths stretching on and on
- Longhouses with steep, thatched roofs
- Smell of woodsmoke, dung, wet fur
- Huge rams pulling plows, wagons
- Scarred faces, hard glares, weapons at hand even while working fields

sections.title: Spring
sections.body:
- Pale wildflowers bobbing in the wind
- Damp smell of mud or fresh-tilled earth

sections.title: Summer
sections.body:
- Baled hay, laid out to bake
- Sun beating down on you, sweaty clothes sticking to you

sections.title: Autumn
sections.body:
- Crop fields, neatly clipped after harvest
- A startled deer, bounding away

sections.title: Winter
sections.body:
- Tracks in the snow: paw prints, hoof prints, foot prints, sled trails, etc.
- Twinkling stars on a cold night, sure sign of a coming storm

sections.title: The Manmarchers
sections.body:
Thousands of people dwell here, spread out over hundreds of hamlets. Those hamlets cluster around a half-dozen larger, fortified settlements. These hillforts are home to chiefs who (ostensibly) protect the surrounding hamlets.

Manmarchers say "east of the river" the way others say "once upon a time." They migrated from across the **[[11 - The Dread River#The Dread River|Dread River]]** long ago, before the **[[05 - Barrow Builders#Barrow Builders|Barrow Builders]]** called up the**[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]** and made the waters unsafe.

sections.title: Names
sections.body: Germanic-inspired: Alfher, Bathhilde, Berkhard, Bertrim, Clothar, Dagmar, Elfrida, Ganter, Gerhild, Hartig, Hilde, Hiltrude, Hramn, Ludig, Luise, Meike, Modd, Sabrinne, Swanhilde, Ulrike, Urrsla, Weillem, Wiland, Wulfrim

sections.title: Lore
sections.body:
Everyone has heard of the Manmarchers' warlike ways. They fight mostly among themselves, the violence fueled by bloodfeuds, treachery, and ambition. The area might see a decade or two of peace, but only after war leaves the best part of a generation dead or maimed.

**Something interesting:** When one Manmarcher kills or harms another in times of peace, the victim's family is owed a wergild—coin, livestock, or other wealth. The victim's chief sets the wergild (usually Value 2, or Value 3 for a person of import). Failure by the accused or their kin to pay is grounds for blood-feud.

**Something useful:** Scheming chiefs will justify violence by setting an unreasonable or highly specific wergild. Should the wergild be paid, though, the feud must be called off. A paid wergild is sacrosanct—a chief who ignores it invites a curse upon their people.

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- How is the Manmarcher language different from that of Stonetop?
- When did the last round of fighting start among the Manmarchers? Has it ended yet?
- Who all did you lose to the violence? Which clan/family/chief is to blame?
- Which hamlet or hillfort do you hail from? What was it notable for?
- Do they worship \_\_\_ there? How does it differ from what's done in Stonetop?
- The Manmarchers fell trees from the Great Wood—did they break or just never have a compact with the Forest Folk?
- How do Manmarchers treat Green Lord ruins and artifacts? Do they shun them? Treasure them?
- What is this clan best known for? How can you tell them from other clans?
- What tells you that this land has been shaped by human activity, even though there's not a settlement in sight?

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- Manmarchers waylay a merchant south of the Highway, stealing something important/dangerous/valuable.
- News arrives that the kin of a Manmarcher PC (or important NPC) were butchered in the latest round of fighting.
- People are vanishing from some of the western hamlets, and the local chief seems powerless to stop it.
- Manmarcher refugees arrive in Stonetop, promising buried treasure to whomever avenges them.
- An ambitious chief has conquered three of the hillforts, and is preparing to move on a fourth. If they succeed, they will likely unite the Manmarchers, and then what?

sections.title: Terrain
sections.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

sections.title: 1d12 terrain
sections.body:
- 1 Spring/pond/creek/stream
- 2 Ditch/wash/gully/ravine
- 3 Copse/thicket/grove of trees
- 4 Steep slope/rocky outcrop/large hill
- 5 Gentle slope/low ridge/small hill
- 6-7 Open meadow, grass waist-high or shorter
- 8 Footpath/wagon ruts/trail
- 9-10 Farmland/pasture
- 11 Fence/low wall/embankment
- 12 Hamlet/hillfort/isolated longhouse

sections.title: Discoveries
sections.body: For any one of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 discovery
sections.body:
- 1 Change of terrain (previous column) and roll again
- 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.)
- 3 signs of violence (see below)
- 4 Beasts: maybe game (deer, turkey, pheasant, etc.) or a grazing herd watched by a youth and some dogs
- 5 Farmers: working the land, hunting, or heading somewhere
- 6 Warriors: not hostile, certainly wary

sections.title: 1d6 signs of violence
sections.body:
- 1 A ghost [[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Death and the Undying]] lingering near its murder, more confused than dangerous
- 2 A burnt hamlet, thoroughly looted, its inhabitants slaughtered
- 3 Dead bodies: burnt on a pyre, heads on spikes, swinging from trees, or left to rot
- 4 Survivors of a raid, some injured, likely desperate
- 5 Sounds of a fight, or calls for help
- 6 The dead from a recent skirmish, un-looted except by scavengers

sections.title: Hamlet
sections.body:
Here's a typical settlement:

**Size** *hamlet* (~25 to 75 souls)

sections.title: Population +0
sections.body: **Prosperity** -1 Trade with other hamlets

sections.title: Resources (pick 2-3)
sections.body:
- Livestock (pigs, goats, sheep, meat, milk, wool)
- Farming (wheat, barley, oats, honey, mead)
- Timber
- Trapping (fur, meat)
- Mining (amber, salt, and/or iron)

sections.title: Defenses +1
sections.body:
- Warrior culture
- Iron weapons, wooden shields
- Protection from the local chief

Hamlets consist of a longhouse for each family and their livestock, plus smaller outbuildings (sheds, cellars, etc.), clustered together. Inhabitants work the surrounding 1-2 miles of land. Each hamlet bears the name of its most notable resident, possibly truncated, followed by "-hall" (Dagmarhall, Swanhall, etc.).

sections.title: Hillfort
sections.body:
There are about a half-dozen larger, fortified settlements spread across the North Manmarch, each 20-30 miles apart. Most are in the western hills, but a couple sit on the plains to the east, at the western edge of the woodlands that overlook the Dread River.

Hillfort names might include: Wulfberg, Rabenberg, Eikelberg, Hokberg, Tiefberg, Baerinberg, Schutberg, etc.

**Size** *village* (~150 to 350 souls)

sections.title: Prosperity +0
sections.body:
- Trade with other hillforts
- Trade with Marshedge and/or Lygos (textile, spices, glass, herbs, finery)

sections.title: Resources (pick 2-3)
sections.body:
- Taxes from surrounding hamlets
- Trades: smithing, tanning, pottery
- Livestock (pigs, goats, sheep, meat, milk, wool)
- Market (for surrounding hamlets)
- Timber

sections.title: Defenses +2
sections.body:
- A dozen or so professional warriors
- Rotating recruits from the hamlets
- Iron weapons, wooden shields
- Series of earthen ramparts
- Palisades

Each hamlet is bound to one of the hillforts and its chief, through oaths or blood. The chief keeps a standing force of warriors, and is responsible for protecting their hamlets from other clans, or (when that's not possible) demanding wergild or exacting vengeance.

sections.title: Services and favors
sections.body:
The PCs don't need to roll Trade & Barter for the following, but they might need to Persuade the locals—even if they offer something of equal value. A likely way to convince them is to bribe the local elder, or get permission/backing from the local chief.

A hamlet that's well-disposed to the PCs might grant these favors for free, or without needing to be Persuaded, or at a reduced price.

| A night's food and shelter                | 0     |

| A warrior to join you on a nice           | 1     |
| simple raid                               |       |

| Someone to hide you from vengeance        | 2     |
| for a few days                            |       |
| A crew of toughs for a season of action   | 3     |
| A fitting gift for a well-off chief       | 3     |
| A compelling bribe for a well-off chief   | 4     |

sections.title: . trade opportunities .
sections.body:
If the PCs get a trade opportunity (from the Seasons Change move), the Manmarchers might offer the following:

- A good exchange rate, two or even three Value 2 items for 1 Surplus
- A pouch full of amber (Value 3) in exchange for help getting vengeance against a rival clan or chief
  - A small herd of 6-12 tonnerhorn (Value 3 total), for 1 Surplus and help with a problem
- An arcanum of the **[[24 - Green Lords#Green Lords|Green Lords]]**, **[[43 - Stone Lords#Stone Lords|Stone Lords]]**), or **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**

sections.title: Hazards
sections.body:
- Nasty turn in the weather: lashing wind, bitter cold, blistering heat, sleet/ hail/rain/snow; lightning
- Flash flooding in valleys/on slopes
- **[[14 - The Flats#**Dangers**|Prairie fire]]** on the plains
- **Mudslides** and **[[42 - The Steplands#1d6 natural beast or spirit|rockslides]]** on the hills
- **[[32 - Marshedge#Dangers|Fire]]** in a hamlet or hillfort

sections.title: Monsters
sections.body:
- Predators: **[[14 - The Flats#**Hlomren**|pack drakes]]**, **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Cougar**|cougar]]**, **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Feathered drake**|feathered drakes]]**, or **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Raselbaedd**|wolves]]**, maybe even a **[[42 - The Steplands#1d6 natural beast or spirit|kleztigr]]**
- Big ornery beasts: **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Dangers**|boar]]**, **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Cave bear**|cave bears]]**, or **[[14 - The Flats#**Thunder drake**|thunder drakes]]**
- **Chimerae** of the Green Lords [[24 - Green Lords#**Dangers**|Green Lords]]), unearthed by recent logging or mining
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Ghosts]]** or **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|revenants]]**, lurking near old battlefields or places of bloody, one-sided slaughter

sections.title: Chief
sections.body:
Chiefs are the closest things the north has to rulers. The position might pass down to children, or be voted on by the hamlets, or picked from the chosen shields, or taken by cunning or force. Some chiefs are foolish or weak, but they do not last long.

For any given chief, pick or roll 2-4 strengths and 1-3 flaws.

sections.title: 1d12 strength
sections.body:
- 1 The backing of another chief
- 2 A black, fearsome reputation
- 3 A bloodline full of heroes
- 4 Coffers dripping with silver and amber
- 5 The courage to try new things
- 6 Deep, deep cunning
- 7 Many bloodthirsty warriors
- 8 A pet sorcerer/spirit, or a powerful artifact
- 9 A silver tongue, or regal presence, or both
- 10 Strength-at-arms worthy of the songs
- 11 An utter lack of scruples
- 12 The well-earned loyalty of

sections.title: 1d12 flaw
sections.body:
- 1 Debts owed
- 2 Difficult/bickering/ambitious underlings
- 3 A reputation for faithlessness
- 4 Few warriors who will answer their call
- 5 Frailty, from age/sickness/corruption
- 6 Many who curse their name
- 7 The need to prove themselves
- 8 Overconfidence; a belief in their own songs
- 9 A rigid sense of honor
- 10 Seething, venomous ambition
- 11 A temper to rival Tor's
- 12 Utter lack of a warrior's skills

sections.title: Chosen shield
sections.body:
*Group*, *organized*, *brave*, *vicious*

**HP** 6; **Armor** 3 (iron hauberk, shield) **Damage** iron battleaxe d8 (*close*, *messy*, 1 piercing), spear d8 (*close*, *thrown*, 1 piercing), knife d6 (*hand*, 1 piercing)

**Instinct** to suffer no insult or disrespect

- ä Lash out with sudden, casual violence
- ä Fight with grim purpose or reckless abandon
- ä Pry apart an enemy's defenses

The chosen shields are a chief 's best: the most loyal, the bravest, the bloodiest. They're professional fighters, their chief 's bodyguards, enforcers, and lieutenants. They sit closest to the chief by the fire, and share the best haunches of meat.

They take a share, too, of their chief 's loot and their chief 's glory. The wise chief lets them have it, for a chief is nothing without their chosen shields. And when a chief is toppled, it's usually one of their chosen shields that does the toppling.

sections.title: Manmarcher recruit
sections.body:
*Horde*, *young*

**HP** 3; **Armor** 2 (thick hides, shield) **Damage** iron battleaxe d6 (*close*, *messy*), knife d6 (*hand*), or javelins d6 (*thrown*) **Instinct** to prove themselves, one way or

the other

- ä Boast and brag
- ä Form a ragged shield wall
- ä Hesitate ä Act rashly

Every able-bodied youth does their turn at their chief 's hillfort. They spend a year or two drilling, marching, learning the spear, and standing in the shield wall. They sing songs, and talk big, and maybe even get their hands red. A few are picked for their chief 's standing force, but most head home to their hamlets, prepared to defend their families and swell their chief 's ranks when called. That's the idea, at least.

sections.title: Manmarcher veteran
sections.body:
*Horde*, *organized*, *cautious*

**HP** 3; **Armor** 2 (thick hides, shield) **Damage** iron battleaxe d6 (*close*, *messy*),

knife d6 (*hand*), or javelins d6 (*thrown*) **Instinct** to protect their homes, their kin, their honor

- ä Form/hold a shield wall
- ä Pick their battles
- ä Charge!

No longer young, no longer eager or scared. Just grim folk with something to fight for, something to lose. They might not be as spry as once they were, but they're no strangers to the black business.

*Horde*, *hardy*, *powerful*, *grumpy*, *loyal*

**HP** 7; **Armor** 0 **Damage** butt/gore d6+2 (*hand*, *close*, *forceful*)

- **Instinct** to protect its herd ä Relentlessly pull against a load or restraint
- ä Grunt, huff, paw, or bellow to make its displeasure known
- ä Smash into whatever offends it, repeatedly

Tonnerhorns are great big sheep, as big and strong as a donkey and twice as ornery. The ewes have good-sized horns, but the rams? The rams' horns are massive, curved, and nigh-unbreakable. Which is good—for the rams at least—because they do love to just smack-smack-smack them into things.

Manmarchers raise them as beasts of burden, lashing them to plows, carts, sleighs, or even chariots. They bottle-feed the lambs and as a result, tonnerhorns are fiercely loyal to those who raised them. They're all kinds of unfriendly towards anyone else.

sections.title: War-chariot
sections.body:
*Group*, *large*, *terrifying*

**HP** 13; **Armor** 1 (wood/iron construction) **Damage** trample and wheel-scythes d8+5 (*hand*, *close*, *forceful*, *messy*, 1 piercing), or iron battleaxe d8+3 (*close*, *messy*, *forceful*), or long bow d8 (*far*)

**Special qualities** pulled by two tonnerhorns; carries a driver, two warriors

- **Instinct** to rush inexorably forward ä Ride in broad arcs around the battle, peppering foes
- ä Ride down foes, filling them with dread
- ä Crash spectacularly, d8+3 damage (*area*, *forceful*)

Most violence in the North Manmarch is small-scale, intimate even. A nighttime raid on a steading. An ambush in the hills. A war-band's throats slit around their campfire. When chiefs do raise large forces, and meet on the open plains, well… they bring their very best.

sections.title: The Pale Hunter
sections.body:
On certain nights, the Pale Hunter stalks the **[[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|Great Wood]]** with his ghostly hounds. When he does, even the bravest hunters stay in town. The **[[17 - Forest Folk#Forest Folk|Forest Folk]]** spoke of him as something like a force of nature, less a god to worship than a storm to endure.

Tales differ as to what happens should you cross the Hunter's path. He might ignore you, or call you to join him, or mark you as his quarry. But the tales all agree: impress him, and you'll be well rewarded. Get caught or fail to do your part—well, best not to think of it.

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- Someone's still out in the Woods, but the Pale Hunter roams tonight!
- The Pale Hunter is said to bear an artifact the PCs want or need.
- The PCs wish to rescue a soul that the hunter holds bound.
- The PCs meet a traveler, secretly undead. The Hunter has his scent.

sections.title: Lore
sections.body:
The Pale Hunter values worthy prey and worthy company: the fleet, the clever, the brave. But he always hunts something when he roams the Wood, even if it's beneath him. Everyone knows this.

**Something interesting:** He forgoes all other prey to hunt the undead, pursuing them even outside the Great Wood and on nights he would not normally roam.

**Something useful:** To set the Hunter upon an undead being, acquire an item that bears its spirit-scent. Burn it on a moonlit night, in a smokey fire of fresh-cut cedar boughs.

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
According to the tale(s) you've heard…

- What is he? Spirit, ghost, or what?
- How is he connected to the moon?
- What exactly are his hounds?
- What is his relationship with the **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death's Door**|Lady of Crows]]**?
- What hero won his respect, and how? What reward did they earn?
- What grisly fate befell the hero who dared to stand against him?
- What **[[20 - Gods and Religion#**Other gods**|observances]]** did the Forest Folk use to appease him?

And also…

- What tales do they tell of him in **[[32 - Marshedge#Marshedge|Marshedge]]**? Or among the **[[34 - North Manmarch#North Manmarch|Manmarchers]]**?
- Who never came home, and is said to have crossed his path?
- Who claims to have seen him? When and where? What happened? What left them (you?) so unnerved?

sections.title: On certain nights...
sections.body:
When does the Pale Hunter roam? Work with the PCs to pick 1:

- When the moon is high and full
- When the moon is new, missing
- On nights of a lunar eclipse
- On moonlit nights in winter
- On moonless nights in summer
- On each season's first full moon
- Some other nights you all agree on

Why are these nights special? Why does he stalk the Wood then?

If the PCs are in the Great Wood on such a night, choose or roll for the Pale Hunter's quarry:

sections.title: 1d6 quarry
sections.body:
- 1-2 Nothing… yet.
- 3-4 Paltry prey: a fox/rabbit/boar/deer, **[[07 - Crinwin#Crinwin|crinwin]]**, a fool who crossed his path, etc.
- 5 Worthy prey: a **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Cave bear**|ceirwmawr]]**), a **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Raselbaedd**|raselbaedd]]**, a **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Elemental spirits**|beast warden]]**, a **[[12 - Fae#Fae|Fae]]** with whom he's made a bet, etc.
- 6 Undead prey: a **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|ghost]]** or **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|revenant]]**, caught in the open, away from any **[[10 - Death and the Undying#Dool trees|dool trees]]**.

When the time is right, **point to a looming danger** (his horn blows, his hounds bay, his prey rushes past). **Offer an opportunity** (to run, to hide, to seek him out, etc.). **Announce trouble** (mist rises, his hounds appear).

If the PCs cross the Hunter's path, what happens next depends on his current quarry, whether they hide or face him, how they comport themselves, whether there are undead among them, and the fickle whims of the dice.

sections.title: Dangers
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *athletic*, *cunning*, *hoarder*, *magical*, *terrifying*, *tireless*

**HP** 26; **Armor** 4 (resilience)

**Damage** silver spear d12+3 w/advantage (*close*, *reach*, *thrown*, *forceful*, *messy*, 2 piercing) or silver antlers d12+5 w/advantage (*close*, *forceful*) or grapple d10+3 w/advantage (*hand*, *grabby*, *forceful*)

**Special qualities** surrounded by mist; can grab/grapple undead spirits; keen senses; uncanny reflexes

**Instinct** to revel in the hunt, to claim undead souls

- ä Perform a feat of prowess
- ä Disperse into mist, reform
- ä Summon his spear to hand
- ä Call up more hounds
- ä Claim a dead or dying soul

A looming, antlered figure, luminous white, his face in shadow. His spear is long and straight, tipped with a silver crescent. He is draped with trophies of past hunts, jangling ominously or strangely silent as suits his need.

**Tip:** If you must speak, speak softly. Slowly. Just above a whisper. With frequent pauses. And a flat affect.

When you *deal damage to the Pale Hunter that he might dodge or roll with*, roll with disadvantage.

The Pale Hunter's true nature is for you to decide, but perhaps he is…

- … the **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Other primordial entities**|incarnation]]** of a primordial entity of cycles and the moon;
- … a **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|spirit of the wild]]**;
- … the **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|ghost]]** of a **[[24 - Green Lords#Names|Green Lord]]**, who hunts forever;
- … a mighty **[[12 - Fae#Fae|Fae]]**;
- … one of the **[[17 - Forest Folk#Forest Folk|Forest Folk]]**, exalted and accursed;
- … some variation on the above; or
- … something else entirely.

Adjust the Hunter's stat block to reflect his nature. An incarnation of cycles and the moon might be *primordial*, with a move like "<sup>ä</sup> Anticipate their actions." A Green Lord's ghost would have the *spirit* and *undead* tags, a tether somewhere in the Wood, and 0 Armor vs. silver.

sections.title: Ghostly hounds
sections.body:
*Horde*, *spirit*, *terrifying*, *fierce*, *organized* **HP** 10; **Armor** 1 (lacks organs), harmed only by salt or as it bites

**Damage** bite d6 w/advantage (*hand*, *grabby*) **Special qualities** bound to the Hunter;

can roam only ~1 mile from him

**Instinct** to give chase

- ä Manifest a form made of mist
- ä Sniff out prey, or the dead
- ä Call the pack and the Hunter
- ä Harry, surround, drag down

A score or so of these savage white dogs accompany him on any given night. But there are more. So many, many more.

Are they undead souls he has claimed, vulnerable to silver? Fae hounds, hurt by iron? Manifestations of some primordial urge? That's all for you to say.

**The Call of the Hunt** When *the Pale Hunter races past*, your soul thrills to the call of his horn and the baying of his hounds—mark XP if you join the hunt.

If you *fail to join the hunt* or *the Hunter casts you out*, your instinct becomes "Doubt: To balk at danger, to second-guess yourself " and you cannot Burn Brightly. Both last until you succeed at some bold and reckless act.

**The Hunter's regard** The Pale Hunter welcomes company but expects them to keep up and do their part. Start the following. Track the grim portents separately for each PC.

l He grows annoyed l He expresses his disdain l He gives you one last chance l **Impending doom:** He casts you out, strikes you down, sets hounds on you, and/or marks you as quarry

When a *PC impresses the Pale Hunter*, clear any marks they have accumulated.

sections.title: Pursuit
sections.body:
When *the Pale Hunter marks the* 

*PCs as quarry*, he gives them a running start. Wait a bit, then start the following. If the PCs split up, track the grim portents separately for each.

l They hear his horn in the distance l The baying of hounds grows near l Mist rises, the first hounds appear l The hounds surround/harry them l **Impending doom:** The Pale Hunter arrives, and moves to strike

sections.title: Claimed
Running the Hunt
sections.body:
The Pale Hunter and his hounds are tireless and relentless. To survive, their quarry must last until sunrise or reach the edge of the Great Wood.

Run the hunt as a series of scenes and challenges. If you're unsure what sort of scene or challenge to present, ask a player to roll the Die of Fate, adding 1 on each subsequent roll (cumulative).

sections.title: 1d6 scene/challenge
sections.body:
- 1 An open stretch, a sprint
- 2 A choice of paths: one longer, one riskier
- 3 An obstacle, hazard, or monster
- 4 A difficult slog
- 5 One side splits up, perhaps to outflank/trick/entrap the other
- 6 A good place to hide
- 7 A breather, as the Hunter and his hounds find the trail
- 8 If part of the hunt, the quarry is in sight/reach; if the quarry, a place the hunter can't/won't go
- 9 If part of the hunt, a chance for mercy, to let the quarry go; if the quarry, a chance to slip away or catch the Hunter off guard
- 10 A final mad dash before dawn or to the edge of the Wood

Change the **[[23 - The Great Wood#. steading improvement .|terrain]]** with each scene or challenge.

sections.title: Evading the Hunter
Places he won't follow
Special move
sections.body:
**PUSH ON!** When you *try to keep up with or ahead of the Hunter and his hounds*, and you carry a normal or light load, roll +CON: **on a 10+**, you do so, and even have a moment to think, catch your breath, or help an ally; **on a 7-9**, you keep going but pick 1:

- You're flagging; mark a debility
- Drop down to a light load, or drop if your load is already light
- You lose ground or the Hunter's regard; tell the GM to advance towards impending doom
- You need an ally's help

**On a 6-**, or **if you carry a heavy load**: pick 1 of the above and ask the GM what else goes wrong, or what new challenge you now face.

**Note:** The Home on the Range and Trailblazer moves (from the Ranger playbook) apply to this move, too.

sections.title: Rewards
sections.body:
The Pale Hunter rewards those who join and impress him, and quarry who manage to escape. The reward might be:

- A marker of his favor, which the **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death's Door**|Lady of Crows]]** will take in lieu of a soul
- One of his many trophies (and the soul it binds, perhaps)
- An artifact or arcanum, something portable, hidden on his person
- His blessing upon their blade(s), which now harm undead as if silver
- An honest answer
- A **silvery horn** (below)

When you *blow the horn outside, at night, in the presence of the undead*, the horn crumbles and a mist rises. The undead flee, and the Pale Hunter and his hounds soon appear to chase them.

sections.title: Hunter of the Dead
Primordial powers
sections.body: The village of Stonetop sits upon ancient ruins, built centuries or even millennia ago by the **[[30 - The Makers#The Makers|Makers]]**. But there are things in the world that were old when the Makers were young. And there are things older still, older even than the world, bigger than the world. Strange and alien things. Primordial things and primordial powers, from the very dawn of time.

sections.title: Themes
sections.body: Choose or roll 1, or 2 and combine.

sections.title: 1d12 theme
sections.body:
- 1 Vastness, enormity, incomprehensibility, the primordial void
- 2 Truth, purity, quintessence, the raw stuff of creation
- 3 Fundamental forces: order, chaos, time, space, gravity, light, death, etc.
- 4 The spirit world: consciousness, symbols and thought, the connection of all things
- 5 True names, words of power, speech that transcends language
- 6 Celestial bodies: their movements, their mysteries, their influence on the world
- 7 Paradox: destiny and free will, causal loops, infinity, mutually exclusive truths
- 8 Natural forces and the shaping of the word: slow and subtle, violent and cataclysmic
- 9 The binding of the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**
- 10 Mythic times and mythic deeds, the birth and death of gods and legends
- 11 Long-forgotten peoples/beings, from before the rise of the Makers
- 12 Portals, possibilities, other worlds, beings/things out of time and space

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- A grand conjunction of the stars approaches, signaling momentous change.
- A meteorite crashes not far from Stonetop.
- A scholar returns from an ancient monument, preaching gibberish that infects the minds of others.
- A ritual can be performed only at a site of primordial power.
- Centuries ago, the spirit of War renounced their role and duties and incarnated as a reclusive shepherd, to live a simple life.
- A sleeping titan—big as a mountain, covered in millennia of soil, debris, and growth—starts to stir.
- A clan of refugees from no known region emerges from the portal below the **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#The Ruined Tower|Ruined Tower]]**.

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- The **[[35 - The Pale Hunter#The Pale Hunter|Pale Hunter]]** is associated with "the" moon, but are there other moons? What are they associated with?
- What tends to make you feel insignificant and small?
- Do you believe in fate? Or do we chart our own destinies?
- What strange place did you once visit that was clearly touched by ancient, eldritch powers?

sections.title: Cosmology
sections.body:
Many aspects of the setting's cosmology are intentionally left vague, for you and your group to flesh out as needed. Here are a few things to ask about or consider:
