- How mythic is your cosmos? Like, is the sun a star, orbited by the world? Or does **[[25 - Helior, the Daybringer#Helior, the Daybringer|Helior]]** drive his chariot across the sky? Or what?
- Do other worlds float in the void? Can they be reached via the spirit world or **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Primordial powers**|portals]]**?
- What lies beyond the **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Questions**|Last Door]]**)? Is the afterlife the same for all, or do different fates await each soul?
- What exactly is the relationship between the First Creators, the great spirits, the gods of forgotten peoples, and the gods worshipped today?
- Did life evolve over eons? Or did the First Creators populate the world with much of the flora and fauna we know today? Or what?
- Where did humanity come from? Do we predate the Makers or were we made by them?

The answers to these questions will depend on how your players answer questions about other topics, and decisions you make about them. In particular, see **[[03 - Aratis, the Lawkeeper#Aratis, the Lawkeeper|Aratis]]**, **[[09 - Danu, the Earth Mother#Danu, the Earth Mother|Danu]]**, **[[25 - Helior, the Daybringer#Helior, the Daybringer|Helior]]**, **[[49 - Tor, the Rain-maker#Tor, the Rain-maker|Tor]]**, **gods and religion** (page [[20 - Gods and Religion#Gods and religion|178\)]], **[[10 - Death and the Undying#Death and the undying|death and the undying]]**, the **[[30 - The Makers#The Makers|Makers]]**, **[[29 - Lygos and the South#Lygos and the South|Lygos and the south]]**, and **Stonetop** itself (page [[02 - The Village of Stonetop#The village of Stonetop|12\)]].

sections.title: The First Creators
sections.body:
They were the first minds to know themselves, the first beings to give themselves names. They were born of the cosmic void, of light and possibility and the churning chaos of nothingness and all. They spoke reality into being, set down its fundamental laws. They made the world and bound the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**.

The First Creators are unlikely to feature directly in your game. Mostly, they serve as background, an excuse for why things are the way they are. Some might linger as what we now call **gods** (page [[20 - Gods and Religion#Gods and religion|178]]), but as a rule they no longer actively shape the world. Perhaps they have forgotten it entirely.

Beings of darkness, chaos, and corruption, older even than the First Creators. They are jealous and hateful and full of infinite malice, and so the First Creators bound them in the depths of the earth.

Though imprisoned, they still influence the world. The **[[24 - Green Lords#Names|Green Lords]]** called on them when the **[[12 - Fae#Fae|Fae]]** rebelled. The servants of the **[[43 - Stone Lords#Stone Lords|Stone Lords]]** succumbed to their whispers and ushered in the **[[55 - Appendix A - Ages of the World#Time of Cataclysm|Time of Cataclysm]]**. The **[[05 - Barrow Builders#Barrow Builders|Barrow Builders]]**) courted them openly and worshipped them as gods. Their influence has lessened in the **[[46 - The Things Below#**The current age**|current age]]**, but it lingers still.

sections.title: Great spirits
sections.body:
Impossibly potent entities, numinous and grand. The First Creators made some as experiments, some to be companions, some to serve as stewards of the world. Other great spirits arose spontaneously, birthed from the cosmos or the beliefs of mortal beings.

Many of these great spirits are aloof, alien, and disinterested in the world (or at least, in this world). Others are active, but shape the world in subtle ways. Their numbers might well include the **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death's Door**|Lady of Crows]]**, and some or all of the **major gods** (page [[20 - Gods and Religion#Gods and religion|178]]).

To create a great spirit for your game, decide whether it's worshipped as a god. If it is, create it using the procedure for **[[20 - Gods and Religion#**Other gods**|other gods]]**. Otherwise, pick or roll 2 primordial themes [[36 - Primordial Powers#**1d12** marker|Primordial Powers]] and imagine the entity suggested by the combination. Consider why it goes unworshipped—is it unknown, uncaring, alien, or what?

Should one of the great spirits become active in your game, write it up as a threat (a *magical entity*). Even benevolent beings of such cosmic power can't help but cause trouble.

sections.title: Incarnations
sections.body:
The great spirits sometimes manifest directly in the world. Such incarnations are likely to become figures of myth and legend. For examples, see:

- The **[[13 - Ferrier's Fen#Mind your manners|Willow Witches]]**
- The **[[49 - Tor, the Rain-maker#**Nine-fingered stranger**|Nine-fingered stranger]]** if you assume that Tor is a great spirit
- The **[[35 - The Pale Hunter#The Pale Hunter|Pale Hunter]]**, if you decide he's a primordial entity

To create an incarnation, first establish what great spirit it's a manifestation of. Then choose or roll for why it incarnated, its manifestation, and 1-3 constraints. Write it up as a monster, and possibly as a threat, with all manner of powerful moves—such beings are not bound by the normal laws of reality. See the examples (previous column) for inspiration.

Be cautious about introducing incarnations into your game. They should make the world feel rich and mysterious and help you punctuate the PCs' lives with adventure; they should not overshadow the PCs, or serve as a convenient contrivance. Their involvement should always feel dangerous, fraught, and complicated.

sections.title: 1d12 why it incarnated
sections.body:
- 1-2 As a punishment/penance/escape
- 3-4 Because potent magic summoned it
- 5-6 In pursuit of some agenda
- 7-8 Out of curiosity/boredom/despair
- 9-10 Through fate or cosmic accident
- 11-12 To learn, to experience the world

sections.title: 1d12 manifestation
sections.body:
- 1-2 A seemingly normal person
- 3-4 A seemingly natural beast
- 5-6 A spirit or other mysterious being
- 7-8 A clearly supernatural person
- 9-10 A monster or supernatural beast, possibly a **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Other primordial entities**|titan]]**
- 11-12 Roll 1d10 again, but it manifests as a group or multitude of the result

sections.title: 1d12 constraint
sections.body:
- 1 Doesn't realize what it is
- 2 Is subservient to another
- 3-4 Has a **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Tethers|tether]]**
- 5-7 Is bound by certain inviolate rules
- 8 Can incarnate only briefly, in or at certain circumstances/times
- 9 Is subject to mortal urges/needs/ emotions/weakness
- 10 Refuses to get involved
- 11 Is hunted by other supernatural beings
- 12 Can't use its power without dire consequences, for itself/the world

*[[39 - The Ruined Tower#The Ruined Tower|The PCs are searching the bowels of the Ruined Tower]]* *[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Mysteries of the Mindgem|for the bronze helm that serves as a casing for the Mindgem]]**[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Og'molok, the Faceless Horde** Threat (*magical entity*)|. In the next session, they're likely to find the oubliette]]*) *and on a whim, I decide that one of the cells holds an imprisoned incarnation.* 

*I don't have much more than that in mind, so I roll two primordial themes. I get a 4 ("the spirit world: consciousness, symbols and thought, the connection of all things") and a 12 ("portals, possibilities…"). That seems like an entity who breaks down barriers between minds, dreams, and reality.* 

*From there, I roll a 5 for why it incarnated ("in pursuit of some agenda"), a 4 for its manifestation ("a seemingly natural beast") and a 3 for a constraint ("a tether"). I think that it incarnated to make dreams and desires come true. I pick an additional constraint: "It can't use its powers without dire consequences." It came to grant wishes, but in truth it spreads chaos—which is why Stormcatcher locked it away.* 

THE NIGHTINGALE

 Solitary, tiny, primordial, magical, beautiful

HP 19; ARMOR 5 (size, resilience), 1 vs. black iron (size)

DAMAGE ear-piercing screech d12-2 w/ disadvantage (near, area, loud, ignores armor)

SPECIAL QUALITIES ageless; reads minds; tethered to a particular ever-blooming rose bush somewhere in the Foothills

INSTINCT to grant unasked-for wishes

- > Entrance with its beautiful song
- > Bring one's deepest desires to mind
- > Manifest those desires in the world, however unrealstic, consequences be damned
- > Flit away, go elsewhere

*If the PCs set it loose, I'll write it up as a threat (an* affliction*) that spreads chaos throughout the region.*

sections.title: Archons
sections.body:
Archons are constructed **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Tethers|spirits]]**, made by the First Creators or one of the great spirits as guardians and enforcers of the cosmic order.

Start with the base archon stat block below. Pick or roll for their purpose, their manifestation, and up to 3 powers. Adjust the stat block to reflect these choices or results, writing at least one move to reflect its purpose and one to reflect it manifesting (unless its manifestation is a construct/ animate statue).

sections.title: Base archon
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *primordial*, *spirit*, *construct*, *magical*, *terrifying*

**HP** 23; **Armor** 5 (resilience, lacks vitals), 1 vs. black iron

**Damage** physical blows d12+3 w/advantage (*close*, *reach*, *forceful*)

**Special qualities** ageless, immune to the elements, perceives thought and matter, omnilingual

**Instinct** per its purpose

sections.title: 1d12 purpose
sections.body:
| To seek out and destroy    |  |
|----------------------------|--|
| To find, judge, and punish |  |
| To capture/retrieve        |  |
| To warn/castigate/correct  |  |
| To stand watch and protect |  |
|                            |  |

sections.title: 1d12 manifestation
sections.body:
- 1-2 Pure energy/light/fire: harmed only by black iron
- 3-4 A construct/animate statue: -*spirit*, Armor 6, or 4 vs. black iron
- 5-6 A mass of vapor/dust/ash/soil/ etc.: takes half damage except from black iron
- 7-8 A ripple in reality: +*stealthy*, harmed only by black iron
- 9-10 Someone plucked from the mind of whoever it interacts with
- 11-12 An impossible entity, surreal and weird and awe-inspiring

sections.title: 1d12 powers
sections.body:
- 1 Smites at range (*far* or *near*, *area*)
- 2 Manipulates the environment
- 3 Teleports, or close enough
- 4 Pierces deception, compels truth
- 5 Stuns/deafens/blinds/hobbles foes
- 6 Marks/curses/binds targets
- 7 Compels mortals/lesser spirits to \_\_
- 8 Foresees the future, likely outcomes
- 9 Harms weapons used against them
- 10 Dispels/disenchants magical effects
- 11 Quells emotions, instills peace
- 12 Something special, unique, thematic

Archons have unique names with unlikely combinations of sounds, with majestic and enigmatic titles. For example: Qrenithar, Dawn Herald; Klythorim, Arrow of Time; Xezaltran the Inevitable; Jharvakus, the Silent Witness; Phrysthalin, Bearer of Scales; and Lyvontis, the Judge Incorruptible.

For an example, see **[[28 - The Labyrinth#**Soithech**|Tcaventes, Shackle and Key]]**.

sections.title: Primordial powers
sections.body:
*The Seeker acquires the Ineffable Words arcanum [[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Ineffable Words|Ineffable Words]] and eventually unlocks their mysteries, only to then mark a consequence that has an "eternal, ancient being of Order" seek them out and reprimand them. That sounds like an archon to me!* 

*I pick a purpose of "To warn/castigate/ correct," and roll a 2 for its manifestation ("pure energy/light/fire"). For powers, I roll a 3 ("teleports, or close enough") and pick both "stuns/deafens/ blinds/hobbles" (it's made of light!) and "dispels/disenchants magic." I ponder a little, and write up the following:*

sections.title: LIVROTHOS, CLEANSING FLAME
sections.body:
Solitary, large, primordial, spirit, construct, magical, terrifying HP 23, ARMOR 1 (lacks vitals), harmed only by black iron DAMAGE white flame d12+3 w/

advantage (hand, close, forceful, messy, 3 piercing) SPECIAL QUALITIES: ageless, immune to the elements, perceives

thought and matter, omnilingual INSTINCT: to correct mistakes

- > Detect magic run amok
- > Appear as a column of white flame, painful to look upon
- > Vanish, maybe appear elsewhere
- > Burn magic away from the world

sections.title: Titans
sections.body:
Legends speak of a time when beasts and beings of impossible scale roamed the world. The god **[[49 - Tor, the Rain-maker#Tor, the Rain-maker|Tor]]** is said to have slain three such beasts, and everyone knows about the skeleton on the side of **[[48 - Titan Bones#Titan Bones|Titan Bones]]**.

Perhaps the First Creators placed these titans in the world. Perhaps they evolved amid the early world's riot of vitality and life. Some could have been incarnations of the great spirits, or **[[46 - The Things Below#**Wretch**|emanations]]** of the Things Below.

Regardless, titans no longer roam the world like they once did. The **[[08 - The Crombil#Hooks|Crombil]]** might be one, but it wakes only once every few decades. The **[[11 - The Dread River#**The Suileach**|Suileach]]** might be another, but it is confined to the Dread River. The hidden lands around the **[[21 - The Golden Oak#The Golden Oak|Golden Oak]]** are full of gargantuan beasts, but whether they are truly titans is up to you.

There might be other such titans, hidden away in remote corners of the world, sleeping the centuries away, waiting to rise once more.

sections.title: Sites
sections.body: There are places in the world marked or marred by primordial power. If such a site needs to be established, combine the terrain in which it's located with a marker.

sections.title: 1d12 marker
sections.body:
- 1 A veil, hiding it from the world
- 2-3 Dramatic geology (a peak, a crater, a rift, a geyser, etc.)
- 4 Desolation/**[[45 - Tempest Lords#**Storm-cloud potion**|radiation]]**
- 5 Remains of a **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Other primordial entities**|titan]]**
- 6-7 Megalith(s)/petroglyphs/runes/cave paintings/etc.
- 8-9 Ruin(s) of the **[[30 - The Makers#The Makers|Makers]]**, built near or around it
- 10 Mysterious structure(s), placed by primordial entities
- 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice and combine

Once the site itself is established, pick or roll for a theme [[36 - Primordial Powers#**1d12** marker|Primordial Powers]], an origin, and 1-3 features that make it interesting.

sections.title: 1d12 origin
sections.body:
- 1-2 The intent of a (the) great spirit(s)
- 3-4 A confluence of geomantic/cosmic/ celestial forces
- 5-6 A conflict between mythic beings
- 7-8 The death of a primordial entity
- 9-10 The presence of a primordial **[[36 - Primordial Powers#Artifacts|artifact]]**
- 11-12 The working (successful or not) of a potent spell or artifice

sections.title: 1d12 features
sections.body:
- 1 An **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Other primordial entities**|archon]]**, watching over it or what it contains
- 2 A trapped primordial entity or **[[46 - The Things Below#**Wretch**|emanation]]**
- 3 An abundance of certain spirits
- 4 The **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|shade]]** of a dead god or primordial entity, easily called up
- 5 Anomalies (time loops or fluxes, spatial warps, weird gravity, thoughts projected out loud, etc.)
- 6 A thin barrier with the spirit world
- 7 A **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Primordial powers**|portal]]**, or a place with the potential for one
- 8 A font of power; a good place to work particular types of magic
- 9 Visions/omens/prophecies/insights, sometimes bestowed on visitors
- 10 **[[50 - Useful or Valuable Flora#Useful or valuable flora|Useful or valuable flora]]**, something weird, found only here
- 11 The raw stuff of creation: **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Primordial powers**|black iron]]**, water of life, **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Instinct** to eat and spawn|primordial flame]]**, etc.
- 12 One or more **artifacts** of primordial power [[36 - Primordial Powers#Artifacts|Artifacts]]

If such a site lies near areas that are or were once populated, consider the locals' attitudes towards it (reverence, awe, terror, avoidance, etc.), and what additional signs they might have left.

sections.title: Primordial powers
sections.body:
*The Seasons Change and the PCs get "interesting news." I want to give them a lead on the Ineffable Words* [[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Ineffable Words|Ineffable Words]]*[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#The Whitefang Mountains|, to be found in a primordial site in the Whitefang Mountains]]**.* 

*I roll a 4 on the Whitefangs terrain table ("waterlogged soil, frozen or muddy") and a 9 for a marker ("ruin of the Makers"). In the Whitefangs, that's probably a Rime Lord ruin.* 

*I roll a 10 for theme ("mythic times, the birth and death of gods and legends") and an 8 for origins ("death of a primordial entity"). For features, I roll a 4 ("the shade of a dead god…"), and pick "one or more artifacts of primordial power" (the Ineffable Words). I create the Rime Lord ruin using the tables on [[38 - Rime Lords#**A ruin**|Rime Lords]] and the dead god using the steps on [[20 - Gods and Religion#**Other gods**|Gods and Religion]]* 

*I picture a high place, where a demon killed Nangbu—a goddess of writing, wisdom, and prophecy. The Rime Lords carved a temple into the slope, to honor her and commune with her shade, but avalanches buried the temple. What remains is an alpine meadow with a few chunks of Maker-carved stone sticking out of muddy soil. There's a stillness to the air, in which one can sometimes hear a whispering voice.* 

*For their interesting news, I think the PCs will acquire a scroll describing this temple of Nangbu, and how the dead god's shade dwells there and bestows gifts of wisdom and prophecy upon petitioners (the Ineffable Words).*

sections.title: Other sites of primordial power
sections.body:
- A small lake: cold, acidic, devoid of life. During thick mists, it connects to other times, places, or realities.
- A hill overlooking a river, where an **[[46 - The Things Below#**Wretch**|emanation]]** is impaled on a rod of black iron. An archon hides the hill and its prisoner from the world.
- The base of a rusty metal tower, 6' wide, rising over a lush meadow that teems with nature spirits. The bones of a titan are buried below.
- A fallen megalith, half-sunk in peat and mire. When the stars align, a mortal here can ask one question and get a total, overwhelming answer.
- A lonely peak where, if one is not careful, their spirit will be drawn from their body and up into the void.

- An underground arch marked with runes, opening to a long straight hall. The hallway is a loop; you always return to where you started.
- Plenty of **[[46 - The Things Below#**1d12** origin|corrupted sites]]**
- Certain chambers below the **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#The Ruined Tower|Ruined Tower]]**.
- The **[[21 - The Golden Oak#The Golden Oak|Golden Oak]]**.
- The **[[28 - The Labyrinth#**Tcaventes, Shackle and Key**|Utterwell]]**.
- **[[48 - Titan Bones#Titan Bones|Titan Bones]]**, perhaps.
- Maybe **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#Chart a Course|Tor's Fist]]**?
- Possibly the **[[02 - The Village of Stonetop#**The village of Stonetop**|Stone]]** itself.

sections.title: Portals
sections.body:
There are places throughout the world that touch each other on a fundamental level, despite being hundreds of miles apart. The Makers knew how to create portals in such places, allowing for instantaneous travel between them. One might find such a portal…

- … beneath the **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#The Ruined Tower|Ruined Tower]]**
- … in the **[[28 - The Labyrinth#The Labyrinth|Labyrinth]]**
- … hidden away near **[[47 - Three Coven Lake#Three Coven Lake|Three Coven Lake]]**
- … among the ruins of **[[52 - Vor Svetelik#Vor Svetelik|Vor Svetelik]]**
- … up on **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#Chart a Course|Tor's Fist]]**, perhaps
- … maybe in certain ruins down in **[[29 - Lygos and the South#Lygos and the South|Lygos and the South]]**
- … anywhere else you think makes sense.

Portals can be activated via **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A time-worn missive|Opening the Way]]**, which requires inscribing the sigil sequence of one portal within Should the PCs learn to use portals, they might wish to create new ones. To do so, they must:

- Locate a suitable site of primordial power
- Transport a slab of pure white stone (Value 2 or 3, depending on size) to the site
- Discern the correct sigil sequence for this site
- Have an *exceptional* stonecutter spend a season (Value 2) carving the sigils into the stone
- Risk damage to the stone or a flawed portal

The Makers' portals were safe and stable, but flawed or damaged portals are terribly dangerous. One might explode, tear travelers apart, and/or open a **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Instinct** to eat and spawn|rift]]** in the world. See the **[[52 - Vor Svetelik#The cracked portal|cracked portal]]** of Vor Svetelik for an example.

sections.title: Black iron
sections.body:
A few rare veins of this metal exist in the world, but mostly it's retrieved from meteorites. It is completely, utterly immune to magic.

- **[[12 - Fae#Fae|Fae]]** treat it like iron, but worse—its touch unravels their magic and they take +1d6 damage (*messy*, ignores armor) from it
- Spirits can't touch it, perhaps can't even perceive it
- It can damage even insubstantial forms of manifest **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Tethers|spirits]]**, but does no true harm to the spirit itself
- It damages or destroys any open portal it touches, going nowhere
- Spells, rituals, and other magic cannot affect it, pierce it, detect it, or perceive things enclosed by it

When the PCs encounter some, pick or roll for its form:

sections.title: 1d12 form
sections.body:
- 1-2 Ore/lump/meteorite
- 3-4 Raw material (spool/ingot/rod/etc.)
- 5-6 Charm/talisman/jewelry/symbol
- 7-8 Tool (nail/hammer/tongs/etc.)
- 9-10 Weapon/shield/armor
- 11-12 Construction (door/plating/throne/ etc.), likely *immobile*

Pick or roll for its size and its Value to those who understand what it is. For worked items made of black iron, add their base Value to what's listed below. For example, a sword (Value 1) made of black iron would be Value 3.

sections.title: 1d12 size
sections.body:
- 1-4 A small object/amount (Value 1)
- 5-8 A object/amount (Value 2)
- 9-11 A object/amount (Value 2)
- 12 An *immobile* object/amount (Value 3 at least)

Black iron is strong and hard like steel and worked much the same way. It's a little on the heavy side, and doesn't hold quite as keen an edge. Black iron weapons don't have "*x* piercing," but a black iron warhammer would still have 2 piercing.

sections.title: Various treasures
sections.body:
- Rocks with fossilized sea shells (Value 0), found far from any water
- A stack of tin plates, with punchedout stencils; arranged correctly, they lay out the sigils of a specific portal (Value 1, or 3 to the right buyer)
- A pouch of odd, white coins bearing odd symbols, much lighter and harder than you'd expect (Value 1, maybe?)
- A… spearhead (?), wedged deep into bedrock, shining with eternal starlight (*hand*, *magical*, *beautiful*, Value 2)
- A silver chalice (Value 2), dented and tarnished, engraved with images of monsters being trapped in a globe
- An 8' statue of an unknown goddess bearing stars and moons in her many hands (*immobile*, *fragile*, Value 2)
- A gem of blue, crystallized flame; cool to the touch but melts metal and starts fires given time (*dangerous*, Value 3)
- A black iron hauberk (2 armor, *warm*, *cumbersome*, Value 4), utterly immune to magic
- An abandoned quarry of pure white stone, like you need for a portal; flooded but still full of good material

**A bejeweled helm**

, *magical*, *loud*, *dangerous*, 1d4+1 uses Bronze, battered, and adorned with seven fittings, 1d4+1 of which bear purple gems that hum ominously. When you *don the helm*, you sense the gems' power and how to use them but can hear nothing over their incessant noise.

When you *unleash a gem's power*, pick a target you can see and roll +WIS: **on a 10+**, your target freezes in time and space for as long as you wear the helm and maintain focus; **on a 7-9**, you must focus for a few seconds, but then the effect kicks in as per a 10+.

*While your target remains frozen*, the gem hums louder and louder, painfully so. When *the effect ends*, the gem crumbles to dust.

When you *try to maintain focus*, roll +CON: **on a 10+**, you're fine for now; **on a 7-9**, either mark a debility or let the effect end; **on a 6-**, mark a debility and either let the effect end or ask the GM how time and space start to go haywire around you.

While frozen, the target (and anything on their person) is inviolate—they cannot be harmed, moved, or otherwise interacted with. They rejoin the timeline as if no time had passed for them, with whatever momentum they previously had.

sections.title: Primordial powers
sections.body:
**A sealed amphora**

 , *fragile*, *magical*, 3 uses A ceramic, two-armed jug with a wax seal. Inside is water of life, the quintessence of water, so pure that it washes away all impurity, corruption, poison, or disease from any living thing. It becomes sullied and loses its potency when used, or if left unsealed for more than a minute or so.

The amphora itself is a perfect vessel: as long as it is sealed, no substance within will rot, putrefy, or otherwise spoil.

sections.title: A fossilized horn
sections.body:
, *magical*

A spiral thing, maybe 2' long, the remnant of some long-gone **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Other primordial entities**|incarnation]]**. Those sensitive to the spirit world can sense its lingering, radiant power.

When you *bear the fossilized horn*, you have advantage on any move to impress, intimidate, or threaten a spirit (though it grants no special ability to communicate with spirits in the first place). Should you roll a 6- on any such roll, the spirit can choose to destroy the horn and end its effect, in addition to whatever else the GM says.

sections.title: Minor arcana
sections.body: Pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
sections.body:
- 1 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A time-worn missive|A time-worn missive]]**
- 2 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A… key?|A… key?]]**
- 3 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A crumbling arch|A crumbling arch]]**
- 4 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#Sigil of Authority Authority:|A silvery signet ring]]**
- 5 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A metal puzzle box|An oversized crown]]**
- 6 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A metal puzzle box|A metal puzzle box]]**

sections.title: Major arcana
sections.body:
The following are (or could be) associated with primordial powers.

- The **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Ineffable Words|Ineffable Words]]**
- **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Mysteries of the Storm Markings|Storm Markings]]**
- **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Noruba's Ice Sphere|Noruba's Ice Sphere]]**

sections.title: Natural forces
sections.body:
- Storms, violent weather, **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A cloak, richly embroidered|lightning]]**, flooding
- **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Ice drake**|Avalanches]]**, **rockslides** and **[[42 - The Steplands#1d6 natural beast or spirit|mudslides]]**
- **[[14 - The Flats#**Dangers**|Prairie fire]]** run amok
- **[[27 - Huffel Peaks#**Volcanic activity**|Volcanic activity]]**
- **[[27 - Huffel Peaks#**Volcanic activity**|Earthquakes]]**
- **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**Storm-cloud potion**|Radiation]]**

sections.title: Fate
sections.body:
Threat (*affliction*)

**Instinct** to push towards the inevitable

There are those whose fates are heralded by prophecy, omen, and vision. And if you know your fate, maybe, just maybe, you can change it.

- <sup>ä</sup> Reveal that recent events were foretold long ago
- <sup>ä</sup> Reveal things yet to come
- <sup>ä</sup> Use coincidence to push them towards their fate
- ä Show how attempts to avert fate actu-

sections.title: Incomprehensible knowledge Threat (affliction)
sections.body:
**Instinct** to unravel any sense of safety/self

When one delves into primordial mysteries, one must grapple with alien and unsettling ideas. Dwell on them too long, and you find your mind turning against itself.

l You fidget, fixate, have trouble sleeping or relaxing

l Change your instinct to… (pick 1)

- Paranoia: To invent dangers
- Compulsion: To obsessively repeat certain actions

become a threat (a *wildcard*)

sections.title: Primordial flame
sections.body:
*Primordial*, *terrifying*, *magical* **Damage** 1d12 w/advantage (*hand*, *messy*, ignores armor)

- <sup>ä</sup> Radiate blistering heat, 1d10 damage (*reach*, *area*)
- <sup>ä</sup> Start normal fires nearby
- <sup>ä</sup> Reduce what it touches to ash

A tongue of perfect blue fire, somehow brought into the world. It is likely found held in stasis by some potent magic or artifice. For while it consumes and destroys

all it touches, it cannot last long in contact with base reality.

sections.title: Rift
sections.body:
When primordial power runs amok, it can damage reality itself. Such rifts might manifest as places where:

- Time runs slowly/quickly/not at all
- Things emerge from other times/ places/realities
- Natural laws like light, heat, gravity, and death behave strangely
- The world breaks down into chemics/ crystals/raw elements
- New lifeforms/consciousnesses arise
- **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Fundamental**|Fundamentals]]**, **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Primordial powers**|voidblights]]**, or other dangerous spirits enter the world
- The Things Below spread their influence (see **[[46 - The Things Below#**1d12** origin|corrupted sites]]**)

Most rifts are temporary, soon sealed by **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Other primordial entities**|archons]]** or other primordial

sections.title: Fundamental
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *spirit*, *primordial*

**HP** 19; **Armor** 1 (lacks vitals), harmed only by black iron

**Damage** destructive touch 1d10 w/advantage (*hand*, *grabby*, ignores armor)

**Instinct** see below

- <sup>ä</sup> Manifest in the proper conditions
- <sup>ä</sup> Communicate poorly
- <sup>ä</sup> Burden with **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Fundamental**|incomprehensible knowledge]]**
- <sup>ä</sup> Refuse to cooperate
- <sup>ä</sup> Manipulate/manifest its element, locally and dramatically

These are **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Tethers|spirits]]** of fundamental forces, far removed from mortal concerns. They are active only near sites of primordial power and uncontrolled **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Instinct** to eat and spawn|rifts]]**, or when called forth by powerful, dangerous magic.

Example fundamentals include:

sections.title: Time
sections.body: **Manifests** as a form of flowing sand **Instinct** to plod forward

sections.title: Gravity
sections.body: **Manifests** as a voice from below **Instinct** to be greedy, selfish

sections.title: Fate
sections.body: **Manifests** as a weaver or a spider **Instinct** to be coy

sections.title: Light
sections.body: **Manifests** as a prismatic pattern **Instinct** to overwhelm

sections.title: Void
sections.body: **Manifests** as a hole in reality **Instinct** to get confused/annoyed

sections.title: Order
sections.body: **Manifests** as an unmoving crystalline form **Instinct** to arrange, normalize, preserve

sections.title: Chaos
sections.body:
**Manifests** as a bewildering series of impressions

**Instinct** to undermine, to surprise

sections.title: Voidblight
sections.body:
*Horde*, *spirit*, *primordial*, *stealthy*, *hungry*, *cautious*

**HP** 10; **Armor** 1 (lacks vitals) **Damage** spirit-rending bite d6+2 (*hand*, *grabby*)

**Special qualities** can harm only spirits and mortals sensitive to the spirit world; harmed only by other spirits or magic attacks

sections.title: Instinct to eat and spawn
sections.body:
- <sup>ä</sup> Follow the "scent" of a rift or a spirit-walker
- <sup>ä</sup> Mimic a spirit it has eaten
- <sup>ä</sup> Pounce on unsuspecting prey
- <sup>ä</sup> After a good meal: spawn another of its kind

To those who can perceive them, they seem like many-limbed salamanders made of starfilled night. Their broad, flat, eyeless heads split open into rows of glittering teeth.

They are alien things, spiritual scavengers and predators. They haunt forgotten corners of the spirit world, the dead worldmotes that float through the cosmic void. Mostly, they wait and hunger. But if they catch the scent of a traveler, or worse, a **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Instinct** to eat and spawn|rift]]** into our world, they will follow it.

Treat an incursion of voidblights as a threat (type: *beasts*) with the following grim portents:

l A few local spirits are killed; others are spooked; sensitive mortals are unnerved l "Softer" local spirits die or flee; plants fail, animals grow sickly, misfortune abounds; voidblights attack sensitive mortals

l Almost all local spirits flee or are eaten; local systems collapse and die

l **Impending doom**: voidblights spread into new colonies, all over the region and soon the world

**Stakes**: Will any of the PCs' enemies join them to face this threat? How will the environment be permanently changed? What will happen if the voidblights are corrupted by the Things Below?

sections.title: Other primordial entities
sections.body:
- The **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**
- **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Other primordial entities**|Great spirits]]**, possibly including the **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death's Door**|Lady of Crows]]** and some or all of the major **gods** (page [[20 - Gods and Religion#Gods and religion|178\)]]
- **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Other primordial entities**|Incarnations]]**, including the **[[13 - Ferrier's Fen#Mind your manners|Willow Witches]]** and perhaps the **[[49 - Tor, the Rain-maker#**Nine-fingered stranger**|Nine-fingered stranger]]** and/or the **[[35 - The Pale Hunter#The Pale Hunter|Pale Hunter]]**
- **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Other primordial entities**|Archons]]**, including **[[28 - The Labyrinth#**Soithech**|Tcaventes, Shackle and Key]]**
- **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Other primordial entities**|Titans]]**, perhaps including the **[[08 - The Crombil#Hooks|Crombil]]** and/or the **[[11 - The Dread River#**The Suileach**|Suileach]]**

sections.title: Red Groves
sections.body:
Half a day east of Stonetop is a copse of trees that turn blood red (leaves, bark, and all) at the autumnal equinox. By spring, the color has returned to normal. The **[[17 - Forest Folk#Forest Folk|Forest Folk]]** warned the villagers to avoid this place. They never said why.

- An iron tang in the air
- The scrutiny of the forest upon you, heavy like a blanket
- Words of warning, faintly heard in the breeze, in the rustling leaves, in the insect hum and birdsong.
- **Spring/Summer:** bright green shoots/ leaves bursting from black bark and mud
- **Autumn/Winter:** overwhelming, glossy red

**Tip:** faintly whisper things like "turn back" or "not welcome" or "go away" and then pretend you didn't say anything

sections.title: Lore
sections.body:
**Something interesting:** A dozen such groves dot the Great Wood. Some redden in the fall, others in spring. Some, like the one near Stonetop, are large and old; others are just a few young trees.

**Something useful:** When you *enter a red grove*, you draw the forest's wrath unless…

- … you come naked and empty handed, having been ritually purified;
- … you bear a dying spirit, wishing to pass on; or
- … you are cloaked in subtle magic.

Redwood serves as a conduit for **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|spirits of the wild]]**. Some of the Forest Folks' most powerful totems are made of redwood. Alas, harvesting redwood without the permission of the forest spirits is sure to draw their primal, relentless ire.

sections.title: Why are they so special?
Hooks
sections.body:
- A dying **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#The Brennauwed|glasbren]]** seeks a mortal to convey her to the Red Grove.
- The Red Grove is corrupted by the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**. Its spirits are confused and unwell, and the Grove will soon start spawning horrors.
- A villager steals a branch of redwood, and is cursed with the forest's wrath.

sections.title: Inhabitants
sections.body: Any **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|spirit of the wild]]** might be found here, but each grove hosts at least a few **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#The Brennauwed|brennauwed]]** and is protected by **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Coedwaig**|coedwaig]]**. **[[23 - The Great Wood#Monsters|Butcherbirds]]** frequent the Groves while they are red.

sections.title: The Forest's Wrath
sections.body:
Threat (*affliction*)

**Instinct** to punish the perpetrator and those who protect them

The greater the transgression, the more quickly the curse escalates and the greater the penance required.

l Nightmares, ire from beasts and birds, plants tend to wither and die l Friends and family are affected, too; birds and small beasts harass them in town

l The whole village is affected; sleep is scarce, nerves frayed, the Wood is hostile l Saplings sprout in town overnight, or when folk aren't looking

l **Impending doom**: Crops fail, livestock revolt, homes collapse, the granary rots

sections.title: Rime Lords
sections.body:
In the utmost north, the Rime Lords made their domain. They spread through the Whitefang Mountains [[54 - The Whitefang Mountains|The Whitefang Mountains]] and the tundra beyond, embracing the ice and the cold and the terrible howling emptiness.

The Rime Lords developed all manner of ascetic disciplines and esoteric arts. They inured their bodies to the cold. They sent their minds wandering through spirit realms. They shaped matter and energy with their will, imposed stillness on the world, froze pockets of reality in timeless stasis.

Disciples came from the farm-valleys and the mammoth herders, to study at the feet of these living gods. Their teachings were harsh, rigorous, and demanding. But for a time, monasteries dotted the north and the ways of the Rime Lords flourished.

But then the servants of the Stone Lords [[43 - Stone Lords|Stone Lords]] rebelled and set loose the Things Below [[46 - The Things Below|The Things Below]]. Something cracked open at the very roof of the world, and the voice of Y'aaw'kara (the Howling Wind, the Flayer of Flesh) was unleashed. Bloodthirst and madness spread.

Some Rime Lords fell to the **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Shantyara**|Howling Curse]]**. Others were brought low by mortal mobs run rampant. The thirteen survivors agreed that something must be done. They each retreated to a place of power, sent forth their minds, and together sealed the rift beneath a glacier of **[[38 - Rime Lords#Dark ice|dark ice]]**, quieting Y'aaw'kara's voice.

The Howling Curse dulled to a constant whisper. But the last thirteen Rime Lords had sacrificed themselves. Their bodies remain encased in ice, their minds working the magic intended to keep Y'aaw'kara's voice at bay for eternity.

sections.title: Names
sections.body:
Sanskrit-inspired: Akativet, Artarapishi, Dahishika, Goranat, Kanishanu, Mamaranu, Marash, Nilani, Prayanavi, Ralananan, Ranaji, Shayand, Supnam, Vitatana

Often combined with a title of "Gar" ("teacher") or appellations of "Div" ("the holy") or Kav ("the wise"). For example "Gar Akativet," "Kanishanu Div," or "Ranaji Kav."

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- A rogue has some **[[38 - Rime Lords#Dark ice|dark ice]]** to trade, and knows where to find more
- Timber-cutters in the **[[16 - The Foothills#The Foothills|Foothills]]** disturb a Rime Lord-era ruin
- Prospectors from **[[22 - Gordin's Delve#Gordin's Delve|Gordin's Delve]]** find one of the last thirteen Rime Lords, still in stasis, encased in ice
- A new glacier starts to form in the **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#The Whitefang Mountains|Whitefang Mountains]]**, spreading into the **[[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|Great Wood]]**) and upsetting the spirits there

sections.title: Themes
sections.body: Pick, or roll 1d12.

sections.title: 1d12 theme
sections.body:
- 1 Ice: shaped into grand and intricate designs
- 2 The all-pervasive cold: bone-chilling, shivering, numbing, desperate
- 3 Stasis/stillness/silence/permanence/ preservation
- 4 Ascetic practice: the ruthless perfection of mind/body/will
- 5 Chants/gestures/rituals: to focus the will, to tame reality/chaos/entropy
- 6 The mind set free: spirit-sight, spirit-walking, thought-crafting
- 7 Writing and records: the preservation of the past
- 8 Mortal servants/disciples, with their sects, schisms, and rivalries
- 9-12 Sized for giants, and roll 1d8 again

sections.title: Sites
sections.body: Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 site
sections.body:
- 1-3 lingering signs of their presence and influence (see below)
- 4-6 A **[[38 - Rime Lords#**A ruin**|ruin]]**

sections.title: Lingering signs
sections.body: Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 sign
sections.body:
- 1 **Something preserved** in ice/cold/ stasis (next column)
- 2 **An entity, pacified** by the Rime Lords or their disciples (next column)
- 3 Fragments of old construction (roads, stairs, walls, foundations, chimneys, etc.), built by their disciples, barely visible after centuries of wind, snow, and ice
- 4 A **thoughtform** (next page)
- 5 A **monument, or marker** (next page)
- 6 A formation of **[[38 - Rime Lords#Dark ice|dark ice]]**

sections.title: Something preserved
sections.body:
For example:

- Mortal disciples, frozen in a remote cave, mummified by dry, cold air
- An entire valley—fields, houses, livestock, and villagers—encased in ice
- A trove of scrolls in some dry mountain cave, chronicling the fall of the Rime Lords and the plight of their surviving disciples
- A meadow in spring bloom, trapped in stasis, the grass and flowers never fading, never growing
- One of the last thirteen Rime Lords, alive but in stasis, their spirit perpetuating the glacier that keeps **[[46 - The Things Below#**L'bin'bozia, Flesh-Candle, the Wax Skull, Roiling Tomb**|Y'aaw'kara]]** contained

sections.title: An entity, pacified
sections.body:
For example:

- Some monster, encased in dark ice
- A **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Spirits of the wild|spirit of the wild]]** or **[[12 - Fae#Fae|Fae]]**, itself a disciple of the Rime Lords, found meditating
- A **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|specter]]**, compelled to perform an endless task (counting the snowflakes on a mountain, emptying a river with a thimble, etc.)
- An **emanation** of the Things Below [[46 - The Things Below#**Wretch**|The Things Below]], terrible and powerful, held in stasis by the will of an ageless, meditating **[[38 - Rime Lords#**Dangers**|adept]]**
- A feral **[[38 - Rime Lords#**False adept**|Rime Lord]]**, held in a stasis field and ringed by glyphs, its wolfen eyes open and hate-filled

sections.title: Thoughtform
sections.body:
Objects, sensations, or vignettes of manifest will, formed of base matter or energy, like how a spirit manifests a body. Not real, but real enough.

- A grand tree, growing in a lightless cave. Its bark and wood are shaped of stone, but its petals/leaves/fruits crumble to dust and water when shed.
- A bubble of stillness on a wind-swept summit, boasting an elaborately decorated altar. The altar is flimsy as frost, dissolving if touched.
- A chant that fills a valley, echoing endlessly from the mountainsides.
- A spring in a low valley, the water never freezing. Drinking vessels are found at the water's edge, impossibly beautiful, but melting into water if removed.

sections.title: A monument, or marker For example:
sections.body:
- An enormous, stylized statue of a Rime Lord, carved from the mountainside
- A vast cave, its walls plastered over, bearing a vivid painting of a Rime Lord radiating power and mortals/spirits/demons bowing before it
- A column of dark ice, tall as a tower, embossed with symbols and glyphs
- A 50-foot statue of a Rime Lord atop a mountain, made of pebbles held impossibly in place

sections.title: A ruin
sections.body: Pick or roll for themes [[38 - Rime Lords#**A ruin**|Rime Lords]], structure, purpose, architectural elements, and condition. Place the ruin in an appropriate terrain (see [[54 - The Whitefang Mountains|The Whitefang Mountains]] for the **[[16 - The Foothills#The Foothills|Whitefang Mountains]]**, for the **Foothills**). Populate it with **[[38 - Rime Lords#Lingering signs|lingering signs]]**, **[[38 - Rime Lords#Dark ice|artifacts]]** and/or **dangers** of the **[[38 - Rime Lords#Dangers|Rime Lords]]** or the appropriate region.

sections.title: 1d6 structure
sections.body:
- 1 Terraced compound of smaller buildings
- 2 Single, block-like building
- 3 Large, open space
- 4 Natural cavern(s), partially worked/ carved
- 5 Chambers dug into the stone itself
- 6 Dome/hall/tower

sections.title: 1d6 purpose
sections.body:
- 1 Dwelling (home, dormitory, barracks, etc.)
- 2 Infrastructure (stepwell, aqueduct, road, bridge, etc.)
- 3 Storage (cellar, vault, library, granary, cemetery, etc.)
- 4 Production (workshop, kitchen, farm, abattoir, mine, etc.)
- 5 Fortification (wall, rampart, gatehouse, tower, etc.)
- 6 Study/practice (shrine, meditation chamber, prayer hall, training hall, etc.)

*The PCs have sent a logging crew up to the Foothills, but there's trouble! I think they stumbled on a Rime Lord ruin and upset whatever dwells there. I roll on the*  terrain *table and get "ravine/gorge/gully/ canyon." I get a* theme *of "mortal servants/ disciples, with their sects/schisms/rivalries." For* structure *I get "chambers dug into the stone itself," and the purpose is "storage (cellar, vault, library, granary, cemetery, etc.)." It features "elaborate decorations" and is "buried, submerged and/or overgrown, but mostly intact." I ponder and come up with this:*

*A series of tombs, carved into the side of a ravine, containing the remains of a Rime Lord's mortal disciples. The lower tombs are obscured by brush and debris, but the upper tombs are still visible, their entrances carved with guardians and ice-crystal patterns. I suspect someone from the logging crew stumbled in here and upset a guardian spirit.*

sections.title: 1d6 architectural elements
sections.body:
- 1 Trapezoidal building(s), prominent eaves, windows on upper stories
- 2 Elaborate decorations: geometric/ crystalline patterns, glyphs, carvings of spirits/monsters/teachers
- 3 Walls/floors/ceilings of dark ice, perhaps in impossible shapes
- 4 Statues formed of ice/snow/sand/ gravel, held in stasis
- 5 Large, empty, spacious places
- 6 Sound and light behaving strangely (endlessly echoing/reflecting, emanating from nowhere, strangely muffled/dimmed, changing/warping, etc.)

Most Maker-ruins are sized for giants people two or even three times as tall as humans—but often with smaller-scale rooms, passages, and so forth for their servants.

sections.title: 1d6 condition
sections.body:
- 1-2 Shattered and laid waste by time, war, or vandalism; mostly buried, overgrown, collapsed; its purpose unrecognizable
- 3-4 Crumbling, largely buried, overgrown, or hidden away
- 5 Buried, overgrown, or hidden, but mostly intact
- 6 Fully visible and strangely well-preserved

sections.title: Dark ice
sections.body:
Purplish ice, which stays cold and frozen except in the hottest furnace. The Rime Lords could form and shape it at will. Some of their disciples learned the trick, too, but the art has largely been lost.

Pick or roll for form and characteristic.

sections.title: 1d12 form
sections.body:
- 1-3 Fragment (chip/shard/chunk/etc.), from something larger
- 4-5 Enigmatic shape (cube/sphere/spiral/etc.), its purpose unclear
- 6-7 Mundane object (tool/utensil/ container/etc.), though perhaps not easily recognized as such
- 8-9 Art or decoration (jewel/statue/ tiles/torc/amulet/ring/etc.)
- 10-12 Construction (door/stairway/arch/ bricks/walls/etc.), likely *immobile*

Consider rolling 1d6 to inform size/scale.

sections.title: 1d12 characteristic
sections.body:
- 1 Breathtakingly beautiful
- 2 Dims light/color in its presence
- 3 Absorbs/baffles sound
- 4 Exceptionally hard, roll 1d6 for degree (otherwise, hard as mundane ice)
- 5 Presents a tangible barrier to spirits, even when unmanifest
- 6 Pieces persist when removed, instead of melting like normal ice
- 7-8 Roll 1d6 twice and combine
- 9-12 Just (just) a bit of never-melting ice

Consider rolling 1d6 to inform how pristine the dark ice is.

sections.title: Various treasures
sections.body:
- A drum, wood and stretched skin, impossibly preserved (*loud*, *fragile*)
- A 1-inch cube of dark ice (*magical*, Value 1)
- A 2-foot length of chain, the links made of stone-hard dark ice, bronze clasps at either end (*magical*, Value 1)
- A thumb-sized topaz, set in bronze on a… belt (?); fills you with dread to look at it (*magical*, *terrifying*, Value 2)
- Giant-sized robes of strange silk that's almost impossible to cut/tear; quickly frays and unravels with even minor damage (*warm*, *clumsy*, Value 2)
- A deep, round brass bowl, strangely resonant, the outer edges adorned with sapphires (Value 3)
- A child-sized golden statue (*immobile*, *magical*, Value 4) of a meditating Rime Lord, the head radiating cold light

sections.title: A column of ice
sections.body:
*magical*, *immobile*

Amidst a high, lonely meadow stands a pillar of dark purple ice, 12 feet tall and 2 feet thick. It rests on a circular slab of rune-etched stone, a dozen paces across, untouched by time or nature. There's a stillness to the place, a tension in the air.

**Something interesting:** The runes appear to be some sort of spell, waiting to be triggered; it'd take days of study to puzzle out the command word (and you risk triggering it accidentally).

**Something useful:** You can determine the command word with a few hours of study (less if you're versed in Rime Lord script).

When you *speak the command word near the column*, the dark ice expands in a flash, encasing the stone slab and anything on it. The ice traps even spirits (manifest or not). Mortal prisoners soon perish, their souls ensnared. The dark ice never melts and resists all but the mightiest magic and blows.

sections.title: A dark ice knife
sections.body:
*hand*, *magical*, *crude*

A hilt of bone, with a spike of dark ice tapering to a sharp point. The ice is hard as stone, and not especially sharp.

When you *stab an undead entity with the knife*, ask the GM whether it is driven by obsession, hunger, and/or vengeance. If the answer is "yes," then deal +1d4 damage (ignores armor), and leave the entity briefly disoriented. Should this reduce the entity to 0 HP, the dark ice knife melts away along with whatever bound the entity to this world.

sections.title: A small black sphere
sections.body:
*magical*, *indestructible*, *immobile* Found in or near some Rime Lord ruin, this… thing… appears to be a perfect sphere of lead, maybe 2 inches across. It hovers motionless in the air. No amount of physical force can mar it, nor move it from its relative position.

When you *focus all your will upon the black sphere*, roll +WIS: **on a 10+**, you can slowly move the sphere with your mind, so long as you keep your attention fixed upon the task; **on a 7-9**, pick 1:

- Mark a debility and move the sphere as per a 10+
- Move the sphere, but only a few inches
- Fling the sphere in a random vector (if it matters: d8 damage, *forceful*, *messy*)

sections.title: Minor arcana
sections.body: Pick 1 or have someone roll.

sections.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
sections.body:
- 1 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#Ice Weaving|An oversized codex]]**
- 2 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#Peacebond|A sealed cave]]**
- 3 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A timeless vault|A timeless vault]]**
- 4 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A length of prayer beads|A length of prayer beads]]**
- 5 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A gold butter lamp|A beautiful scroll]]**
- 6 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A gold butter lamp|A gold butter lamp]]**

sections.title: Major arcana
sections.body:
These might very well be associated with the Rime Lords or their disciples.

- **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Noruba's Ice Sphere|Noruba's Ice Sphere]]**
- **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Rune-laden Scales|Rune-laden Scales]]**

sections.title: Tulpa
sections.body:
A tulpa is a constructed **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Spirits of the wild|spirit]]**, created by the powerful will of another. The Rime Lords crafted many such entities, which linger on in their creators' wake.

A tulpa has the *spirit* and *construct* tags, with a move that reflects them manifesting or possessing a physical form. They are animated by more than biology (+4 HP) and usually lack vital organs (+3 HP, +1 Armor). Pick or roll a purpose, size, ability, tether, and disposition. Use these elements to inform how it manifests and behaves.

sections.title: 1d12 purpose
sections.body:
- 1-2 Battle/sparring/protection
- 3-4 Companionship/advice/diplomacy/ subterfuge
- 5-6 Labor/mundane tasks/convenience
- 7-8 Curiosity/instruction/assistance/ practice
- 9-10 Something obscure, or very specific
- 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice, combine

sections.title: 1d12 size
sections.body:
- 1 Cat-sized or smaller (*tiny*)
- 2-3 Like a human child (*small*)
- 4-7 Human-sized
- 8-10 Like a horse, a cart, etc. (*large*)
- 11-12 Like an elephant, or bigger (*huge*)

sections.title: 1d12 ability
sections.body:
- 1 Possession (people and/or things)
- 2 Emotional influence/control
- 3 Elemental manipulation (ice/wind/ water/etc.)
- 4 Energy manipulation (light/heat/ cold/darkness/etc.)
- 5 Invisibility/stealth/observation/ tracking
- 6 Depths of knowledge/lore/ experience
- 7 Mind-reading/aura-sense/ true-seeing
- 8 Illusions/false senses/thoughtforms
- 9 Strength/speed/prowess/mobility
- 10 Dangerous physical attack(s)

sections.title: 1d12 tether
sections.body:
- 1-2 *Immobile* object: unable to leave its presence
- 3-4 Small, portable object: unable to manipulate it or leave its presence
- 5-6 Sculpture or construct: can animate it, but cannot leave its presence
- 7-8 Building or complex: can freely move about within, but cannot leave
- 9-10 Place or object (roll 1d6 for size/ scale): can travel but must periodically return

11-12 Roll 1d10 twice, combine

sections.title: Gingkara
sections.body:
*Horde*, *spirit*, *construct*, *organized* **HP** 10; **Armor** 1 (lacks organs) or 4 vs. stabbing (just bones)

**Damage** bony fingers d4 (*hand*, *grabby*) **Special qualities** tethered to one specific bone

**Instinct** to keep their master's house in order

- ä Animate its particular skeleton
- ä Grab on and pull them down with numbers
- ä Reassemble themselves (regaining 1d4 HP), unless their tether is shattered

Some of the simplest tulpas to create, each of these spirits is bound to a skeleton that's been stripped of flesh and intricately carved. The Rime Lords and their disciples used them as domestic servants, tending to material needs and serving as guardians when needed. Gingkara still attempt to preserve their master's homes, for what else should they do with their existence?

sections.title: Khyagseng
sections.body:
*Group*, *spirit*, *construct*, *resolute*, *observant*, *fierce*

**HP** 13; **Armor** 2 (icy "skin," lacks organs) **Damage** icy jaws and paws d8+2 with advantage (*hand*, *close*, *grabby*, *forceful*) **Special qualities** tethered to its pedestal; body made of dark ice; cold-proof

**Instinct** to repel intruders

- ä See through illusions and deceptions
- ä Offer a rumbling (though polite) warning
- ä Repair itself while on its pedestal

These tulpas inhabit dark-ice sculptures of three-eyed, stylized lions—muscular, proud, fierce—standing atop decorated pedestals. Always found in pairs, guarding doorways or thresholds. They dutifully challenge any who approach without an invitation from their master. Never mind that their masters are all long gone.

Some of the grander Rime Lord ruins boast large khyagseng made of dark ice that is strong as stone (+8 HP, +1 Armor, +1 damage). These greater guardians can also unleash a thunderous roar, deafening and terrifying, likely to trigger avalanches.

sections.title: Mkhalang
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *huge*, *spirit*, *construct*, *cautious*, *devious*

**HP** 27; **Armor** 1 (lacks organs)

**Damage** trample d8+3 with disadvantage (*hand*, *close*) or ice-tusks d8+7 with disadvantage (*reach*, *forceful*, *messy*, *crude*, 1 piercing)

**Special qualities** tethered to its dome but far-ranging; perfect memory; unable to lie

**Instinct** to amuse itself at the expense of others

- ä Fly quickly across the skies
- ä Manifest a mammoth-like form of freezing mist and ice (harmed only by salt, fire, heat)
- ä Suck the strength and warmth from anyone they trample
- ä Reveal just enough to cause trouble

The Rime Lords created these mighty spirits to act as emissaries and spies. A few still remain, tethered to the meditation-halls in which they were made but roaming the Whitefangs in search of novelty and diversion. They have grown bored in their masters' absence, and from boredom has grown a subtle cruelty.

The people of the Whitefangs treat them with respect, for they are still the emissaries of the Rime Lords. But they also know better than to trust their counsel.

sections.title: Shaksa
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *spirit*, *construct*, *hoarder*, *magical*, *terrifying*

**HP** 19; **Armor** 1 (lacks organs)

**Damage** antler of jagged bone d10+2 (*close*, *messy*, 1 piercing) or stone hooves d8 (*hand*)

**Special qualities** tethered to its cemetery, can only leave to track looters/vandals

**Instinct** to preserve the sanctity of the dead

- ä Fill an intruder with dread
- ä Manifest a form of bone and gravedirt (unharmed by stabbing; vulnerable to rain/running water)
- ä Track a looter or vandal, unerringly

The Rime Lords and their disciples created shaksa to guard over cemeteries and charnel grounds. They usually watch, unseen and unmanifest.

A shaksa manifests only within its cemetery, appearing as a powerful goat made of rock and soil, with a human (?) skull for a head, three eyes of floating light, and a single great antler of jagged bone. Once manifest, it can leave the cemetery in pursuit of looters or vandals. Should this form be destroyed, the spirit returns to the cemetery and cannot remanifest until the next sunset.

sections.title: Entities
sections.body:
- **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Spirits of the wild|Spirits of the wild]]**, following the Rime Lord's teachings and/or acting as guardians
- Spirits, beasts, and people **corrupted** by the Things Below [[46 - The Things Below#**1d12** origin|The Things Below]]: encased in dark ice, bound by glyphs, and/or trapped in stasis
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Wraiths]]** of the Rime Lord's disciples, who failed to tame their body's needs and succumbed to eternal hunger
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Ghosts]]** of the Rime Lord's disciples, who pushed themselves too far and cannot accept their failure

sections.title: Hazards
sections.body:
- **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Ice drake**|Altitude sickness]]**
- **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Ice drake**|Avalanches]]**
- **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Ice drake**|Hypothermia & frostbite]]**
- Ice: slippery, jagged, unstable, very cold
- Pools of liquid air, so cold it burns (d8 damage, *messy*, ignores armor)
- Pockets of frozen time, ensnaring any who enter; or fragile and holding something dangerous in place
- **[[38 - Rime Lords#**Hazards**|Thoughtforms]]** composed of dense particulate matter on the verge of collapse or hiding some danger

sections.title: Deadly cold
sections.body:
Rime Lord ruins sometimes include areas that are much, much colder than even the surrounding mountains. Dangerously, lethally cold.

- <sup>ä</sup> Show a warning: a subtle change in light/sound, breath or smoke condensing/freezing, hoarfrost
- <sup>ä</sup> Weigh them down with frost
- <sup>ä</sup> Have gear stick/freeze/break
- <sup>ä</sup> Inflict **[[54 - The Whitefang Mountains#**Ice drake**|hypothermia & frostbite]]**, quickly and with little warning

sections.title: Adept
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *cautious*, *cunning*, *magical* **HP** 16; **Armor** 2 (toughness, skill)

**Damage** freezing touch or aura d8 (*hand* or *reach*, *area*, ignores armor)

**Special qualities** cold-proof; spirit sight; no longer ages; needs little food/drink/ sleep; powerful will

sections.title: Instinct see below
sections.body:
- ä Send their mind forth into the spirit world, their bodies in stasis
- ä Reveal a teaching/technique of the Rime Lords
- ä 1-4 additional moves (next page)

The Rime Lords taught that the self could be perfected. That the needs and frailties of the body could be purged. That the mind could free itself of the flesh and know the unseen world of spirits and fundamental forces. That the will could conquer all. The goal was nothing short of immortality, the preservation of the perfect self.

For most of the Rime Lords' mortal disciples, these teachings did little more than strengthen the body and clear the mind. But for some—the adepts—these teachings unlocked a fragment of their masters' power.

Rivalries were fierce among the adepts even when the Rime Lords walked the land. When the last Rime Lords disappeared, these rivalries flourished into schisms, sects, and violent purges. Some of the adepts fell. Others went into hiding. Still others passed their knowledge on, building schools and monasteries around them.

To create an adept, start with the stats above. Pick or roll for an instinct and 1-4 moves to represent their capabilities.

sections.title: 1d12 instinct
sections.body:
- 1-2 To atone
- 3-4 To avoid disruption or distraction
- 5-6 To drive their students towards perfection
- 7-8 To prove their superiority
- 9-10 To tame chaos
- 11-12 To test themselves or their students

sections.title: 1d12 move
sections.body:
- 1 <sup>ä</sup> As a spirit: animate a large mass of ice (*large*, +8 HP, +2 armor, +3 damage, *forceful*)
- 2 <sup>ä</sup> As a spirit: manifest a body from snow/frost/mist (+4 HP, +1 armor, unharmed by stabbing or cutting)
- 3 <sup>ä</sup> Calm/diminish/suppress the elements
- 4 <sup>ä</sup> Conjure ice/dark ice (*near*, *area*, *grabby*), but lose 1d4 HP
- 5 <sup>ä</sup> Craft thoughtforms from base matter/light/energy
- 6 <sup>ä</sup> Create a minor tulpa (*tiny*, 8 HP, 1 armor, 1d4 damage), or a more potent one with some sacrifice
- 7 <sup>ä</sup> Dismiss/suppress magic with a touch
- 8 <sup>ä</sup> Exert great force with their mind (*near*, *forceful*)
- 9 <sup>ä</sup> Impose a compulsion/command/ taboo upon a spirit
- 10 <sup>ä</sup> Slow/stop time in an area
- 11 <sup>ä</sup> Calm/suppress emotions
- 12 <sup>ä</sup> Absorb intense heat/energy/ power/force unharmed

sections.title: False adept
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *spirit*, *undead*, *magical*, *devious*, *organized*

**HP** 19; **Armor** 1 (lacks organs) **Damage** life-draining touch d8 (*hand*, ignores armor)

**Special qualities** tethered to some object; telepathic; powerful will

**Instinct** to amass more followers, more power

- ä Attract followers with promises of greatness, easily achieved
- ä Eat away at someone's emotions/ memories/will
- ä Possess a follower (or one's corpse)
- ä Siphon a follower's lifeforce to mimic some art of the Rime Lords

Some who practice the Rime Lord's teachings push themselves beyond their limits. Their bodies fail but they become ragefilled **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|specters]]** or ever-hungry **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|wraiths]]**. The Rime Lords had many techniques for dealing with such abominations.

Imagine, though, an adept who saw their body and their life as a resource to expend rather than a liability to overcome. One who burned health and vitality to empower their practice. One who saw vast, unused potential in those around them. Such an adept might deliberately sever their mind and spirit from their body and gather a herd of followers (victims) around them, to serve as fuel for their mighty arts.

sections.title: Text
sections.body:
RRiimmee LLoorrddss
Rime Lord
Solitary, large, cautious, cunning, magical, organized,
terrifying
HP 26; Armor 5 (potent wards, skill)
Damage conjured ice d12+3 (any range, area, grabby,
forceful) or heat-drain d12+1 (reach, ignores armor)
Special Qualities spirit sight; telepathic; cold-proof;
ageless; no need to eat/drink/sleep/breathe; aura of
bitter cold
Instinct to seek perfection (in themselves, in their
surroundings, in others)
ä
Overawe those in its presence
ä

physical and spirit worlds

stilling emotions, freezing time, dismissing magic,
reshaping reality, etc.

Only thirteen Rime Lords survive; each remains in stasis in
some key place of power. Their spirits are merged in an eter-
nal dance and song, continuously weaving the dark ice glacier
that keeps the voice of Y’aaw’kara bound and prevents
madness from spreading unchecked across the world.
Pray, o mortal. Pray that they continue their vigil. Pray that
their wills do not falter. Pray that no interloper is so foolish
as to disturb them. Pray for your family,
your friends, and all that you know.
Pray that they do not wake.

sections.title: The Ruined Tower
sections.body:
A long day's march from Stonetop, a wide, low hill rises from the **[[14 - The Flats#The Flats|Flats]]**. A pile of earth and stone squats atop that hill. Metal spires reach out of the pile, piercing the sky. If you squint, you can see the rubble for what it is: a broken tower, once grand in scale, long ago brought low.

Everyone from Stonetop knows about the Ruined Tower and the giant-sized tunnels beneath it. Adventuresome folks sometimes think to explore them. Most of the time, they get spooked and don't go far. But those who've delved deeper have come back with wondrous things. Or never come back at all.

sections.title: Stormcatcher's tower
sections.body:
Before the tower fell, this was home to Stormcatcher, last (?) of the **[[45 - Tempest Lords#Tempest Lords|Tempest Lords]]**. The tower was like the mast of some mighty ship, catching wind and lightning, holding their power in the **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#Shards of the Storm Orb|Storm Orb]]**, fueling her many magics.

For centuries, Stormcatcher's tower stood betwixt the realms of the other **[[30 - The Makers#The Makers|Makers]]**. They would trade with her, and seek her council or her aid. But though the **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#The Makers' Roads|Makers' Roads]]** ran to her very gate, she stood apart from them all.

Yet she did not live alone. A bustling community grew up around her tower. Laborers, artisans, and scholars. Farmers and domestic help. Soldiers, even. Generations of families. Refugees seeking new homes. Ambassadors and outcasts from the other Makers. Seekers, misfits, and castaways from distant lands.

When the Time of Cataclysm came when the **Stone Lords**' [[43 - Stone Lords|Stone Lords]] slaves rebelled and unleashed the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**, when the **Rime Lords**' [[38 - Rime Lords|Rime Lords]] disciples turned savage, when the **Forge Lords**' [[18 - Forge Lords|Forge Lords]] cities were buried by ash and war—Stormcatcher's tower endured. A bastion of civilization. An island of safety. A refuge.

It could not last. Cultists of **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Skittering horror**|Og'molok the Faceless Horde]]** swarmed the ramparts, driving the defenders back. Og'molok itself burst forth from below as a wave of skittering horrors.

Stormcatcher unleashed a mighty spell that turned the attackers into glass. But that same spell shattered the Storm Orb. It laid waste to the tower and broke a stretch of the Makers' Roads. It wrought Stormcatcher's doom.

Many of the tower's residents fled through a **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Primordial powers**|portal]]** beneath the tower. But most were slaughtered by the attackers, or vitrified along with them, or caught in the Storm Orb's explosion. A slim few survivors remained and tried to rebuild, but they soon fell prey to the horrors now loose upon the world.

In the centuries since, many have tried to claim the ruin, or plunder its treasures and its secrets. But all have failed, for one reason or another.

And the Ruined Tower remains.

sections.title: Everyone from Stonetop knows:
sections.body:
- Where the tower is
- About the giant-sized tunnels below
- That some brave (foolish) folk have explored them, and found

terrible and wondrous things

sections.title: One might know:
sections.body:
- A tale or two about folks who've explored here and what they found
- Details about the ruins above ground, or some of the upper tunnels
- That the tower is a ruin of the Tempest Lords
- That it was the last of the Makers' strongholds to fall
- That pieces of the tower can be found strewn all around the Flats
- That the outer ruins, the tower itself, and the upper tunnels have all been heavily looted, but still yield the occasional trinket

sections.title: Very few know:
sections.body:
- Stormcatcher's story
- Her relationship with the other Makers
- How exactly the tower fell
- Reliable details about the depths
- Anything at all about Og'molok, the Faceless Horde

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- Which one of you has braved these ruins before? What kept you from going very far?
- What wondrous trinket back in Stonetop supposedly came from here? Who has it now, and what do they use it for?
- What marvel is said to lie below the Ruined Tower? From whom did you hear such tales?
- When the nightmares come and you dream of the tower's fall, are you one of the doomed defenders? Or part of the slavering horde?

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- Bandits squat in the ruins, and find an artifact that makes them extra dangerous.
- A sorcerer is raising her demonic child in the tower's depths.
- A drunk wanderer claims to have explored the depths, but his friends didn't make it out.
- A southern scholar plans to delve the tower and seeks local help.
- Frequent earthquakes coincide with terrible nightmares, especially for those touched by violence. The nightmares feature the tower, its fall, or the tunnels below.

sections.title: Getting there
sections.body:
To get there from Stonetop, take the West Road 3-4 hours to the **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#The Makers' Roads|Crossroads]]**. From there, it's 5-6 hours southwest across the **[[14 - The Flats#The Flats|Flats]]**.

- It'll take at least a day each way, and a corresponding amount of supplies
- You'll need to bring light sources (if you intend to explore the tunnels)
- You need to watch out for **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#**Fellow travelers**|nosgalau]]** and other **[[14 - The Flats#**Dangers**|dangers]]** of the Flats
- If there have been heavy snows, you must wait until the thaw, or…
  - You'll need snowshoes, and…
  - The way will still be grueling; you risk exhausting yourselves and/or your gear

sections.title: Areas
sections.body:
- **Overgrown berm**, about 15-20' tall, encircling the tower ~3/4 mile out. Huge dressed stones poke out, similar to the **[[02 - The Village of Stonetop#**The village of Stonetop**|Old Wall]]**.
- **Outer ruins** between the berm and the tower. Once a thriving town, now just a jumble of ruins mostly swallowed by the prairie.
- **A shallow crater**, stretching ~400' from the tower's northeast edge. Grass/soil/ mud/standing water cover glassy, slaglike bedrock.
- **The tower itself**: ~300' across. Northeast third is mostly rubble, but to the south and west a few floors remain, with walls ~200' tall.
- **The upper tunnels** start ~100' below the surface. Heavily looted, very haunted, mostly maintained by a ghostly Forge Lord and his **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Patchwork construct**|patchwork constructs]]**.
- **The depths** are even further down, a warren of chambers, halls, stairs, chutes, and burrows, all in various states of repair. Full of infrastructure, artifacts, experiments, prisons, and vitrified horrors.

outer ruins

sections.title: Aboveground
sections.body: The Ruined Tower sits atop a wide, low, lightly wooded hill. From a distance, it's just a landmark. As one gets closer, it becomes clear just how massive the tower once was.

sections.title: Anywhere
sections.body:
- Southerly wind, ever-blowing
- Squeak and shriek of metal spires when the wind picks up
- Metallic barks and chirps from high up, the snap of a metal cable
- The looming scale of the thing, big as the landscape, hard to take in
- Smell of ozone, even on a clear day
- A fluting warble; a mocking laugh, a flash of bright blue feathers

sections.title: Outer ruins
sections.body:
- Piles of earth dotting the land, tufts of grass and brush growing on them
- The uneven, rugged terrain, making it so you can never catch your stride
- Occasional bits of broken pottery or metal, centuries-old, just lying there, turned up from the dirt

sections.title: Inside a building or the tower
sections.body:
- Cave-like darkness, coolness, and stillness as they step inside
- Faded mosaics/carvings, obscured by time, vandalism, erosion, lichen
- Dim sunbeams, full of floating dust
- Wind whistling in through cracks in walls/ceilings; water dripping through those cracks in the rain

sections.title: Terrain
sections.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

sections.title: 1d12 terrain
sections.body:
- 1 Barren patch of sand/dust/glass
- 2 Mud/standing water/deep snow
- 3 Ditch, gully, or embankment; the outline of buried ruins
- 4 Exposed wall(s), crumbling and covered in moss/lichen
- 5-6 Stretch of grass, 1d6+2 feet tall
- 7 Shrubs, thicket, tree(s)—maybe even **[[10 - Death and the Undying#Dool trees|dool trees]]**
- 8 Huge stone slab, partly buried; a fallen piece of the tower
- 9-10 Pile of dirt/stone and a nearby pit; an excavation or burrow
- 11-12 building (see below), at least somewhat intact

sections.title: 1d12 building
sections.body:
- 1-8 From before the tower's fall; pick or roll for its purpose
- 9-11 Built after the tower's fall; pick or roll for its purpose
- 12 A **[[05 - Barrow Builders#Barrow|barrow]]** roll 1d8 for size)

sections.title: 1d12 purpose
sections.body:
- 1-2 Home/barracks/living space
- 3-4 Kitchen/laundry/bath/latrine
- 5-6 Gathering/meetings/civic life
- 7-8 Storage/cellar/stable/tomb
- 9-10 Work/production/creation
- 11-12 Esoterica/experimentation

sections.title: The Tower itself
sections.body:
The base of the tower is ~300' across; rubble and piled-up soil spread out even farther. The south and west sections still stand tall, rising ~200' from the hilltop.

**Spires:** Four **metal spires** support the tower's walls, one at each cardinal direction: massive, rusted, straining towards the sky. The south and west spires each stretch another 200' or so above the walls (~400' tall total). A length of cable dangles from the tip of the south spire.

**Rubble:** The **northeast third** of the tower is just rubble, thick walls, and soil piled ~30' high, topped with grass and shrubs. The rubble is riddled with trenches, pits, old excavations, buried rooms, burrows.

**Ground floor:** The **south and west thirds** of the tower are intact at ground level, containing huge vaulted chambers (~40' ceilings), including:

- A **parlor** with tall narrow windows. Stylized friezes (faded, vandalized, full of bird nests) show scenes of the **[[45 - Tempest Lords#Tempest Lords|Tempest Lord]]** empire.
- The **baths**, a deep pool full of vile, stagnant water. The walls are busted open, the pipes looted.
- The **great hall**, mostly intact, with a giant stone table. The ceiling is enchanted to show the night sky.

Human-scale servant passages/rooms parallel the larger chambers and halls.

**Inlays:** Rooms inside the tower once had **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Ancient wonders**|aetherium inlays]]** all along their walls and ceilings, but those were gouged away long ago by looters and **[[14 - The Flats#**Thunder drake**|thunder drakes]]**.

**Main stairwell:** A **circular shaft** at the tower's center, 30' wide. Goes up to the second floor, down ~100' to the upper tunnels. Human-scale stairs spiral along the outer wall, far too small for Makers. Did they *fly*?

**Upper floors:** The **second floor** is ~50' up and partly open to the sky, covering the southwest half of the tower. Accessible via the stairwell, holes in ceilings, or climbing the outer walls. A few intact rooms remain, where **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#Dangers|blue magpies]]** stash shinies and build elaborate mosaics.

The **third and fourth floors** are another 40' up each, accessible only via tricky climbs. Unstable, with only a few "rooms," all of which are at least partly open to the air. Magpie nests abound.

sections.title: Creating rooms
sections.body:
For any specific room that you establish, pick or roll for a purpose (previous page) and combine it with a Tempest Lord theme [[45 - Tempest Lords#**A ruin**|Tempest Lords]].

Imagine the room before the fall. Now, how was it affected by the attack? By the Storm Orb exploding? By centuries of looting, vandalism, weathering, and neglect? Who or what has lived here since? What details remains to tell this story? What **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#1d6 discovery|discoveries]]** are waiting to be found?

sections.title: Discoveries
sections.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 discovery
sections.body:
- 1 Change of terrain [[39 - The Ruined Tower#**1d6** discovery|The Ruined Tower]] and roll again
- 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.)
- 3 remnants of the past (see below)
- 4 An encounter (next column)
- 5 **[[50 - Useful or Valuable Flora#Useful or valuable flora|Useful or valuable flora]]**, growing among the ruins
- 6 Something valuable (next column)

sections.title: 1d6 remnants of the past
sections.body:
- 1 Exposed stonework, marred by cataclysmic violence (melted, shattered, etc.)
- 2 Bits of ancient daily life (pots, tools, utensils, etc.), probably broken, possibly giant-sized
- 3 Signs of squatters/explorers: bones, litter, graffiti, excavation, crude construction, etc.
- 4 **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#Shards of the Storm Orb|Vitrified remains]]**
- 5 Ancient writing or artwork, faded/ obscured but still legible (*immobile*, *fragile*, Value 0-2)
- 6 A building/room, undisturbed for centuries, with treasure! [[39 - The Ruined Tower#1d6 discovery|1d6]]) you'd otherwise find only in the depths

sections.title: 1d6 encounter
sections.body:
- 1 A **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Shade**|pyped]]**, hiding from the sun or out at night, digging old bones to sup on the marrow
- 2 **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#Dangers|Blue magpies]]**, digging for shinies, foraging, or gossiping, maybe mocking/pranking the PCs
- 3 Critter(s)—fox, skunk, stoat, hares, prairie dogs, starlings, vultures, etc. —curious, annoying, maybe just cute
- 4 Larger animal(s)—deer, a ground sloth, wild sheep, maybe some **[[14 - The Flats#Aurochs|aurochs]]**—grazing near the ruins
- 5 Someone else, exploring/making camp, wary but not hostile: some **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk|Hillfolk]]**, **adventurers** and an **[[29 - Lygos and the South#**Dangers**|antiquarian]]**, **[[46 - The Things Below#**Cultist**|cultists]]**, a **[[46 - The Things Below#**Cultist**|sorcerer]]**, etc.
