- 6 A hidden entrance to the upper tunnels or the even the depths (a caved-in ceiling, a burrow, a chute, a secret door, etc.)

sections.title: 1d6 valuable
sections.body:
- 1 Big bits of metal (Value 1): melted/cracked/corroded/rusted
- 2 A gold ingot (Value 2), once jewelry, fused into a lump
- 3 A piece of **[[43 - Stone Lords#Various treasures|makerglass]]**, overlooked by prior looters
- 4 A **shard of the Storm Orb** (next page)
- 5 A **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A half-buried plaque|half-buried plaque]]**, or some other **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**Artifacts**|artifact]]** of the Tempest Lords.
- 6 An **[[05 - Barrow Builders#**Artifacts**|artifact]]** of the Barrow Builders, evidence that they were here, too

sections.title: Shards of the Storm Orb
sections.body:
It was a filigree sphere of aetherium [[45 - Tempest Lords#**Aetherium**|Tempest Lords]], maybe 50' across. It floated near the tower, a battery and a focus for tremendous elemental power.

Stormcatcher's final spell caused the Orb to explode. Shards were flung across the Flats [[14 - The Flats#The Flats|The Flats]], into the tower itself, and deep into the soil-to someday be unearthed by erosion, earthquakes, or burrowing beasts.

If the PCs encounter a shard, pick or roll for its size and signs .

| 1d6   | size                       |
|-------|----------------------------|
| 1     | Small (Value 0)            |
| 2     | (Value 1)                  |
| 3-4   | (Value 2)                  |
| 5     | ( cumbersome , Value 2)    |
| 6     | Immobile (Value 3 or more) |

sections.title: 1d6 signs
sections.body:
- 1 Hums/whines, painfully loud to those who can sense spirits
- 2 Vibrations make odd patterns in soil, prevent growth
- 3 Floats in a pocket of air, loosely covered by rock/soil
- 4 Makes skin tingle, hair stand up
- 5 Nearby soil/stone is blue-white, plants/lichen glow
- 6 Roll twice

Shards of the Storm Orb are unstable aetherium ( magical , dangerous ).  If damaged or overloaded, they might explode or violently discharge energy. But they are also etched with arcane runes. Find a big enough piece, or assemble enough smaller ones, and you might unlock some of the Tempest Lords' secrets.

sections.title: Vitrified remains
sections.body:
Stormcatcher's last spell turned anyone it touched into lumps of jagged glass. It was directed at Og'molok [[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Skittering horror**|The Ruined Tower]] and the skittering horrors [[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Patchwork construct**|The Ruined Tower]], but many of the attacking cultists were caught by the spell as well. So were some of the tower's residents.

Above ground, vitrified remains are mostly people (attackers or residents), with a few domesticated animals. Most are jagged lumps of black-grey glass, only vaguely recognizable as what they once were, and/ or broken by time or vandalism. These are covered in sharp edges (1d6 damage, messy , 1 piercing).

A few of these vitrified remains, though, are perfect glass statues, the fear or frenzy of their final moments frozen on their faces ( immobile , fragile ,  V alue 3 to the right buyer).

Vitrification [[45 - Tempest Lords#Vitrification|Tempest Lords]] can be reversed, but it's up to you to decide how.

sections.title: Hazards
sections.body:
- Old construction, giving way under foot or collapsing on someone
- A difficult climb or some missing stairs, a reason to risk it
- Earthquakes and tremors
- Hidden holes/burrows, twisting an ankle or worse
- Nasty weather: heat, cold, wind, sleet, hail, rain, snow, thunder (not so much lightning, as the spires draw it all)
- **[[14 - The Flats#**Dangers**|Prairie fire]]** among the outer ruins
- Unstable spells, **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**Aetherium**|aetherium]]**, **[[43 - Stone Lords#Perilous stone|perilous stone]]**

sections.title: Monsters and such
sections.body:
- The **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#**Fellow travelers**|nosgalau]]**, at night
- **[[14 - The Flats#**Thunder drake**|Thunder drakes]]**, digging for metal, especially aetherium
- Any of the other beasts of the **[[14 - The Flats#The Flats|Flats]]**
- An encampment of **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk|Hillfolk]]**, **[[40 - South Manmarch#**Horned drake**|bandits]]**, **[[46 - The Things Below#**Cultist**|cultists]]**, etc.
- Looters/explorers: **[[29 - Lygos and the South#**Dangers**|adventurers]]**, an **[[29 - Lygos and the South#**Dangers**|antiquarian]]**, a **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk rider|hdour]]**, a **[[46 - The Things Below#**Cultist**|sorcerer]]**, etc.
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Ghosts]]** or **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|revenants]]** of those who died when the tower fell, or from some later misadventure
- A **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Shade**|pyped]]**, digging up old bones where they can

sections.title: Blue magpie
sections.body:
*Horde*, *tiny*, *devious*, *organized*,

*hoarder*, *magical*

**HP** 1; **Armor** 1 (size)

**Damage** peck d4-2 w/disadvantage (hand) **Special qualities** smart as a child (at least); can speak Tempest Lord (understood by all)

**Instinct** to amuse/impress its friends

- ä Pretend to be just a dumb bird
- ä Mimic sounds or voices
- ä Mock or trick someone
- ä Steal something
- ä Vanish with a hrummm and reappear nearby (*reload*)

Like big crows with white-and-azure plumage. Their songs are piping and musical, playful and complex, sprinkled with actual voices and other unlikely sounds. A few dozen of them nest in the tower and the surrounding ruins. Smaller mischiefs nest in the Flats.

Stormcatcher kept blue magpies as pets, and their descendants have passed down much oral lore. Sneak up on them, and you might overhear magpies telling each other tales that southern scholars would kill to know.

Magpies find humans boorish, but fun to mess with, and they have such neat stuff. They love shiny objects, especially glass and fragments of larger things, and spend years making elaborate mosaics or piecing puzzles together.

sections.title: Belowground
sections.body: Most of the rooms and halls below the tower are spacious, vaulted to ~30'. Doorways are ~25' tall and mostly ~10' wide. There are a few human-scale rooms, but not many.

sections.title: Throughout
sections.body:
- A sense of smallness, of being swallowed by the dark
- Walls and ceilings beyond the limit of your light
- Distant sounds: dripping, rattling clanging, moaning (?), footfalls, skittering
- The thought of all that stone and earth above you

sections.title: The upper tunnels
sections.body:
- Surprisingly little dust, mud, debris
- Paths worn smooth, as from the tread of countless feet
- Faded gouges in flagstones, like heavy, sharp things were dragged across them, long long ago
- Carefully stacked rubble, plugging holes in walls, blocking off passages
- The sense that you're not alone, that you're being watched

sections.title: The depths
sections.body:
- Kicked-up dust, catching in your throat, making eyes water
- The looming menace of a vitrified horror, the skip in your heart before you realize it's just dead glass
- A thrum or whine, felt in your gut, your teeth, the back of your eyes
- Wait, haven't we been here before?

sections.title: Aetherium inlays
sections.body:
The four **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**1d12** feature|metal spires]]** extend down through the bedrock. They run through a number of underground rooms and passages, as freestanding pillars or embedded in walls.

Most rooms in and below the tower bear **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**Aetherium**|aetherium]]** inlays, spreading out from the metal spires. The inlays distribute power and anchor certain spells. Or at least, they used to.

The aetherium has been stripped from most of the upper tunnels, either carefully salvaged by **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Patchwork construct**|patchwork constructs]]** or gouged out by looters. More inlays survive in the depths, but even there, many have been marred by **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Various entities**|star-moles]]** or the claws of **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Patchwork construct**|skittering horrors]]**, or burnt out and reduced to crumbly chalk.

Where intact, inlays glow and pulse in response to winds above ground. When lightning strikes a spire, the aetherium flashes a bright blue-white. Some inlays are stable, but others are erratic and prone to discharge (1d8 damage, *forceful*, maybe *loud*).

sections.title: Sites
sections.body:
- **Central chamber** at the base of the **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**1d12** feature|main stairwell]]**. Big, wellkept, drains in floor, multiple exits.
- **Forge** and nearby rooms, haunted by **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Various entities**|Ferocedes]]**, packed with tools/parts and constructs in various states of repair.
- **Aetherium crucible**, near the forge. Dusty, unused, broken beyond Ferocedes's ability to fix with what's currently available to him.
- **Barracks** for soldiers who guarded the **Portal** (next column). Haunted by evacuees who hid and died there.
- **Latrine** near the barracks, with rattling, moaning, clanging pipes and still-working (!) showers.
- **Large chamber**, out of the way, the entrance blocked by piled stones. Packed full of vitrified horrors by Ferocedes's constructs.
- **Frozen room**, a chokepoint filled with **[[38 - Rime Lords#Dark ice|dark ice]]**, encasing dozens of **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Patchwork construct**|skittering horrors]]** and an **[[51 - Ustrina#Ustrina|Ustrina]]** who served as Stormcatcher's steward.
- **Oubliette**, a dead-end hall with multiple cells/vaults. Each holds or held a dangerous artifact or entity in stasis. Some are torn open. The rest are guarded by wards, devices, constructs, magic.
- **Bottommost chamber**, 400' across, partly flooded. Holds the feet of all four spires, a massive toroidal coil, hundreds of **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#Ancient wonders|vitrified horrors]]**), and the trapped manifestation of **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Skittering horror**|Og'molok, the Faceless Horde]]**.

sections.title: The Portal
sections.body:
In the upper tunnels, one can find a pair of double-doors: locked and warded, barricaded from within, with countless gouges clawed across their surface.

Beyond those doors: an austere room with a dozen vitrified people, blown-out **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#Ancient wonders|lightning sconces]]**, a makerglass window to the next room, and yet another locked door.

Beyond that: a once-stately sitting room (the furniture used for the barricade), a few more vitrified evacuees. Another door, this one ajar.

One last room. The far wall is a solid white slab, etched with a magic circle. It's a **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Primordial powers**|portal]]**, connected to those in the **[[28 - The Labyrinth#The Labyrinth|Labyrinth]]**, **[[47 - Three Coven Lake#Three Coven Lake|Three Coven Lake]]**, **[[52 - Vor Svetelik#Vor Svetelik|Vor Svetelik]]**, and so forth. It is fully functional, and can be used via **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A time-worn missive|Opening the Way]]**.

Layers of wards are etched in the floor, directed towards the portal, guarding against things coming out.

sections.title: Rooms & passages
sections.body: When you need to describe a room or passage, pick a shape and one or more features, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

sections.title: 1d12 shape
sections.body:
| 1-3 | Hallway, relatively narrow      |
|-----|---------------------------------|
| 4-5 | Square/rectangle/block          |
| 6-7 | Intersection                    |
| 8   | Round/sphere/dome/bowl          |
| 9   | Hexagon/octagon/etc.            |
| 10  | Irregular (L-shape, blob, etc.) |
|     |                                 |

11-12 Cluster: roll d10, 1d4+1 times

sections.title: 1d12 feature
sections.body:
- 1 Obstruction: locked door, rubble, cave-in, barricade, many **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Dangers**|vitrified horrors]]**, etc.
- 2 Fungus/lichen/slime/vermin
- 3 Drips/leaks/mud/standing water
- 4 Pipes, valves, pumps
- 5 Human-scale construction
- 6 Stairs/ramp/chute/platforms
- 7 Grandiose scale, a massive room
- 8 Unplanned exit/passage (burrow, collapsed construction, etc.)
- 9 Extensive and/or notably intact **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Ancient wonders**|aetherium inlays]]**; perhaps a metal pillar (one of the **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**1d12** feature|four spires]]**)
- 10 A wonder (see below)
- 11 Roll 1d10 twice
- 12 Roll 1d10 three times

Consider rolling 1d6 for the scope or condition of any room/passage/feature. Fill it with **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Discoveries**|discoveries]]** and/or **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Dangers**|dangers]]** as you see fit.

sections.title: Wonders
sections.body: Stormcatcher kept many experiments, curiosities, and dangerous phenomena. Pick 1, roll 1d12, or make up your own.

sections.title: 1d12 wonder
sections.body:
- 1 A fountain, its hidden pumps powered by aetherium.
- 2 Nexus of pipes, pumps, boilers. By a stairwell, kept up by **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Patchwork construct**|patchwork constructs]]**.
- 3 Deep pit, smooth walls, a **[[11 - The Dread River#**Luglfsk**|jellied horror]]** trapped below. Used as a midden pit.
- 4 A huge stone slab, with fittings that make hot, steady flame on demand.
- 5 Room full of plants (*exotic*, *useful*, *valuable*, *dangerous*). Lit by a makerglass sun, watered by pipes, tended by a fertility spirit.
- 6 Huge orrery. Bits are melted, fused, burnt out; "stars" stuck in a potent cosmic alignment.
- 7 Knee-high dust, shapes itself into whatever an occupant thinks of. Crumbles if removed from room.
- 8 Freestanding arch. Pass through and you vanish. Exit ~5 seconds later, seems instantaneous to you.
- 9 Tight-packed aetherium inlays. Hazy images show what anyone in the room is about to do.
- 10 Sliding tiles in walls/ceiling/floor change gravity's direction and/or intensity in the room.
- 11 Airless room with small floating black sphere, annihilates any matter it touches.
- 12 Black lightning crackles between two prongs, ~3' apart. Fine glass dust all around. A **[[45 - Tempest Lords#Vitrification|vitrifying bolt]]**, just barely contained.

sections.title: Discoveries
sections.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 discovery
sections.body:
- 1 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.)
- 2-3 A vignette of the fall of the tower (see below): a vitrified tableau, remains, physical evidence, psychic imprints, writing, etc.
- 4 An encounter (next column)
- 5 An opportunity (next page)
- 6 treasure! (next page)

sections.title: 1d6 vignette of the fall
sections.body:
- 1 A wanton slaughter
- 2 A desperate flight
- 3 A game of cat and mouse
- 4 A grim last stand
- 5 A heroic sacrifice
- 6 A cruel betrayal

sections.title: 1d6 encounter
sections.body:
- 1 A dangerous entity: imprisoned, bound, in stasis
- 2 A **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Shade**|pyped]]**, trawling the depths for bones to sup on
- 3 The **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|ghost]]** of someone who died in the attack, still running, trying to escape
- 4 **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Various entities**|Star-moles]]**: digging, foraging, or being hunted
- 5 Uncanny fungus/lichen/vermin (glowing, humming, feeding on ghosts, etc.), maybe dangerous but not outright hostile
- 6 A **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Patchwork construct**|patchwork construct]]**, on some task for Ferocedes

sections.title: 1d6 opportunity
sections.body:
- 1 Something **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Various entities**|Ferocedes]]** would want: a construct's **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Patchwork construct**|core]]**, a part for the **[[45 - Tempest Lords#AETHERIUM CRUCIBLE|aetherium crucible]]**, etc.
- 2 A map/path to a place they seek
- 3 A hidden door/room/passage (secret, buried, or obscured)
- 4 A good/safe place to rest
- 5 Lore about the portal: a **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A time-worn missive|time-worn missive]]**, sigils of other portals, a treatise on the axiom of local contagion, etc.
- 6 A key (or similar), a way to bypass an obstacle elsewhere

sections.title: 1d6 treasure!
sections.body:
- 1 Cache of metal ingots, tools, parts, etc. (*immobile*, Value 2 or 3)
- 2 Some… (roll 1d6 or pick)
  - 1 …**[[38 - Rime Lords#Dark ice|dark ice]]**
  - 2 …**[[43 - Stone Lords#Various treasures|makerglass]]**
  - 3 …**[[18 - Forge Lords#**1d6** architectural elements|orichalcum]]**
  - 4 …**[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Primordial powers**|black iron]]**
  - 5-6 …**[[45 - Tempest Lords#**Aetherium**|aetherium]]**
- 3-4 An artifact of the **[[45 - Tempest Lords#Tempest Lords|Tempest Lords]]**
- 5 An artifact from another group of **[[30 - The Makers#The Makers|Makers]]**: a gift, a trophy, an object of study, or a danger locked away
- 6 An artifact of **[[36 - Primordial Powers#Primordial powers|primordial power]]** or the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**, kept for study and/or safekeeping

sections.title: The environment
sections.body:
- Getting lost in the maze of tunnels
- Torches running low, time passing faster than you think
- **[[43 - Stone Lords#**Dangers**|Unstable construction]]**, caused by cataclysm, time, earthquakes, excavation, star-moles
- Open stairwells, worn-smooth stairs, a lack of railings, a nasty fall
- The exhausting trudge back up hundreds of stairs
- Floors wet from rain, snow melt, or leaky pipes, slick and/or muddy
- Weird growths/bugs: filling the air, shrieking if disturbed, absorbing raw power, spitting it back out
- Earthquakes or tremors, even worse down here than up above

sections.title: Ancient wonders
sections.body:
- Damaged **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Ancient wonders**|aetherium inlays]]**, erratic and unstable (1d8 damage, *forceful*, maybe *loud*)
- Pressurized pipes, spewing steam or scalding water if ruptured (1d8 damage, *area*)
- Metal bulkheads, rigged to drop and contain runaway spells/experiments/ prisoners
- Raw chemics, neatly stowed, spilled, seeping
- Machines, running erratically, fully able to rend flesh and bone
- Broken laws of reality, and whatever that does to mind/body/soul
- **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**Storm-cloud potion**|Radiation]]**, hopefully contained, probably not
- **[[18 - Forge Lords#**Smoke, fire, and heat**|Smoke, fire, and heat]]** in the haunted forge
- **[[43 - Stone Lords#Perilous stone|Perilous stone]]**: stolen, imported, borrowed, replicated

sections.title: Corruption, as one goes deeper
sections.body:
- **[[46 - The Things Below#**Voice of the Eternal Maw**|Disquiet and unease]]**, especially near masses of vitrified horrors
- **[[46 - The Things Below#**Voice of the Eternal Maw**|Whispers and visions]]**, coming from the horrors, or from below, or from everywhere at once
- **[[46 - The Things Below#**On a 6-**, pick 1:|Unnatural phenomena]]**, as one approaches the bottommost chamber and what remains of **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Skittering horror**|Og'molok]]**.

sections.title: Vitrified horrors
sections.body: **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Patchwork construct**|Skittering horrors]]**, fused into black, jagged glass by Stormcatcher's last spell. There are more and more as you go deeper. In some places, they clog stairs or passages, making it difficult to squeeze by without your gear or your flesh getting shredded (1d4 to 1d8 damage, *messy*, 1 piercing).

sections.title: Lightning sconce
sections.body:
*Magical*, *construct*, *immobile*

**HP** 1; **Armor** 4 (tiny, metal fittings, made of crystal)

**Damage** lightning 1d6+2 (*near*, *forceful*, *reload*, ignores armor)

A glowing gem, set in a fixture of aetherium. Found in pairs, or sets of up to six. Each set is keyed to something (a pass phrase, a hand signal, a color of robes, a style of locket, etc.). Get too close without the key, and *zzzolt*, they start blasting.

sections.title: Various entities
sections.body:
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Ghosts]]** or **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|revenants]]** of looters or residents
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Shade**|Pypeds]]** seeking bones
- **[[18 - Forge Lords#**Metal constructs**|Metal constructs]]**, lost to Ferocedes, or else greatly prized
- **[[45 - Tempest Lords#Vitrification|Elemental vortices]]**, guarding something
- **Cultists**, a **thrall**, and/or a **[[46 - The Things Below#**Cultist**|sorcerer]]**, eking out a life
- A **[[05 - Barrow Builders#**Deathless ones**|deathless one]]**, here to claim the tower's secrets, now trapped
- An **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Other primordial entities**|archon]]** or **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|spirit of the wild]]**, bound and possibly put to use.

sections.title: Star-mole
sections.body:
*Horde*, *cautious*, *devious*, *organized*, *hardy*

**HP** 7; **Armor** 0

**Damage** caustic spit or blood d6 w/disadvantage (*hand*, *close*, *messy*, 1 piercing)

**Special qualities** blind; tremorsense; bioluminescent

**Instinct** to lure predators into traps

- ä Soften stone with its spit, then dig through it
- ä Spurt caustic blood when hurt
- ä Scamper away, hide, act as bait
- ä Trigger a collapse or cave-in

Big, naked mole-rats, like 5' long, with saggy skin, glowing freckles, and clusters of wriggling feelers on their noses. They eat vermin and fungus that grow near leaking pipes. They also tunnel through bedrock, collapse passages, and expose sealed-off rooms.

Every so often, they wake a **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Patchwork construct**|skittering horror]]** from its torpor. This spells doom for the colony, unless they can trap it or cut off access to the nest. They'll treat the PCs as a similar threat.

sections.title: Ferocedes Ogran, ghostly Forge Lord
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *spirit*, *undead*, *magical*, *organized*, *obsessive*, *absentminded*

**HP** 23; **Armor** 4 (metal, amorphous) **Damage** pummel d12+3 (*close*, *reach*, *forceful*) or barrage of metal d10+1 (*near*, *area*, *messy*, *forceful*, *reload*, 1 piercing)

**Special qualities** tethered to his forge, and can't venture far from it; speaks Tempest Lord (understood by all)

**Instinct** to preserve/restore the tower, to ignore harsh truths

- ä Manifest a cloud of ember and ash, a voice of crackling flame
- ä Ask what they've brought him, if that one task is done yet, etc.
- ä Fail to understand their speech
- ä Grow annoyed, angry, violent
- ä Manipulate metal, telekinetically
- ä Repair metal, or shape it anew
- ä Animate a body formed of metal tools, parts, and scrap

In life, he was a brilliant but awkward Maker. Rivals sabotaged him, and he left his people in shame. Stormcatcher took him in, trusted him, gave him purpose and for that, Ferocedes was rabidly loyal. Loyal unto death, and beyond.

He died without noticing it. He had no time to treat the wounds he had suffered. Stormcatcher needed his skills. Needed his creations. Needed him.

So Ferocedes was already a ghost when Stormcatcher cast her final spell. And in the centuries since, he has fixated on his task: to reclaim, rebuild, and repair, little by little, with what tools he has, and preserve the memory of his friend.

sections.title: Patchwork construct
sections.body:
*Group*, *construct*, *cautious*

**HP** 14; **Armor** 3 (metal)

**Damage** tool at hand d8 (*hand* or *close*) **Special qualities** must recharge every few days; dog-like intellect; blind; tremorsense

**Instinct** to complete its given task

- ä Size up an obstacle or task
- ä Reveal/use a tool, feature, or attachment for its current task
- ä Plod away, raise the alarm

Ferocedes once had dozens of constructs to defend and maintain the tower. But the years have not been kind. Only ten or so remain, and one or two are usually offline for modifications or repairs.

Each one has an aetherium core (*magical*, *fragile*, Value 2 to the right buyer). Ferocedes can repair minor damage to a core, and/or install it in a new body, but he can't make new ones without the **[[45 - Tempest Lords#AETHERIUM CRUCIBLE|aetherium crucible]]**.

Each construct is generally humanoid, though most lack a "head" and all are made of mismatched parts. Pick or roll a current task for each. Consider picking or rolling for an attribute [[18 - Forge Lords#**Metal constructs**|Forge Lords]] and adjusting its stat block accordingly.

sections.title: 1d6 current task
sections.body:
- 1 Patrolling/guarding/defending
- 2 Masonry/repairing walls
- 3 Plumbing/other delicate work
- 4 Sweeping/cleaning/pest control
- 5 Hauling/storing/reorganizing
- 6 Seeking/retrieving/inspecting

sections.title: Skittering horror
sections.body:
*Horde*, *emanation*, *terrifying*, *sadistic*, *aggressive*

**HP** 7; **Armor** 4 (resilience), 0 vs. bronze **Damage** scythe-like limbs d6+2 w/advantage (*hand*, *close*, messy, 2 piercing except vs. bronze)

**Special qualities** spider-climb; blind; senses life (heat, heartbeats, breathing, movement, etc.); disoriented by intense noise/heat

**Instinct** to hurt, terrorize, kill

- ä Investigate sounds/signs of life
- ä Chitter, trill, loudly drone
- ä Call others to the hunt
- ä Attack with shocking fury
- ä Savage a fallen foe

6 feet long, with segmented bodies and too many legs. Scythe-like forelimbs. Feathery antennae that caress the air and quiver when they sense prey. No eyes, just red crystal pulsing within its skull. No mouth, for it never eats and lives on hate and malice.

Each is a manifestation of Og'molok's cruel will. Born of blood, given shape, sent skittering up to slaughter. And slaughter they did, until Stormcatcher's final spell turned them all into jagged lumps of glass.

Or, almost all of them. A few dozen avoided the vitrifying spell. They linger on, in a state of torpor until they sense a living, feeling being. Then they wake. Then they hunt. Then they slaughter once more.

sections.title: Og'molok, the Faceless Horde Threat (magical entity)
sections.body:
**Instinct** to revel in slaughter, pain, and terror

**Themes** cruelty/torture/violence and destruction/chaos/ruin

- ä Send terrible dreams/visions to those touched by violence
- ä Urge bloodshed, cruelty, rage
- ä Shake the earth in a fit

In the **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Og'molok, the Faceless Horde** Threat (*magical entity*)|bottommost chamber]]** of the Ruined Tower is a cone of jagged black glass, a pile of vitrified horrors that reaches almost to the ceiling. This is where Og'molok burst into the world as a fountain of blood, coalescing into a skittering swarm. This is where it met its doom, fused into a pillar of **[[46 - The Things Below#**Unnatural phenomena**|red crystal]]**, encased in a pile of slag.

Og'molok, of course, is not truly dead. Just bound, neutered, diminished. It still corrupts the lowest levels beneath the tower, still calls to those who might hear. It is trapped, and it cannot escape. Not yet. Not without help.

l Someone sets out to free it l They learn how to release it from its **vitrified** state (see [[45 - Tempest Lords#Vitrification|Tempest Lords]]) l They acquire what they need l They begin the process l **Impending doom**: Og'molok and its swarm are loosed upon the world

sections.title: South Manmarch
sections.body:
If you descend from the **[[42 - The Steplands#The Steplands|Steplands]]** to the southeast, you'll find yourself on a rolling prairie. The ground slopes gently down toward the east, to the banks of the **[[11 - The Dread River#The Dread River|Dread River]]**.

Travelers and merchants aplenty cross these plains. They come north, from **[[29 - Lygos and the South#Lygos and the South|Lygos and the civilized south]]**, heading north to the **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#The Makers' Roads|Highway]]** and from there to **[[32 - Marshedge#Marshedge|Marshedge]]**. Or they head the other way, bearing herbs and textiles, metals and gems, back to the southern cities. Or they come from beyond the Highway, from the **[[34 - North Manmarch#North Manmarch|North Manmarch]]**, to hunt or trade or raid.

But for all those who pass through the South Manmarch, few call it home. Sure, the **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk|Hillfolk]]** graze their herds here in the warmer months. And some new settlement is always popping up. But the land is dotted with burnt-out homesteads and ruined forts: a mute testament to those who have tried to settle here before. Tried, and failed.

sections.title: Always
sections.body:
- The eternal, unresponsive sky
- Shallow, rocky soil broken by outcrops of white limestone and gray shale
- Smoke from a lone homestead, overlooking a watercourse
- A stream or dry gully, winding lazily down toward the Dread River
- An abandoned, collapsed longhouse
- A Hillfolk horseband, watching from higher ground

sections.title: Spring
sections.body:
- A mottled carpet of new grass and wildflowers—yellow, pale green, lilac
- Pocket wetlands, home to nesting ducks recently returned from the south
- A great cloud of birds, bursting forth from the brush

sections.title: Summer
sections.body:
- Squinting into a blinding sky
- The fervid and unrelenting chorus of summer insects, shifting in tone and rhythm with time of day
- Tallgrass in full flush, waist-high and rippling in a southerly wind.
- A mob of white-tailed deer or mighty elk, grazing on a long slope

sections.title: Autumn
sections.body:
- Grassheads gone rust-colored, stalks still green
- A streamside stand of cottonwoods, glowing gold in the dusk light

sections.title: Winter
sections.body:
- Days of scouring, southwesterly wind
- Powdery snowdrifts, stretching leeward from ripples in the landscape
- Patches of dead grass, showing orange through the snow

sections.title: A long-disputed land
sections.body:
Before the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Cataclysm]]** the Dread River formed the eastern border of **[[43 - Stone Lords#Stone Lords|Stone Lord]]** territory. After the Stone Lords fell, the **[[05 - Barrow Builders#Barrow Builders|Barrow Builders]]** fought each other back and forth across this expanse, grinding themselves down, like shards of shale abraded to dust over time.

Now, the South Manmarch is the border between the civilized south and the hinterlands of the World's End. It's a lawless place, full of dangerous beasts and even more dangerous people. Nomadic Hillfolk claim much of the region, and **[[40 - South Manmarch#**Horned drake**|bandits]]** are a constant threat.

And yet, the lure of the prairie is great. Every year or so, someone tries to carve out a piece of for themselves. A new settlement might be:

- A family from Marshedge or the North Manmarch, starting their own farm
- An ambitious Lygosi merchant, investing in a new trading post or caravansary
- Some would-be chief or warlord from the **[[34 - North Manmarch#North Manmarch|Manmarchers]]**, building a new hamlet or hillfort
- The **conquering armies** of the South [[29 - Lygos and the South#**Conquering army**|Lygos and the South]], establishing a foothold

Such settlements rarely last. They fall prey to natural disaster or roving beasts, to rivals or bandits, or to the certain wrath of the Hillfolk who claim these lands as theirs.

Or perhaps, if you believe the stories, there's something darker afoot. Perhaps these lands really are haunted. Haunted by the numerous souls who've died here. Or by foul things, slithering out of the Dread River. Perhaps the land is cursed, and no settlement can ever survive. Perhaps.

sections.title: The Flintwash
Questions
sections.body:
- Who did you know who tried to settle in the South Manmarch? What fate befell them?
- What bandit crew is currently the most feared? Who leads them, and what makes them so dangerous?
- At what landmark do merchant caravans typically encamp? Why do they stop there?
- What landmark does everyone who travels these lands know to avoid? Why?

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- Someone has seized the Flintwash crossing, and is charging exorbitant tolls
- An ambitious chief from the North Manmarch is sending settlers south of the Highway
- The PCs eagerly await a caravan, but it gets waylaid by bandits
- A ruin in the region becomes a place of pilgrimage, drawing the faithful of Helior/Aratis from all over

sections.title: Terrain
sections.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12) once or twice.

sections.title: 1d12 terrain
sections.body:
1 Ash field/barrens/sand/rocky stretch 2 Burrow/dugout/warren 3 Sodden soil/mud/pocket wetland 4 Spring/pond/creek/stream/river 5-6 Open meadow, waist-high grass 7-8 Shortgrass prairie, vast and open 9 Footpath/wagon ruts/trail 10 Tree(s), savanna, thickets 11 Gentle slope/low ridge/small hill

If you like, roll 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

12 Steep slope/rocky outcrop/large hill

sections.title: Discoveries
sections.body: For any one of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 discovery
sections.body:
- 1 Change of terrain (previous page) and roll again
- 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.)
- 3 **[[50 - Useful or Valuable Flora#Useful or valuable flora|Useful or valuable flora]]**
- 4 beast (see below)
- 5 An encounter (see below)
- 6 A point of interest (next column)

sections.title: 1d6 beast
sections.body:
- 1-3 Mostly harmless but prone to bolt: deer, hares, prairie dogs, birds, etc.
- 4-5 A herd: **[[14 - The Flats#**Monsters**|aurochs]]**, goats, sheep, **[[14 - The Flats#**Thunder drake**|wild horses]]**, etc.
- 6 Something big and majestic: a **[[42 - The Steplands#**Bat swarm**|brawzbedr]]**, a giant ibis, a **[[40 - South Manmarch#**Dangers**|horned drake]]**, a **[[14 - The Flats#**Thunder drake**|thunder drake]]**, etc.

sections.title: 1d6 encounter
sections.body:
- 1 **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk|Hillfolk]]**: encamped, herding, hunting, on patrol, come to trade, or maybe on a raid
- 2 **Merchant** caravan [[29 - Lygos and the South#**Merchants**|Lygos and the South]], coming or going
- 3 **[[40 - South Manmarch#**Horned drake**|Bandits]]**, encamped, scouting, returning from a raid
- 4 Settlers, hoping to carve out a home here
- 5 Someone in dire straits; roll 1d4 again to see who
- 6 **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Spirits of the wild|Spirit of the wild]]**, curious or indifferent

sections.title: 1d6 point of interest
sections.body:
- 1 A place corrupted by the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**
- 2 A tomb, site, or artifact of the **[[05 - Barrow Builders#Barrow Builders|Barrow Builders]]**
- 3 A battlefield or remains (below)
- 4 A ruin or fortification of the **[[43 - Stone Lords#Stone Lords|Stone Lords]]**
- 5 A place sacred to the **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Spirits of the wild|spirits of the wild]]**
- 6 A new settlement

sections.title: 1d12 battlefield or remains
sections.body:
- 1 Truly ancient: from the time of the **[[30 - The Makers#The Makers|Makers]]**
- 2 From the **[[55 - Appendix A - Ages of the World#Time of Cataclysm|Time of Cataclysm]]**, when humanity rebelled and woke the Things Below
- 3-4 From a conflict among the **[[05 - Barrow Builders#Barrow Builders|Barrow Builders]]**
- 5-7 From a previous attempt at settlement: burnt out, collapsed, overgrown
- 8-10 Recent: Hillfolk, bandits, travelers, settlers, beasts
- 11-12 Very recent: the buzzards and crows still feasting

Many such places are **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|haunted]]** by the dead.

sections.title: Hazards
sections.body:
- Baking sun, no shade for miles
- Nasty turn in the weather: lashing wind, bitter cold, blistering heat, sleet/ hail/rain/snow; lightning
- Flash flooding in valleys/on slopes
- Lack of shelter, water, or wood for a long-burning fire (try dried aurochs dung, if you can find it)
- Hidden holes/burrows, twisting an ankle or worse
- **[[14 - The Flats#**Dangers**|Prairie fire]]**

sections.title: Monsters
sections.body:
- Predators: **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Cougar**|cougars]]**, **[[42 - The Steplands#1d6 natural beast or spirit|kleztigr]]**, **[[14 - The Flats#**Hlomren**|pack drakes]]**, or **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Raselbaedd**|wolves]]**
- Big ornery beasts: **[[14 - The Flats#**Monsters**|aurochs]]**, **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Dangers**|boar]]**, or a **[[14 - The Flats#**Thunder drake**|thunder drake]]**
- **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Spirits of the wild|Spirits of the wild]]**
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Ghosts]]** or **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|revenants]]**, lurking near ancient battlefields or ruined settlements

sections.title: People
sections.body:
- **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk|Hillfolk]]**, if they're of a mind to do violence
- **[[34 - North Manmarch#North Manmarch|North Manmarchers]]**, heading south or defending a new settlement
- Merchants from **[[32 - Marshedge#Marshedge|Marshedge]]**), heading south or coming home
- Southern **[[29 - Lygos and the South#**Merchants**|merchants]]** and their caravan guards, plus those traveling with them, like adventurers or an **[[29 - Lygos and the South#**Dangers**|antiquarian]]**
- **[[29 - Lygos and the South#**Conquering army**|Armies of the south]]**, coming north to exact vengeance, secure a resource, establish a foothold

sections.title: Horned drake
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *huge*, *hardy*

**HP** 24; **Armor** 3 (stony hide)

**Damage** trampling charge d10+5 (*hand*, *area*, *forceful*) or horn gore d10+5 (*close*, *forceful*, *messy*, 1 piercing)

**Instinct** to protect their young

- <sup>ä</sup> Issue a honking call
- <sup>ä</sup> Stand its ground, still as a rock
- <sup>ä</sup> Lurch suddenly into a charge
- <sup>ä</sup> Absorb raw elemental power (regaining 1d8 HP)

The behemoth of the southern grasslands is striking for its great size, the quartz-like plates of its segmented hide, and its fearsome horns. The horns vary greatly from beast to beast, ranging from a single spike on its snout or forehead to a rack of five or six curved points edging the flanges of its massive skull. An intimidating beast, even at rest.

To the Hillfolk, the downing of a *d'lkornek* ("thorn-dragon") is a hero's feat, to be followed by a week of harvest and celebration at the kill site.

**Something interesting**: Some scholars hold that the Stone Lords imbued horned drakes with elemental qualities. Specimens of their hide are commonly mistaken for dolostone or some other mineral, and Hillfolk prize their horns (Value 3).

**Something useful:** The horns of a *d'lkornek* act like **[[43 - Stone Lords#Power-crystal|power-crystals]]**, but larger and with great capacity. They are extremely dangerous to harvest; a wise butcher slowly bleeds away their energy over the course of days.

Adult horned drakes might be accompanied by their young (*group*, *large*, *hardy*; **HP** 14; **Damage** d8+3; **Instinct** to get curious, to play fight), whom they aggressively defend.

*Horde*, *stealthy*, *cautious*, *devious* **HP** 3; **Armor** up to 2 (hides, shields) **Damage** spear d6 (*close*, *thrown*) or sling d6 (*near*, *awkward*, *reload*)

- **Instinct** to survive at the expense of others ä Block passage, issue a blunt demand
- ä Ambush under cover of darkness
- ä Ransom the best, kill the rest

The **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#The Makers' Roads|Makers' Roads]]** limit banditry throughout most the World's End. Not so in the South Manmarch. Here, merchants travel without the safety of warded pavestones. And those merchants draw bandits like dung draws flies. They're motley crews, these bandits. Rogues from Marshedge. Deserters from the armies of North and South. Exiled Hillfolk. Scoundrels. Miscreants. Anyone who's willing to get their hands red, really.

sections.title: Bandit chief
sections.body:
*Group*, *leader*, *stealthy*, *cautious*, *devious*, *organized*

**HP** 8; **Armor** 3 (hides, shield, skill) **Damage** spear d8 (*close*, *thrown*, 1 piercing)

or bow d8 (*near*) **Instinct** to live their best life beyond the reach of the law

- ä Exude a brutal charisma
- ä Demonstrate great skill at arms
- ä Brook no disagreement

Every group demands a boss; the exiled and forsaken moreso. What makes this leader stand apart? (pick 2 or 3)

- Infamy, from deeds real or fabricated
- Fearlessness, borne of a veteran's confidence or amateur's stupidity
- Fragile alliance with a Hillfolk band
- A **spirit-of-the-wild** familiar [[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Spirits of the wild|Spirits of the wild]]
  - A sidekick, in the form of a **[[46 - The Things Below#**Cultist**|sorcerer]]** or **[[26 - Hillfolk#Upland horse|spirit-talker]]**

sections.title: Spirits of the wild
sections.body:
The spirit realm is filled with entities, especially those of the natural world. Each beast, each tree, each gust of wind or flickering flame—they all have spirits. So do the soil, the rocks, the mountains and the streams.

Most of these spirits are quiet, slumbering or barely conscious or operating on a scale so alien as to be impenetrable to human minds. But some mortals can speak to them nonetheless, and goad them into action. Sometimes, these sleepers shift and mumble and stretch of their own accord.

The most active spirits are those tethered to physical objects, or those associated with beasts or other "animate" things—things full of life and energy. These are the spirits that mortals encounter most often.

sections.title: Themes
sections.body: Choose or roll 1, or 2 and combine.

sections.title: 1d12 theme
sections.body:
- 1 Trees/plants/flora/growing things
- 2 Beasts/vermin/fauna
- 3 Air/wind/weather/fire
- 4 Spring/birth/fertility/sex/healing
- 5 Summer/heat/growth/vitality
- 6 Autumn/harvest/sustenance/plenty
- 7 Winter/cold/hunger/sleep/death
- 8 Savagery/the hunt/dominance
- 9 Nurture/shelter/provision
- 10 The land/soil/stone/terrain
- 11 Blight/decay/reclamation/ destruction
- 12 Water/streams/ponds/lakes (possibly corrupted by **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|the Things Below]]**

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- A villager (or visitor) has been cursed by a powerful elemental spirit, maybe in a way that puts others in danger.
- A child hears voices and sees spirits; maybe they're crippled with fear, or maybe they recklessly ask spirits for petty favors.
- Something that the PCs want can be found in in a sacred site, one that's known to house unfriendly, powerful spirits.
- Someone has unwittingly brought a spirit's tether into the village, and it starts wreaking havoc.
- A PC learns that a particular spirit knows an important secret, something they desperately want to learn.

sections.title: Regarding spirits
sections.body: The following apply not just to spirits of the wild, but also to **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|ghosts]]**, **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|dool spirits]]**, some **[[36 - Primordial Powers#Primordial powers|primordial powers]]**, the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**, and **[[20 - Gods and Religion#**Little gods**|little gods]]**.

sections.title: Manifestation
sections.body:
Spirits lack physical form, existing in the realm of thought and symbol. They can perceive and influence the mortal realm in subtle ways, but to directly affect it, a spirit must manifest or possess a physical form.

Each spirit manifests differently. Some shape a body from crude matter. Others appear as intangibles: cold, shadow, moonlight, etc. Some can only "ride" living beings. Others can animate beings that were once alive, or vessels specifically prepared to hold them. A rare few can manifest bodies of pure spirit-stuff.

Spirits manifest or possess forms that make symbolic sense. A fire spirit won't possess water; it might manifest as a flame, or animate a cloud of cinders, or possess a hot-headed youth.

sections.title: Tethers
sections.body:
Some spirits are bound to a thing in the physical world. The tether might occur naturally (like a brennauwed and its tree), by choice or mutual benefit (like an altar where mortals leave offerings for the spirt), or via trickery, force, or coercion.

A tether anchors and empowers its spirit, granting it a stronger connection to the physical world. If harmed or weakened, the spirit can retreat to its tether to rejuvenate.

But a tether is not just a boon; it's also a vulnerability. Harming the tether harms the spirit. Destroying the tether destroys the spirit—or at least weakens, diminishes, and greatly changes it.

Some tethers don't just anchor their spirits: they bind and imprison them. A spirit so bound can't leave its tether's presence. The most powerful bindings seal their spirits away, greatly restricting their perceptions and ability to influence the world. But no binding is perfect, and a spirit so bound might tempt or trick others into releasing it.

sections.title: Fighting Spirits
sections.body:
One can fight a spirit only when it manifests, and even then they can be difficult to harm. Manifest forms often have lots of HP and might be vulnerable to only certain materials or attacks.

Even if one can harm a manifestation, they're not really harming the spirit. Reducing it to 0 HP ends the manifestation. This weakens the spirit, forcing it to retreat to its tether (if it has one). But it doesn't truly harm it.

To truly harm a spirit, one must use potent magic, damage or destroy its tether, or fight it directly in the spirit realm. A spirit reduced to 0 HP in one of these ways is actually destroyed—or at least so thoroughly diminished that it won't be a problem for many human lifetimes.

You might want to track a spirit's "manifest" HP vs. "true" HP. If the PCs hurt a manifest spirit with something that could truly harm it, reduce both sets of HP.

sections.title: Communion
Elemental spirits
sections.body:
These spirits mostly just do their thing, with little concern for humanity unless some mortal offends them or seeks them out for aid. An elemental spirit can:

- <sup>ä</sup> Reveal what it knows, from its own perspective
- <sup>ä</sup> Spread word to other spirits
- <sup>ä</sup> Bestow a blessing or curse, tied to its element
- <sup>ä</sup> Shift, move, or direct that which falls in its domain

Elemental spirits rarely manifest. When they do, they often possess or animate the element itself, or appear as ghostly figures somehow reflecting their element. They're more likely to be NPCs than *monsters*.

Here are examples; feel free to modify and expand on them. Maybe you picture cave spirits differently than this. Maybe a decorated limestone cave has different tags, instincts, and notes than a cave-bear's den. Or maybe this particular cave's spirit is different than other cave spirits. Consider asking a character (who would know) what certain types of spirits are usually like, and use what they give you.

sections.title: hates the light. Cold spirit
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *spirit*, *quiet*, *patient*, *bitter* **Instinct** to slow things down, to preserve **Notes** Whispery voice; finds warmth and exuberance distasteful.

**Notes** Drops hints about what's inside;

sections.title: Fertility spirit
sections.body: *Group*, *spirit*, *earthy*, *sensual*, *positive* **Instinct** to encourage growth **Notes** Giggles, touchy; frustrated by prudery, inhibition, restraint.

sections.title: Fire spirit
sections.body:
*Group*, *spirit*, *needy*, *intense*, *aggressive* **Instinct** to consume

**Notes** Cackles gleefully; fears water, smothering; always hungry.

sections.title: Log spirit
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *spirit*, *sickly*, *cheerful*, *giving* **Instinct** to not mind, really

**Notes** Ho-hum, it's okay; falls apart under serious pressure.

sections.title: Meadow/prairie/grass spirit
sections.body:
*Horde*, *spirit*, *lazy*, *bashful*, *chatty*

**Instinct** to gossip amongst themselves **Notes** Like easily embarrassed teens; "Shh, don't tell them!"

**Mist/fog spirit**

 *Solitary*, *spirit*, *presumptuous*, *hazy* **Instinct** to get intimate, blur boundaries **Notes** Hushed, dripping voice; spaced-out; touchy-feely.

sections.title: Mud spirit
sections.body: *Group*, *spirit*, *cheerful*, *grimy*, *clingy* **Instinct** to make a mess of things **Notes** Genuinely doesn't get why you're upset or frustrated.

sections.title: Peat/bog/fen spirit
sections.body: *Solitary*, *spirit*, *lazy*, *jealous*, *gluttonous* **Instinct** to take whatever it can **Notes** Deep froggy voice; licks "lips," makes baseless accusations.

sections.title: Pond spirit
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *spirit*, *placid*, *watchful*, *callous* **Instinct** to remain uninvolved

**Notes** Sighs frequently; ignores them, unless they provoke it.

 *Solitary*, *spirit*, *moody*, *overbearing* **Instinct** to give, but to be easily offended **Notes** Tut-tuts; wants to be needed, appreciated; passive-aggressive.

sections.title: Rock/stone/boulder/hill spirit
sections.body: *Group* or *solitary*, *spirit*, *reliable*, *chill* **Instinct** to remain unperturbed **Notes** Speaks very slowly; friendly; no urgency; "Relax, man."

sections.title: Sapling spirit
sections.body: *Group*, *spirit*, *earnest*, *chipper* **Instinct** to seek what it needs to grow **Notes** Wants attention, makes big promises.

sections.title: Snow spirit
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *spirit*, *secretive*, *aloof*, *quiet* **Instinct** to conceal things

**Notes** Prim and proper; talks around the issue, always.

sections.title: Storm spirit
sections.body: *Solitary*, *spirit*, *brash*, *arrogant*, *potent* **Instinct** to test any who stand before it **Notes** Big, rumbling voice; intense but distracted, has places to be.

sections.title: Stream/creek spirit
sections.body: *Solitary*, *spirit*, *bubbly*, *restless*, *forgetful* **Instinct** to offer surprises, to wash away **Notes** Rarely stops talking, fidgeting; repeats self.

sections.title: Trail spirit
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *spirit*, *inviting*, *yielding*

**Instinct** to reveal

**Notes** Agitated, nervous voice; draws attention to those it helps

sections.title: Tree spirit
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *spirit*, *proud*, *stubborn*, *stoic*

**Instinct** to take its time

**Notes** Speaks slowly; clings to hurts, grudges, kindnesses.

sections.title: Wind spirit
Sacred sites
sections.body: These are places where spirits of the wild are tethered, or where they tend to be active. Pick or roll for terrain based on the region, theme [[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Sacred sites**|Spirits of the Wild]] to determine the nature of the spirit(s) present, a marker, and the spirits' activity and disposition. Consider rolling the Die of Fate to determine the spirit or spirits' relative power.

sections.title: 1d12 marker
sections.body:
- 1 Nothing you can put your finger on, just the sense of being watched
- 2-4 A place that resonates strongly with the spirit's theme
- 5-6 A remarkable natural feature, particularly large/beautiful/ancient/etc.

sections.title: 1d12 activity
sections.body:
- 1 Missing, dissipated, greatly weakened
- 2 Imprisoned, probably seeking release
- 3-4 Slumbering, waiting for something
- 5-6 Sporadic, often slumbering or away 7-9 Alert, aware, unlikely to manifest
- 10-12 Alert, aware, manifesting freely

sections.title: 1d12 disposition
sections.body:
- 1-2 Dangerous, possibly corrupted by **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|the Things Below]]**
- 3-4 Vengeful, grumpy, protective, angry at intrusion
- 5-6 Plaintive, needful, wronged, demanding
- 7-8 Aloof, shy, dismissive, has their own concerns
- 9-10 Curious, playful, mischievous,

*The PCs are in the Steplands, heading towards Three Coven Lake, and they've just run afoul of some Hillfolk. They escaped, but only because the Hillfolk turned back from the cave they hid in, like they were scared to enter. I think this is a sacred site. I roll a 3 for theme (air/wind/weather/fire) and a 4 for the marker (place that resonates with the theme). I'm picturing one of those eternal flames, where a methane pocket seeps out and burns forever. I get 10 for activity (alert, aware, manifesting freely) and roll a 12 for disposition (content, pleasant, helpful, beneficent) which is odd given the Hillfolk's reaction.*

*So: a cave lit by a flame with no apparent source, issuing from a crack in the earth. The flame houses an old, powerful, talkative spirit that's been burning since the days of the Makers. It blames the Hillfolk for their ancestors' role in*

sections.title: Length of braided leather magical
sections.body:
3 feet long, frayed on one end, and crusty with flaking... something. The braids entwine 2d4 beads of redwood. Each bead contains some harm: a wound (1d10 damage, *messy*, ignores armor), a sickness, a poison, a delusion. The whole thing binds a spirit of blight.

When you *unweave the braid and let a redwood bead drop upon the earth*, the spirit manifests as a warm, damp wind. Name someone in your presence; the spirit inflicts upon them whatever harm was stored in the bead. If you do not name someone in your presence, the spirit will choose someone at random (maybe even you).

sections.title: A tightly-woven net
sections.body:
*close*, *thrown*, *magical*, *crude* Woven from Flats-grass by a Hillfolk sorcerer or witch. Hundreds of minor root-spirits are tethered to the strands.

When you *cast the net over a spirit*, it is ensnared—even if its form is intangible or could flow through the net. The spirit can't escape on its own as long as the net is intact. Don't try to use this on fire spirits they'll burn right through it.

sections.title: A fur-lined bedroll
sections.body:
, *magical*

This bedroll is decorated with Forest Folk beadwork and lined with the softest rabbit fur. It tethers a nurturing, protective spirit, one that is quiet and comforting.

When you *Make Camp in the fur-lined bedroll*, you regain 1d6 (extra) HP and gain advantage on your next roll.

sections.title: A stone cup
sections.body:
*magical*

A lump of limestone, big as a large man's fist, sheared off smooth at one end and hollowed out. Water slowly seeps from the interior wall, filling the cup in about half an hour. The water is hard, mineral-rich but potable, and just a little cursed.

When you *drink water produced by the cup*, you'll have troubling dreams the next time you sleep—dreams of suffocation and endless wet caverns and huge uncaring things stirring in the lightless depths. Roll 1d6: **on a 1**, you wake up screaming and will get no rest this evening; **on a 2-6**, the dreams are merely unsettling.

sections.title: A cracked femur
sections.body:
, *magical*

This leg bone is always cold and covered in rime. It's the bone of a witch who broke her leg and froze to death. It houses not a ghost, but a potent spirit of winter and cold.

When you *first grip the bone*, roll +CON: **on a 10+**, the spirit acknowledges you as master and you can wield the bone; **on a 7-9**, pick 1:

- Drop the bone; you can never wield it
- Endure as the spirit sucks away your heat—take 2d4 damage (ignores armor) and mark *weakened*—but you then master the spirit and can wield the bone.

**On a 6-**, the spirit sucks away your heat—take 2d4 damage (ignores armor) and mark *weakened*—and you can never wield the bone.

When you *wield the bone as a weapon* (*hand*, *crude*, *magical*, ignores armor) it deals 2d4 damage instead of your normal damage, sucking the warmth from your foe.

sections.title: A crown of flowers
sections.body:
*fragile*, *magical*

A wreath of living vines, sprouting flowers of great variety and beauty. A woman who dons the crown becomes greatly attuned to the fertility of everything around her, and the whispering spirits of growing things. She also becomes aware of the rites of spring.

When a *woman wears the crown and performs the rites of spring*—dancing around an oak tree, making offerings to the spirits, and joining with her lover in the light of the full moon—then her steading rolls with advantage to generate Surplus in both summer and autumn. Also, if the woman wearing the crown so chooses, she will conceive and bear a healthy child, regardless of any barriers.

sections.title: A forked stick of redwood
sections.body:
, *magical*

A stout stick about 2 feet long, the bark removed, with fine woodworm trails making entirely-too-regular patterns. If you hold it gently, with a hand on each tine of the fork, you can feel it tugging, pulling you gently in certain directions.

When you *dowse with the redwood stick*, it will guide you to the nearest site sacred to the spirits of the wild. With practice, you can estimate the distance and even the disposition of the spirits that dwell there.

sections.title: A hunk of amber
sections.body:
, *magical*, Value 2

It's about the size of two fists, with a mass of blood-sucking insects floating in its center. It tethers an ambitious spirit of insects, blight, and disease. Treat it as a threat (type: *magical entity*, **Instinct** to foster worship) with the following moves:

- <sup>ä</sup> Demand a blood sacrifice, poured over the amber
- <sup>ä</sup> Shield someone from sickness, vermin, and poison
- <sup>ä</sup> Blight an enemy with sickness, vermin, or both

sections.title: Minor arcana
sections.body: Pick 1 or have someone roll.

sections.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
sections.body:
- 1 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A path in the woods|A path in the woods]]**
- 2 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A beaded satchel|A cloak, richly embroidered]]**
- 3 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A beaded satchel|A beaded satchel]]**
- 4 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A wolf pelt|A cracked flute]]**
- 5 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A wolf pelt|A wolf pelt]]**
- 6 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A patch of rainbow moss|A patch of rainbow moss]]**

sections.title: Major arcana
sections.body:
These could be associated with the spirits of the wild.

- The **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Redwood Effigy|Redwood Effigy]]**
- The **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Twisted Spear , reach, magical|Twisted Spear]]**
- The **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Shield of the Wisent Witch|Shield of the Wisent Witch]]**

sections.title: The Brennauwed
sections.body: The Tall Ladies, Daughters-of-Seed, the Slow Sisters: these are all titles given to the Brennauwed, powerful tree spirits and, if you believe them, kin to **[[09 - Danu, the Earth Mother#Danu, the Earth Mother|Danu]]** herself. Each is tethered to a single tree. The younger ones can roam, but the older a Brennauwed is, the more bound she is to her tree.

sections.title: Glasbren
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *spirit*, *clever*, *magical*, *stealthy*, *beautiful*

**HP** 19; **Armor** 2 (bark, no vital organs) **Damage** raking fingers d8 (*hand*, disadvantage)

**Special qualities** tethered to its tree **Instinct** to stretch and grow and experience life

- ä Travel freely between young trees
- ä Spy on someone or something interesting
- ä Manifest a beautiful form of moving wood (unharmed by stabbing, vulnerable to fire)
- ä Call on the forest and its creatures for aid
- ä Spread word through the Great Wood

Youngest of the Brennauwed, a glasbren's tree is barely more than a sapling. The world is full of wonder and life, and they would see it all.

From time to time, a comely mortal will catch a glasbren's eye. If the mortal proves kind and curious and full of life, it might reveal itself and shyly pursue a friendship—or more.

When you *give your heart or loyalty to a glasbren*, your instinct becomes "Devotion: act in the glasbren's interests." The glasbren becomes a follower (Cost: wonder and excitement; holds 2 Loyalty to start).

When *a season passes without you spending time with the glasbren*, alone at her tree, she loses all Loyalty and you mark *miserable*. You cannot clear this debility without spending time with her.

When you *spend time with the glasbren*, alone with her at her tree, roll +CHA: **on a 7+**, clear all your debilities and ask the GM to reveal a secret about the Great Wood or the things that can be found there; but **on a 7-9**, also pick 1:

- Days or weeks pass without either of you realizing it
- You offend or upset her; she loses any Loyalty she held and she isn't speaking to you right now (tell us what you did wrong)
- Her "family" would have words with you, right now

sections.title: Birdwaig
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *spirit*, *cautious*, *clever*, *magical*

**HP** 23; **Armor** 3 (woody, no vital organs) **Damage** crushing branches d10+3 (*close*, *reach*, *forceful*), choking vines/roots d10+1 (*reach*, *grabby*)

**Special qualities** tethered to its tree **Instinct** to care for the forest, especially the trees

- ä Wield the plants around her like a weapon
- ä Possess a nearby tree (vulnerable to fire, all but impossible to harm with most weapons)
- ä Purge or bind an unnatural taint
- ä Grant a secret or a boon to a friend of the forest

As they mature, Brennauwed grow more sedentary, manifesting only via their own tree or possessing other trees within a few miles. A birdwaig has a motherly regard for all natural things in her territory, and will turn the very forest against any that seek to do it harm.

sections.title: Hennain
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *huge*, *spirit*, *cautious*, *clever*, *magical*, *ancient*, *terrifying*

**HP** 31; **Armor** 4 (woody, no vital organs) **Damage** walloping branches d12+5 (*close*, *reach*, *area*, *forceful*)

**Special qualities** tethered to its tree **Instinct** to take the long, long view

- ä Remain unmoved
- ä See through any tree in the forest
- ä Animate her own tree (almost impossible to harm with weapons, or even fire)
- ä Call upon the power of Danu herself
- ä Lay a lasting curse on a defiler

Old grandmothers of the Wood, the hennain are few and ancient and mighty. They watch, and they wait, and they endure. It takes a dire threat indeed to rouse one, and even then it acts in slow and inscrutable ways. For a hennain cares not one bit for any given creature or tree; she cares only for the slow turn of the forest itself.

sections.title: Andalau
sections.body:
*Horde*, *tiny*, *spirit*, *stealthy*, *mischievous*

**HP** 8; **Armor** 2 (tiny, agile)

**Damage** none

**Instinct** to play and frolic

- ä Manifest as a fluttering gust of wind (harmed only by lead or salt)
- ä Flit things about (dust, leaves, etc.)
- ä Annoy or spook someone
- ä Deliver a whispery message

There are places where the wind dances constantly, fluttering and flitting, and here the andalau dwell. They are more prone to mischief than violence. Win their favor, and you might convince them to help you—especially if it involves going somewhere new or playing pranks. Just don't be surprised when their attention flits elsewhere.

sections.title: Ceffylwraig
sections.body: *Solitary*, *large*, *spirit*, *swift*, *tireless*, *cautious*, *magical*, *beautiful*

sections.title: HP 24; Armor 0
sections.body:
**Damage** kicking hoofs d8+3 (*hand*, *close*, *forceful*, disadvantage)

**Instinct** to be fiercely independent

- ä Appear as a majestic horse among the herd
- ä Slip free of any restraint, disappearing into the wind if need be
- ä Guide the herd like it was a single, living entity
- ä Curse someone with wild, unbridled emotions

A herd of wild horses has a life, nay, a spirit all its own, a spirit as free and fickle as the wind. Take care, you who would poach wild horses for your own purpose, lest you find your heart turned against you!

**Something interesting:** "Ceffylwraig" means "horse wife," but she sees herself more as queen than wife: proud and haughty, disdainful of the weak and the slow, the herd her subjects more than her children.

**Something useful:** A ceffylwraig is scornful and suspicious of praise or fancy words, but can be appeased by thoughtful gifts, offered with appropriate respect from proud women of noble bearing.

- What sort of gift do the ceffylwraig most value?
- What sort of gift will do in a pinch?

sections.title: Coedwaig
sections.body:
*Horde*, *tiny*, *spirit*, *magical* **HP** 5; **Armor** 2 (bark, no vital organs) **Damage** nasty thorns d6 (*hand*, *grabby*) **Special qualities** thorns all over, to touch them is to bleed

**Instinct** to viciously defend its ward

- ä Possess thorny plants or vines and shape them into a body
- ä Cause vicious thorns and spines to sprout from nearby plants
- ä Dig in deep, make them bleed (*messy*)

Each thorn on a plant is a sword, brandished boldly against a world that would defile its flowers, steal its fruit, and devour its delicate buds. What, then, are the coedwaig? Only Danu's swords, brandished by the Mother herself in defense of her most beautiful and vulnerable children.

sections.title: Fadyn
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *spirit*, *magical*, *stealthy*, *devious*, *audacious*, *cautious*, *beautiful*

**HP** 16; **Armor** 1 (reflexes)

**Damage** bite, scratches, or knife d6 (*hand*, disadvantage)

**Special qualities** tethered to a fox skull **Instinct** to make mischief

- ä Manifest as a fox (*small*) or a charming stranger
- ä Be all manner of charming and ingratiating
- ä Steal something when no one's looking
- ä Make something seem like something else

Every child has heard stories of the fox-spirit, the trickster, the fadyn. In some tales, a fadyn makes fools of the cruel or the proud. In others, they steal chickens from the slow-witted. And in still others, they seduce a beautiful human, disappearing when bored or discovered. Truth be told, there's little pattern to who a fadyn torments or fancies. They are fickle, fickle things.

**Something interesting:** When a fadyn takes the form of a person, it always has some fox-like feature (a tail, feet, hands, ears) that it strives to hide. It is easily embarrassed by this feature, and flees if it's discovered.

**Something useful:** Fadyn are in fact the spirits of old, cagey foxes who passed away, and they're tethered to their old skulls. One can't travel far from its skull, and often carries it hidden on its manifest person or buries it near the community that it torments. If you want to destroy a fadyn, or bind it to your service, getting your hands on its skull is the best way to do so.

sections.title: Grwgnach
sections.body:
*Solitary, huge, spirit, magical, mesmerizing*

**HP** 27; **Armor** 2 (reflexes, swarm) **Damage** whirlwind of pecks, claws, buffets d8+3 (*hand*, *area*, disadvantage)

**Instinct** to absorb individual identity

- <sup>ä</sup> Manifest in a murmuration of starlings (nigh impossible to harm with personal weapons)
- <sup>ä</sup> Strip a swath of land clean of seed, saplings, insects
- <sup>ä</sup> Overwhelm with sound and the weight of numbers

When the sun sets, starlings flock in absurd numbers: thousands upon thousands dancing and wheeling in the sky, like a chittering thunderhead, like a cloud of living smoke.

Farmers do what they can to keep starlings far from town, and not just because they eat precious seeds and bury the land in droppings. They do so for fear of the grwgnach.

The grwgnach is a spirit of the mob, of the many coming together to become something more. It's not a king or a queen of the starlings. Rather, it's what a million starlings become when each bird loses itself to the flock.

When you *gaze rapt upon a murmuration of starlings*, tell us of a time when you felt small and insignificant, or when you lost yourself in a crowd.

Then, roll +WIS: **on a 10+,** you feel the pull of the grwgnach but shake it off; **on a 7-9**, your mind is briefly adrift among a thousand-thousand others—mark *dazed* but also ask the GM one question about the region, something that at least one starling might know; **on a 6-**, your mind is sucked into the grwgnach—mark *dazed* and you stand rapt until the flock disperses or someone removes you, physically. Also, until you Level Up, your instinct becomes "Birdlike: To act strangely, like a bird."

sections.title: The Halfeyd
sections.body:
*Spirit*, *magical*, *fickle*, *sadistic*, *terrifying* **Instinct** to terrorize its prey

- <sup>ä</sup> Manifest as eyes in the dark, watching
- <sup>ä</sup> Mark someone as prey
- <sup>ä</sup> Set all manner of hunting beasts on their trail
- <sup>ä</sup> Put ambush predators in their path

The hunters and trappers of Stonetop know. So do the Hillfolk. The Manmarchers know, and Fen-walkers, too. When you make a kill, you leave an offering. An offering for the Halfeyd. Fail to do so, and you might find yourself as prey—for everything.

**Something interesting:** If the Halfeyd marks you, you'll be hounded for 3 days and 3 nights—even by small predators who would never normally hunt you. **Something useful:** A spirit of running water (like that of a stream) can wash away the Halfeyd's mark.

sections.title: Nerth serpent
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *spirit*, *fearless*

**HP** 20; **Armor** 4 (resilience), or 0 against bare hands

**Damage** bite and constrict d10+3 (*hand*, *close*, *reach*, *forceful*, *grabby*)

**Special qualities** tethered to the Nerth Tree **Instinct** to test the might of supplicants

- <sup>ä</sup> Politely offer a chance to back down
- <sup>ä</sup> Restrain an opponent's limb
- <sup>ä</sup> Whisssper a foe's own doubts in their ear

The Nerth Tree grows in the Great Wood. It can only be found by one mortal each midsummer, by following a particular **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A path in the woods|path in the woods]]**. The roots are littered with bones of former supplicants.

Hanging from the tree: a great serpent. Crush it and eats its heart, and you will gain its vitality. But good luck with that.

sections.title: Prenysbyrd
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *spirit*, *magical*, *stubborn*, *terrifying*

**HP** 23; **Armor** 2 (woody, lacks organs) **Damage** club-like fists d10+3 (*close*, *forceful*) **Instinct** to punish trespassers

- <sup>ä</sup> Blame one for the sins of another
- <sup>ä</sup> Fill minds with leering images and unreasoning dread
- <sup>ä</sup> Form a body of fallen trees, leaves, rotting wood (vulnerable to fire)
- <sup>ä</sup> Smother a prone foe with soil, leaves, wood (d8+1 damage, *near*, *grabby*)

What becomes of a dead tree's spirit? Most linger as simple log-spirits, weakening as the log rots to mulch. But when generations of trees grow and die in the same place, they might become something more. Something that defends the grove, and its current trees, and all the trees that are yet to come.

sections.title: Troelloff
sections.body:
*Spirit*, *magical*, *playful*, *cruel*

- **Instinct** to confuse, frustrate, delay <sup>ä</sup> Show warnings left by others: spiral
- carvings, many-arrowed icons, etc. <sup>ä</sup> Inflict a brief, intense vertigo
- <sup>ä</sup> Make them lose their way
- <sup>ä</sup> Show a landmark they've passed before
- <sup>ä</sup> Obscure trails, way-signs, and markings

Some places never want you to leave: deep woods, twisting caverns, rugged hills that all look same. Such places give rise to troelloff, maze-spirits, who delight in mischief and feed on the desperation of the lost.

**Something interesting:** Where a troelloff dwells, there's always a true path. It can't harass anyone who stays on this path, but good luck finding it. **Something useful:** The path is never obvious, but it's always marked, by a particular flower, a pattern in the moss, a vein in the stone, etc.

sections.title: The Steplands
sections.body:
The Steplands are a rugged hill-country south of the **[[14 - The Flats#The Flats|Flats]]**. To the north, they touch **[[13 - Ferrier's Fen#Ferrier's Fen|Ferrier's Fen]]** and the **[[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|Great Wood]]**. To the east, they give way to the **[[40 - South Manmarch#South Manmarch|South Manmarch]]**. To the west, they climb higher and higher, like a stairway to the **[[27 - Huffel Peaks#Huffel Peaks|Huffel Peaks]]**.

The region is a karst—a plateau of limestone and quartzite and granite, carved away by ages of runoff and dissolved by groundwater, leaving a jumble of hills and valleys and rocky cliffs, all shot through with caves, sinkholes, and streams.

The land seems wild and untamed to outsiders, but in truth it is home to dozens of small nomadic bands, the **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk|Hillfolk]]**.

sections.title: Always
sections.body:
- Bare rock, host to lichen, moss, and stubborn grass/shrubs
- Cloud-shadows drifting over hillsides
- The feeling of being surrounded, closed in by the valleys
- The burning in your legs as you climb another damn hill
- The grandeur when you reach a summit: vista on vista marching, hills on hills, slope beyond slope

sections.title: Spring
sections.body:
- Snow still clinging to south-facing slopes, never touched by sun
- Sudden warmth as you step out of a shadow; chill as you step into one
- Gurgle of water downhill, through ancient culverts under the Highway

sections.title: Summer
sections.body:
- Emerald meadows, grass waving in wind, splashes of wildflower color
- Flutter of birds taking flight, surprised when you round a bend
- Blessed shade as you pass beneath some trees or into the shadow of a hill

sections.title: Autumn
sections.body:
- Fog in the valleys, the hills like islands in clouds
- Tickle on your face, from mist or an early snow
- Mournful songs of cranes or owls or other large birds, echoing from far away

sections.title: Winter
sections.body:
- White drifts half-covering the hills, rocks still poking through
- Bitter winds on your face as you crest a hill or trudge through a pass
- The breath of your companions, white clouds hanging in the air
- The sense of stillness and quiet, like the world sleeping

sections.title: A rugged land
Questions
sections.body:
- What do the Hillfolk call the Steplands?
- Which of you once explored the caves that dot these lands? What did you find there that haunts you still?
- What treasure or wonder is said to be hidden in the Steplands?
- What landmark, visible from the Highway, have you always wanted to explore?

sections.title: Places
sections.body:
- **[[06 - Blackwater Lake#Blackwater Lake|Blackwater Lake]]**
- **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#The Makers' Roads|The Makers' Roads]]**, specifically the Highway
- **[[47 - Three Coven Lake#Three Coven Lake|Three Coven Lake]]**

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- Harsh weather threatens PCs on the Highway; a nearby cave might provide shelter
- A Hillfolk band is plagued by a bloodthirsty **[[42 - The Steplands#**Gouzadn**|gouzadn]]**, but a concerned parent begs for their lost child's life to be spared
- A **[[42 - The Steplands#**Bat swarm**|brawzbedr]]** naps on the Highway, blocking a merchant's wagon
- Someone the PCs care about went to explore a cave and never came out

sections.title: Terrain
sections.body: Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

sections.title: 1d12 terrain
sections.body:
| 1 | Creek, gulley, stream, river | |---|-------------------------------------------------------------------| | 2 | Stream, disappearing into cave,
sinkhole, or fractured bedrock | | 3 | Sinkhole, crevasse, gorge | | 4 | Barren patch (sand, scree, rocks) | | | | - 5 Copse of pines, gnarled shrubs 6 Stretch of **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#**The Crossroads**|Broken Roads]]** - 7 Gentle slope, meadow - 8-9 Steep slope, treacherous, unstable 10 Sheer rock face, bluff, rocky outcrop - 11-12 **[[42 - The Steplands#Caves|Cave]]**

sections.title: Discoveries
sections.body: For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 discovery
sections.body:
- 1 A change of terrain (previous column) and roll again.
- 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.)
- 3 **[[50 - Useful or Valuable Flora#Useful or valuable flora|Useful or valuable flora]]**
- 4 A point of interest (next page) 5 A hillfolk encounter (next page)
- 6 Some other encounter (next page)

| 1d6 | point of interest |
|---|---|
| 1 | A place corrupted by the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below\|Things Below]]** |
| 2 | A place **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**\|haunted]]** by death or the undying |
| 3 | A site or artifact of the **[[05 - Barrow Builders#Barrow Builders\|Barrow Builders]]** |
| 4 | A **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk\|Hillfolk]]** encampment or sacred place, occupied or not |
| 5 | A place sacred to the **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**\|spirits of the wild]]** |
| 6 | A ruin or artifact of the **[[43 - Stone Lords#Stone Lords\|Stone Lords]]** |

| 1d6 | hillfolk encounter |
|---|---|
| 1 | Pair or trio of riders, not immediately hostile |
| 2 | Herd of goats or sheep, tended by teen(s) and hound(s) |
| 3 | Signs of their passing/encampment/borders |
| 4 | Foragers, not far from camp |
| 5 | A whole band, on the move |
| 6 | A meeting between two or more bands |

For more about Hillfolk bands, see [[26 - Hillfolk#**Nomads**\|Hillfolk]].

| 1d6 | other encounter |
|---|---|
| 1 | Deadly beast, not actively hostile but easily provoked: a **[[42 - The Steplands#**Bat swarm**\|brawzbedr]]**, **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Cave bear**\|cave bear]]**, **[[14 - The Flats#**Thunder drake**\|thunder drake]]**, etc. |
| 2-3 | Potential game, but big and dangerous if provoked: **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Dangers**\|boars]]**, **[[14 - The Flats#Aurochs\|aurochs]]**, etc. |
| 4-5 | Game, mostly harmless but prone to bolt: deer, fowl, rabbits, etc. |
| 6 | **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**\|Spirit of the wild]]**, curious or indifferent |

sections.title: Hazards
sections.body:
- Getting lost (maze of hills, valleys, streams; trees blocking views)
- Sheer rock face to climb, and a reason to climb it
- Steep slope, unstable footing (scree, mud, fallen needles, crumbling rock)
- Flash flooding in valleys/on slopes

sections.title: Mudslide
sections.body:
Start with a slope built up with soil. Peel away the vegetation through over-grazing, or fire, or drought, or too much rain entirely. Add a sudden heavy rain or some fools walking across it. Hey, look, a mudslide!

- <sup>ä</sup> Point out the lack of ground cover, the sodden soil, the weight of waiting earth
- <sup>ä</sup> It'll take time/effort/risk to avoid the unstable slope
- <sup>ä</sup> The hillside shifts and buckles, underfoot or overhead
- <sup>ä</sup> People, stones, and trees are swept away or buried, d6+2 damage (*area*, *forceful*, *grabby*, ignores armor)
- <sup>ä</sup> Trap them, crush them, deprive them of air
- <sup>ä</sup> Leave behind a slick, treacherous, muddy swath

sections.title: Rockslide
sections.body:
Some slopes are often covered with scree or strewn with boulders, fist-sized, head-sized, some as big as horses. Mostly they stay put. Mostly.

- ä A loose stone clatters downhill, skitterscatter, billowing dust, but over soon enough
- <sup>ä</sup> A smaller rock smacks them, d6 damage
- <sup>ä</sup> A bigger stone teeters, starts to slip
- <sup>ä</sup> A whole field of boulders gives way
- <sup>ä</sup> Clunk, clunk, smash, d8+2 damage (*area*, *forceful*)
- <sup>ä</sup> Bury them under tons of rock and dirt (Death's Door)

sections.title: Bat swarm
sections.body:
Stepland caves are often home to thousands upon thousands of bats. They're nothing to fear individually. But a swarm of them, when you stand in their way? Oh dear.

- <sup>ä</sup> Squeaks, chitters, and flapping are heard in the distance
- <sup>ä</sup> The sounds get closer, louder, and louder still
- <sup>ä</sup> Overwhelm them with sound and scratches and fluttering wings, d6 damage (*area*)

- A pair or trio of **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk rider|Hillfolk riders]]**), hunting or on patrol
- A half-dozen riders or more, trailing the PCs
- A **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk rider|hdour]]** and/or their attendant spirits, beasts, demons, or followers

sections.title: Monsters
sections.body:
