- Ornery beasts, all riled up: **[[14 - The Flats#Aurochs|aurochs]]**, **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Dangers**|boar]]**, **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Cave bear**|cave bears]]**, or **[[14 - The Flats#**Thunder drake**|thunder drakes]]**.
- Predators: **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Cougar**|cougar]]**, **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Feathered drake**|feathered drakes]]**, **[[14 - The Flats#**Hlomren**|pack drakes]]**, **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Raselbaedd**|wolves]]**
- **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|Spirits of the wild]]**
- **[[05 - Barrow Builders#**Deathless ones**|Deathless ones]]**, **[[05 - Barrow Builders#**People**|barrow wights]]**, or **[[05 - Barrow Builders#**Phantom rider**|phantom riders]]**, lurking in their barrows or set free of them
- The **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|Ghostly Legion]]**, on the hunt or marching mortals to their cave
- Other **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|ghosts]]** or **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|revenants]]**, victims of tragedy or violence
- One of the other dangerous beasts that roam the Steplands

sections.title: Brawzbedr
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *huge*, *stubborn*, *grumpy* **HP** 24; **Armor** 3 (thick hide, boney nodules) **Damage** tree-rending claws d10+7 w/ disadvantage (*reach*, *close*, *forceful*, *messy*, 1 piercing)

**Special qualities** poor vision and hearing; excellent sense of smell

sections.title: Instinct to overreact
sections.body:
- ä Not really notice something
- ä Tear up trees like a child tears up grass
- ä Swat at something that annoys it
- ä Flail about in a rage (+*area*), then get tired

Think of something like a badger or a sloth, but big as a house: 20 feet long, 9 feet at the shoulder, forearms nearly 12 feet long when you include those shovel-like claws. Mostly they just lumber about the Steplands, looking for their favorite trees to tear up and chew through, sometimes clearing whole slopes. Just about everything gives them a wide berth, when they can. Even apex predators like a kleztigr don't think they're worth the trouble.

You'd think that they'd be easy to spot and avoid, and mostly you'd be right. But sometimes you'll round a bend or clear a slope and there one'll be, just plodding along or munching on a log or taking a nap, and you'd best try to circle around, quiet-like.

sections.title: Gouzadn
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *magical*, *cautious*, *stealthy*, *shapeshifter*

**HP** 16; **Armor** 4 (resilience), 0 vs. certain sacred wood

**Damage** tooth and claw d10 (*hand*, *grabby*), hoof and antler d10+2 (*close*, *forceful*), rending strength d10+2 (*hand*, *forceful*, *messy*)

**Special qualities** confused and frustrated by human speech, host to dozens of beast-spirits

**Instinct** to see people as either threat or prey

- ä Assume the aspect(s) of one or more beasts
- ä Do something impossible for a human, easy for a beast
- ä Suffer through a terrible wound unfazed, healing with a few days' rest
- ä Ask beasts or spirits (even quiet ones) for information or favors

When a young Hillfolk shows signs of being touched by spirits, their band had best seek out a **[[26 - Hillfolk#Upland horse|spirit-talker]]** to mentor the child. Without such guidance, these youths might forsake their humanity, forget their language, and eventually bind spirits of the wild to their very flesh.

A gouzadn shuns people, living with only spirits for company. They're more likely to flee and hide than fight when people encroach on their territory. An aggressive or unwary party, though, might find their numbers picked off one-by-one in the dark or when separated. And when that happens, a gouzadn might get a taste of killing people, maybe a taste for their flesh. Such monsters haunt the tales and nightmares of the Hillfolk.

Gouzadn gain their spirits by bonding with a beast, then consuming its flesh in a special, intuitive ritual. They might be reasoned with by the Blessed or someone else who speaks the Spirit Tongue. They might even be re-taught language, and convinced to divest their flesh of its surplus spirits. But most will be loath to do so; at best, they'll insist on keeping these spirits with them in a sacred pouch or similar vessel.

**Something interesting:** Tales tell of using "sacred wood" to put down a gouzadn. Maybe a Hillfolk elder could tell you the specific type of wood to use?

**Something useful:** To kill this gouzadn, you'll need a weapon made of... (roll the Die of Fate)

sections.title: 1d6 sacred wood
sections.body:
- 1 **[[37 - Red Groves#Red Groves|Redwood]]**
- 2 Heartwood of lightning-struck ash
- 3 Elder wood, blackened in sacrificial fire
- 4 Oak, etched with words of grounding and renewal
- 5 Yew, carved with a sorcerer's bone
- 6 Willow, the tree felled by an innocent, the wood carved by one stooped with regrets

sections.title: Grochslon
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *patient*, *stealthy*

**HP** 12; **Armor** 0

**Damage** outer jaws d10 (*close*, *grabby*), inner jaws d10+2 (*hand*, *messy*, 1 piercing)

**Special qualities** blind; senses prey by scent, hearing, vibrations

**Instinct** to feed on the unwary, then flee

- ä Lurk unseen in a dark, sheltered spot
- ä Spring forward without warning, chomping down
- ä Hold tight with outer jaws while tearing away with the inner
- ä Squirm through a narrow opening

Pale, eyeless things that spawn in the lightless lakes below the Steplands. Like a great big pale salamander or snake with two stubby, muscular legs. As they grow, they find food down there sparse, and make their way to the surface. Mostly they stick to cave mouths, but sometimes—sometimes—they get bold enough to leave their sheltered holes of rock, and they find other places to lurk, places where you'd never expect them.

sections.title: Kleztigr
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *cagey*, *stealthy*, *agile*, *powerful*

**HP** 16; **Armor** 1 (reflexes)

**Damage** huge saber-like fangs d10+3 w/ advantage (*hand*, *grabby*, *messy*, 1 piercing), claws or pounce d10+1 w/ advantage (*close*)

**Instinct** to bring down a good, solid meal

- ä Follow prey for hours, even days
- ä Pounce on prey from hiding (*reach*, *forceful*, *grabby*)
- ä Inflict a deadly, bleeding wound

Like a **[[23 - The Great Wood#**Cougar**|cougar]]** but twice as big, with 8-inch fangs hanging out of their mouths. Hillfolk tales claim they once were much more common, pets of the Stone Lords even. Now they are (thankfully) rare.

**Something interesting:** Some Hillfolk bands "adopt" a kleztigr, making offerings of meat or young goats, sheep, or even horses. The kletztigr follows the band about, protecting them from outsiders.

**Something useful:** Such kleztigr won't harm anyone wearing some item or clothing distinctive to "their" band (but they might expect a present).

sections.title: Spitting drake
sections.body:
*Group*, *stealthy*, *organized*

**HP** 6; **Armor** 2 (pebbly scales)

**Damage** vice-like jaws d8 (*hand*, *grabby*, *messy*, 1 piercing)

**Special qualities** chameleon skin, excellent climbers

**Instinct** to pin down a good meal, then swarm it

- ä Lay an ambush, where they can surround prey
- ä Spit a head-sized gob of sticky mucus (*near*, *grabby*, *reload*)
- ä Sprint much more quickly than you'd expect

They're about the size of a big wolf, but lower and squatter, with arms out to the sides like lizards and a big old frill that pops up when they're startled or bearing down on prey. They favor canyons, defiles, or overlooks where they can bask in the sun but have a good angle to ambush passersby.

**Something interesting:** Their mucus adheres quickly to rock, pinning prey in place. Even if it doesn't pin prey down, it gloms on and slows them up. It'll dry out and start to flake away after a few hours.

**Something useful:** Strong spirits (yes, like whisky) quickly dissolve the mucus. Also, you can identify their territory by the pale chalky stains their mucus leaves on stone.

sections.title: Caves
sections.body: The Steplands are lousy with caves, carved out over centuries by the slow patient work of water, or more dramatically by the magic of the Stone Lords.

sections.title: Impressions
sections.body:
- Instinctive hunch of your shoulders under a low stone ceiling
- Prickly, humid air, thick and still and cloying
- Slow, quiet drip… drip… drip… in the distance
- Darkness so thick and deep, it could swallow you
- Every sense heightened: rough stone underfoot, the breath of your companions, the damp mineral smell, the way your eyes strain in flickering light

sections.title: Structure
sections.body: Choose or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 structure
sections.body:
- 1 Small, contained, only a few chambers
- 2 Long, narrow, meandering
- 3 Twisting, interlaced tunnels; multiple chambers
- 4 A few distinct sets of chambers, connected by long twisting tunnels
- 5 Few small passages to a larger, massive chamber
- 6 Large and open main tunnel, smaller side passages and chambers

For any given opening, passage, or chamber, choose or have someone roll for clearance, footing, water, and decoration. For each, consider rolling 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

sections.title: 1d6 clearance
sections.body:
- 1 Basically a crack, too small for grown humans without excavation
- 2 A squeeze—most folk can wriggle through without gear on
- 3-4 Small, cramped—might need to duck, crawl, go single file
- 5 Roomy, comfortable to walk through
- 6 Yawning, vast, lots of space

sections.title: 1d6 footing
sections.body:
- 1 Steep slope, vertical ascent/descent
- 2 Slippery—water, mud, scree, guano, etc.
- 3 Rocky, debris-filled, unstable
- 4 Uneven, ridged, potholes, awkward
  - 5 Rolling, undulating, twisting
  - 6 Surprisingly smooth, level

sections.title: 1d6 water
sections.body:
- 1 Submerged
- 2 Partially flooded
- 3 A stream or rivulet, flowing steadily
- 4-6 Just a trickle, drips, or seepage; maybe not even that

sections.title: 1d6 decoration
sections.body:
- 1 Stalactite(s) or soda straws, clinging tight to the ceiling
- 2 Stalagmite(s), growing from the floor
- 3 Column(s), where stalactites and stalagmites have grown together
- 4 Crystals, crystalline growths, "honeycomb" patterns
- 5 Flowstone, rimstone, "draperies," "shields"
- 6 Fossils

sections.title: Inhabitants, past or present
sections.body: Choose or have someone roll, up to 3 times. What story does this tell?

sections.title: 1d6 inhabitant
sections.body:
- 1 Thralls or victims of the Things Below [[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|The Things Below]]
- 2 Barrow Builders [[05 - Barrow Builders|Barrow Builders]] or their victims
- 3 Truly ancient primordial powers [[36 - Primordial Powers#Primordial powers|Primordial powers]]
- 4 natural beast or spirit (see below)
- 5 Stone Lords [[43 - Stone Lords|Stone Lords]] or their servants
- 6 Hillfolk [[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk|Hillfolk]] or those they have cast out

sections.title: 1d6 natural beast or spirit
sections.body:
- 1 Bats. So many bats [[42 - The Steplands#1d6 natural beast or spirit|The Steplands]]
- 2 Fish, salamanders, olms, crawfishpale blind things dwelling in dark waters
- 3 Cave bears [[23 - The Great Wood#**Cave bear**|The Great Wood]], a kleztigr [[42 - The Steplands#1d6 natural beast or spirit|The Steplands]], or similar large beast, making its den here
- 4 Grochslon [[42 - The Steplands#1d6 natural beast or spirit|The Steplands]], or some other subterranean predator
- 5 Spirit, bound to or happily dwelling in this sacred site [[41 - Spirits of the Wild#Sacred sites|Sacred sites]]
- 6 A malicious spirit, like a troelloff [[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**The Halfeyd**|Spirits of the Wild]]

sections.title: 1d6 discovery
sections.body:
- 1 Connection to another cave system
- 2 A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.)
- 3-5 Signs of a (prior) inhabitant (previous column), like a site, artifact(s), or remains
- 6 A relatively good place to rest/hide

sections.title: Dangers
sections.body:
- Cold, especially if they've gotten wet
- Heat and humidity, especially if they carry heavy loads
- Getting lost
- Cave-ins, rock-falls
- Getting stuck in a squeeze
- Running out of/losing your light
- Fear, panic, or confusion in the face of any of the above

sections.title: Stone Lords
sections.body:
In the time of the **[[30 - The Makers#The Makers|Makers]]**, the Stone Lords ruled the **[[42 - The Steplands#The Steplands|Steplands]]**, the southern stretch of the **[[27 - Huffel Peaks#Huffel Peaks|Huffel Peaks]]**, and the **[[40 - South Manmarch#South Manmarch|South Manmarch]]**. They carved cities out of the hills. They raised towering monuments. They sang stone, crystal, and glass into impossible shapes.

The Stone Lords' empire was grand, but it was not all glory. It was built on the backs of countless human servants. And while the Stone Lords were not especially cruel, neither were they kind. They imposed order on their world, by whatever means necessary.

For, better than the other Makers, the Stone Lords knew the**[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**. Knew the insidious songs that echoed in the deep. Knew the chaos promised. Knew that they must never, ever be freed.

And so, when the **[[24 - Green Lords#Names|Green Lords]]** called on the Things Below to put down their own servants' rebellion, the Stone Lords turned on their brethren. They shattered the Green Lords' lands and hunted down the survivors.

Yet: the seals had cracked. The Things Below sang all the louder. The Stone Lords themselves stood resolute, but their servants did not. Corruption seeped through their society like water finding cracks in a poorly laid foundation.

The end came swiftly. Sorcerers turned the city of Dulenn into a massive sinkhole, creating what is now **[[06 - Blackwater Lake#Blackwater Lake|Blackwater Lake]]**. Seals were broken across the land and horrors slithered up through the tunnels. Mobs turned on their former masters. One by one, the domains and cities of the Stone Lords fell into ruin.

**Names**

Breton- and Cornish-inspired, but with extra vowels for a drawn-out sound: Aenedellion, Caedour, Daenizel, Eelowain, Gaeille, Iaves, Kaeteal, Loiawen, Mearauwd, Neassa, Oaneaz, Paerrain, Rozainne, Saennaen, Taenguey, Wainoac, Yainniac

Combine with prefixes like "Aalz" ("the great"), "Skol" ("master"), or "Loaji" ("apprentice"), etc.

The**[[55 - Appendix A - Ages of the World#Time of Cataclysm|Time of Cataclysm]]** had arrived.

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- Someone claims to have found a Stone Lord ruin abundant with precious gems
- A petrified prisoner of the Stone Lords was recently reanimated, and must now make sense of this new age
- While seeking shelter from the elements, the PCs stumble on a Stone Lord ruin
- Hillfolk need help dealing with dangerous stone constructs that are running amok

sections.title: Themes
sections.body: Pick, or roll 1d12.

sections.title: 1d12 theme
sections.body:
- 1 Rock and stone: excavated and shaped into grandiose structures
- 2 Crystals/gems/**[[43 - Stone Lords#Various treasures|makerglass]]**): decorative, practical, or thrumming with power
- 3 Songs/resonance/vibrations/echoes: to empower, shape, transform, or destroy
- 4 Seals/bindings/things trapped in stone: creatures/spirits/ideas/

sections.title: Sites
sections.body: Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 site
sections.body:
- 1-3 **Lingering signs** of their presence and influence (see below)
- 4-6 A **[[43 - Stone Lords#Ruin|ruin]]**

sections.title: Lingering signs
sections.body: Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 sign
sections.body:
- 1 **Something trapped** in stone, crystal, or makerglass (next column)
- 2 **Strange geologies** (next page)
- 3-4 Fragments of old construction (stairs, walls, foundations, chimneys), all that's left of once-grand structures
- 5 A stretch of the **[[31 - The Makers' Roads#The Makers' Roads|Makers' Roads]]**, intact or broken but unearthed/barely visible
- 6 A **monument, marker, or enigma** (next page)

sections.title: Something trapped
sections.body:
For example:

- Someone trapped in a stalagmite, their grasping hands, anguished face, and mournful moans just barely discernible
- A hollow, seamless cube of rune-carved white stone, 6 feet per side, bitterly cold; a dozen **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|specters]]** are sealed within, impotent and mad
- A stretch of shining black flagstones, showing reflections of people long past; those who open their minds see a parade of memories, clear as day
- A **[[43 - Stone Lords#**Construct**|construct]]** with a crack in its "head," possessed by an evil spirit; bound in a circle of deeply-cut runes
- A labyrinth of stone walls, the walkways forming a rune of binding, some sort of demon trapped in the center

sections.title: Strange geologies
sections.body:
For example:

- A large grotto, unnaturally smooth and precise, like part of a sphere just vanished from the stone
- A gorge cutting perpendicularly through a valley, the walls lined with glass
- A tunnel or valley, shaped so that the smallest sounds echo echo echo
- A cliff face, the striations perfectly smooth and parallel, the different strata strikingly different colors or types of stone
- A slope entirely made of smooth, rounded stone; like an old lava flow, but chert, granite, or limestone
- An enormous rock formation, shot through with dozens of small holes that catch the wind and sing a haunting melody.

sections.title: Monument, marker, or enigma For example:
sections.body:
- A crater of bare stone, 100 paces across, with a lozenge of black stone floating in the center, thrumming
- A statue carved from a mountainside, fragments of its stone sword littering the valley below
- A pedestal bearing two vast and trunkless legs of stone; hints of an inscription, now unreadable
- Giant stone heads arrayed upon a slope, their faces faded yet still disapproving
- A pylon of glass, 100 feet tall at least (and who knows how deeply set into the earth), faintly humming, faintly glowing
- Great lines gouged into the earth, running for miles; meaningless from the surface, but making enormous runes when seen from on high
  - A large room in some cave or ruin, the charred bones of a giant at the center; bits of blackened bone/tooth/carapace litter the edge; hundreds of fist-sized shards of crystal are embedded in the walls
- A chamber with dozens of glass cylinders, fixed vertically against a wall, each of differing length and width—some sort of instrument, perhaps?

sections.title: Ruin
path: Monument, marker, or enigma For example:
sections.body: Pick or roll for themes [[43 - Stone Lords#**Ruin**|Stone Lords]], structure, purpose, architectural elements, and condition. Place the ruin in an appropriate terrain (see [[27 - Huffel Peaks|Huffel Peaks]]  for the **[[42 - The Steplands#The Steplands|Huffel Peaks]]**, for the **Steplands**, or [[40 - South Manmarch|South Manmarch]] for the **South Manmarch**). Populate it with **[[43 - Stone Lords#**Ruin**|lingering signs]]**, **[[43 - Stone Lords#Makerglass|artifacts]]** and/or **dangers** of the Stone Lords [[43 - Stone Lords#**Dangers**|Stone Lords]] or the appropriate region.

sections.title: 1d6 structure
sections.body:
- 1 Tightly packed, human-sized structures/rooms
- 2 Single, bunker-like building
- 3 Large, open space
- 4 Tower, rising/jutting out from a hill/mountain
- 5 Chambers/tunnels dug into the sides of cliffs/hills/mountains
- 6 Grandiose building of impossible construction

sections.title: 1d6 purpose
sections.body:
- 1 Dwelling (home, barracks, dormitory, prison, etc.)
- 2 Infrastructure (aqueduct, rampart, road, bridge, etc.)
- 3 Storage (vault, warehouse, library, museum, etc.)
- 4 Production (workshop, factory, mine, farm, etc.)
- 5 Esoteric (laboratory, experiment, power source, etc.)
- 6 Civic life (agora, theater, shrine, monument, tomb, etc.)

sections.title: 1d6 architectural elements
sections.body:
- 1 Great slabs of marble/granite/limestone/etc., far too big for humans to lift
- 2 Seamless stone structures/tunnels, like they were poured or molded that way
- 3 Runes carved into stone and shaped out of walls, roads, tunnels, etc.
- 4 Crystals/makerglass: catching light/ wind, humming/glowing, chiming/ gonging
- 5 Stones/crystals that emanate emotion/mood/thoughts/memories
- 6 Grandiosity of scale, big enough to humble even the Makers

Most Maker-ruins are sized for giants people two or even three times as tall as humans—but often with smaller-scale rooms, passages, and so forth for their servants.

sections.title: 1d6 condition
sections.body:
- 1-2 Shattered and laid waste by time, war, or vandalism; mostly buried, overgrown, collapsed; its purpose unrecognizable
- 3-4 Crumbling, largely buried, overgrown, or hidden away
- 5 Buried, overgrown, or hidden, but mostly intact
- 6 Fully visible and strangely well-preserved

Many Stone Lord ruins were **[[05 - Barrow Builders#Reclaimed Maker-ruin|reclaimed]]** by the Barrow Builders at some point. See **[[06 - Blackwater Lake#**1d12** ruin or barrow|Blackwater Lake]]** and **[[47 - Three Coven Lake#**1d12** stone lord site|Three Coven Lake]]**.

The PCs cut north from Three Coven Lake and seek out a place to camp. I decide that they find a Stone Lord ruin. I roll 6 for theme (authority/order/hierarchy/tradition), a 5 for structure (chambers dug into the sides of hills), a 6 for purpose (civic life), a 3 for architectural elements (crystals/makerglass), and 4 for condition (buried or hidden, mostly intact). I imagine some sort of belltower, once used to signal the hour and give order to the day.

What remains is the hint of stone stairs leading up a rocky outcrop. Near the top, a partially collapsed tunnel opens into a smooth-walled, domed chamber. A makerglass chime hangs from the ceiling, just a few inches in diameter but a hundred feet tall or more, dropping down a shaft barely wider than it is. If tapped, the chime rings painfully loudly. If struck firmly, it resonates with a thunderous, deafening noise. I think there's also a great big bronze maul still in the chamber, and that a kleztigr [[42 - The Steplands#1d6 natural beast or spirit|The Steplands]] has been using this place as a den.

sections.title: Makerglass
sections.body:
Cut or blown into wondrous shapes, in many colors and textures, unbreakable and unworkable by mortal means. Often found near Stone Lord ruins, and highly prized.

Pick or roll for form and characteristic.

sections.title: 1d12 form
sections.body:
- 1-2 Enigmatic shape (chip/shard/ chunk/block/sphere/etc.), its purpose unclear
- 3-4 Mundane object (tool/utensil/container/lantern/etc.), though perhaps not easily recognized as such
- 5-6 Object of war (weapon/armor/ shield/helm/etc.)
- 7-8 Art or decoration (jewel/statue/ tiles/torc/amulet/ring/etc.)
- 9-10 Musical instrument (bell/chime/ flute/horn/etc.)
- 11-12 Construction (door/stairway/arch/ bricks/walls/etc.), likely *immobile*

Consider rolling 1d6 to inform size/scale.

sections.title: 1d12 characteristic
sections.body:
- 1-2 Breathtakingly beautiful
- 3-4 Glows/shines (roll 1d6 for scale)
- 5 Sings/hums/thrums (roll 1d6 for scale)
- 6 Absorbs something (heat/sound/ light/energy/vibration/etc.)
- 7 Roll 1d6 twice
- 8-12 Just (just) an indestructible object made of glass

sections.title: Various treasures
sections.body:
- A severed, three-fingered hand of a stone construct (Value 0), cleverly articulated but inert
- A single makerglass arrowhead (*indestructible*, 2 piercing, Value 1)
- A makerglass jar with a threaded lid, seals airtight (*indestructible*, Value 2)
- A stack of engraved ceramic tiles that fit together like a jigsaw puzzle; properly assembled, the engravings form a potent ring of runes for binding demons (Value 1, or Value 3 to the right person)
- A ring of petrified wood, set with a shining diamond (?) (Value 2, *beautiful*)
- A huge bronze gong (*immobile*, Value 2), carved along the edge with runes of amplification
- A makerglass sword (*close*, *messy*, *indestructible*, *beautiful*, +1 damage, 2 piercing, Value 3)
- A fist-sized ruby (*beautiful*, Value 4); if placed in a fire or otherwise heated, it hums beautiful, haunting notes

sections.title: A chalky potion
sections.body:
*magical*, *fragile*, *slow*

1d4 glass vials containing thick, gritty, whitish fluid. The stopper bears a mark of transformation, flesh, and stone.

When you *drink the whole potion while touching stone*, you transform (over a matter of minutes) into a mass of stone, similar to that which you touch. You retain awareness of your surroundings, blind but with excellent hearing.

When you *attempt to end your lithic vigil*, roll +CON: **on a 10+**, you return to your original form, rested and invigorated—regain all your HP and clear all your debilities; **on a 7-9**, the transformation back is slow and arduous, mark a debility; **on a 6-**, you are stuck until someone pours another dose of the potion over you or communes with your spirit and helps you try again.

sections.title: Power-crystal
sections.body:
, *magical*, *dangerous*

 A chunk of crystal the size of a clenched fist. If charged, it thrums or crackles with power. If depleted, it fills you with a sense of emptiness and longing.

When you *brandish the depleted powercrystal at a source of elemental energy*, roll +CON: **on a 10+**, the crystal absorbs the energy fully and as safely as possible (it is now charged); **on a 7-9**, the crystal absorbs the energy (it's now charged) but pick 1:

- You are harmed by the coursing energy, taking damage as the GM sees fit
- The power stored in the crystal is extremely unstable, likely to discharge without warning
- The crystal cracks; henceforth, treat a 10+ on this move as a 7-9, and you can't pick this option again.

In the hands of a skilled arcanist, a charged power-crystal can fuel or empower a magical effect (though this is dangerous). In the hands of the incautious, the reckless, or the desperate, it can split or crack, leading to a violent explosion.

sections.title: A scarred slab of marble
sections.body:
*immobile*, *magical*

A slab of once-beautiful stone, some 30 feet tall and about half again as wide, standing in a vaulted chamber, surrounded by deeply etched runes that speak of union and protection, consumption and sacrifice. The stone bears a multitude of chips, gouges, and cracks. Dust and debris surround it.

When you *pry a chip of stone from the marble and swallow it whole, while standing within the ring of runes*, your flesh becomes like stone and your DEX becomes -2 until the effect ends. The next three times you would suffer physical harm, the marble slab suffers the harm instead. The effect ends after the third blow.

sections.title: Minor arcana
sections.body: Pick 1 or have someone roll.

sections.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
sections.body:
- 1 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A giant's dormitory magical|A giant's dormitory]]**
- 2 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#Rune-etched pillars|Rune-etched pillars]]**
- 3 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A timeless vault|A timeless vault]]**
- 4 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#Peacebond|A sealed cave]]**
- 5 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A makerglass chime|A makerglass chime]]**
- 6 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A fine ceramic urn|A fine ceramic urn]]**

sections.title: Major arcana
sections.body:
These might very well be associated with the Stone Lords.

- The **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Mysteries of the Mindgem|Mindgem]]**
- **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Whispering Rocks|Whispering Rocks]]**

sections.title: Bound Entities
sections.body:
- **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Spirits of the wild**|Spirits of the wild]]**, especially elementals associated with earth, stone, and terrain, yoked by runecraft to serve the Stone Lord's needs
- Spirits and beasts corrupted by **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|the Things Below]]**: petrified, encased in stone, or sealed away behind layers of runes
- Would-be **[[05 - Barrow Builders#Barrow Builders|sorcerer-kings]]**, petrified or otherwise imprisoned before they could cause too much trouble
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Ghosts]]**, mindlessly performing duties they were bound to perform in life, unaware that they could want anything else
- **[[36 - Primordial Powers#Primordial powers|Primordial entities]]**, imprisoned by elaborate workings of crystal and rune, bound to serve or else locked away for safekeeping

**Unstable construction** The Stone Lords' work has stood for centuries, and they were the cleverest architects to ever walk this world. But the problem with grand, clever architecture is that when it fails, it fails spectacularly.

- <sup>ä</sup> A wall/floor/building is precariously perched, or a pillar/support/buttress supports an impossible load
- <sup>ä</sup> Something creaks/teeters/sways/groans
- <sup>ä</sup> Cracks form/debris tumbles/something starts to tip
- <sup>ä</sup> Age-old construction starts to fall like dominoes (run!)
- <sup>ä</sup> All is reduced to rubble, and dust, and death

sections.title: Perilous stone
sections.body:
Pick or roll for form , original

purpose , and peril , possibly rolling the Die of Fate for scope/scale. Consider how it fit into the daily lives of the Stone Lords or their servants-what they intended it for, how they avoided it, etc.

sections.title: 1d12 form
sections.body:
- 1-2 Mechanism/construction
- 3-5 Rune(s): engraved in stone, worked into tile, formed out of architecture
- 6-8 Crystal(s)/gem(s)/glass
- 9-11 Strange stone
- 12 Makerglass

sections.title: 1d12 original purpose
sections.body:
- 1-2 Convenience/storage/infrastructure
- 3-4 Entertainment/art/pleasure
- 5-6 Violence/war/defense
- 7-8 Production/power/industry
- 9-10 Esoteric/research/worship
- 11-12 Control/order/authority

sections.title: 1d12 peril
sections.body:
- 1 Discharges energy
- 2 Crushes/smashes/grinds
- 3 Captures/imprisons/isolates/ separates
- 4 Forbids/punishes certain actions
- 5 Drains energy/emotion/memory
- 6 Overwhelms senses/emotions
- 7 Lures/entices/deceives
- 8 Draws attention
- 9-10 Roll 1d8 twice
- 11-12 Roll 1d10, but make it grandioseit doesn't just capture, it petrifies; it doesn't just grind, it reduces something to dust, etc.

sections.title: Some examples:
sections.body:
- Peacebond [[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#Peacebond|Peacebond]] runes, worked into roads, halls, public places
- A glossy cube of blue-black stone that steals memories from those who touch it, playing them back for only the strongest of wills
- A stone vault that might slip closed if folks are careless, no way to open it from the inside
- A human-sized crystal, cracked, absorbing telluric energies, occasionally discharging them as bolts of lightning
- A pile of shimmery sand; when someone touches it, it absorbs an imprint of their mind and forms itself into a simulacrum of them (convinced that it is the original)

sections.title: Construct
sections.body:
The Stone Lords were masters not only of shaping stone, but also of animating it and giving it the semblance of life. Many such creations remain, though only a few still function and many of those function poorly.

Pick or roll a purpose, ability, size, power, sapience, and condition. Allow these elements to inform its physical form and construction.

sections.title: 1d12 purpose
sections.body:
- 1-2 War/destruction/violence/coercion
- 3-4 Defense/protection/companionship
- 5-6 Experimentation/curiosity/practice
- 7-8 Construction/labor/convenience
- 9-10 Something obscure, or very specific
- 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice, combine

sections.title: 1d12 ability
sections.body:
- 1 Nothing of note, just animated stone
- 2 Mental influence/control
- 3 Energy/elemental manipulation 4 Cunning/alertness/special senses
- 5 Disguise/camouflage/stealth
- 6 Restraints/snares/subdual 7 Ranged/area attack
- 8 Transformation/multipart form/ swarm
- 9 Prowess/mobility/speed
- 10 Destructive/vicious weaponry
- 11-12 Roll 1d10 twice

Stone Lord constructs are typically blind but able to navigate by sound and sensing vibrations. Almost all are highly durable (+4 HP) and animated by more than biology (another +4 HP), with at least 3 Armor.

sections.title: 1d12 size
sections.body:
- 1 Cat-sized or smaller (*tiny*)
- 2-3 Like a human child (*small*)
- 4-7 Human-sized
- 8-10 Like a horse, a cart, etc. (*large*)
- 11-12 Like an elephant, or bigger (*huge*)

sections.title: 1d12 power
sections.body:
- 1-3 Requires the presence of external source
- 4-8 Stores power, but must return "home" to recharge
- 9-11 Self-charging, but limited range/ activity
- 12 Self-powered/indefinite charge

Power sources might include: singing crystals that broadcast energy; ingots of **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**Aetherium**|aetherium]]**; veins of special stone transmitting power through floors; crystals that absorb telluric currents/vibrations; spirits bound in makerglass; etc.

sections.title: 1d12 sapience
sections.body:
- 1-3 Puppet: requires active control by outside intellect
- 4-6 Bug-like: can follow limited range of programs/tasks
- 7-10 Beast-like: can take basic direction, complete some complex tasks independently
- 11 Automaton: can handle complex tasks, learn; lacks true emotion/ creativity
- 12 Person: fully sapient, potentially quite intelligent

Ask yourself: where does this construct's sapience come from? Clever scripting? Spirits bound to stone? The will drained from living servants? Something else?

sections.title: 1d12 condition
sections.body:
- 1-2 No power/power source failing
- 3-4 Broken/impaired limb(s) or chassis
- 5-6 Trapped/pinned/buried
- 7-8 Behaving erratically
- 9-10 Roll 1d8 twice
- 11-12 Fully functional

sections.title: Lithic servant
sections.body:
*Horde*, *construct*, *implacable*

**HP** 11; **Armor** 3 (made of stone)

**Damage** stone fists d6 (*hand*, *grabby*) **Special qualities** externally powered;

bug-like intellect; blind; tremorsense

**Instinct** to follow its last orders

- <sup>ä</sup> Act with eternal patience and endurance
- <sup>ä</sup> Act with no sense of consequence
- <sup>ä</sup> Hurt someone unintentionally while performing its tasks

Imagine one of those artists' mannequins, but full-sized and made of polished rock. Two fingers and a thumb on each hand. Faceless, expressionless, making a stoneon-stone grinding noise as they move.

They're most often found inert and covered in dust, waiting for some energy source like a Stone Lord's song or a thrumming power-crystal—to give them "life." They'll then follow any orders given to them in the Stone Lord's tongue.

A few of these servants are still active, powered by inexhaustible sources in ancient halls. Their joints are worn smooth, their feet and hands ground down to nubs. Some crawl on broken limbs, attempting to complete tasks that will never end.

sections.title: Stone sentinel
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *construct*, *implacable* **HP** 24; **Armor** 3 (made of stone) **Damage** crushing fists d10+3 (*hand*, *close*,

*grabby*, *messy*, *forceful*, 3 piercing) **Special qualities** can animate for only a few minutes at a time; dog-like mind; blind; tremorsense

sections.title: Instinct to protect its ward
sections.body:
- <sup>ä</sup> Emit an ominous hum, a warning as it powers up
- <sup>ä</sup> Move with shocking, unexpected speed (but lose 1d4 HP)

Articulated statues, squat and wide, with huge stone hands, set to watch forever over some ruin, passage, or treasure. Their stone "minds" are full of vigilance, and violence.

Each sentinel bears a power-crystal in its chest, slowly absorbing vibrations through earth and stone. A few days of charging is enough to animate the construct for only a few minutes—which is usually more than enough time for it to repel (or crush) an invader.

sections.title: Mosaic swarm
sections.body:
*Solitary*, *large*, *construct*, *amorphous*, *stealthy*

**HP** 24; **Armor** 3 (made of thousands of ceramic tiles)

**Damage** jagged fists of ceramic d10+3 (*hand*, *close*, *forceful*)

**Special qualities** externally powered; buglike mind; blind; tremorsense; unharmed by piercing weapons; half damage from cutting weapons; vulnerable to attacks that are blunt and *forceful* or *area*

**Instinct** to destroy intruders

- <sup>ä</sup> Wait for intruders to get close, rattling softly
- <sup>ä</sup> Explode into a cloud of whirling ceramic tiles, d8+1 damage (*area*, *messy*, 1 piercing)
- <sup>ä</sup> Reform into a vaguely humanoid mass

Stone Lords adorned their halls with elaborate tilework and mosaics. Most of them were decorative. Most of them.

**Something interesting**: Either some spirit is possessing these tiles, or this is some sort of construct. If it's a spirit, there must be a tether. If it's a Stone Lord construct, well, the individual tiles are too small to power or direct themselves.

**Something useful**: The mosaic's frame remained on the wall, and it's glowing blue—aetherium runes! Destroy the runes, and the construct should fall apart.

sections.title: Pacifier
sections.body:
*Group*, *small*, *construct*, *amorphous* **HP** 10; **Armor** 4 (just a stone sphere)

**Damage** stupefying pulse of sound d6 (*reach*, *area*, *reload*, ignores armor)

**Special qualities** must recharge after a few hours; limited autonomy; blind; tremorsense; has trouble with stairs; vulnerable to blunt, forceful attacks

**Instinct** to keep mortals docile

- <sup>ä</sup> Sense and follow strong emotions
- <sup>ä</sup> Hum a soothing, pacifying sound (*near*, *area*)
- <sup>ä</sup> Sound the alarm, call for reinforcements

They're basically hollow stone spheres, maybe scratched and dinged but otherwise featureless. Often found in their little alcoves, charging. The Stone Lords would deploy them among the rowdier servants to keep things running smoothly.

When you *are in range of a pacifier's hum*, your emotions lose their edge. When you *rouse yourself to act violently*, urgently, or passionately, roll +WIS. If you *are in range of multiple pacifiers humming in unison*, you have disadvantage. **On a 10+**, you shake off the effect; **on a 7-9**, you shake off the effect but pick 1.

- Your mind is addled; mark *dazed*
- Your freedom is short-lived; the effect returns if you pause for any reason
- You get carried away or take things too far, resulting in a consequence of the GM's choice

sections.title: The Stream
sections.body:
[[23 - The Great Wood#The Great Wood|200\)]]. Wide, shallow, swift, lined with small stones. Folks come here to wash and fetch water, but rarely alone. It's not deep, but it's still a body of water.

- The current's constant rush and murmur
- Flat plain on either side, gravelly, dotted with saplings, weeds, wildflowers
- Vague sense of dread, of being watched
- **Spring**: cold snowmelt swelling the Stream
- **Summer**: dragonflies, lightning bugs
- **Spring/Autumn**: leaves in current
- **Winter**: eerie stillness, icy surface

Tip: raise your voice a little, like you're talking over background sound

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- What little ritual do villagers perform every time they cross the Stream? Why?
- How far have you followed the Stream? Which way? What strange sight did you see on the way?
- Which of you have encountered the Quiet Twins, the ghostly children who haunt the Stream?
- What's the scariest, darkest story you've ever heard about the Stream? Who told it to you?
- Who do you think the Quiet Twins were? Why?

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- A dangerous beast takes to watering near town.
- The Stream runs dry, despite ample rains
- Something mysterious (dangerous?) is deposited by the current.
- Haunting songs are heard on misty days; hot-blooded youths are intent on finding the source (an entrancing Fae, like **[[12 - Fae#**Blush-of-Dawn**|Blush-of-Dawn]]**).
  - The Quiet Twins appear more frequently, urgently trying to warn of… something.

sections.title: The Quiet Twins
sections.body:
*Group*, *small*, *spirit*, *undead*, *cautious*, *unnerving*

**HP** 13; **Armor** 1 (lacks organs)

**Damage** icy touch d6 (*hand*, disadvantage, ignores armor)

**Special qualities** mute, bound to the Stream **Instinct** to warn of some danger (real or not)

- ä Watch what happens at the Stream (seen or unseen)
- ä Manifest a ghostly form of mist, cold air (harmed only by silver or salt)
- ä Mouth and gesture in silent terror

Tip: shrink into your seat, to make yourself smaller, bug eyed, while mouthing gibberish.

> A pair of ghostly children who haunt the Stream. Sightings are uncommon, but common enough.

sections.title: Tempest Lords
sections.body:
**Tempest Lords**

They were, perhaps, the first of the **[[30 - The Makers#The Makers|Makers]]**. They charted the seas and the stars. They leashed the elements to their will. They built mighty ships and flying castles, sailed forth from their island home and conquered. All who stood against them were reduced to ash and glass and slag.

The pride of the Tempest Lords was warranted, but their hubris led to their fall. They sought to tear their entire homeland free from the earth, to set it floating above the world they ruled.

They failed. Calamity followed.

The seas boiled and belched forth horrors. Fire rained from the sky. Their island capital sank beneath the waves, and the spine of their empire was shattered. Civil war erupted. Colonies rebelled.

The Tempest Lords who survived were scattered to the winds. Some tried to restore the empire, but met violent ends at the hands of rivals or would-be subjects. Soon, only a few Tempest Lords remained, enigmas even among other Makers.

One of these survivors, Stormcatcher, made her way to the **[[14 - The Flats#The Flats|the Flats]]**, beyond even the most far-flung of the empire's old outposts. There, between four other nations of Makers, she built her tower. And when the horrors of the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]** were set loose upon the world, Stormcatcher's tower—what folks now call the **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#The Ruined Tower|Ruined Tower]]** was the last of the Makers' enclaves to fall.

sections.title: Language and names
sections.body:
Tempest Lord writing was ideogrammatic (characters represent ideas, not sounds), and they spoke a primordial language—all who heard it understood it as their native tongue. As such, the names of Tempest Lords are rendered as descriptions, titles, or honorifics.

Example names: Brinedrinker, Eyes-Like-Lightning, Fardweller, Foehammer, Lightning-Is-Her-Spear, His-Smile-Isa-Harbor, Mind-Like-Fire, Moonspeaker, Oarbreaker, Rainchaser, Soother-Of-Waves, Sparkspitter, Squalltalker, Starfinder, Taller-Than-Masts, Voice-of-Thunder, Wavecutter, Waymaker, Windwalker

When other Makers spoke or wrote of them, they used their own tongue. Windwalker was called *Vetrodokha* by the **[[24 - Green Lords#Green Lords|Green Lords]]** and *Avaelkerzhaer* by the **[[43 - Stone Lords#Stone Lords|Stone Lords]]**.

sections.title: Hooks
sections.body:
- A terrible storm sweeps through, a sky-island floating in its eye
- A scholar believes that Stonetop hides a trove of Tempest Lord artifacts
- Cultists seek an orrery with the power to change the stars, to hasten a conjunction that will free their master
- Bandits unearth a Tempest Lord weapon and threaten Stonetop with it
- A **[[26 - Hillfolk#Hillfolk rider|hdour]]** seeks to revive a vitrified Maker

sections.title: Themes
sections.body: Pick, or roll 1d12.

sections.title: 1d12 theme
sections.body:
- 1 Elemental forces and **[[36 - Primordial Powers#Primordial powers|primordial power]]**: wielded and harnessed, perhaps imperfectly
- 2 Storms: thunder and lightning, wrath and fury, power unleashed
- 3 The heavens: weather and clouds, sky-islands, stars and celestial bodies
- 4 Sailing: seas and ships, navigation and exploration
- 5 Machines and devices, **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**Aetherium**|aetherium]]** and stored energy
- 6 Curiosity/experimentation/ invention/hubris
- 7 Loss of control: unexpected consequences/berserk rage/cataclysmic failure/experiments run amok
- 8 An empire collapsed: conquering armies, colonies, plunder, lost glory, resentment
- 9-12 Sized for giants, and roll 1d8 again

sections.title: Sites
sections.body: Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 site
sections.body:
- 1-3 **Lingering signs** of their presence (see below)
- 4-6 A **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**A ruin**|ruin]]**

sections.title: Lingering signs
sections.body: Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

sections.title: 1d6 sign
sections.body:
- 1 A place bearing **scars of unchecked power** (next column)
- 2 **Fragments of a ship or sky-island**  (next page)
- 3-4 **Enigmatic structures** (next page)
- 5 An **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Elemental spirits**|elemental spirit]]** of sky, storm, weather, etc.; bound and tethered
- 6 Ruins of other **[[30 - The Makers#The Makers|Makers]]** or the **[[05 - Barrow Builders#Barrow Builders|Barrow Builders]]**: blasted, shattered, vitrified

sections.title: Scars of unchecked power For example:
sections.body:
- A crater, lousy with fulgurites and shards of glass
- A fractal, branching pattern etched in a bare expanse of stone
- A rune-etched menhir or large hunk of **[[43 - Stone Lords#Makerglass|makerglass]]**, near which some fundamental law or force (time, gravity, space, light, etc.) is warped
- A barren stretch of glass pebbles, strangely colored and always warm
- The **[[45 - Tempest Lords#Vitrification|vitrified]]** remains of a Maker, a monster, a small army, etc. Crude glass shapes hinting at what they once were, flash-frozen in time

Places scarred by such power might very well be **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**Storm-cloud potion**|radioactive]]**, **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|haunted sites]]**, and/or corrupted by **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|the Things Below]]**.

sections.title: Fragments of a ship or sky-island For example:
sections.body:
- A ship's hull, mostly buried but somehow still intact
- A torn sail of **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**Aetherium**|aetherium]]** mesh, strapped to a rusty metal cross
- Strange objects, strewn about and long-buried: a crumpled brass cylinder, an etched metal disk, an oversized ship's wheel, a rudder, etc.
- A rocky outcrop, the stone alien to the surrounding terrain, tilted at an odd angle
- A cracked slab of granite, big as the granary and shot through with aetherium, floating a few feet above the ground and spinning listlessly
- Slabs of worked stone: broken, buried, and scattered, like a child's toy dashed from impossible heights

sections.title: Enigmatic structures For example:
sections.body:
- Iron spires, like hollow rusted trees, placed in a line across the landscape
- A black cube the size of a house; when lightning strikes, the earth lights up for miles with glowing lines that fade over the next few minutes
- Stone plinths along a ridge line, some of them topped with 6-foot-tall sculptures, like tulips, spinning in the breeze
- Copper cylinders, each maybe 8 feet tall and caked in verdigris; mostly buried in white and chalky soil
- Megaliths, etched with ideographs that describe the motions of the stars
- A bronze plaque upon a stone slab, the only notable feature for miles; untouched by lichen; deeply etched ideographs begin with the phrase, "This is not a place of honor"

sections.title: A ruin
sections.body: Pick or roll for themes [[45 - Tempest Lords#**A ruin**|Tempest Lords]], structure, purpose, architectural elements, and condition. Place the ruin in an appropriate terrain (see [[14 - The Flats|The Flats]]  for the **Flats** and [[40 - South Manmarch|South Manmarch]] for the **South Manmarch**, but Tempest Lords ranged far and wide). Populate it with **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**A ruin**|lingering signs]]**, **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**Artifacts**|artifacts]]** and/or **[[45 - Tempest Lords#**Storm-cloud potion**|dangers]]** of the Tempest Lords or the appropriate region.

sections.title: 1d12 structure
sections.body:
- 1-2 Cluster of smaller buildings around some central feature
- 3 Single, larger building (block, dome, ziggurat, etc.)
- 4 Bunker/vault carved into the stone
- 5 Platform/amphitheater/terrace(s) 6 Tower/spire
- 7-9 Sky-island, crashed/grounded (and roll d6 again)
- 10-11 Sky-island, afloat but anchored/ fixed over a particular place (and roll d6 again)
- 12 Sky-island, free-floating across the landscape (and roll d6 again)

sections.title: 1d12 purpose
sections.body:
- 1-2 Dwelling (home, barracks, dormitory, etc.)
- 3-4 Infrastructure (cistern, sewer, lighthouse, harbor, etc.)
- 5-6 Storage (vault, batteries, warehouse, library, museum, etc.)
- 7-8 Production (generator, workshop, shipworks, factory, farm, etc.)
- 9-10 Study/Experimentation (laboratory, observatory, orrery, etc.)
- 11-12 Enigma (magical device, artistic endeavor, etc.)

sections.title: 1d12 architectural elements
sections.body:
- 1-2 Metal beams/cables/lattices/trusses
- 3-4 Open spaces: shafts, atriums, vaulted ceilings, overlooks, windows, skylights
- 5-6 Lines of power: **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#**Ancient wonders**|aetherium inlays]]**, runes, conduits, nodes, currents
- 7-8 Stylized mosaics/statuary: geometric patterns, fractals, celestial bodies, angelic beings, ships
- 9-10 Brass fixtures, pipes and pumps, water and steam, gears and flywheels
- 11-12 Verticality: standing stones, menhirs, obelisks, towers, spires, masts

Most Maker-ruins are sized for giants people two or even three times as tall as humans—but often with smaller-scale rooms, passages, and so forth for their servants.

sections.title: 1d12 condition
sections.body:
- 1-4 Shattered and laid waste by time, disaster, or vandalism; mostly buried, overgrown, collapsed; its purpose unrecognizable
- 5-8 Crumbling, largely buried, overgrown, or hidden away
- 9-10 Buried, overgrown, or hidden, but mostly intact
- 11-12 Fully visible and strangely well-preserved

For more examples, see the **[[39 - The Ruined Tower#The Ruined Tower|Ruined Tower]]**.

*[[48 - Titan Bones#Titan Bones|One of the PCs is heading to Titan Bones]]**, and I'd like some sense of what's out there. I think it holds a Tempest Lord ruin, probably once associated with Stormcatcher and the now-Ruined Tower* [[39 - The Ruined Tower|The Ruined Tower]]*.* 

*I roll a 3 for theme ("the heavens: weather and clouds..."), a 4 for structure ("bunker/ vault"), a 3 for purpose ("infrastructure"), and an 11 for architectural elementals ("verticality"). I then roll a 5 for condition ("largely buried... hidden"). I ponder a bit and come up with this:*

*Stormcatcher erected a spire on Titan Bones, like a lighthouse, topped with an aetherium weathervane that modulated the weather. When the Stone Lords' rebellious servants attacked, the tower's custodian took the weathervane and hid beneath the spire. The spire fell and the custodian was trapped. All that remains are scattered stones and a circular foundation. And beneath centuries of dirt and rubble, there's a locked trapdoor. It reveals a spiral staircase, a deep shaft, an intact cellar, a skeleton, and an aetherium weathervane.*

sections.title: Aetherium
sections.body:
An alloy of copper and lightning. Pure aetherium is light, soft, and ductile (similar to tin), but the Tempest Lords and **[[18 - Forge Lords#Forge Lords|Forge Lords]]** could further alloy it or manipulate it with magic to have a wide range of properties and uses.

Pick or roll for form and characteristic.

sections.title: 1d12 form
sections.body:
- 1-3 Raw material (ingot/spool of wire/ rod/plate/lamé/shard/etc.)
- 4-5 Enigmatic shape (torus/orb/tube/ hoop/runes/etc.), its purpose unclear
- 6-7 Mundane object (tool/vessel/ lantern/etc.), though perhaps not recognized as such
- 8-9 Art or decoration (jewel/statue/ inlay/torc/amulet/ring/etc.)
- 10 Clothing/armor (robe/tunic/apron/ cuirass/brigandine/etc.)
- 11 Weapon (spear/arrow/sword/maul/ shield/trident/wand/etc.)
- 12 Construction (archway/truss/beam/ door/plating/pipes/etc.), likely *immobile*

Consider rolling 1d6 to inform size/scale.

sections.title: 1d12 characteristic
sections.body:
- 1 Breathtakingly beautiful
- 2 Hard and strong as bronze
- 3 Absorbs/stores/discharges/ transforms an elemental force
- 4 Suppresses/repels/directs/impedes a particular element/force
- 5 Vibrates in ways that interact with spirits/magic
- 6 Extremely light/levitates/defies or reverses gravity
- 7 Attunes to another item/locale, for communication/detection
- 8 Roll 1d8 twice
- 9 Unstable, prone to discharge/failure (*dangerous*) and roll d8 again
- 10 Relatively soft/ductile/malleable (*crude*)
- 11-12 Roll again with 1d8, but it becomes unstable and/or soft (as above) after any serious use

sections.title: Making aetherium
sections.body:
A clever arcanist might rediscover the process of making aetherium. To do so, you must…

- Study a largely-intact aetherium crucible for a few weeks
- Study both active and depleted aetherium for a few weeks
- Consult a knowledgeable metalsmith (or be one yourself )
- Acquire the appropriate chemics (Value 0) and a sizeable mass of copper (Value 1, *immobile*)
- Devise some way to introduce lightning to molten copper
- Risk injury, fire, destruction, and failure

When you *meet all the requirements*, you can transform a purse of coppers (Value 0) and the appropriate chemics (Value 0) into an ingot or casting of pure aetherium (*magical*, *crude*, possibly dangerous, Value 1 to those-who-know). The process is risky; you are Defying Danger each time you undertake it.

To imbue aetherium with one of the characteristics above, you'll need access to a trove of Tempest Lord or Forge Lord lore, at least. Beyond that, **Make a Plan** with the GM.

sections.title: AETHERIUM CRUCIBLE
sections.body:
Requires all of the following:

- Learning the process of **making aetherium** (at left)
- Learning to imbue aetherium with at least one special characteristic
- A reliable means of capturing, storing, and directing lightning that strikes the Stone
- Securing a steady supply of the chemics needed to make aetherium
- Securing a steady supply of copper
- An exceptional smith who knows the secrets of aetherium

When you *mark all the requirements*, add "Aetherium Crucible" to the Resources list and the map. As long as you trade aetherium to the outside world, increase Prosperity by 1.

Henceforth, small amounts of raw aetherium (*magical*, Value 1) are commonly available in Stonetop, and you have advantage to **Trade & Barter** for the aetherium items with special characteristics (+2 Value).

sections.title: Various treasures
sections.body:
- A small lump of aetherium (Value 0 to most, Value 1 to those-who-know)
- An oversized aetherium spear-head, dull and soft (*awkward*, *crude*, Value 1)
- A big brass locket, with a needle inside that points towards a specific Tempest Lord site (Value 2)
- A few bolts of canvas, the material unfamiliar but very sturdy (Value 2)
- A thunderstorm trapped in a big **[[43 - Stone Lords#Makerglass|makerglass]]** bottle, capped with aetherium (*dangerous*, Value 3)
- A few tarnished silver coins from the Tempest Lords' empire (Value 1 to most, Value 3 to the right collector)
- A codex of thin, metallic sheets, stamped with star charts and Tempest Lord ideographs (Value 3)
- A wall-sized bronze plaque, showing a map of the entire Tempest Lord empire (*immobile*, Value 4)

sections.title: Aetherium urn
sections.body:
, *magical*, *dangerous*

The lid is held fast by no apparent means. It takes some effort to pry open and eagerly snaps back into place.

When *the urn is open and empty*, it draws raw elemental force into it like a lodestone draws iron. With the lid in place, the force is safely stored. If the urn is damaged or dented (not hard), any stored force escapes, violently.

sections.title: Bronze ring
sections.body:
, *magical*, *fireproof*, lll hours About 8 inches in diameter, an inch thick throughout, scorched and smeared with ash. A button protrudes, with a Tempest Lord glyph that might mean "stubborn."

When you *find the ring*, it has enough power to run for 1d4-1 hours.

When you *press the button*, the ring fixes in place, defying gravity. It simply will not budge. The effect ends if the ring is bent or broken, or when it runs out of power. There's no obvious way to end the effect early. When *the ring runs out of power*, the button pops out and the effect ends.

When *you heat the ring in a forge or kiln* for about eight hours, it gains an hour of use (up to 3 hours).

sections.title: Floating sash
sections.body:
*magical*

A length of bluish-white lamé, maybe 6 inches wide by 6 feet long, snaking about in the air. Like a belt, but for someone far larger than you.

When you *wrap the sash securely around your waist*, it buoys you against falls—you never fall faster than if you had fallen your own height.

sections.title: Silvery tuning fork
sections.body:
*magical*, *loud*

When you *strike this tuning fork against a hard surface*, it emits a loud tone for about a minute. Nearby (*reach*, *area*) magical effects and creatures become visible: shaking, vibrating, distorted. The noise draws the attention of any spirits or magically attuned beings for miles around.

sections.title: Storm-cloud potion
sections.body:
*magical*, *fragile*

A glass vial of dark-grey liquid, roiling and shot through with sparks.

When you *drink a storm-cloud potion*, lightning courses through you. Take 1d10 damage (ignores armor) and mark a debility. Should you survive, hold 3 Storm.

While you *hold any Storm*, you cannot feel pain or fear, tell friend from foe, speak coherently, or exercise patience or care. Add your Storm to your Armor, and your melee and thrown attacks do +Storm damage (+*loud*, +*forceful*).

When you *reduce someone or something to 0 HP*, lose 1 Storm.

sections.title: Minor arcana
sections.body: Pick 1 or have someone roll.

sections.title: 1d6 minor arcanum
sections.body:
- 1 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A half-buried plaque|A half-buried plaque]]**
- 2 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A cloak, richly embroidered|A cloak, richly embroidered]]**
- 3 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A copper-banded staff|A corroded spearhead]]**
- 4 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A copper-banded staff|A copper-banded staff]]**
- 5 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A bow with no string|An odd conveyance]]**
- 6 **[[57 - Appendix C - Minor Arcana#A bow with no string|A bow with no string]]**

sections.title: Major arcana
sections.body:
These might very well be associated with the Tempest Lords.

- The **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Azure Hand|Azure Hand]]**
- **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Ineffable Words|Ineffable Words]]**
- **[[58 - Appendix D - Major Arcana#Mysteries of the Storm Markings|Storm Markings]]**

sections.title: Monsters and entities
sections.body:
- A **[[14 - The Flats#**Thunder drake**|thunder drake]]** or **[[11 - The Dread River#**Luglfsk**|luglfsk]]**, drawn to aetherium
- **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Pyped**|Ghosts]]** or **[[10 - Death and the Undying#**Death and the undying**|revenants]]**, victims of the Tempest Lord's experiments, callousness, or wrath
- An **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Other primordial entities**|archon]]**, ensuring some Tempest Lord artifact goes unused
- An **[[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Elemental spirits**|elemental spirit]]** or a **[[36 - Primordial Powers#**Fundamental**|fundamental]]**, tethered to a device or artifice
- A **[[36 - Primordial Powers#Primordial powers|primordial entity]]** or something **corrupted** by the Things Below [[46 - The Things Below#**1d12** origin|The Things Below]], **[[45 - Tempest Lords#Vitrification|vitrified]]**) and held in stasis.

sections.title: Radiation
sections.body:
Sometimes, the Tempest Lords unleashed such terrible power that it poisoned all it touched: soil, bone, stone, metal. Poisoned them all so deeply that now, still, centuries and centuries later, those things poison all that draws near.

Such poisonous places are often corrupted by the **[[46 - The Things Below#The Things Below|Things Below]]**. But even when they are not, they are dangerous.

- <sup>ä</sup> Point out flora or fauna, stunted and strange
- <sup>ä</sup> Make their skin itch, make them feel unwell
- <sup>ä</sup> Inflict a debility that refuses to go away
- <sup>ä</sup> Have symptoms appear (or return) hours or days after exposure
- <sup>ä</sup> Have symptoms linger or worsen, perhaps for months

PCs or NPCs exposed to such radiation are prone to developing terrible, wasting illness, months or years later. Write this up as a threat (an *affliction*).

sections.title: Vitrification
Elemental vortex
sections.body:
Picture a filigree sphere of aetherium, maybe 6 inches across, a glowing mote swirling at its center. This device-called a matrix-binds an elemental spirit [[41 - Spirits of the Wild#**Elemental spirits**|Spirits of the Wild]] and absorbs a particular force, with which the spirit is able to manifest a swirling vortex.

A spirit bound to a matrix must follow any orders given in the Tempest Lord's primordial language. As few alive can give them such orders, any matrices encountered will likely be acting on orders from centuries ago.

Power and size: Each matrix absorbs a particular type of power, and current HP of its vortex reflects its current power. As a vortex gains or loses HP, its size, armor, and damage change as well. At 0 HP, it dissipates and its matrix falls to the ground, inert until it absorbs more HP.

Pick or roll to determine a vortex's starting size, HP, Armor, and Damage.

sections.title: 1d12 hp stats
sections.body:
| 5-7   | 6 to 11   | Damage d8-2 ( hand ) small ; Armor 2; Damage d8 ( hand )                |
|-------|-----------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 8-9   | 12 to 18  | Armor 2; Damage d10 ( hand , close )                                    |
| 10-11 | 19 to 23  | large ; Armor 3; Damage d10+3 ( hand , close , reach , forceful )       |
| 12    | 24 to 28  | huge ; Armor 3; Damage d10+5 ( hand , close , reach , area , forceful ) |

**Attacking the matrix:** A matrix itself has only 3 HP. If reduced to 0 HP, the spirit within escapes, dealing the vortex's current damage (+*area*, one range tag greater than normal).

When *a PC tries to strike the matrix inside an active vortex*, the vortex's Armor applies. Also, tell them the requirements and/ or consequences. such as:

- You'll have disadvantage to Clash/ Let Fly/deal damage
- You'll need to create or wait for an opening, where the matrix is exposed
- You'll need to get in close/let it engulf you
- You'll need a longer/heavier/*magical* weapon
- You'll risk losing/ruining your weapon
- You'll suffer its attack, no matter what

Pick options based on the nature of the vortex, the PC's weapon, and the rest of their fictional positioning. The larger the vortex, the harder it is to strike the matrix.

sections.title: Earth vortex
sections.body:
*Solitary*, size by HP, *spirit*, *construct*, *amorphous*, *grumpy*, *clumsy*

**HP** varies; **Armor** by HP, +1 (made of rock) **Damage** smash (damage and tags by HP) **Special qualities** half damage from stabbing or slashing weapons (unless *magical*)

**Instinct** to topple and undermine

- ä Manifest a churning mass out of nearby earth, sand, and rocks
- ä Pin a smaller foe (+*grabby*)
- ä Grind a pinned foe, dealing damage w/advantage (+*messy*)
- ä Ruin a sturdy object or structure (costs 1d4 HP)
- ä Shake the earth (+*area*, +*forceful*, one range greater than normal; costs 1d4 HP)

An earth matrix absorbs power from pressure, blunt force, and seismic activity. It gains 1d4 HP from a week buried under earth or stone, from being at the bottom of a rockslide or cave-in, or from touching the ground during a major earthquake (not one it created).

When you *strike an earth matrix with a bludgeoning attack*, it takes no damage. If the blow was forceful and its vortex has 10 HP or less, it gains 1 HP. (The vortex itself has no such immunities, smash away!)

sections.title: Fire vortex
sections.body:
*Solitary*, size by HP, *spirit*, *construct*, *amorphous*, *intense*, *aggressive*

**HP** varies; **Armor** by HP

**Damage** flames & fire (damage and tags by HP, +*dangerous*)

**Special qualities** fireproof; harmed only by *magical* attacks, water, or smothering

sections.title: Instinct to consume
sections.body:
- ä Set nearby combustibles alight
- ä Manifest a vortex out of nearby fire, cinder, smoke, and ash
- ä Extinguish fires, absorbing their power (*near*, *area*)
- ä Engulf a smaller foe (+*messy*, +ignores armor)
- ä Fling flames (+*near*; costs 1d4 HP)
- ä Leap across a barrier (but lose 1d4 HP)

A fire vortex absorbs power from heat and flames. It gains 1d4 HP by spending a day in an oven or an hour in a forge or kiln, or by absorbing a raging fire.

When you *target a fire vortex or its matrix with* **magical** *fire or heat*, it takes no damage. If the vortex has 18 HP or less, it gains 1d4 HP instead.

sections.title: Lightning vortex
sections.body:
*Solitary*, size by HP, *spirit*, *construct*, *amorphous*, *brash*, *arrogant*

**HP** varies; **Armor** by HP

**Damage** zap (damage and tags by HP) **Special qualities** flies; harmed only by *magical* attacks

- **Instinct** to lash out
- ä Manifest a crackling pillar of lightning
- ä Change position, incredibly fast (costs 1 HP)
- ä Stun someone that they zap (costs 1d4 HP)
- ä Fling a lightning bolt (+*near*, +*forceful*, +*loud*, ignores armor; costs 1d4 HP)
- ä Tear the lightning from unattended aetherium, gaining 1d4 HP

A lightning vortex absorbs power from, well, lightning. When it is struck by lightning (mundane or *magical*), it gains HP instead of taking damage.

sections.title: Water vortex
sections.body:
*Solitary*, size by HP, *spirit*, *construct*, *amorphous*, *relentless*, *callous*

**HP** varies; **Armor** by HP

**Damage** pummel (damage and tags by HP) **Special qualities** harmed only by *magical* weapons, cold, or *forceful* blunt attacks

**Instinct** to adjust, flow, and adapt

- ä Manifest a churning wave out of water it touches
- ä Vanish into/burst out of a body of water
- ä Engulf a smaller foe (+*grabby*, see below)
- ä Surge past foes, dragging them off their feet (+*area*, +*forceful*, costs 1d4 HP)
- ä Move across a porous surface (costs 1d4 HP)

A water matrix absorbs power from currents, tides, waves, and falling water. It gains 1d4 HP from a few days in a flowing stream, a few hours in a rushing river, or a few minutes in rapids or a major waterfall.

When you *are engulfed by a water vortex*, hold 1d4 Breath. When you *make a move while engulfed*, and that move involves a roll, spend 1 Breath or mark a debility after rolling. If you can't do either, you drown and are at Death's Door.

sections.title: Wind vortex
sections.body:
*Solitary*, size by HP, *spirit*, *construct*, *amorphous*, *restless*, *fickle*

**HP** varies; **Armor** by HP

**Damage** lashing grit/debris (damage and tags by HP, but w/disadvantage)

**Special qualities** flies, harmed only by *magical* weapons, salt, or lead

sections.title: Instinct to play
sections.body:
- ä Manifest a vortex of churning wind, grit, and debris
- ä Engulf a smaller foe (+*grabby*)
- ä Carry an engulfed foe aloft (costs 1d4 HP)
- ä Fling objects (+near; costs 1d4 HP)
- ä Flee when hurt, frightened, or bored

A wind matrix absorbs power from moving air. It gains 1d4 HP from weeks exposed to normal weather, an hour in high winds, or a minute or so in a tornado.

When you *strike a wind vortex with a salt or lead*, it takes 1d4 damage from a handful, 2d4 damage from a item, and 3d4 damage from a item or amount.

sections.title: The Things Below
sections.body:
**The Things Below**

They are older than the world. They are **[[36 - Primordial Powers#Primordial powers|primordial entities]]** of darkness, chaos, and corruption.

When light first filled the void, they were huddled in the shadows. When matter coalesced, they were there, chewing at its edges. When the First Creators thought "I am me" and named themselves, Those Who Dwelt in Darkness sought their ruin. They whispered lies and stoked egos. They urged the Creators to war amongst themselves. To lay waste to their creations. To let chaos reign.

But the First Creators saw the lies for what they were. They saw the danger posed by the Eldest of Evils. But they saw, too, the utility of such deception.

And so, the Creators laid a trap. They came together to forge the world itself: matter and energy, land and sea and sky, life and death. They set in motion a system so vast and intricate and wondrous that They Who Whispered could not help but be jealous, could not help but try to destroy it.

When the Terrible Ones moved to destroy the world, the First Creators sprung their trap. They folded the world around the Things That Knew Only Hate, sealed them away in rock and stone, bound them in the earth itself. The world— this world, what would become our world—became their tether and their prison. And they, so shackled, became the Things Below.

No prison is perfect. With the slow turn of ages, the bindings weakened. The Things Below whispered once again, and those whispers found ears.

The **[[43 - Stone Lords#Stone Lords|Stone Lords]]** learned firsthand the chaos that those whispers could unleash. They found the cracks in the world-prison and laid great seals upon them. For a time, the whispers were quiet.

But the **[[24 - Green Lords#Green Lords|Green Lords]]** remembered those whispers and what they promised, and called upon the Things Below to crush the traitorous **[[12 - Fae#Fae|Fae]]**. The Green Lords were ultimately defeated, but the real damage had been done: the bindings were weakened. The whispers of the Things Below had returned, had become a song.

The Stone Lords' own servants heard that song. Sorcerers arose among them, gathered followings, then cults. In secret, they laid their plans and spread their lies. When the time was right, the sorcerers broke the seals and ushered in the Time of Cataclysm.

The Stone Lords' cities sank. Mobs of their servants turned against them. In the north, a dark and terrible howling arose and the **[[38 - Rime Lords#Rime Lords|Rime Lords]]** sacrificed themselves to stop it. A wave of horrors burrowed up from the **[[14 - The Flats#The Flats|Flats]]**, bringing the last of the **Tempest Lords** low [[45 - Tempest Lords|Tempest Lords]]. The Things Below stoked the jealousies and fears of the **[[18 - Forge Lords#Forge Lords|Forge Lords]]**), turning them against each other.

The time of the Makers was over.

sections.title: The current age
Hooks
sections.body:
- A local unwittingly offers sacrifice to a corrupted spirit, gaining power; the spirit asks for (and offers) more
- A conundrum vexes the PCs; consulting **[[46 - The Things Below#**A black menhir**|a black menhir]]** might help
- **[[07 - Crinwin#Crinwin|Crinwin]]** have started to worship at a **[[46 - The Things Below#**1d12** origin|corrupted site]]**) becoming corrupted in turn
- **[[29 - Lygos and the South#Adventurer|An antiquarian]]** is secretly a thrall to a Thing Below, and seeks to unleash a more powerful servant
- A spot in the fields around Stonetop goes bad, festering like an infected boil, corrupted by a discarded artifact

sections.title: Everyone knows:
sections.body:
- Evil things are bound deep within the earth
- Sometimes, they send forth demons to wreak havoc upon the world
- Deep water is the most likely place for such horrors to emerge
- **[[32 - Marshedge#Marshedge|Marshedge]]** keeps such horrors at bay by constantly burning bendis root

sections.title: One might know:
sections.body:
- About the Time of Cataclysm and what came after
- The name, titles, aspects, powers, or minions of a given Thing Below
- How their power corrupts places, artifacts, people, beasts, and spirits
- How to bind, cleanse, or ward off their taint/power/minions
- That unclean things recoil from bendis root smoke, but truly fierce or willful ones can overcome it
- That bronze is bane to the Things Below, and that **[[18 - Forge Lords#**1d6** architectural elements|orichalcum]]** is even more effective
- The difference between corrupted beings and emanations

sections.title: Very few know:
sections.body:
- Of their true nature
- How they came to be imprisoned
  - Any way to truly destroy one

sections.title: Questions
sections.body:
- What frightening tale from childhood featured the Things Below?
- What little thing do villagers do to ward off their attention or influence?
- When did you first feel their presence? What happened? What still haunts you about that experience to this day?
- What place nearby is said to be tainted by their influence? What sign marks it as such?
- How do the locals speak of them? In hushed tones? In dire sermons? Somewhere in between?
- When was the last time the village dealt with a demon, cult, or sorcerer?

sections.title: Demons
sections.body: In truth, there's no such thing as demons. Or rather, "demon" is not a meaningful term. It's often used to refer to **[[46 - The Things Below#**Wretch**|emanations]]** of or **[[46 - The Things Below#**1d12** origin|beings corrupted]]** by the Things Below. But folks might use the term to apply to any number of supernatural terrors. If it's hostile, magical, and has (or can take) a physical form, someone is likely to call it a demon.

sections.title: Themes
sections.body: When you introduce a Thing—or more likely, when you introduce a site, artifact, or danger associated with one—choose or roll 2 or more prompts from the theme table here. Combine the results and see where they take you. Alternatively, pick one of the **[[46 - The Things Below#**For We Are Many**|established Things Below]]**, and use its themes.

sections.title: 1d12 theme
sections.body:
- 1 Darkness/cold/despair: the swallowing of light, warmth, and hope (obsidian, hoarfrost)
- 2 Denial/shame/secrets/deceit: the rejection of truth, forgiveness, trust (pearls, eyes, masks)
- 3 Delusion/delirium/nightmare: the loss of self, the breaking of minds and wills (quicksilver, mirrors, reflections)
- 4 Hunger/need/addiction: lack and desperation, the howling want (wind, fangs, meat)
- 5 Cruelty/torture/violence/rage: the urge to hurt, kill, and dominate (red crystal, spilled blood)
- 6 Destruction/chaos/ruin/ignorance: the tower crumbling; anxiety and loss (fire, ash, dust)
- 7 Wounds/injury/pain: the rending of flesh, the breaking of bone (blood, bones, claws, blades)
- 8 Corruption/disease/decay: the loss of strength, wholeness, wellness (vermin, poison, acid, filth)
- 9 Gluttony/greed/jealousy: the need for more, ever more (mouths, serpents, gems, swarms)
- 10 Confinement/suffocation/pressure/ abuse: the crushing weight of helplessness (chains, water, earth)
- 11 Mutation/transformation/roiling flesh: the malleability of the body (wax, clay, slime, tentacles)
- 12 Death/undeath/loss/grief: the end of all things, the refusal to let go (skulls, tombs, pyres)

sections.title: Creating a Thing
sections.body:
As it becomes necessary to flesh out a Thing Below, combine its themes (previous page) with 2 or more aspects to inspire your descriptions of it. Choose or roll an instinct, or invent one of your own.

If a Thing Below becomes an ongoing source of problems for the PCs (and it probably will), then give it a name and one or more grandiose titles. Write it up as a threat (a *magical entity*).

sections.title: 1d12 aspect
sections.body:
- 1 Chitin/shell/carapace/armor 2 An elder/a crone/a stooped old man 3 Fire/light/haze/cloud/vortex/void 4 Horns/wings/tail(s)/hooves
- 5 The inanimate, animated/an amalgamation of things
- 6 Many-limbed/many-headed/
- many-eyed/many-bodied 7 Moisture/tentacles/slime/ooze
- 8 A predator/a warrior/a warlord/ a ruler
- 9 Scales/sinew/suppleness
- 10 Skulls/bones/corpses/death
- 11 Vastness/immensity
- 12 A youth/a child/an infant

Remember that the Things Below are not bound by constraints of biology, physics, or reality. Make their aspects impossible and weird, things of fever-dream and drugfueled vision.

sections.title: 1d12 instinct
sections.body:
- 1 To stoke conflict, confusion, distrust
- 2 To erode hope/faith/honor/ self-image
- 3 To hide/bury/smother things or ideas
- 4 To deprive others of what they need
- 5 To inflict harm, cruelly and unnecessarily
- 6 To desecrate/mutilate/ruin things of value
- 7 To shock/terrify/horrify others
- 8 To wantonly devour and consume
- 9 To befoul and corrupt beauty/ strength/dignity
- 10 To fight and hurt and kill and rage and hurt some more
- 11 To dominate, punish, and crush
- 12 To simply destroy, nothing more and nothing less

Tweak the instinct you choose to reflect your growing understanding of this particular Thing Below.

sections.title: Names & titles
sections.body:
The true names of the Things Below are nigh impossible for humans to comprehend, much less pronounce. What names we have for them are crude approximations. Still, they work well enough to call upon their power.

To name a Thing Below, try one of these:

- Pick an unpleasant word or concept, translate it into a language you and your players don't know ("hunger" >> "Hlad" in Czech)
- Take the name of a supernatural entity from pop culture, literature, or another game; change it a little or a lot ("Dagon" >> "Daagon").
- Combine short, unlikely sounds, often with apostrophes to denote short pauses or inflection ("Hec'tumel")
- Some combination of the above

Then, give each Thing one or more evocative titles, keeping in mind the themes and aspects already established. Look to the lists below for inspiration, altering word forms to suit (i.e., "suppurate" might becomes Suppurating, Suppuration, or Suppurator).

**Article/preoposition:** the, a, an, who, which, that, of, in, on, through, with, without, o

**Verb:** bargain, bleed, break, bring, burn, burrow, bury, butcher, chew, consume, crawl, crush, dance, deceive, deny, desire, despoil, destroy, eat, end, engulf, feed, fester, flense, freeze, haunt, hide, hoard, howl, hunger, keep, know, lack, laugh, lie, melt, offer, promise, rage, rend, roil, rot, scatter, scurry, seep, shape, shatter, sing, slaughter, sleep, slough, smother, snuff, spoil, starve, steal, strain, suppurate, swallow, swarm, topple, unravel, wallow, want, weave, whisper, wither, etc.

**Adjective:** all-consuming, awful, black, blackest, broken, crimson, cruel, dark, dead, desperate, eldest, false, forty, endless, first, forgotten, forlorn, gray, great, hidden, hundred, hungry, implacable, inexorable, lost, mad, many, pale, pallid, red, rusty, secret, terrible, thousand, unkind, unliving, vast, wrathful, yellow

**Role:** child, daughter, father, heir, herald, king, lady, last, lord, master, mistress, mother, prince, princess, thief, queen, son, tyrant, etc.

sections.title: Example names
sections.body:
Use these as a starting point to develop your own Things.

- Ha'z-Ra'iel, Herald of Grief, Thief of a Thousand Hopes
- Na'ieraak, the Liar in Shadows, He Who Freezes the Heart
- Unhlef'k, the Nightmare that Eats, the Dream with Teeth
- Ta'l Themyin, Burning Child, Cruel Infant, Prince of Rage
- H'nisat, the Suppuration, the Wound that Festers
- Hakog, False Wind and Last Whisper of Deceit
- Lalacheewa, Who Sups on Ash and Sorrow

sections.title: For We Are Many
sections.body:
